---
title: "Numerical algorithms, optimisation, and computer performance: a selection of research from 2020"
categories:
- Parallel computing
- Linear algebra
---

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>I won't bore you with clichés about how weird and difficult this year has been. Instead, I'd like to point out straightaway how large this year's selection of papers is: more than 400 papers, which is more than twice the papers I selected last year.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>This year, I haven't changed my Google Scholar query:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:preformatted -->
```algorithm sparse method parallel performance matrix numerical optimization graph```
<!-- /wp:preformatted -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>but I have added some more supplemental papers, as I found fit.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>In the coming year, I expect to add some papers about <em><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computational_sustainability" data-type="URL" data-id="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computational_sustainability" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Computational Sustainability</a></em>, a field I'm getting very interested in. </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2>Some general trends</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul><li>This year, I found several papers about more or less "classic" <em>mathematical optimisation</em> applied to <em>machine learning</em> and <em>deep learning</em>. To me, this was quite predictable, but it's always good to see how powerful and applicable some of those "classic" techniques are.</li><li>Multi-precision algorithms are continuing to grow in popularity in the field of numerical analysis. The idea behind these algorithms is already very in <em>deep learning.</em></li></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Since we have 400 papers this year, I'm going to get some help from <em>machine learning </em>to analyse the data further. <a rel="noreferrer noopener" href="http://picciau.net/wp-content/uploads/2020/12/Papers2020.html" target="_blank"><strong>Click here to see the LiveScript</strong></a><strong>.</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>From the data analysis, I see:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul><li>among the list of papers, the topic of numerical methods applied to tensors occurred a couple of times (<em>topic 15 </em>in my analysis). This is most likely a new trend that I'd like to keep an eye on, even if I don't know much about tensors;</li><li>reading more about the topic of error analysis for multi-precision algorithms (<em>topic 17 </em>in my analysis) matched what I expected from last year's post;</li><li>the topic of matrix partitioning for parallel algorithms and distribution of matrix blocks (<em>topic 8</em> in my analysis) is still occurring in my selection;</li><li><em>sparsity </em>isn't occurring that much in the papers anymore. In practice, it's in only in one of the topics/subtopics my analysis identified (<em>topic 13</em>). This might be because the papers in the selection have increased, but not the papers in this area.</li></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2>List of publications, with abstracts</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<h3 class=3D"sectionHead"><a id=3D"x1-1000"></a>2020 papers:</h3>
<!-- l. 30 -->
<p class=3D"noindent">
</p>
<dl class=3D"thebibliography">
    <dt id=3D"X0-Abdelfattah2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-1" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><a id=3D"page.1"></a><a
                href=3D"file:///C:/U=sers/Andrea%20Picciau/Downloads/bibliography.html" id=3D"X0-"></a>Abdelfatt=
            ah, Ahmad, Hartwig
            Anzt, et al. =E2=80=9CA Survey of Numerical Methods Utilizing M=
            ixed
            Precision Arithmetic=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.066=
            74v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Within the past years, hardware
                vendors have started designing low precision special
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">function units in response to the =
                    demand of the Machine Learning community and
                    their demand
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for high compute power in low prec=
                    ision formats. Also the server-line products
                    are increasingly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">featuring low-precision special fu=
                    nction units, such as the NVIDIA tensor cores
                    in ORNL=E2=80=99s Summit
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">supercomputer providing more than =
                    an order of magnitude higher performance than
                    what is available
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in IEEE double precision. At the s=
                    ame time, the gap between the compute power on
                    the one hand and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the memory bandwidth on the other =
                    hand keeps increasing, making data access and
                    communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prohibitively expensive compared t=
                    o arithmetic operations. To start the
                    multiprecision focus effort, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">survey the numerical linear algebr=
                    a community and summarize all existing
                    multiprecision knowledge,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">expertise, and software capabiliti=
                    es in this landscape analysis report. We also
                    include current efforts
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and preliminary results that may n=
                    ot yet be considered =E2=80=9Dmature technology,=E2=80=9D but
                    have the potential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to grow into production quality wi=
                    thin the multiprecision focus effort. As we
                    expect the reader to be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">familiar with the basics of numeri=
                    cal linear algebra, we refrain from providing a
                    detailed background on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the algorithms themselves but focu=
                    s on how mixed- and multiprecision technology
                    can help improving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the performance of these methods a=
                    nd present highlights of application
                    significantly outperforming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the traditional fixed precision me=
                    thods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Abdelfattah2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-2" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Abdelfattah, Ahmad, Stan Tomov, and Jack Don=
            garra. =E2=80=9CInvestigating the Benefit of
            FP16-Enabled Mixed-Precision Solvers for Symmetric Positive Def=
            inite Matrices Using GPUs=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Lecture Notes in Computer Science</= span>. Springer International Publishing,
                2020,
                pp. 237=E2=80=93250.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                    <= /span>: 10.1007/978-3-030-50417-5=CB=9918.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Half-precision computation refers
                to performing floating-point operations in a 16-bit format.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">While half-precision has been driv=
                    en largely by machine learning applications,
                    recent algorithmic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">advances in numerical linear algeb=
                    ra have discovered beneficial use cases for
                    half precision in accelerating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the solution of linear systems of =
                    equations at higher precisions. In this paper,
                    we present a high-performance,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mixed-precision linear solver (</s= pan><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"0" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%=; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mj= x-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel=" space=3D" 4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mj=x-i" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-m= ath>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi mathvariant==3D"italic">Ax</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                        <mi>b</mi>
                                    </math></m= jx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">)
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for symmetric positive definite sy=
                        stems in double-precision using graphics
                        processing units (GPUs).
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The solver is based on a mixed-pre=
                        cision Cholesky factorization that utilizes the
                        high-performance
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensor core units in CUDA-enabled =
                        GPUs. Since the Cholesky factors are affected
                        by the low precision,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an iterative refinement (IR) solve=
                        r is required to recover the solution back to
                        double-precision accuracy.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Two different types of IR solvers =
                        are discussed on a wide range of test matrices.
                        A preprocessing
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">step is also developed, which scal=
                        es and shifts the matrix, if necessary, in
                        order to preserve its
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">positive-definiteness in lower pre=
                        cisions. Our experiments on the V100 GPU show
                        that performance
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">speedups are up to 4.7</span>
                    <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"1" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position=: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-ma= th>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mo class=3D"MathCl=ass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">against a direct double-precision solver.=
                        However, matrix properties such as the
                        condition number and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the eigenvalue distribution can af=
                        fect the convergence rate, which would
                        consequently affect the overall
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Abdelfattah2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-3" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Abdelfattah, Ahmad, Stanimire Tomov, and Jac=
            k Dongarra. =E2=80=9CMatrix multiplication on batches
            of small matrices in half and half-complex precisions=E2=80=9D.=
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of Parallel and
                Distributed
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computing </span>145 (Nov. 2020)=
            , pp. 188=E2=80=93201. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                    doi</span></span>: 10.1016/j.jpdc.2020.07.001.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Machine learning and artificial
                intelligence (AI) applications often rely on performing many
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">small matrix operations =E2=80=93 =
                    in particular general matrix-matrix multiplication
                    (GEMM). These operations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are usually performed in a reduced=
                    precision, such as the 16-bit floating-point
                    format (i.e., half precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or FP16). The GEMM operation is al=
                    so very important for dense linear algebra
                    algorithms, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">half-precision GEMM operations can=
                    be used in mixed-precision linear solvers.
                    Therefore, high-performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">batched GEMM operations in reduced=
                    precision are significantly important, not
                    only for deep learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">frameworks, but also for scientifi=
                    c applications that rely on batched linear
                    algebra, such as tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">contractions and sparse direct sol=
                    vers.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">This paper presents=
                    optimized batched GEMM kernels for graphics processing units (GPUs) in
                    FP16
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arithmetic. The paper addresses bo=
                    th real and complex half-precision computations
                    on the GPU.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The proposed design takes advantag=
                    e of the Tensor Core technology that was
                    recently introduced in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CUDA-enabled GPUs. With eight tuni=
                    ng parameters introduced in the design, the
                    developed kernels
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have a high degree of flexibility =
                    that overcomes the limitations imposed by the
                    hardware and software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(in the form of discrete configura=
                    tions for the Tensor Core APIs). For real FP16
                    arithmetic, performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">speedups are observed against cuBL=
                    AS for sizes up to 128, and range between
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"2" style=3D"fon=t-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidde=n=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-m= n>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2E"></mjx-c>
                                <= /mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c35">
                                            </mjx-= c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                            <= /mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"i=nline">
                            <mn>1</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">.</mo>
                            <mn>5</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"=MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"3" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math cl=ass=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c2E">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c35">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-cD7">
                            </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml u=nselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998=/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>2</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">.</= mo>
                                <mn>5</mn>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-= mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">For the complex FP16 GEMM kernel, =
                    the speedups are between </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"4" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-= mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2E"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo=>
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c37"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx= -mn>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>=
                                </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inlin=e"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <m= n>1</mn>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">.</mo>
                                <mn>7</mn>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass=-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"5" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math cl=ass=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c37"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>7</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            </= math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">thanks to a design that uses the standard=
                    interleaved matrix layout, in contrast with
                    the planar layout
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">required by the vendor=E2=80=99s s=
                    olution. The paper also discusses special optimizations
                    for extremely small
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, where even higher perfor=
                    mance gains are achievable.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Acebron2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-4" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Acebr=C3=B3n, Juan A. =E2=80=9CA Probabilist=
            ic Linear Solver Based on a Multilevel Monte Carlo Method=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of Scientific Computing </s= pan>82.3 (Feb. 2020). <span
                    class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">d=
                        oi</span></span>: 10.1007/s10915-020-01168-2.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We describe a new Monte Carlo
                method based on a multilevel method for computing the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">action of the resolvent matrix ove=
                    r a vector. The method is based on the
                    numerical evaluation of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Laplace transform of the matrix ex=
                    ponential, which is computed efficiently using
                    a multilevel Monte
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Carlo method. Essentially, it requ=
                    ires generating suitable random paths which
                    evolve through the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">indices of the matrix according to=
                    the probability law of a continuous-time
                    Markov chain governed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by the associated Laplacian matrix=
                    . The convergence of the proposed multilevel
                    method has been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">discussed, and several numerical e=
                    xamples were run to test the performance of the
                    algorithm. These
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">examples concern the computation o=
                    f some metrics of interest in the analysis of
                    complex networks,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the numerical solution of a bo=
                    undary-value problem for an elliptic partial
                    differential equation. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">addition, the algorithm was conven=
                    iently parallelized, and the scalability
                    analyzed and compared with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the results of other existing Mont=
                    e Carlo method for solving linear algebra
                    systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Acer2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-5" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Acer, Seher and Cevdet Aykanat. =E2=80=9CReo=
            rdering sparse matrices into block-diagonal
            column-overlapped form=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Jo=
                urnal of Parallel and Distributed Computing
            </span>(Mar. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sma=ll-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.jpdc.2020.03.002.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many scientific and engineering
                applications necessitate computing the minimum norm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution of a sparse underdetermin=
                    ed linear system of equations. The minimum
                    2-norm solution of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such systems can be obtained by a =
                    recent parallel algorithm, whose numerical
                    effectiveness and parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalability are validated in both =
                    shared- and distributed-memory architectures.
                    This parallel algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">assumes the coefficient matrix in =
                    a block-diagonal column-overlapped (BDCO) form,
                    which is a variant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the block-diagonal form where t=
                    he successive diagonal blocks may overlap along
                    their columns.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The total overlap size of the BDCO=
                    form is an important metric in the performance
                    of the subject
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel algorithm since it determ=
                    ines the size of the reduced system, solution
                    of which is a bottleneck
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operation in the parallel algorith=
                    m. In this work, we propose a hypergraph
                    partitioning model for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reordering sparse matrices into BD=
                    CO form with the objective of minimizing the
                    total overlap size
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the constraint of maintaining =
                    balance on the number of nonzeros of the
                    diagonal blocks. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model makes use of existing partit=
                    ioning tools that support fixed vertices in the
                    recursive bipartitioning

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paradigm. Experimental results val=
                    idate the use of our model as it achieves small
                    overlap size and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">balanced diagonal blocks.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Aggarwal2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-6" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Aggarwal, Charu C. =E2=80=9CThe Linear Algeb=
            ra of Similarity=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Linear
                Algebra and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Optimization for Machine Learnin=
                g</span>. Springer International Publishing,
            2020, pp. 379=E2=80=93410. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-40344-7=CB=999.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A dot-product similarity matrix
                is an alternative way to represent a multidimensional data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">set. In other words, one can conve=
                    rt an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attache=d_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"6" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c1D451 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectabl=e=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Math=ML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>n</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            <mi=>d</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">data matrix </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-con= tainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    c=txtmenu_counter=3D"7" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">

                        </mjx-mi>

                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/199=8/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>D</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mj= x-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">into an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-contain= er class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtm=enu_counter=3D"8" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-mat= h class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-b=in" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx= -math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>n</mi>
                            <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            <mi>n</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx= -container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">similarity matrix </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"9" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relativ=e;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c1D446 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-= mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I">
                                    </mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D447 TEX-I">
                                        <= /mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"i=nline">
                                <mi>S</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"ita=lic">DDT</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">(which contains </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx= -container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"=0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"10" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;"=>
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unse=lectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Ma=th/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>n</mi>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mm= l>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">pairwise dot products between points). On=
                                    e can use </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Att=ached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"11" style=3D"font=-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden==3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D446 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c=>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inli=ne"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <= mi>S</mi>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">instead of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-cont= ainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ct=xtmenu_counter=3D"12" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml=unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/199=8/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>D</mi>
                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mj= x-container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">for machine learning algorithms. The reas=
                                            on is that the similarity matrix contains
                                            almost the same
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">information about the data as the =
                                            original matrix. This equivalence is the
                                            genesis of a large class of
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods in machine learning, refer=
                                            red to as kernel methods. This chapter builds
                                            the linear algebra
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">framework required for understandi=
                                            ng this important class of methods in machine
                                            learning. The real
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">utility of such methods arises whe=
                                            n the similarity matrix is chosen differently
                                            from the use of dot
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">products (and the data matrix is s=
                                            ometimes not even available).</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Agullo2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-7" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Agullo, Emmanuel, Mirco Altenbernd, et al. =
            =E2=80=9CResiliency in Numerical Algorithm Design for
            Extreme Scale Simulations=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 201=
            0.13342 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This work is based on the seminar
                titled =E2=80=9CResiliency in Numerical Algorithm Design for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Extreme Scale Simulations=E2=80=9D=
                    held March 1-6, 2020 at Schloss Dagstuhl, that was
                    attended by all the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">authors. Naive versions of convent=
                    ional resilience techniques will not scale to
                    the exascale regime: with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a main memory footprint of tens of=
                    Petabytes, synchronously writing checkpoint
                    data all the way
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to background storage at frequent =
                    intervals will create intolerable overheads in
                    runtime and energy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consumption. Forecasts show that t=
                    he mean time between failures could be lower
                    than the time to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">recover from such a checkpoint, so=
                    that large calculations at scale might not
                    make any progress if
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">robust alternatives are not invest=
                    igated. More advanced resilience techniques
                    must be devised. The key
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">may lie in exploiting both advance=
                    d system features as well as specific
                    application knowledge. Research
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">will face two essential questions:=
                    (1) what are the reliability requirements for
                    a particular computation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and (2) how do we best design the =
                    algorithms and software to meet these
                    requirements? One avenue
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">would be to refine and improve on =
                    system- or application-level checkpointing and
                    rollback strategies
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in the case an error is detected. =
                    Developers might use fault notification
                    interfaces and flexible runtime
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems to respond to node failure=
                    s in an application-dependent fashion. Novel
                    numerical algorithms or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">more stochastic computational appr=
                    oaches may be required to meet accuracy
                    requirements in the face
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of undetectable soft errors. The g=
                    oal of this Dagstuhl Seminar was to bring
                    together a diverse group
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of scientists with expertise in ex=
                    ascale computing to discuss novel ways to make
                    applications resilient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">against detected and undetected fa=
                    ults. In particular, participants explored the
                    role that algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and applications play in the holis=
                    tic approach needed to tackle this
                    challenge.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Agullo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-8" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Agullo, Emmanuel, Siegfried Cools, et al. <s= pan class=3D"cmti-10">On soft errors in the
                Conjugate Gradient method:
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">sensitivity and robust numerical=
                    detection</span>. Tech. rep. INRIA, 2020. HAL:
                hal-02495301. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-cap=s">url</span></span>:
                <a href=3D"https://hal.inria.fr/hal-02495301" class=3D"url">
                    <sp= an class=3D"cmtt-10">https://hal.inria.fr/hal-02495301</span>
                </a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The conjugate gradient (CG)
                method is the most widely used iterative scheme for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution of large sparse systems o=
                    f linear equations when the matrix is symmetric
                    positive definite.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Although more than sixty year old,=
                    it is still a serious candidate for
                    extreme-scale computation on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large computing platforms. On the =
                    technological side, the continuous shrinking of
                    transistor geometry
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the increasing complexity of t=
                    hese devices affect dramatically their
                    sensitivity to natural radiation,

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and thus diminish their reliabilit=
                    y. One of the most common effects produced by
                    natural radiation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is the single event upset which co=
                    nsists in a bit-flip in a memory cell producing
                    unexpected results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at application level. Consequently=
                    , the future computing facilities at extreme
                    scale might be more
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prone to errors of any kind includ=
                    ing bit-flip during calculation. These
                    numerical and technological
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">observations are the main motivati=
                    ons for this work, where we first investigate
                    through extensive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical experiments the sensitiv=
                    ity of CG to bit-flips in its main
                    computationally intensive kernels,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">namely the matrix-vector product a=
                    nd the preconditioner application. We further
                    propose numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">criteria to detect the occurrence =
                    of such soft errors; we assess their robustness
                    through extensive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical experiments.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ahmad2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-9" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ahmad, Najeeb, Buse Yilmaz, and Didem Unat. =
            =E2=80=9CA Prediction Framework for Fast Sparse
            Triangular Solves=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Euro-Pa=
                r 2020: Parallel Processing</span>. Springer
            International Publishing,
            2020, pp. 529=E2=80=93545. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-57675-2=CB=9933.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse triangular solve (SpTRSV)
                is an important linear algebra kernel, finding extensive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">uses in numerical and scientific c=
                    omputing. The parallel implementation of SpTRSV
                    is a challenging
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">task due to the sequential nature =
                    of the steps involved. This makes it, in many
                    cases, one of the most
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time-consuming operations in an ap=
                    plication. Many approaches for efficient SpTRSV
                    on CPU and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU systems have been proposed in =
                    the literature. However, no single
                    implementation or platform
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(CPU or GPU) gives the fastest sol=
                    ution for all input sparse matrices. In this
                    work, we propose a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">machine learning-based framework t=
                    o predict the SpTRSV implementation giving the
                    fastest execution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time for a given sparse matrix bas=
                    ed on its structural features. The framework is
                    tested with six
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SpTRSV implementations on a state-=
                    of-the-art CPU-GPU machine (Intel Xeon Gold
                    CPU, NVIDIA
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">V100 GPU). Experimental results, w=
                    ith 998 matrices taken from the SuiteSparse
                    Matrix Collection,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show the classifier prediction acc=
                    uracy of 87% for the fastest SpTRSV algorithm
                    for a given input
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix. Predicted SpTRSV implement=
                    ations achieve average speedups (harmonic mean)
                    in the range of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">1.4=E2=80=932.7</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -=
-><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabind=
ex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"13" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rela=
tive;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mo class=3D"mj=
x-n MathClass-bin"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-math><mj=
x-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http:=
//www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"><mo class=3D"MathClass-bi=
n">=C3=97</mo></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">against the six SpTRSV implementations us=
ed in the evaluation.</span>
         </p>
      </dd>
      <dt id=3D"X0-Ahmadi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
      </dt>
      <dd id=3D"bib-10" class=3D"thebibliography">
         <!-- l. 30 -->
                <p class=3D"noindent">Ahmadi, Amir Ali and Jeffrey Zhang. =E2=80=
                    =9CComplexity aspects of local minima and related
                    notions=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). arXiv: 2008.06148v1 <span cl=ass=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
                </p><!-- l. 30 -->
                <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We consider the notions of (i)
                        critical points, (ii) second-order points, (iii) local minim=
                        a,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and (iv) strict local minima for m=
                            ultivariate polynomials. For each type of
                            point, and as a function
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the degree of the polynomial, w=
                            e study the complexity of deciding (1) if a
                            given point is of that
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">type, and (2) if a polynomial has =
                            a point of that type. Our results characterize
                            the complexity of
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">these two questions for all degree=
                            s left open by prior literature. Our main
                            contributions reveal that
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many of these questions turn out t=
                            o be tractable for cubic polynomials. In
                            particular, we present
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an efficiently-checkable necessary=
                            and sufficient condition for local minimality
                            of a point for a cubic
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">polynomial. We also show that a lo=
                            cal minimum of a cubic polynomial can be
                            efficiently found by
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving semidefinite programs of s=
                            ize linear in the number of variables. By
                            contrast, we show that it
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is strongly NP-hard to decide if a=
                            cubic polynomial has a critical point. We also
                            prove that the set of
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">second-order points of any cubic p=
                            olynomial is a spectrahedron, and conversely
                            that any spectrahedron
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is the projection of the set of se=
                            cond-order points of a cubic polynomial. In our
                            final section, we
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">briefly present a potential applic=
                            ation of finding local minima of cubic
                            polynomials to the design of a
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">third-order Newton method.</span>
                </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ahrens2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-11" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ahrens, Peter. =E2=80=9CLoad Plus Communicat=
            ion Balancing in Contiguous Partitions for Distributed
            Sparse Matrices: Linear-Time Algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: (July 202=
            0). arXiv: 2007.16192v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We study partitioning to
                parallelize multiplication of one or more dense vectors by a=
                sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix (SpMV or SpMM). We consider=
                    contiguous partitions, where the rows (or
                    columns) of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix are split into </span>
                <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"14" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positio=n: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <= mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>K</mi>
                        </math></mjx-= assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">parts without reordering. We present exac=
                    t and approximate contiguous partitioning
                    algorithms that

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">minimize the runtime of the longes=
                    t-running processor under cost models that
                    combine work factors
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and hypergraph communication facto=
                    rs. This differs from traditional graph or
                    hypergraph partitioning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">models which minimize total commun=
                    ication under a work balance constraint. We
                    address regimes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where partitions of the row space =
                    and column space are expected to match (the
                    symmetric case) or are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">allowed to differ (the nonsymmetri=
                    c case). Our algorithms use linear space. Our
                    exact algorithm runs in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear time when </span>
                <!-- l. 30=
 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabi=ndex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"15" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: re=lative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"=mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>K</mi>
                            <mn>2</mn>
                        </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is sublinear. Our </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"16" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relati=ve;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo cla=ss=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-= mrow>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx= -mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B">
                                        </mj= x-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close"=>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assis= tive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w=3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-ope=n">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                    <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96=
                                    </mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assisti= ve-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-approximate
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm runs in linear time when=
                        </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"17" style=3D"font-size: 11=7.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"=>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi=>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-n qopname" space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6C">
                                            </mjx= -c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c67"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-= mi>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2061"></mjx-c>
                                                <= /mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin"=space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-=i" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-mro= w>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>=
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                                    <mi>K</mi>
                                    <mi class=3D"qopname">log</mi>
                                    <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <m= o class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mn>1</mn>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin=">=E2=88=95</mo><mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi></mrow><mo class=3D"
                                                    MathClass-close">)=
                                                </mo>
                                            </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">is sublinear. We combine concepts from hi=
                            gh-performance computing and computational
                            geometry.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Existing load balancing algorithms=
                            optimize a linear model of per-processor work.
                            We make minor
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adaptations to optimize arbitrary =
                            nonuniform monotonic increasing or decreasing
                            cost functions which
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">may be expensive to evaluate. We t=
                            hen show that evaluating our model of
                            communication is equivalent
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to planar dominance counting. We s=
                            pecialize Chazelle=E2=80=99s dominance counting
                            algorithm to points in the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bounded integer plane and generali=
                            ze it to trade reduced construction time for
                            increased query time,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">since our partitioners make very f=
                            ew queries. Our algorithms split the original
                            row (or column) ordering
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">into parts to optimize diverse cos=
                            t models. Combined with reordering or embedding
                            techniques, our
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms might be used to build =
                            more general heuristic partitioners, as they
                            can optimally round
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">one-dimensional embeddings of dire=
                            ct </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0=" jax=3D" CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"18" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tru=e">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-= mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                <ma= th xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>K
                </m= i></math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-way
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">noncontiguous partitioning problem=
                    s.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ahrens2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-12" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ahrens, Peter and Erik G. Boman. =E2=80=9COn=
            Optimal Partitioning For Sparse Matrices In Variable
            Block Row Format=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.12414v1 =
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Variable Block Row (VBR)
                format is an influential blocked sparse matrix format
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">designed to represent shared spars=
                    ity structure between adjacent rows and
                    columns. VBR consists of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">groups of adjacent rows and column=
                    s, storing the resulting blocks that contain
                    nonzeros in a dense
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">format. This reduces the memory fo=
                    otprint and enables optimizations such as
                    register blocking and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instruction-level parallelism. Exi=
                    sting approaches use heuristics to determine
                    which rows and columns
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">should be grouped together. We ada=
                    pt and optimize a dynamic programming algorithm
                    for sequential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hypergraph partitioning to produce=
                    a linear time algorithm which can determine
                    the optimal partition
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of rows under an expressive cost m=
                    odel, assuming the column partition remains
                    fixed. Furthermore, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show that the problem of determini=
                    ng an optimal partition for the rows and
                    columns simultaneously
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is NP-Hard under a simple linear c=
                    ost model. To evaluate our algorithm
                    empirically against existing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">heuristics, we introduce the 1D-VB=
                    R format, a specialization of VBR format where
                    columns are left
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ungrouped. We evaluate our algorit=
                    hms on all 1626 real-valued matrices in the
                    SuiteSparse Matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Collection. When asked to minimize=
                    an empirically derived cost model for a sparse
                    matrix-vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication kernel, our algorit=
                    hm produced partitions whose 1D-VBR
                    realizations achieve a speedup
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of at least 1.18 over an unblocked=
                    kernel on 25% of the matrices, and a speedup
                    of at least 1.59 on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">12.5% of the matrices. The 1D-VBR =
                    representation produced by our algorithm had
                    faster SpMVs than
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the 1D-VBR representations produce=
                    d by any existing heuristics on 87.8% of the
                    test matrices.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ahrens2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-13" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ahrens, Peter, James Demmel, and Hong Nguyen=
            . =E2=80=9CAlgorithms for Efficient Reproducible
            Floating Point Summation=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                ACM Transactions on Mathematical Software </span>0.ja
            (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">iss=
                    n</span></span>:
            0098-3500. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                    doi</span></span>: 10.1145/3389360. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D="small-caps">url</span></span>: <a href=3D"https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.11=45/3389360"
                class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://dl.acm.org/doi/ab=
                    s/10.1145/3389360</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We define reproducibility to mean
                getting bitwise identical results from multiple runs of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">same program, perhaps with differe=
                    nt hardware resources or other changes that
                    should not change
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the answer. Many users depend on r=
                    eproducibility for debugging or correctness.
                    However, dynamic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scheduling of parallel computing r=
                    esources, with nonassociativity of
                    floating-point addition, makes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reproducibility a challenge even f=
                    or summing a vector of numbers, or the Basic
                    Linear Algebra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Subprograms (BLAS). We describe an=
                    algorithm that computes a reproducible
                    floating point sum,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">independent of summation order. Th=
                    e algorithm uses only a subset of IEEE Standard
                    754-2008. It is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication-optimal, i.e. it doe=
                    s just one pass over the data in the sequential
                    case, or one reduction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operation in parallel, requiring a=
                    n </span><span class=3D"cmti-9">accumulator
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of just 6 words (higher precision =
                    is possible). The

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arithmetic cost is </span>
                <!-- l. =
30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" ta=bindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"19" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:=relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class==3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c37"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                            </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assist= ive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3=.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>7</mn>
                            <mi>n</mi>
                        </math></mjx-= assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">additions to sum </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mj= x-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D="0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"20" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;="><mjx-math class=3D"
                    MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.o=rg/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>n</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mm= l>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">words, and the error bound can be up to <= /span>
                                <!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" ja=x=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"21" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <= mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c30"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-open">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-m= n class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c38"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mn>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx= -mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-= mo>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"=inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inlin=e">
                                            <mn>10</mn>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mo class=3D"M=athClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                                    <mn>8</mn>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">=
                                                    )</mo>
                                            </mrow>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">times smaller than for conventional summa=
                                    tion. We describe the algorithm, the software
                                    infrastructure
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for reproducible BLAS (ReproBLAS),=
                                    and performance results. For example, for the
                                    dot product of
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">4096 doubles, we get a 4</span>
                                <!-= - l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTM=L" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"22" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posit=ion: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                            <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
