                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx= -math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">slowdown compared to Intel MKL on an Inte=
                                        l Core i7-2600 CPU operating at 3.4 GHz and 256
                                        KB
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">L2 Cache.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-AlAhmadi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-14" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">AlAhmadi, Sarah et al. =E2=80=9CPerformance =
            Characteristics for Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiplication
            on GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Smart Infrastruct=
                ure and Applications: Foundations for Smarter Cities
                and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Societies</span>. Ed. by Rashid =
            Mehmood et al. Cham: Springer International
            Publishing, 2020,
            pp. 409=E2=80=93426. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">isbn</span></span>:
            978-3-030-13705-2. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <= span class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>: 10.1007/978-3-030-13705-2=CB=
            =9917.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The massive parallelism provided
                by the graphics processing units (GPUs) offers tremendous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance in many high-performan=
                    ce computing applications. One such application
                    is Sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Matrix-Vector (SpMV) multiplicatio=
                    n, which is an essential building block for
                    numerous scientific and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">engineering applications. Research=
                    ers who propose new storage techniques for
                    sparse matrix-vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication focus mainly on a s=
                    ingle evaluation metrics or performance
                    characteristics which is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">usually the throughput performance=
                    of sparse matrix-vector multiplication in
                    FLOPS. However, such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an evaluation does not provide a d=
                    eeper insight nor allow to compare new SpMV
                    techniques with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">their competitors directly. In thi=
                    s chapter, we explain the notable performance
                    characteristics of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU architectures and SpMV computa=
                    tions. We discuss various strategies to improve
                    the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of SpMV on GPUs. We also discuss a=
                    few performance criteria that are usually
                    overlooked by the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">researchers during the evaluation =
                    process. We also analyze various well-known
                    schemes such as COO,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CSR, ELL, DIA, HYB, and CSR5 using=
                    the discussed performance
                    characteristics.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Alappat2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-15" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Alappat, Christie L., Andreas Alvermann, Ach=
            im Basermann, Holger Fehske, Yasunori
            Futamura, Martin Galgon, Georg Hager, Sarah Huber, Akira Imakur=
            a, Masatoshi Kawai,
            Moritz Kreutzer, Bruno Lang, Kengo Nakajima, Melven R=C3=B6hrig=
            -Z=C3=B6llner, et al. =E2=80=9CEquipping
            Sparse Solvers For Exascale=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-1=0">Software for Exascale Computing =E2=80=93
                SPPEXA
                2016-2019</span>.
            Lecture Notes on Computational Science and Engineering (2020), =
            pp. 143=E2=80=93187.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The ESSEX project has
                investigated programming concepts, data structures, and nume=
                rical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms for scalable, efficient=
                    , and robust sparse eigenvalue solvers on
                    future heterogeneous exascale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. Starting without the burd=
                    en of legacy code, a holistic performance
                    engineering process could
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be deployed across the traditional=
                    software layers to identify efficient
                    implementations and guide
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sustainable software development. =
                    At the basic building blocks level, a flexible
                    MPI+X programming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approach was implemented together =
                    with a new sparse data structure
                    (SELL-C-</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax=CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"23"=style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX=" aria-hidden=3D" true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D70E T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" di=splay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi>=CF=83</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span= class=3D"cmr-9">)
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to support heterogeneous architect=
                        ures by design. Furthermore, ESSEX focused on
                        hardware-efficient
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels for all relevant architect=
                        ures and efficient data structures for block
                        vector formulations of the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">eigensolvers. The algorithm layer =
                        addressed standard, generalized, and nonlinear
                        eigenvalue problems
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and provided some widely usable so=
                        lver implementations including a block
                        JacobiDavidson algorithm,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">contour-based integration schemes,=
                        and filter polynomial approaches. Adding to
                        the highly efficient
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernel implementations, algorithmi=
                        c advances such as adaptive precision,
                        optimized filtering coefficients,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and preconditioning have further i=
                        mproved time to solution. These developments
                        were guided by the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">field of quantum physics applicati=
                        ons, and especially by current topics such as
                        topological insulator
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems or problems from graphene =
                        research. For these, ScaMaC, a scalable matrix
                        generation framework
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for a broad set of quantum physics=
                        problems, was developed. As the central
                        software core of ESSEX, the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PHIST library for sparse linear an=
                        d eigenvalue problems has been established. It
                        abstracts algorithmic
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developments from low-level optimi=
                        zation. Finally, central ESSEX software
                        components and solvers
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have demonstrated scalability and =
                        hardware efficiency on up to 256 K cores using
                        million-way process/thread-level
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelism.</span>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Alappat2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-16" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CESSEX: Equipping Sparse S=
            olvers For Exascale=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Software for
                Exascale Computing =E2=80=93
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">SPPEXA 2016=E2=80=932019</span>.=
            Ed. by Hans-Joachim Bungartz et al. Springer
            International Publishing,
            2020, pp. 143=E2=80=93187. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">isbn</span></span>:
            978-3-030-47956-5.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The ESSEX project has
                investigated programming concepts, data structures, and nume=
                rical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms for scalable, efficient=
                    , and robust sparse eigenvalue solvers on
                    future heterogeneous exascale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. Starting without the burd=
                    en of legacy code, a holistic performance
                    engineering process could
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be deployed across the traditional=
                    software layers to identify efficient
                    implementations and guide
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sustainable software development. =
                    At the basic building blocks level, a flexible
                    MPI+X programming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approach was implemented together =
                    with a new sparse data structure
                    (SELL-C-</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax=CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"24"=style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX=" aria-hidden=3D" true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D70E T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" di=splay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi>=CF=83</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span= class=3D"cmr-9">)
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to support heterogeneous architect=
                        ures by design. Furthermore, ESSEX focused on
                        hardware-efficient
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels for all relevant architect=
                        ures and efficient data structures for block
                        vector formulations of the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">eigensolvers. The algorithm layer =
                        addressed standard, generalized, and nonlinear
                        eigenvalue problems
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and provided some widely usable so=
                        lver implementations including a block
                        Jacobi=E2=80=93Davidson algorithm,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">contour-based integration schemes,=
                        and filter polynomial approaches. Adding to
                        the highly efficient
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernel implementations, algorithmi=
                        c advances such as adaptive precision,
                        optimized filtering coefficients,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and preconditioning have further i=
                        mproved time to solution. These developments
                        were guided by
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quantum physics applications, espe=
                        cially from the field of topological insulator-
                        or graphene-based
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. For these, ScaMaC, a scal=
                        able matrix generation framework for a broad
                        set of quantum
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">physics problems, was developed. A=
                        s the central software core of ESSEX, the PHIST
                        library for sparse
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems of linear equations and ei=
                        genvalue problems has been established. It
                        abstracts algorithmic
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developments from low-level optimi=
                        zation. Finally, central ESSEX software
                        components and solvers
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have demonstrated scalability and =
                        hardware efficiency on up to 256 K cores using
                        million-way process/thread-level
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelism.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Alghunaim2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-17" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Alghunaim, Sulaiman A. =E2=80=9COn the Perfo=
            rmance and Linear Convergence of Decentralized
            Primal-Dual Methods=E2=80=9D. PhD thesis. University of Califor=
            nia Los Angeles, 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This dissertation studies the
                performance and linear convergence properties of primal-dual
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods for the solution of decent=
                    ralized multi-agent optimization problems.
                    Decentralized multi-agent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization is a powerful paradig=
                    m that finds applications in diverse fields in
                    learning and engineering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">design. In these setups, a network=
                    of agents is connected through some topology
                    and agents are allowed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to share information only locally.=
                    Their overall goal is to seek the minimizer of
                    a global optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem through localized interact=
                    ions. In decentralized consensus problems, the
                    agents are coupled
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through a common consensus variabl=
                    e that they need to agree upon. While in
                    decentralized resource
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">allocation problems, the agents ar=
                    e coupled through global affine
                    constraints.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-=9">Various decentralized consensus
                    optimization
                    algorithms already exist in the literature. Some methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are derived from a primal-dual per=
                    spective, while other methods are derived as
                    gradient tracking
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mechanisms meant to track the aver=
                    age of local gradients. Among the gradient
                    tracking methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are the adapt-then-combine impleme=
                    ntations motivated by diffusion strategies,
                    which have been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">observed to perform better than ot=
                    her implementations. In this dissertation, we
                    develop a novel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adapt-then-combine primal-dual alg=
                    orithmic framework that captures most
                    state-of-the-art gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based methods as special cases inc=
                    luding all the variations of the
                    gradient-tracking methods. We also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">develop a concise and novel analys=
                    is technique that establishes the linear
                    convergence of this general
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">framework under stronglyii convex =
                    objectives. Due to our unified framework, the
                    analysis reveals
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">important characteristics for thes=
                    e methods such as their convergence rates and
                    step-size stability
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ranges. Moreover, the analysis rev=
                    eals how the augmented Lagrangian penalty term,
                    which is utilized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in most of these methods, affects =
                    the performance of decentralized
                    algorithms.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9=">Another important question that we answer is
               whether decentralized proximal gradient methods can
            </span><span class=3D" cmr-9">achieve global linear convergence =
                    for non-smooth composite optimization. For
                    centralized algorithms,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear convergence has been establ=
                    ished in the presence of a nonsmooth composite
                    term. In this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dissertation, we close the gap bet=
                    ween centralized and decentralized proximal
                    gradient algorithms and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show that decentralized proximal a=
                    lgorithms can also achieve linear convergence
                    in the presence of a

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-smooth term. Furthermore, we s=
                    how that when each agent possesses a different
                    local non-smooth
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">term then global linear convergenc=
                    e cannot be established in the worst
                    case.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">Most=
                    works that study decentralized optimization
                    problems assume that all agents are involved
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in computing all variables. Howeve=
                    r, in many applications the coupling across
                    agents is sparse in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the sense that only a few agents a=
                    re involved in computing certain variables. We
                    show how to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">design decentralized algorithms in=
                    sparsely coupled consensus and resource
                    allocation problems. More
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">importantly, we establish analytic=
                    ally the importance of exploiting the sparsity
                    structure in coupled
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large-scale networks.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Aliaga2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-18" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Aliaga, Jos=C3=A9 I. et al. =E2=80=9CCompres=
            sed Basis GMRES on High Performance GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept.
            2020). arXiv: 2009.12101 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>=
            .
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Krylov methods provide a fast and
                highly parallel numerical tool for the iterative solution of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many large-scale sparse linear sys=
                    tems. To a large extent, the performance of
                    practical realizations of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">these methods is constrained by th=
                    e communication bandwidth in all current
                    computer architectures,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">motivating the recent investigatio=
                    n of sophisticated techniques to avoid, reduce,
                    and/or hide the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">message-passing costs (in distribu=
                    ted platforms) and the memory accesses (in all
                    architectures).
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This paper introduces a new commun=
                    ication-reduction strategy for the (Krylov)
                    GMRES solver that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">advocates for decoupling the stora=
                    ge format (i.e., the data representation in
                    memory) of the orthogonal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">basis from the arithmetic precisio=
                    n that is employed during the operations with
                    that basis. Given that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the execution time of the GMRES so=
                    lver is largely determined by the memory
                    access, the datatype
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">transforms can be mostly hidden, r=
                    esulting in the acceleration of the iterative
                    step via a lower volume
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of bits being retrieved from memor=
                    y. Together with the special properties of the
                    orthonormal basis
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(whose elements are all bounded by=
                    1), this paves the road toward the aggressive
                    customization of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">storage format, which includes som=
                    e floating point as well as fixed point formats
                    with little impact
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the convergence of the iterativ=
                    e process. We develop a high performance
                    implementation of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">=E2=80=9Dcompressed basis GMRES=E2=
                    =80=9D solver in the Ginkgo sparse linear algebra library and
                    using a large set
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of test problems from the SuiteSpa=
                    rse matrix collection we demonstrate robustness
                    and performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">advantages on a modern NVIDIA V100=
                    GPU of up to 50% over the standard GMRES
                    solver that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stores all data in IEEE double pre=
                    cision.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Alimisis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-19" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Alimisis, Foivos et al. =E2=80=9CPractical A=
            ccelerated Optimization on Riemannian Manifolds=E2=80=9D. In:
            (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.04144 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC=
                ]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We develop a new Riemannian
                descent algorithm with an accelerated rate of convergence.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We focus on functions that are geo=
                    desically convex or weakly-quasi-convex, which
                    are weaker function
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">classes compared to prior work tha=
                    t has considered geodesically strongly convex
                    functions. Our proof
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of convergence relies on a novel e=
                    stimate sequence which allows to demonstrate
                    the dependency of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rate on the curvature =
                    of the manifold. We validate our theoretical
                    results empirically on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">several optimization problems defi=
                    ned on a sphere and on the manifold of positive
                    definite matrices.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Alimo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-20" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Alimo, Ryan, Pooriya Beyhaghi, and Thomas R.=
            Bewley. =E2=80=9CDelaunay-based derivative-free
            optimization via global surrogates. Part III: nonconvex constra=
            ints=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of
                Global
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Optimization </span>(Jan. 2020).=
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">issn</span></span>: 15=
            73-2916. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></sp= an>:
                10.1007/s10898-019-00854-2.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper introduces a
                Delaunay-based derivative-free optimization algorithm, dubbe=
                d</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"25" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6E5 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c2212"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212">
                                    </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I">
                                    </mjx-c=>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D446 TEX-I="></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mrow space=3D" 2">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-o=pen">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"m=jx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FA TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo= class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>=
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mi>=CE=94</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                            <mo class=3D"MathC=lass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">DOGS</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class==3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=CE=A9</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClas=s-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span clas=s=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for problems with both (a) a nonco=
                    nvex, computationally expensive objective
                    function </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJa=x CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"26"=style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TE=X" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <m= jx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mj= x-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"i=nline">
                            <mi>f</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>x</mi>
                                    <= /mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml=>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and (b) nonlinear, computationally=
                    expensive constraint functions </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"27" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D450=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0=.192em;">
                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=2195"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I">
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-= mrow>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unsel=ectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Mat=h/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>c</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=E2=86=
                                        =95</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>x</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo= class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </mrow>
                            </msub>
                        </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">which, taken together, define a nonconvex=
                    , possibly even disconnected feasible domain
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"

                <span class=3D"cmr-9">which is assumed to lie within a known re=
                    ctangular search domain </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->

                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"29" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I"></mjx-c>

                                </mjx-mrow>

                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,

                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"=CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"

                        </mjx-math>
                        <mj= x-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math

                                <mi>f</mi>

                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"31" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                <mjx-s= cript style=3D"vertical-align: -0.192em;">

                                            <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c29"></mjx-c>

                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-script>

                            </mj= x-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmln=s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <msub>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi=>c</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>=E2=86=95</mi>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(<= /mo>

                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">may be evaluated. Approximations of both =
                        the objective function </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"32" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mj= x-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-=n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                </m= jx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>f</mi>
                                        <mro= w>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>x</mi>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-close">)</mo>
                                            </mrow>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>

                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">as well as the feasible domain </span>
                    <!-= - l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTM=L" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"33" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posit=ion: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi cl=ass=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FA TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math=>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"h=ttp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>=CE=A9</mi>
                                        </math=>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">are developed and refined as the iteratio=
                            ns proceed. The approach is practically limited
                            to the problems
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with less than about ten adjustabl=
                            e parameters. The work is an extension of our
                            original Delaunay-based
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization algorithm (see JOGO D=
                            OI: 10.1007/s10898-015-0384-2), and inherits
                            many of the constructions
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and strengths of that algorithm, i=
                            ncluding: (1) a surrogate function </span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"34" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mj= x-mi>
                                    <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-=n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    </m= jx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>p</mi>
                                            <mro= w>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mi>x</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-close">)</mo>
                                                </mrow>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">interpolating all existing function evalu=
                            ations and summarizing their trends, (2) a
                            synthetic, piecewise-quadratic
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">uncertainty function </span>
                        <!-- l=
. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"35" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position=: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D452 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow spa=ce=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                            </= mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close"=>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assis= tive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w=3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>e</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>x</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</= mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">built on the framework of a Delaunay tria=
                            ngulation amongst existing datapoints, (3) a
                            tunable balance
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">between global exploration (large =
                        </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" j=ax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"36" style=3D"font-size: 117=.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">=
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>=
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                <math=xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>K</mi>
                                    <= /math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">)
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and local refinement (small </span=><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"=CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"37" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; p=osition: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-= math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>K</mi>
                                                </math=>
                                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">),
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(4) provable global convergence fo=
                                r a sufficiently large </span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"38" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mj= x-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                    <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>K<= /mi></math>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">under the assumption that the obje=
                                ctive and constraint functions are twice
                                differentiable with bounded
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Hessians, (5) an Adaptive-K varian=
                                t of the algorithm that efficiently tunes
                            </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"39" style=3D"fo=nt-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidd=en=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I">
                                            </mjx= -c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"in=line"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"=>
                                        <mi>K</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">automatically based on a target value of =
                                the objective function, and (6) remarkably fast
                                global
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence on a variety of benchm=
                                ark problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Allman2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-21" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Allman, Andrew and Qi Zhang. =E2=80=9CBranch=
            -and-Price for a Class of Nonconvex Mixed-Integer
            Nonlinear Programs=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). arXiv: 2001.01794=
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This work attempts to combine the
                strengths of two major technologies that have matured
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">over the last three decades: globa=
                    l mixed-integer nonlinear optimization and
                    branch-and-price. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consider a class of generally nonc=
                    onvex mixed-integer nonlinear programs (MINLPs)
                    with linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complicating constraints and integ=
                    er linking variables. If the complicating
                    constraints are removed,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the problem becomes easy to solve,=
                    e.g. due to decomposable structure.
                    Integrality of the linking
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variables allows us to apply a dis=
                    cretization approach to derive a Dantzig-Wolfe
                    reformulation and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solve the problem to global optima=
                    lity using branch-and-price. It is a remarkably
                    simple idea; but
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to our surprise, it has barely fou=
                    nd any application in the literature. In this
                    work, we show that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many relevant problems directly fa=
                    ll or can be reformulated into this class of
                    MINLPs. We present
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the branch-and-price algorithm and=
                    demonstrate its effectiveness (and sometimes
                    ineffectiveness) in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an extensive computational study c=
                    onsidering multiple large-scale problems of
                    practical relevance,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">showing that, in many cases, order=
                    s-of-magnitude reductions in solution time can
                    be achieved.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Altschuler2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-22" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Altschuler, Jason M. and Pablo A. Parrilo. =
            =E2=80=9CApproximating Min-Mean-Cycle for low-diameter
            graphs in near-optimal time and memory=E2=80=9D. In: (Apr. 2020=
            ). arXiv: 2004.03114v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We revisit Min-Mean-Cycle, the
                classical problem of finding a cycle in a weighted directed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph with minimum mean weight. De=
                    spite an extensive algorithmic literature,
                    previous work falls short
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a near-linear runtime in the nu=
                    mber of edges </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu=_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"40" style=3D"=font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hi=dden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I">
                                </m= jx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"=inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inlin=e">
                            <mi>m</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-=9">=E2=80=93in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fact, there is a natural barrier w=
                    hich precludes such a runtime for solving
                    Min-Mean-Cycle exactly.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Here, we give a much faster approx=
                    imation algorithm that, for graphs with
                    polylogarithmic diameter,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">has near-linear runtime. In partic=
                    ular, this is the first algorithm whose runtime
                    for the complete graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scales in the number of vertices <= /span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" ja=x=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"41" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <= /mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>n</mi>
                                    </= math>
                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">as </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container cl=ass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_c=ounter=3D"42" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math cl=ass=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-utext var=iant=3D"italic" style=3D"font-size: 85.2%; padding: 0.88em 0px
                                        0.235em; fon=t-family: MJXZERO, serif; font-style: italic;">=C3=95</mjx-utext>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <= mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-c=lose">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>=C3=95</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>n</mi>
                                            <mn>2</mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"=MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <s= pan class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Moreover=E2=80=93unconditionally o=
                    n the diameter=E2=80=93the algorithm uses only </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"43" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-=n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            </m= jx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>O</mi>
                                    <mro= w>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>n</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">memory beyond reading the input, making i=
                    t =E2=80=9Dmemory-optimal=E2=80=9D. The algorithm is also
                    simple to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implement and has remarkable pract=
                    ical performance. Our approach is based on
                    solving a linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programming (LP) relaxation using =
                    entropic regularization, which effectively
                    reduces the LP to a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Matrix Balancing problem=E2=80=93a=
                    la the popular reduction of Optimal Transport to
                    Matrix Scaling. We then
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">round the fractional LP solution u=
                    sing a variant of the classical
                    Cycle-Cancelling algorithm that is sped
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">up to near-linear runtime at the e=
                    xpense of being approximate, and implemented in
                    a memory-optimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">manner. We also provide an alterna=
                    tive algorithm with slightly faster theoretical
                    runtime, albeit worse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory usage and practicality. Thi=
                    s algorithm uses the same rounding procedure,
                    but solves the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">LP relaxation by leveraging recent=
                    developments in area-convexity regularization.
                    Its runtime scales

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inversely in the approximation acc=
                    uracy, which we show is optimal=E2=80=93barring a major
                    breakthrough in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithmic graph theory, namely f=
                    aster Shortest Paths algorithms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Altschuler2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-23" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CRandom Osborne: a simple,=
            practical algorithm for Matrix Balancing in near-linear time=E2=80=9D.
            In: (Apr. 2020). arXiv: 2004.02837v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[m=
                ath.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We revisit Matrix Balancing, a
                pre-conditioning task used ubiquitously for computing eigenv=
                alues
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and matrix exponentials. Since 196=
                    0, Osborne=E2=80=99s algorithm has been the
                    practitioners=E2=80=99 algorithm of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">choice, and is now implemented in =
                    most numerical software packages. However, the
                    theoretical properties
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of Osborne=E2=80=99s algorithm are=
                    not well understood. Here, we show that a simple
                    random variant of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Osborne=E2=80=99s algorithm conver=
                    ges in near-linear time in the input sparsity.
                    Specifically, it balances </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-containe= r class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtme=nu_counter=3D"44" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" disp=lay=3D"inline"><math

                                                <mi>=CE=BA</mi>

                                        </mjx-c= ontainer>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">measures the conditioning of </span>
                                        <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                            jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"51" style=3D"font-size:
                                            117.4%; positio=n: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <= mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mi>K</mi>
                                                </math></mjx-= assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Previous work had established near=
                                            -linear runtimes either only for </span>
                                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"52" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                                                <mjx-msub>
                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2113"=></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;"=>
                                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                                                    </mj= x-c>
                                                            </mjx-mn>
                                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                                    </mjx-script>
                                                </mjx-msub>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <msub>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi>=E2=84=93</mi>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        <= mrow>
                                                            <mn>2</mn>
                                                            </mrow>
                                                    </msub>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">accuracy (a weaker criterion which is les=
                                            s relevant for applications), or through an
                                            entirely different
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm based on (currently) imp=
                                            ractical Laplacian solvers. We further show
                                            that if the graph with
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adjacency matrix </span>
                                        <!-- l. 30=
 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabi=ndex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"53" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: re=lative;">

                                                </mjx-mi>
