                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c1D449 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-re=l">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <= mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            </mjx-mrow=>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                <= /mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displa=y=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"=inline">
                                    <mi>O</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi class==3D"qopname">log</mi>
                                            <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">|</mo>
                                            <m= i>V</mi>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">|</mo>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=
                                                    =88=95</mo>
                                                <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                                <mn>2</mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-clo=se">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">Laplacian linear systems to guarantee a r=
                            elative error, which is still very expensive
                            for large inputs. In
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this paper, we present a novel app=
                            roximation algorithm that requires the solution
                            of only one Laplacian
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear system. The remaining parts=
                            are purely combinatorial =E2=80=93 mainly sampling
                            uniform spanning trees,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which we relate to diag</span>
                        <!--=
 l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML=" tabindex=3D" 0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"74" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mj= x-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I">
                                    </= mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2=020"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.o=rg/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">=
                                            (</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>L</mi>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=80=A0</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo=class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-con= tainer>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">via effective resistances. For small-worl=
                                    d networks, our algorithm obtains a </span>
                                <!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"75" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cB1">
                                                </mjx-c=>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c=>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inli=ne"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <= mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C2=B1</mo>
                                                <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                        </math></mjx-ass= istive-mml>
                                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-approximation
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with high probability, in a time t=
                                    hat is nearly-linear in </span>
                                <!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"76" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C">
                                                </mjx-c=>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D438 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c=>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C">
                    </= mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D="inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inli=ne">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">|</mo>
                            <mi>E</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel"=>|</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and quadratic in </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mj= x-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D="0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"77" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;="><mjx-math class=3D"
                        MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">=
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=bin" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D="mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mj= x-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmln=s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>1</mn>
                            <mo c=lass=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Another positive aspect of our alg=
                            orithm is its parallel nature due to
                            independent sampling. We thus
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provide two parallel implementatio=
                            ns of our algorithm: one using OpenMP, one MPI
                            + OpenMP. In
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our experiments against the state =
                            of the art, our algorithm (i) yields more
                            accurate results, (ii) is
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">much faster and more memory-effici=
                            ent, and (iii) obtains good parallel speedups,
                            in particular in the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed setting.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anikin2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-29" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anikin, Anton, Yuriy Dorn, and Yurii Nestero=
            v. =E2=80=9CComputational Methods for the Stable
            Dynamic Model=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Communicati=
                ons in Computer and Information Science</span>.
            Springer
            International Publishing, 2020, pp. 280=E2=80=93294. <span clas=s=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/978-3-=
            030-38603-0=CB=9921.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Traffic assignment problem is one
                of the central problems in transportation science. Various
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model assumptions lead to differen=
                    t setups corresponding to nonlinear
                    optimization problems.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span cla=ss=3D"cmr-9">In this work, we focus on
                    the
                    stable dynamic model and its generalizations. We propose new=
                    equivalent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">representation for stable dynamic =
                    model [Nesterov and de Palma, 2003]. We use
                    smoothing technique
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to derive new model, which can be =
                    interpreted as a stochastic equilibrium
                    model.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Antonakopoulos2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-30" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Antonakopoulos, Kimon, E. Veronica Belmega, =
            and Panayotis Mertikopoulos. =E2=80=9COnline
            and Stochastic Optimization beyond Lipschitz Continuity: A Riem=
            annian Approach=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 8th International Co=
                nference on Learning Representations</span>.
            ICLR 2020. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Motivated by applications to
                machine learning and imaging science, we study a class of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">online and stochastic optimization=
                    problems with loss functions that are not
                    Lipschitz continuous;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in particular, the loss functions =
                    encountered by the optimizer could exhibit
                    gradient singularities or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be singular themselves. Drawing on=
                    tools and techniques from Riemannian geometry,
                    we examine a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Riemann=E2=80=93Lipschitz (RL) con=
                    tinuity condition which is tailored to the singularity
                    landscape of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem=E2=80=99s loss functions. =
                    In this way, we are able to tackle cases beyond the
                    Lipschitz framework
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provided by a global norm, and we =
                    derive optimal regret bounds and last iterate
                    convergence results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through the use of regularized lea=
                    rning methods (such as online mirror descent).
                    These results are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subsequently validated in a class =
                    of stochastic Poisson inverse problems that
                    arise in imaging science.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anwer2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-31" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anwer, Abdul Rehman et al. =E2=80=9CGPU-Trid=
            ent: Efficient Modeling of ErrorPropagation in GPU
            Programs=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of t=
                he International Conference on High Performance
                Computing,
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Networking, Storage, and Analysi=
                s</span>. SC 20 (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps"=>issn</span></span>:
            978-1-7281-9998-6/20/31.00. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <= span class=3D"small-caps">url
            </span></span>:
            <a href=3D"http://blogs.ubc.ca/karthik/files/2020/08/SC2020-fin=al.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://blogs.ubc.ca/karthik/f=
                    iles/2020/08/SC2020-final.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Fault injection (FI) techniques
                are typically used to determine the reliability profiles of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programs under soft errors. Howeve=
                    r, these techniques are highly resource- and
                    time-intensive. Prior
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">research developed a model, TRIDEN=
                    T to analytically predict Silent Data
                    Corruption (SDC, i.e.,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">incorrect output without any indic=
                    ation) probabilities of single-threaded CPU
                    applications without
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">requiring FIs. Unfortunately, TRID=
                    ENT is incompatible with GPU programs, due to
                    their high degree
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of parallelism and different memor=
                    y architectures than CPU programs. The main
                    challenge is that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">modeling error propagation across =
                    thousands of threads in a GPU kernel requires
                    enormous amounts
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of data to be profiled and analyze=
                    d, posing a major scalability bottleneck for
                    HPC applications.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D="cmr-9">In this paper, we propose
                    GPU-TRIDENT,
                    an accurate and scalable technique for modeling error
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">propagation in GPU programs. We fi=
                    nd that GPU-TRIDENT is 2 orders of magnitude
                    faster than
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">FI-based approaches, and nearly as=
                    accurate in determining the SDC rate of GPU
                    programs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anzt2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-32" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anzt, Hartwig, Erik Boman, et al. =E2=80=9CP=
            reparing sparse solvers for exascale computing=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Philosophical Transactions of the Royal=
                Society A: Mathematical, Physical and
                Engineering
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Sciences </span>378.2166 (Jan. 2=
            020), p. 20190053. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi<= /span></span>:
                10.1098/rsta.2019.0053.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse solvers provide essential
                functionality for a wide variety of scientific applications.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Highly parallel sparse solvers are=
                    essential for continuing advances in
                    high-fidelity, multi-physics and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multi-scale simulations, especiall=
                    y as we target exascale platforms. This paper
                    describes the challenges,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategies and progress of the US =
                    Department of Energy Exascale Computing project
                    towards providing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse solvers for exascale comput=
                    ing platforms. We address the demands of
                    systems with thousands
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of high-performance node devices w=
                    here exposing concurrency, hiding latency and
                    creating alternative
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms become essential. The e=
                    fforts described here are works in progress,
                    highlighting current
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">success and upcoming challenges.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anzt2020c" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-33" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anzt, Hartwig, Terry Cojean, Yen-Chen Chen, =
            et al. =E2=80=9CGinkgo: A high performance
            numerical linear algebra library=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"c=mti-10">The Journal of Open Source Software
            </span>(2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">doi</span></span>:
            10.21105/joss.02260.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Ginkgo is a production-ready
                sparse linear algebra library for high performance computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on GPU-centric architectures with =
                    a high level of performance portability and
                    focuses on software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sustainability.</span><br class=3D="newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">The
                    library
                    focuses on solving sparse linear systems and accommodates a =
                    large variety of matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formats, state-of-the-art iterativ=
                    e (Krylov) solvers and preconditioners, which
                    make the library suitable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for a variety of scientific applic=
                    ations. Ginkgo supports many architectures such
                    as multi-threaded
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CPU, NVIDIA GPUs, and AMD GPUs. Th=
                    e heavy use of modern C++ features simplifies
                    the addition

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of new executor paradigms and algo=
                    rithmic functionality without introducing
                    significant performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overhead.</span><br class=3D"newli=ne"><span class=3D"cmr-9">Solving linear
                    systems
                    is usually one of the most computationally and memory intens=
                    ive aspects of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">any application. Hence there has b=
                    een a significant amount of effort in this
                    direction with software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">libraries such as UMFPACK and CHOL=
                    MOD (=E2=80=9CSuitesparse,=E2=80=9D 2020) for solving linear
                    systems with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">direct methods and PETSc (=E2=80=
                    =9CPETSc,=E2=80=9D 2020), Trilinos (=E2=80=9CTrilinos,=E2=80=9D 2020), Eige=
                    n
                    (=E2=80=9CEigen,=E2=80=9D 2020) and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many more to solve linear systems =
                    with iterative methods. With Ginkgo, we aim to
                    ensure high
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance while not compromising=
                    portability. Hence, we provide very efficient
                    low level kernels
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimized for different architectu=
                    res and separate these kernels from the
                    algorithms thereby ensuring
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extensibility and ease of use.</sp= an><br class=3D"newline"><span
                        class=3D"cmr-9">Ginkgo is also a part of the=
                        xSDK effort (=E2=80=9CxSDK,=E2=80=9D 2020) and available as a Spack (Gambl=
                        in
                        et al.,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">2015) package. xSDK aims to provid=
                        e infrastructure for and interoperability
                        between a collection of
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">related and complementary software=
                        elements to foster rapid and efficient
                        development of scientific
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications using High Performanc=
                        e Computing. Within this effort, we provide
                        interoperability with
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application libraries such as deal=
                        .ii (Arndt et al., 2019) and mfem (Anderson et
                        al., 2020). Ginkgo
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provides wrappers within these two=
                        libraries so that they can take advantage of
                        the features of Ginkgo</span>.
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anzt2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-34" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anzt, Hartwig, Terry Cojean, Goran Flegar, e=
            t al. =E2=80=9CGinkgo: A Modern Linear Operator Algebra
            Framework for High Performance Computing=E2=80=9D. In: (June 20=
            20). arXiv: 2006.16852v2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we present Ginkgo,
                a modern C++ math library for scientific high performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing. While classical linear =
                    algebra libraries act on matrix and vector
                    objects, Ginkgo=E2=80=99s design
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">principle abstracts all functional=
                    ity as =E2=80=9Dlinear operators=E2=80=9D, motivating the
                    notation of a =E2=80=9Dlinear operator
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algebra library=E2=80=9D. Ginkgo=
                    =E2=80=99s current focus is oriented towards providing sparse
                    linear algebra functionality
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for high performance GPU architect=
                    ures, but given the library design, this focus
                    can be easily extended
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to accommodate other algorithms an=
                    d hardware architectures. We introduce this
                    sophisticated software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architecture that separates core a=
                    lgorithms from architecture-specific back ends
                    and provide details
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on extensibility and sustainabilit=
                    y measures. We also demonstrate Ginkgo=E2=80=99s
                    usability by providing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">examples on how to use its functio=
                    nality inside the MFEM and deal.ii finite
                    element ecosystems.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Finally, we offer a practical demo=
                    nstration of Ginkgo=E2=80=99s high performance on
                    state-of-the-art GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architectures.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anzt2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-35" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anzt, Hartwig, Terry Cojean, Chen Yen-Chen, =
            et al. =E2=80=9CLoad-balancing Sparse Matrix Vector
            Product Kernels on GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">A=
                CM Transactions on Parallel Computing </span>7.1 (Mar.
            2020),
            pp. 1=E2=80=9326. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1145/3380930.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Efficient processing of Irregular
                Matrices on Single Instruction, Multiple Data (SIMD)-type
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architectures is a persistent chal=
                    lenge. Resolving it requires innovations in the
                    development of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data formats, computational techni=
                    ques, and implementations that strike a balance
                    between
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">thread divergence, which is inhere=
                    nt for Irregular Matrices, and padding, which
                    alleviates the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance-detrimental thread div=
                    ergence but introduces artificial overheads. To
                    this end, in this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">article, we address the challenge =
                    of designing high performance sparse
                    matrix-vector product (SpMV)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels designed for Nvidia Graphi=
                    cs Processing Units (GPUs). We present a
                    compressed sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">row (CSR) format suitable for unba=
                    lanced matrices. We also provide a
                    load-balancing kernel for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">coordinate (COO) matrix format and=
                    extend it to a hybrid algorithm that stores
                    part of the matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in SIMD-friendly Ellpack format (E=
                    LL) format. The ratio between the ELL- and the
                    COO-part is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">determined using a theoretical ana=
                    lysis of the nonzeros-per-row distribution. For
                    the over 2,800 test
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices available in the Suite Sp=
                    arse matrix collection, we compare the
                    performance against SpMV
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels provided by NVIDIA=E2=80=
                    =99s cuSPARSE library and a heavily-tuned sliced ELL
                    (SELL-P) kernel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that prevents unnecessary padding =
                    by considering the irregular matrices as a
                    combination of matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">blocks stored in ELL format.</span=>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anzt2020e" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-36" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anzt, Hartwig, Eileen Kuehn, and Goran Flega=
            r. =E2=80=9CCrediting Pull Requests to Open Source
            Research Software as an Academic Contribution=E2=80=9D. In: <sp= an class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of
                Computational
                Science</span>
                (2020), p. 101278. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"smal=l-caps">issn</span></span>: 1877-7503.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
                https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jocs.2020.1012=
                78.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                    <= /span>:
                        <a href=3D"http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S18=77750320305743"
                            class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">http://www.sciencedir=
                                ect.com/science/article/pii/S1877750320305743</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Like any other scientific
                discipline, the High Performance Computing community suffers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">under the publish or perish paradi=
                    gm. As a result, a significant portion of novel
                    algorithm designs and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware-optimized implementations=
                    never make it into production code but are
                    instead abandoned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">once they served the purpose of yi=
                    elding (another) publication. At the same time,
                    community
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">software packages driving scientif=
                    ic research lack the addition of new technology
                    and hardware-specific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementations. This results in a=
                    very unsatisfying situation where researchers
                    and software developers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are working independently, and the=
                    traditional peer reviewing is reaching its
                    capacity limits. A
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paradigm shift that accepts high-q=
                    uality software pull requests to open source
                    research software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as conference contributions may cr=
                    eate incentives to realize new and/or improved
                    algorithms in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">community software ecosystems. In =
                    this paper, we propose to complement code
                    reviews on pull requests
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to scientific open source software=
                    with scientific reviews, and allow the
                    presentation and publication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of high quality software contribut=
                    ions that present an academic improvement to
                    the state-of-the-art
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at scientific conferences.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Anzt2020d" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-37" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Anzt, Hartwig, Yuhsiang M. Tsai, et al. =E2=
            =80=9CEvaluating the Performance of NVIDIA=E2=80=99s A100
            Ampere GPU for Sparse and Batched Computations=E2=80=9D. In: <s= pan class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the
                2020 IEEE/ACM
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Conference on Performance Modeli=
                    ng, Benchmarking and Simulation of High
                    Performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computer Systems</span>. PMBS =
                =E2=80=9920. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract:
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU accelerators have become an im=
                portant backbone for scientific high
                performance-computing, and
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the performance advances obtained =
                from adopting new GPU hardware are significant.
                In this paper
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we take a first look at NVIDIA=E2=
                =80=99s newest server-line GPU, the A100 architecture,
                part of the Ampere
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generation. Specifically, we asses=
                s its performance for sparse and batch
                computations, as these routines
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are relied upon in many scientific=
                applications, and compare to the performance
                achieved on NVIDIA=E2=80=99s
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">previous server-line GPU.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Archibald2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-38" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Archibald, Rick et al. <span class=3D"cmti-1=0">Integrating
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Deep Learning in Domain Sciences=
                at Exascale</span>. Tech. rep. University of
            Tennessee, 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-=caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://www.icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2020/icl-=utk-1403-2020.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.icl.ut=
                    k.edu/files/publications/2020/icl-utk-1403-2020.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper presents some of the
                current challenges in designing deep learning artificial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intelligence (AI) and integrating =
                    it with traditional high-performance computing
                    (HPC) simulations.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We evaluate existing packages for =
                    their ability to run deep learning models and
                    applications on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large-scale HPC systems eciently, =
                    identify challenges, and propose new
                    asynchronous parallelization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and optimization techniques for cu=
                    rrent large-scale heterogeneous systems and
                    upcoming exascale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. These developments, along=
                    with existing HPC AI software capabilities,
                    have been integrated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">into MagmaDNN, an open-source HPC =
                    deep learning framework. Many deep learning
                    frameworks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are targeted at data scientists an=
                    d fall short in providing quality integration
                    into existing HPC
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">workflows. This paper discusses th=
                    e necessities of an HPC deep learning framework
                    and how those
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">needs can be provided (e.g., as in=
                    MagmaDNN) through a deep integration with
                    existing HPC libraries,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such as MAGMA and its modular memo=
                    ry management, MPI, CuBLAS, CuDNN, MKL, and
                    HIP.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Advancements are also illustrated =
                    through the use of algorithmic enhancements in
                    reduced- and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mixed-precision, as well as asynch=
                    ronous optimization methods. Finally, we
                    present illustrations and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">potential solutions for enhancing =
                    traditional compute- and data-intensive
                    applications at ORNL and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">UTK with AI. The approaches and fu=
                    ture challenges are illustrated in materials
                    science, imaging, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">climate applications.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Asgari2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-39" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Asgari, Bahar et al. =E2=80=9CCopernicus: Ch=
            aracterizing the Performance Implications of
            Compression
            Formats Used in Sparse Workloads=E2=80=9D. In: (Nov. 2020). arX=
            iv: 2011.10932 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.AR]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse matrices are the key
                ingredients of several application domains, from scientific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computation to machine learning. T=
                    he primary challenge with sparse matrices has
                    been efficiently
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">storing and transferring data, for=
                    which many sparse formats have been proposed
                    to significantly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">eliminate zero entries. Such forma=
                    ts, essentially designed to optimize memory
                    footprint, may not be as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">successful in performing faster pr=
                    ocessing. In other words, although they allow
                    faster data transfer and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improve memory bandwidth utilizati=
                    on =E2=80=93 the classic challenge of sparse problems =E2=80=93
                    their decompression
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mechanism can potentially create a=
                    computation bottleneck. Not only is this
                    challenge not resolved, but
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">also it becomes more serious with =
                    the advent of domain-specific architectures
                    (DSAs), as they intend to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">more aggressively improve performa=
                    nce. The performance implications of using
                    various formats along
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with DSAs, however, has not been e=
                    xtensively studied by prior work. To fill this
                    gap of knowledge,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we characterize the impact of usin=
                    g seven frequently used sparse formats on
                    performance, based on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a DSA for sparse matrix-vector mul=
                    tiplication (SpMV), implemented on an FPGA
                    using high-level
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">synthesis (HLS) tools, a growing a=
                    nd popular method for developing DSAs. Seeking
                    a fair comparison,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we tailor and optimize the HLS imp=
                    lementation of decompression for each format.
                    We thoroughly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">explore diverse metrics, including=
                    decompression overhead, latency, balance
                    ratio, throughput, memory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bandwidth utilization, resource ut=
                    ilization, and power consumption, on a variety
                    of real-world and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">synthetic sparse workloads.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Attouch2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-40" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Attouch, Hedy, Zaki Chbani, and Hassan Riahi=
            . =E2=80=9CFast Convex Optimization Via a Third-Order
            In Time Evolution Equation=E2=80=9D. In: (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In a Hilbert space H, we develop
                fast convex optimization methods, which are based on a third
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">order in time evolution system. Th=
                    e function to minimize </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"78" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c3A"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c210B TEX-SC">
                                    </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-rel" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2192"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi=class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c211D TEX-A"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-= mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <ma= th xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>f</m= i>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">:</mo>
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=E2=84=
                                    =8B</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=86=92</mo>
                                <mi>=E2=84=9D</mi></math>=
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is convex, continuously differentiable, w=
                    ith </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_=0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"79" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                        <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                            <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c61"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c72"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c67">
                                    <= /mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6D"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c69"></mjx-c>
                                        <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c6E">
                                </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mtext>
                        </mjx-mstyle>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mj=x-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mj=x-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2260"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>=
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2205"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mstyl= e class=3D"text">
                                <mtext>argmin</mtext>

                                <mi>=E2=88=85</mi></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-= container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and enters the dynamic via its gra=
                        dient. On the basis of Lyapunov=E2=80=99s analysis and
                        temporal scaling
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques, we show a convergence =
                        rate of the values of the order </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"80" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue"></mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                            <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                </math=>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><sup class=3D"nicefrac">
                        <mn>1</mn>
                    </sup>
                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo><sub class=3D"nice=frac">
                        <mi>t</mi>
                        <mn>3</mn>
                    </sub>
                    =20
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">, and obtain the convergence of the traje=
                        ctories towards optimal solutions. When </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"81" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mj= x-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                            <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>f<= /mi></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">is strongly convex, an exponential rate o=
                        f convergence is obtained. We complete the
                        study of the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">continuous dynamic by introducing =
                        a damping term induced by the Hessian of
                    </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"82" style=3D"fo=nt-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidd=en=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I">
                                    </mjx= -c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"in=line"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"=>
                                <mi>f</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9"=>.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This allows the oscillations to be=
                        controlled and attenuated. Then, we analyze
                        the convergence of
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the proximal-based algorithms obta=
                        ined by temporal discretization of this system,
                        and obtain similar
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rates. The algorithmic=
                        results are valid for a general convex, lower
                        semicontinuous, and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proper function </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 =
-->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabin=dex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"83" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rel=ative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"m=jx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"m=jx-n MathClass-punc" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3A"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>=
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c210B=TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">=
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2192"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space==3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c211D TEX-A"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"m=jx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c222A"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo=>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c7B"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bi=n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c221E">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Mat=hClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7D"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-ma= th>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>f</mi>
                                        <mo class==3D"MathClass-punc">:</mo>
                                        <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=E2=84=8B</mi>
                                        <mo= class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=86=92</mo>
                                            <mi>=E2=84=9D</mi>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathC=lass-bin">=E2=88=AA</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">{</mo>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mo=class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                                    <mi>=E2=88=9E</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathCla=ss-close">}</mo>
                                            </mrow>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span cla=ss=3D"cmr-9">.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Awan2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-41" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Awan, Muaaz G. et al. =E2=80=9CADEPT: a doma=
            in independent sequence alignment strategy for gpu
            architectures=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">BMC Bioinfo=
                rmatics </span>21.1 (Sept. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"s=mall-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1186/s12859-020-03720-1.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Bioinformatic workflows
                frequently make use of automated genome assembly and protein
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">clustering tools. At the core of m=
                    ost of these tools, a significant portion of
                    execution time is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">spent in determining optimal local=
                    alignment between two sequences. This task is
                    performed with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Smith-Waterman algorithm, whic=
                    h is a dynamic programming based method. With
                    the advent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of modern sequencing technologies =
                    and increasing size of both genome and protein
                    databases, a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">need for faster Smith-Waterman imp=
                    lementations has emerged. Multiple SIMD
                    strategies for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Smith-Waterman algorithm are avail=
                    able for CPUs. However, with the move of HPC
                    facilities towards
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accelerator based architectures, a=
                    need for an efficient GPU accelerated strategy
                    has emerged. Existing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU based strategies have either b=
                    een optimized for a specific type of characters
                    (Nucleotides or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Amino Acids) or for only a handful=
                    of application use-cases.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Awwal2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-42" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Awwal, Aliyu Muhammed, Poom Kumam, and Hassa=
            n Mohammad. =E2=80=9CIterative algorithm with
            structured diagonal Hessian approximation for solving nonlinear=
            least squares problems=E2=80=9D. In:
            (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.01871 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC=
                ]</span>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Nonlinear least-squares problems
                are a special class of unconstrained optimization problems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in which their gradient and Hessia=
                    n have special structures. In this paper, we
                    exploit these structures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and proposed a matrix-free algorit=
                    hm with a diagonal Hessian approximation for
                    solving nonlinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">least-squares problems. We devise =
                    appropriate safeguarding strategies to ensure
                    the Hessian matrix is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">positive definite throughout the i=
                    teration process. The proposed algorithm
                    generates descent direction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and is globally convergent. Prelim=
                    inary numerical experiments show that the
                    proposed method is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">competitive with a recently develo=
                    ped similar method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ayala2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-43" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ayala, Alan et al. =E2=80=9CheFFTe: Highly E=
            fficient FFT for Exascale=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Lecture Not=
                es
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">in Computer Science</span>. Spri=
            nger International Publishing, 2020, pp.
            262=E2=80=93275. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-=caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-50371-0=CB=9919.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Exascale computing aspires to
                meet the increasing demands from large scientific applicatio=
                ns.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Software targeting exascale is typ=
                    ically designed for heterogeneous
                    architectures; henceforth, it is not
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">only important to develop well-des=
                    igned software, but also make it aware of the
                    hardware architecture
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and efficiently exploit its power.=
                    Currently, several and diverse applications,
                    such as those part of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Exascale Computing Project (ECP) i=
                    n the United States, rely on efficient
                    computation of the Fast
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Fourier Transform (FFT). In this c=
                    ontext, we present the design and
                    implementation of heFFTe (Highly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Efficient FFT for Exascale) librar=
                    y, which targets the upcoming exascale
                    supercomputers. We provide
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">highly (linearly) scalable GPU ker=
                    nels that achieve more than equation </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"84" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>40</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            <= /math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">speedup with respect to local kernels fro=
                    m CPU state-of-the-art libraries, and over
                    equation </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJa=x CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"85"=style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TE=X" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                </= mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD=7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                            <mn>2</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">speedup for the whole FFT computation. A =
                    communication model for parallel FFTs is also
                    provided to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analyze the bottleneck for large-s=
                    cale problems. We show experiments obtained on
                    Summit supercomputer
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, =
                    using up to 24,576 IBM Power9 cores and 6,144
                    NVIDIA V-100
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPUs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Azad2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-44" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Azad, Ariful, Mohsen Mahmoudi Aznaveh, et al=
            . =E2=80=9CEvaluation of Graph Analytics
            Frameworks Using the GAP Benchmark Suite,=E2=80=9D in: <span cl=ass=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 IEEE
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">International Symposium on Workl=
                oad Characterization</span>. IISWC 2020. 2020.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>:
                    <a href=3D"https://www.cs.utexas.edu/~roshan/GraphAnalyticsFram=eworksStudy.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.cs.utexa=
                            s.edu/~roshan/GraphAnalyticsFrameworksStudy.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Graphs play a key role in data
                analytics. Graphs and the software systems used to work
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with them are highly diverse. Algo=
                    rithms interact with hardware in different ways
                    and which graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution works best on a given pla=
                    tform changes with the structure of the graph.
                    This makes it difficult
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to decide which graph programming =
                    framework is the best for a given situation. In
                    this paper, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">try to make sense of this diverse =
                    landscape. We evaluate five different
                    frameworks for graph analytics:
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SuiteSparse GraphBLAS, Galois, the=
                    NWGraph library, the Graph Kernel Collection
                    (GKC), and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GraphIt. We use the GAP Benchmark =
                    Suite to evaluate each framework. GAP consists
                    of 30 tests: six
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph algorithms (breadth-first se=
                    arch, single-source shortest path, PageRank,
                    betweenness centrality,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">connected components, and triangle=
                    counting) on five graphs. The GAP Benchmark
                    Suite includes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high-performance reference impleme=
                    ntations to provide a performance baseline for
                    comparison. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results show the relative strength=
                    s of each framework, but also serve as a case
                    study for the challenges
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of establishing objective measures=
                    for comparing graph frameworks.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Azad2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-45" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Azad, Ariful, Aydin Bulu=C3=A7, et al. =E2=
            =80=9CA Distributed-Memory Algorithm for Computing a
            Heavy-Weight Perfect Matching on Bipartite Graphs=E2=80=9D. In:=
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">SIAM Journal on Scientific
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computing </span>42.4 (Jan. 2020=
            ), pp. C143=E2=80=93C168. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-cap=s">doi</span></span>:
            10.1137/18m1189348.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We design and implement an
                efficient parallel algorithm for finding a perfect matching =
                in a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">weighted bipartite graph such that=
                    weights on the edges of the matching are
                    large. This problem differs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from the maximum weight matching p=
                    roblem, for which scalable approximation
                    algorithms are known.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">It is primarily motivated by findi=
                    ng good pivots in scalable sparse direct
                    solvers before factorization.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Due to the lack of scalable altern=
                    atives, distributed solvers use sequential
                    implementations of maximum
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">weight perfect matching algorithms=
                    , such as those available in MC64. To overcome
                    this limitation, we

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">propose a fully parallel distribut=
                    ed memory algorithm that first generates a
                    perfect matching and then
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteratively improves the weight of=
                    the perfect matching by searching for
                    weight-increasing cycles of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">length 4 in parallel. For most pra=
                    ctical problems the weights of the perfect
                    matchings generated by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our algorithm are very close to th=
                    e optimum. An efficient implementation of the
                    algorithm scales up
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to 256 nodes (17,408 cores) on a C=
                    ray XC40 supercomputer and can solve instances
                    that are too large
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to be handled by a single node usi=
                    ng the sequential algorithm.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Baayen2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-46" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Baayen, Jorn and Jakub Marecek. =E2=80=9CMix=
            ed-Integer Path-Stable Optimisation, with Applications
            in Model-Predictive Control of Water Systems=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan=
            . 2020). arXiv: 2001.08121 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many systems exhibit a mixture of
                continuous and discrete dynamics. We consider a family
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of mixed-integer non-convex non-li=
                    near optimisation problems obtained in
                    discretisations of optimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">control of such systems. For this =
                    family, a branch-and-bound algorithm solves the
                    discretised problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to global optimality. As an exampl=
                    e, we consider water systems, where variations
                    in flow and variations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in water levels are continuous, wh=
                    ile decisions related to fixed-speed pumps and
                    whether gates that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">may be opened and closed are discr=
                    ete. We show that the related optimal-control
                    problems come from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the family we introduce =E2=80=93 =
                    and implement deterministic solvers with global
                    convergence guarantees.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Barik2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-47" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Barik, Reet et al. =E2=80=9CVertex Reorderin=
            g for Real-World Graphs and Applications: An Empirical
            Evaluation=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of=
                the International Symposium on Workload
                Characterization</span>.
            IISWC 2020. IEEE, Oct. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span cla=ss=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/iiswc50251.2020.00031.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Vertex reordering is a way to
                improve locality in graph computations. Given an input (or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">=E2=80=9Cnatural=E2=80=9D) order, =
                    reordering aims to compute an alternate permutation of the
                    vertices that is aimed at
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">maximizing a locality-based object=
                    ive. Given decades of research on this topic,
                    there are tens of graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reordering schemes, and there are =
                    also several linear arrangement =E2=80=9Cgap=E2=80=9D measures
                    for treatment as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">objectives. However, a comprehensi=
                    ve empirical analysis of the efficacy of the
                    ordering schemes against
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the different gap measures, and ag=
                    ainst real-world applications is currently
                    lacking. In this study, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">present an extensive empirical eva=
                    luation of up to 11 ordering schemes, taken
                    from different classes of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches, on a set of 34 real-wo=
                    rld graphs emerging from different application
                    domains. Our study is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">presented in two parts: a) a thoro=
                    ugh comparative evaluation of the different
                    ordering schemes on their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effectiveness to optimize differen=
                    t linear arrangement gap measures, relevant to
                    preserving locality;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and b) extensive evaluation of the=
                    impact of the ordering schemes on two
                    real-world, parallel graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications, namely, community de=
                    tection and influence maximization. Our studies
                    show a significant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">divergence among the ordering sche=
                    mes (up to 40x between the best and the poor)
                    in their effectiveness
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to reduce the gap measures; and a =
                    wide ranging impact of the ordering schemes on
                    various aspects
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">including application runtime (up =
                    to 4x), memory and cache use, load balancing,
                    and parallel work
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and efficiency. The comparative st=
                    udy also helps in revealing the nuances of a
                    parallel environment
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(compared to serial) on the orderi=
                    ng schemes and their role in optimizing
                    applications.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bauch2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-48" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bauch, Jonathan and Boaz Nadler. =E2=80=9CRa=
            nk
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"86"
                style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mn>
                    <mjx= -mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mj= x-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmln=s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
