                                <mn>2</mn>
                                <mi>r=
                                </mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container>
            iterative least squares: efficient recovery of ill-conditioned =
            low rank matrices from few entries=E2=80=9D.
            In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.01849 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[mat=
                h.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a new, simple and
                computationally efficient iterative method for low rank
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix completion. Our method is i=
                    nspired by the class of factorization-type
                    iterative algorithms,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">but substantially differs from the=
                    m in the way the problem is cast. Precisely,
                    given a target rank</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"87" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>r<= /mi></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instead of optimizing on the manif=
                    old of rank </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"88" style=3D"fo=nt-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidd=en=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45F TEX-I">
                                </mjx= -c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"in=line"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"=>
                            <mi>r</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, we allow our interim estimated =
                    matrix to have a specific over-parametrized
                    rank </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ct=xtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"89" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45F TEX-I">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mn>2</mn>
                            <mi>r</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">structure. Our algorithm, denoted </span>=
                <span class=3D"cmtt-9">R2RILS</span><span class=3D"cmr-9">, for rank </span=><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"=CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"90" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; p=osition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-m= n class=3D"mjx-n">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mn>2</mn>
                                <mi>r</mi>
                                </ma= th>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">iterative least squares, thus has low mem=
                        ory requirements, and at each iteration it
                        solves a computationally
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cheap sparse least-squares problem=
                        . We motivate our algorithm by its theoretical
                        analysis for the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simplified case of a rank-1 matrix=
                        . Empirically, </span><span class=3D"cmtt-9">R2RILS </span><span class=3D"c=mr-9">is able to
                        recover, with machine precision, ill

                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conditioned low rank matrices from=
                        very few observations =E2=80=93 near the information
                        limit. Finally, </span><span class=3D"cmtt-9">R2RILS
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is stable to corruption of the obs=
                        erved entries by additive zero mean Gaussian
                        noise.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Beeumen2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-49" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Beeumen, Roel Van et al. =E2=80=9CEnhancing =
            Scalability of a Matrix-Free Eigensolver for Studying
            Many-Body Localization=E2=80=9D. In: (Dec. 2020). arXiv: 2012.0=
            0217 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cond-mat.dis-nn]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In [Van Beeumen, et. al, HPC Asia
                2020, https://www.doi.org/10.1145/3368474.3368497]
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a scalable and matrix-free eigenso=
                    lver was proposed for studying the many-body
                    localization (MBL)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">transition of two-level quantum sp=
                    in chain models with nearest-neighbor </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"91" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c1D44B TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathC=lass-bin" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi cla=ss=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44C TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi=>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44C TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi=>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <math= xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathva=riant=3D"italic">XX</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                            <mi>Y</mi>
                            <mi>Y</= mi></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">interactions plus </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"92" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relati=ve;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-=i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assis= tive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w=3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>Z</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">terms. This type of problem is computatio=
                            nally challenging because the vector space
                            dimension grows
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exponentially with the physical sy=
                            stem size, and averaging over different
                            configurations of the random
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">disorder is needed to obtain relev=
                            ant statistical behavior. For each eigenvalue
                            problem, eigenvalues
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from different regions of the spec=
                            trum and their corresponding eigenvectors need
                            to be computed.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Traditionally, the interior eigens=
                            tates for a single eigenvalue problem are
                            computed via the shift-and-invert
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Lanczos algorithm. Due to the extr=
                            emely high memory footprint of the LU
                            factorizations, this technique
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is not well suited for large numbe=
                            r of spins </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_At=tached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"93" style=3D"fon=t-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidde=n=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I">
                                        </mjx-= c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                                    <mi>L</mi>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">=
                            ,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">e.g., one needs thousands of compu=
                            te nodes on modern high performance computing
                            infrastructures to
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">go beyond </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mj= x-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D="0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"94" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:
                            relative;="><mjx-math class=3D" MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n M=athClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn= class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D="on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"=display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>L</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                        <mn>24</mn=>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The matrix-free approach does not =
                                    suffer from this memory bottleneck, however,
                                    its scalability is
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">limited by a computation and commu=
                                    nication imbalance. We present a few strategies
                                    to reduce this
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">imbalance and to significantly enh=
                                    ance the scalability of the matrix-free
                                    eigensolver. To optimize
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the communication performance, we =
                                    leverage the consistent space runtime, CSPACER,
                                    and show its
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiency in accelerating the MBL=
                                    irregular communication patterns at scale
                                    compared to optimized
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">MPI non-blocking two-sided and one=
                                    -sided RMA implementation variants. The
                                    efficiency and effectiveness
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the proposed algorithm is demon=
                                    strated by computing eigenstates on a massively
                                    parallel many-core
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high performance computer.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bellavia2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-50" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bellavia, Stefania and Gianmarco Gurioli. =
            =E2=80=9CComplexity Analysis of a Stochastic Cubic
            Regularisation Method under Inexact Gradient Evaluations and Dy=
            namic Hessian Accuracy=E2=80=9D.
            In: (Jan. 2020). arXiv: 2001.10827 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[mat=
                h.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We here adapt an extended version
                of the adaptive cubic regularisation method with dynamic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inexact Hessian information for no=
                    nconvex optimisation in [2] to the stochastic
                    optimisation setting.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">While exact function evaluations a=
                    re still considered, this novel variant
                    inherits the innovative use of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adaptive accuracy requirements for=
                    Hessian approximations introduced in [2] and
                    additionally employs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inexact computations of the gradie=
                    nt. Without restrictions on the variance of the
                    errors, we assume
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that these approximations are avai=
                    lable within a sufficiently large, but fixed,
                    probability and we extend,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in the spirit of [13], the determi=
                    nistic analysis of the framework to its
                    stochastic counterpart, showing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that the expected number of iterat=
                    ions to reach a first-order stationary point
                    matches the well known
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">worst-case optimal complexity. Thi=
                    s is, in fact, still given by </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"95" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c1D452 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I">
                                            </mjx-c=>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D456 TEX-I="></mjx-c><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c1D459
                                                TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D=45C TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-= mrow space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c2212">
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space==3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" sp=ace=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo clas=s=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx= -mrow>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>=
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">epsilon</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-o=pen">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                            <mn>3</mn>
                                            <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                            <mn>2</mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass=-close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                            </ma= th>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with respect to the first-order ep=
                    silon tolerance.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bereznyi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-51" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bereznyi, Daniel et al. =E2=80=9CNode-attrib=
            uted Spatial Graph Partitioning=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proce=
                edings of the
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">28th International Conference on=
                Advances in Geographic Information
                Systems</span>. SIGSPATIAL
            =E2=80=9920. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"smal=l-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1145/3397536.3422198.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Given a spatial graph and a set
                of node attributes, the Node-attributed Spatial Graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Partitioning (NSGP) problem partit=
                    ions a node-attributed spatial graph into k
                    homogeneous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sub-graphs that minimize both the =
                    total RMSErank1 and edge-cuts while meeting a
                    size constraint
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the sub-graphs. RMSErank1 is th=
                    e Root Mean Square Error between a matrix and
                    its rank-one
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">decomposition. The NSGP problem is=
                    important for many societal applications such
                    as identifying
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">homogeneous communities in a spati=
                    al graph and detecting interrelated patterns in
                    traffic accidents.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This problem is NP-hard; it is com=
                    putationally challenging because of the large
                    size of spatial graphs

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the constraint that the sub-gr=
                    aphs must be homogeneous, i.e. similar in terms
                    of node attributes.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This paper proposes a novel approa=
                    ch for finding a set of homogeneous sub-graphs
                    that can minimize
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both the total RMSErank1 and edge-=
                    cuts while meeting the size constraint.
                    Experiments and a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">case study using U.S. Census datas=
                    ets and HP#6 watershed network datasets
                    demonstrate that the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proposed approach partitions a spa=
                    tial graph into a set of homogeneous sub-graphs
                    and reduces the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational cost.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bergamaschi2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-52" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bergamaschi, Luca. =E2=80=9CA Survey of Low-=
            Rank Updates of Preconditioners for Sequences of
            Symmetric Linear Systems=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                Algorithms </span>13.4 (Apr. 2020), p. 100. <span
                class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span=class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.3390/a13040100.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The aim of this survey is to
                review some recent developments in devising efficient precon=
                ditioners
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for sequences of symmetric positiv=
                    e definite (SPD) linear systems </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"96" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0=.15em;">
                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1=D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <= mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>=
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                    <mjx-mro= w size=3D"s">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I">
                                                </mj= x-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-msub=space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15=em;">
                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45=8 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                        <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-ms= pace class=3D"thinspace" style=3D"width: 0.3em;">
                            </mjx-mspace>
                            <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i" space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D">
                                    <= /mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31="></mjx-c></mjx-mn><mjx-mo class=3D" mjx-n
                                                MathClass-punc">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i MathClass-op" space=3D"2">
                                                    <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2026">
                                            </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c2061"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mspace class=3D"thinspace" style==3D"width: 0.3em;"></mjx-mspace>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>A</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>k</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <= /msub>
                                    <msub>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>x</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>k</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </msub>
                                    <mo class==3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                    <msub>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>b</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>k</mi>
                                            </m= row>
                                    </msub>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                    <mspace width=3D"0.3em" class==3D"thinspace"></mspace>
                                    <mi>k</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                    <mn>1=
                                    </mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                    <mi class=3D"MathClass-op">=E2=80=
                                        =A6</mi>
                                    <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                    <mspace width=3D"0.3em" class=3D"thinspace">
                                        </msp= ace>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">arising in many scientific applications, =
                    such as discretization of transient Partial
                    Differential Equations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(PDEs), solution of eigenvalue pro=
                    blems, (Inexact) Newton methods applied to
                    nonlinear systems,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rational Krylov methods for comput=
                    ing a function of a matrix. In this paper, we
                    will analyze a number
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of techniques of updating a given =
                    initial preconditioner by a low-rank matrix
                    with the aim of improving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the clustering of eigenvalues arou=
                    nd 1, in order to speed-up the convergence of
                    the Preconditioned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Conjugate Gradient (PCG) method. W=
                    e will also review some techniques to
                    efficiently approximate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linearly independent vectors which=
                    constitute the low-rank corrections and whose
                    choice is crucial for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the effectiveness of the approach.=
                    Numerical results on real-life applications
                    show that the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a given iterative solver can be=
                    very much enhanced by the use of low-rank
                    updates.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bergamaschi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-53" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bergamaschi, Luca, Jose Marin, and Angeles M=
            artinez. =E2=80=9CCompact Quasi-Newton
            preconditioners for SPD linear systems=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020=
            ). arXiv: 2001.01062 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper preconditioners for
                the Conjugate Gradient method are studied to solve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Newton system with symmetric p=
                    ositive definite Jacobian. In particular, we
                    define a sequence
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of preconditioners built by means =
                    of SR1 and BFGS low-rank updates. We develop
                    conditions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">under which the SR1 update maintai=
                    ns the preconditioner SPD. Spectral analysis of
                    the SR1
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preconditioned Jacobians shows an =
                    improved eigenvalue distribution as the Newton
                    iteration proceeds.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">A compact matrix formulation of th=
                    e preconditioner update is developed which
                    reduces the cost of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its application and is more suitab=
                    le for parallel implementation. Some notes on
                    the implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the corresponding Inexact Newto=
                    n method are given and numerical results on a
                    number of model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems illustrate the efficiency=
                    of the proposed preconditioners.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Berger2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-54" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Berger, Guillaume O. et al. =E2=80=9COn the =
            Quality of First-Order Approximation of Functions with
            H=C3=B6lder Continuous Gradient=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). arXi=
            v: 2001.07946 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We show that H=C3=B6lder continuity of
                the gradient is not only a sufficient condition, but also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a necessary condition for the exis=
                    tence of a global upper bound on the error of
                    the first-order Taylor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approximation. We also relate this=
                    global upper bound to the H=C3=B6lder constant of
                    the gradient. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">relation is expressed as an interv=
                    al, depending on the H=C3=B6lder constant, in which
                    the error of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">first-order Taylor approximation i=
                    s guaranteed to be. We show that, for the
                    Lipschitz continuous case,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the interval cannot be reduced. An=
                    application to the norms of quadratic forms is
                    proposed, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">allows us to derive a novel charac=
                    terization of Euclidean norms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bergou2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-55" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bergou, E., Y. Diouane, and V. Kungurtsev. =
            =E2=80=9CConvergence and Complexity Analysis of a
            Levenberg-Marquardt Algorithm for Inverse Problems=E2=80=9D. In=
            : <span class=3D"cmti-10">JOTA, 2020 </span>(Apr.
            2020). arXiv:
            2004.03005v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm
                is one of the most popular algorithms for finding the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution of nonlinear least square=
                    s problems. Across different modified
                    variations of the basic procedure,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the algorithm enjoys global conver=
                    gence, a competitive worst case iteration
                    complexity rate, and a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">guaranteed rate of local convergen=
                    ce for both zero and nonzero small residual
                    problems, under suitable

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">assumptions. We introduce a novel =
                    Levenberg-Marquardt method that matches,
                    simultaneously, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state of the art in all of these c=
                    onvergence properties with a single seamless
                    algorithm. Numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments confirm the theoretica=
                    l behavior of our proposed algorithm.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bernaschi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-56" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bernaschi, Massimo, Pasqua D=E2=80=99Ambra, =
            and Dario Pasquini. =E2=80=9CBootCMatchG: An adaptive
            Algebraic MultiGrid linear solver for GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: <span=class=3D"cmti-10">Software Impacts
            </span>(Nov. 2020),
            p. 100041.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1016/j.simpa.2020.100041.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse solvers are one of the
                building blocks of any technology for reliable and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high-performance scientific and en=
                    gineering computing. In this paper we present a
                    software package
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which implements an efficient mult=
                    igrid sparse solver running on Graphics
                    Processing Units. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">package is a branch of a wider ini=
                    tiative of software development for sparse
                    Linear Algebra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computations on emergent HPC archi=
                    tectures involving a large research group
                    working in many
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application projects over the last=
                    ten years.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bertsimas2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-57" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bertsimas, Dimitris, Ryan Cory-Wright, and J=
            ean Pauphilet. =E2=80=9CSolving Large-Scale Sparse PCA
            to Certifiable (Near) Optimality=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXi=
            v: 2005.05195v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse principal component
                analysis (PCA) is a popular dimensionality reduction techniq=
                ue
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for obtaining principal components=
                    which are linear combinations of a small
                    subset of the original
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">features. Existing approaches cann=
                    ot supply certifiably optimal principal
                    components with more than</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"97" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c3D"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30">
                                    <= /mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I">
                                    <= /mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"i=nline">
                            <mi>p</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                            <mn>100</mn>
                            <mi>s</mi>=
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">covariates. By reformulating sparse PCA a=
                    s a convex mixed-integer semidefinite
                    optimization problem,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we design a cutting-plane method w=
                    hich solves the problem to certifiable
                    optimality at the scale of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">selecting k=3D10s covariates from =
                    p=3D300 variables, and provides small bound gaps at
                    a larger scale.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We also propose two convex relaxat=
                    ions and randomized rounding schemes that
                    provide certifiably
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">near-exact solutions within minute=
                    s for p=3D100s or hours for p=3D1,000s. Using
                    real-world financial and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">medical datasets, we illustrate ou=
                    r approach=E2=80=99s ability to derive interpretable
                    principal components
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tractably at scale.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Besta2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-58" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Besta, Maciej et al. =E2=80=9CHigh-Performan=
            ce Parallel Graph Coloring with Strong Guarantees on
            Work, Depth, and Quality=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                Proceedings of the ACM/IEEE International Conference on
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">High Performance Computing, Netw=
                orking, Storage and Analysis, November 2020
            </span>(Aug. 2020).
            arXiv: 2008.11321 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We develop the first parallel
                graph coloring heuristics with strong theoretical guarantees=
                on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">work and depth and coloring qualit=
                    y. The key idea is to design a relaxation of
                    the vertex degeneracy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">order, a well-known graph theory c=
                    oncept, and to color vertices in the order
                    dictated by this relaxation.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This introduces a tunable amount o=
                    f parallelism into the degeneracy ordering that
                    is otherwise hard
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to parallelize. This simple idea e=
                    nables significant benefits in several key
                    aspects of graph coloring. For
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">example, one of our algorithms ens=
                    ures polylogarithmic depth and a bound on the
                    number of used
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">colors that is superior to all oth=
                    er parallelizable schemes, while maintaining
                    work-efficiency. In addition
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to provable guarantees, the develo=
                    ped algorithms have competitive run-times for
                    several real-world
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graphs, while almost always provid=
                    ing superior coloring quality. Our degeneracy
                    ordering relaxation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is of separate interest for algori=
                    thms outside the context of coloring.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bian2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-59" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bian, Haodong et al. =E2=80=9CALBUS: A metho=
            d for efficiently processing SpMV using SIMD
            and Load balancing=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Future=
                Generation Computer Systems </span>(Nov. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <= span class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>:
            10.1016/j.future.2020.10.036.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">SpMV (Sparse matrix-vector
                multiplication) is widely used in many fields. Improving the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance of SpMV has been the p=
                    ursuit of many researchers. Parallel SpMV using
                    multi-core
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processors has been a standard par=
                    allel method used by researchers. In reality,
                    the number of non-zero
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">elements in many sparse matrices i=
                    s not evenly distributed, so parallelism
                    without preprocessing will

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cause a large amount of performanc=
                    e loss due to uneven load. In this paper, we
                    propose ALBUS
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(Absolute Load Balancing Using SIM=
                    D (Single Instruction Multiple Data)), a method
                    for efficiently
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processing SpMV using load balanci=
                    ng and SIMD vectorization. On the one hand,
                    ALBUS can achieve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multi-core balanced load processin=
                    g; on the other hand, it gives full play to the
                    ability of SIMD
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vectorization parallelism under th=
                    e CPU. We selected 20 sets of regular matrices
                    and 20 sets of irregular
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices to form the Benchmark sui=
                    te. We performed SpMV performance comparison
                    tests on ALBUS,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CSR5 (Compressed Sparse Row 5), Me=
                    rge(Merge-based SpMV), and MKL (Math Kernel
                    Library)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">under the same conditions. On the =
                    E5-2670 v3 CPU platform, For 20 sets of regular
                    matrices, ALBUS
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can achieve an average speedup of =
                    1.59x, 1.32x, 1.48x (up to 2.53x, 2.22x, 2.31x)
                    compared to CSR5,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Merge, MKL, respectively. For 20 s=
                    ets of irregular matrices, ALBUS can achieve an
                    average speedup
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of 1.38x, 1.42x, 2.44x (up to 2.33=
                    x, 2.24x, 5.37x) compared to CSR5, Merge, MKL,
                    respectively.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Blanco2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-60" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Blanco, Mark P., Scott McMillan, and Tze Men=
            g Low. =E2=80=9CTowards an Objective Metric for the
            Performance of Exact Triangle Count=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020).=
            arXiv: 2009.07935 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.PF]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The performance of graph
                algorithms is often measured in terms of the number of trave=
                rsed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">edges per second (TEPS). However, =
                    this performance metric is inadequate for a
                    graph operation such as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exact triangle counting. In triang=
                    le counting, execution times on graphs with a
                    similar number of edges
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be distinctly different as dem=
                    onstrated by results from the past Graph
                    Challenge entries. We discuss
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the need for an objective performa=
                    nce metric for graph operations and the desired
                    characteristics of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such a metric such that it more ac=
                    curately captures the interactions between the
                    amount of work
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performed and the capabilities of =
                    the hardware on which the code is executed.
                    Using exact triangle
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">counting as an example, we derive =
                    a metric that captures how certain techniques
                    employed in many
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementations improve performanc=
                    e. We demonstrate that our proposed metric can
                    be used to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluate and compare multiple appr=
                    oaches for triangle counting, using a SIMD
                    approach as a case
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">study against a scalar baseline.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bogle2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-61" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bogle, Ian et al. =E2=80=9CDistributed Memor=
            y Graph Coloring Algorithms forMultiple GPUs=E2=80=9D. In:
            (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url=
                </span></span>: <a href=3D"http://www.cs.rpi.edu/~slotag/pub/Coloring-IA320=.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://www.cs.rpi.edu/~slotag/p=
                    ub/Coloring-IA320.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Graph coloring is often used in
                parallelizing scientific computations that run in distribute=
                d
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and multi-GPU environments; it ide=
                    ntifies sets of independent data that can be
                    updated in parallel.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Many algorithms exist for graph co=
                    loring on a single GPU or in distributed
                    memory, but hybrid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">MPI+GPU algorithms have been unexp=
                    lored until this work, to the best of our
                    knowledge. We present
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">several MPI+GPU coloring approache=
                    s that use implementations of the distributed
                    coloring algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of Gebremedhin et al. and the shar=
                    ed-memory algorithms of Deveci et al. The
                    on-node parallel coloring
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">uses implementations in KokkosKern=
                    els, which provide parallelization for both
                    multicore CPUs and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPUs. We further extend our approa=
                    ches to solve for distance-2 coloring, giving
                    the first known
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed and multiGPU algorithm=
                    for this problem. In addition, we propose
                    novel methods to reduce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication in distributed graph=
                    coloring. Our experiments show that our
                    approaches operate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiently on inputs too large to=
                    fit on a single GPU and scale up to graphs
                    with 76.7 billion edges
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">running on 128 GPUs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Boley2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-62" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Boley, Daniel. =E2=80=9COn Fast Computation =
            of Directed Graph Laplacian Pseudo-Inverse=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb.
            2020). arXiv: 2002.12773 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</spa= n>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Laplacian matrix and its
                pseudo-inverse for a strongly connected directed graph is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fundamental in computing many prop=
                    erties of a directed graph. Examples include
                    random-walk
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">centrality and betweenness measure=
                    s, average hitting and commute times, and other
                    connectivity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">measures. These measures arise in =
                    the analysis of many social and computer
                    networks. In this short
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paper, we show how a linear system=
                    involving the Laplacian may be solved in time
                    linear in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number of edges, times a factor de=
                    pending on the separability of the graph. This
                    leads directly to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the column-by-column computation o=
                    f the entire Laplacian pseudo-inverse in time
                    quadratic in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number of nodes, i.e., constant ti=
                    me per matrix entry. The approach is based on
                    =E2=80=9Doff-the-shelf=E2=80=9D iterative

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods for which global linear co=
                    nvergence is guaranteed, without recourse to
                    any matrix elimination
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bolte2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-63" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bolte, Jerome and Edouard Pauwels. =E2=80=9C=
            Curiosities and counterexamples in smooth convex
            optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). arXiv: 2001.07999 <span= class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Counterexamples to some
                old-standing optimization problems in the smooth convex coer=
                cive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">setting are provided. We show that=
                    block-coordinate, steepest descent with exact
                    search or Bregman
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">descent methods do not generally c=
                    onverge. Other failures of various desirable
                    features are established:
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">directional convergence of Cauchy=
                    =E2=80=99s gradient curves, convergence of Newton=E2=80=99s
                    flow, finite length of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Tikhonov path, convergence of cent=
                    ral paths, or smooth Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz
                    inequality. All examples
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are planar. These examples are bas=
                    ed on general smooth convex interpolation
                    results. Given a decreasing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sequence of positively curved C k =
                    convex compact sets in the plane, we provide a
                    level set interpolation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a C k smooth convex function wh=
                    ere k </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attache=d_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"98" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2265">
                                </mj= x-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline="><mo class=3D"
                            MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A5</mo></math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mj= x-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">2 is arbitrary. If the intersection is re=
                        duced to one point our interpolant has positive
                        definite Hessian,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">otherwise it is positive definite =
                        out of the solution set. Furthermore , given a
                        sequence of decreasing
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">polygons we provide an interpolant=
                        agreeing with the vertices and whose gradients
                        coincide with
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prescribed normals.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bonifati2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-64" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bonifati, Angela, Stefania Dumbrava, and Har=
            idimos Kondylakis. =E2=80=9CGraph Summarization=E2=80=9D. In:
            (Apr. 2020). arXiv: 2004.14794v2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DB=
                ]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The continuous and rapid growth
                of highly interconnected datasets, which are both
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">voluminous and complex, calls for =
                    the development of adequate processing and
                    analytical techniques.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">One method for condensing and simp=
                    lifying such datasets is graph summarization.
                    It denotes a series
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of application-specific algorithms=
                    designed to transform graphs into more compact
                    representations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">while preserving structural patter=
                    ns, query answers, or specific property
                    distributions. As this problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is common to several areas studyin=
                    g graph topologies, different approaches, such
                    as clustering,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compression, sampling, or influenc=
                    e detection, have been proposed, primarily
                    based on statistical and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization methods. The focus of=
                    our chapter is to pinpoint the main graph
                    summarization methods,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">but especially to focus on the mos=
                    t recent approaches and novel research trends
                    on this topic, not yet
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">covered by previous surveys.</span=>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Booth2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-65" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Booth, Joshua Dennis. =E2=80=9CAuto Adaptive=
            Irregular OpenMP Loops=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv:
            2007.07977v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">OpenMP is a standard for the
                parallelization due to the ease in programming parallel-for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">loops in a fork-join manner. Many =
                    shared-memory applications are implemented
                    using this model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">despite not being ideal for applic=
                    ations with high load imbalance, such as those
                    that make irregular
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory accesses. One parameter, i.=
                    e., chunk size, is made available to users in
                    order to mitigate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance loss. However, this pa=
                    rameter is dependent on architecture, system
                    load, application, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">input; making it difficult to tune=
                    . We present an OpenMP scheduler that does an
                    adaptive tuning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for chunk size for unbalanced appl=
                    ications that make irregular memory accesses.
                    In particular, this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method(iCh) uses work-stealing for=
                    imbalance and adapts chunk size using a
                    force-feedback model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that approximates variance of task=
                    length in a chunk. This scheduler has low
                    overhead and allows
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for active load balancing while th=
                    e applications are running. We demonstrate this
                    using both sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix-vector multiplication (spmv=
                    ) and Betweenness Centrality (bc) and show that
                    iCh can achieve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">average speedups close (i.e., with=
                    in 1.061x for spmv and 1.092x for bc) of either
                    OpenMP loops
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scheduled with dynamic or work-ste=
                    aling methods that had chunk size tuned
                    offline.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Booth2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-66" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Booth, Joshua Dennis and Gregory Bolet. =E2=
            =80=9CAn On-Node Scalable Sparse Incomplete LU
            Factorization for a Many-Core Iterative Solver with Javelin=E2=
            =80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Parallel Computing
            </span>(Mar.
            2020), p. 102622. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.parco.2020.102622.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a scalable incomplete
                LU factorization to be used as a preconditioner for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving sparse linear systems with=
                    iterative methods in the package called
                    Javelin. Javelin allows for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improved parallel factorization on=
                    shared-memory many-core systems by packaging
                    the coefficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix into a format that allows f=
                    or high performance sparse matrix-vector
                    multiplication and sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">triangular solves with minimal ove=
                    rheads. The framework achieves these goals by
                    using a collection
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of traditional permutations, point=
                    -to-point thread synchronizations, tasking, and
                    segmented prefix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scans in a conventional compressed=
                    sparse row (CSR) format. Moreover, this
                    framework stresses the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">importance of co-designing depende=
                    nt tasks, such as sparse factorization and
                    triangular solves, on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">highly-threaded architectures. We =
                    compare our method to the past distributed
                    methods for incomplete
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization (Aztec) and current =
                    multithreaded packages (WSMP) in order to
                    demonstrate the importance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of having highly threaded factoriz=
                    ations on many-core systems. Using these
                    changes, traditional fill-in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and drop tolerance methods can be =
                    used, while still being able to have observed
                    speedups of up to</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"99" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c223C">
                                </mjx= -c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34">
                                </= mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assist= ive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3=.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=
                                =88=BC</mo>
                            <mn>42</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">on 68 Intel Knights Landing cores and </s= pan><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"100" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c223C"></mjx-c>
                                </m= jx-mo>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>=
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=bin">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mm= l unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1=998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=88=BC</= mo>
                                    <mn>12</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">on 14 Intel Haswell cores. Moreover, this=
                        work provides insight into how the new
                        data-structure impacts
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteration counts, and provides ins=
                        ight into future improvements, such as point to
                        GPUs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bosilca2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-67" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bosilca, G. et al. =E2=80=9CThe Template Tas=
            k Graph (TTG) =E2=80=93 an emergingpractical dataflow
            programming paradigm forscientific simulation at extreme scale=
            =E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of
                the
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">2020 IEEE/ACM 5th International =
                Workshop on Extreme Scale Programming Models
                and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Middleware</span>. ESPM2 =E2=80=
            =9920. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We describe TESSE, an emerging
                general-purpose, open-source software ecosystem that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">attacks the twin challenges of pro=
                    grammer productivity and portable performance
                    for advanced
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scientific applications on modern =
                    high-performance computers. TESSE builds upon
                    and extends the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PARSEC DAG/- dataflow runtime with=
                    a new Domain Specific Languages (DSL) and new
                    integration
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">capabilities. Motivating this work=
                    is our belief that such a dataflow model,
                    perhaps with applications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">composed in domain specific langua=
                    ges, can overcome many of the challenges faced
                    by a wide variety
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of irregular applications that are=
                    poorly served by current programming and
                    execution models. Two
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such applications from many-body p=
                    hysics and applied mathematics are briefly
                    explored. This paper
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">focuses upon the Template Task Gra=
                    ph (TTG), which is TESSE=E2=80=99s main C++ API that
                    provides a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">powerful work/data-flow programmin=
                    g model. Algorithms on spatial trees,
                    block-sparse tensors, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">wave fronts are used to illustrate=
                    the API and associated concepts, as well as to
                    compare with related
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Botoeva2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-68" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Botoeva, lena et al. =E2=80=9CEfficient Veri=
            fication of ReLU-based Neural Networks via Dependency
            Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of t=
                he 34th AAAI Conference on Artificial
                Intelligence</span>. AAI20. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce an efficient method
                for the verification of ReLU-based feed-forward neural
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">networks. We derive an automated p=
                    rocedure that exploits dependency relations
                    between the ReLU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nodes, thereby pruning the search =
                    tree that needs to be considered by MILP-based
                    formulations of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the verification problem. We augme=
