                    nt the resulting algorithm with methods for
                    input domain splitting
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and symbolic interval propagation.=
                    We present Venus, the resulting verification
                    toolkit, and evaluate it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the ACAS collision avoidance ne=
                    tworks and models trained on the MNIST and
                    CIFAR-10 datasets.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The experimental results obtained =
                    indicate considerable gains over the present
                    state-of-the-art tools.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Boukaram2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-69" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Boukaram, Wajih et al. =E2=80=9CHierarchical=
            matrix approximations for space-fractional diffusion
            equations=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Computer Method=
                s in Applied Mechanics and Engineering </span>369
            (Sept. 2020),
            p. 113191. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                    doi</span></span>: 10.1016/j.cma.2020.113191.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Space fractional diffusion models
                generally lead to dense discrete matrix operators, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lead to substantial computational =
                    challenges when the system size becomes large.
                    For a state of size</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"101" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>N=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">full representation of a fractiona=
                    l diffusion matrix would require </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"102" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>=
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"ht=tp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>N</mi>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"=MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>

                <span class=3D"cmr-9">memory storage requirement, with a simila=
                    r estimate for matrix=E2=80=93vector products. In this
                    work, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">present </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-= container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0=" ctxtmenu_counter=3D" 103" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;"=>
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc m=jx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c210B TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mn clas=s=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assis= tive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w=3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=
                                =E2=84=8B</mi>
                            <mn>2</mn>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix representation and algorithms that=
                            are amenable to efficient implementation on
                            GPUs, and that
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can reduce the cost of storing the=
                            se operators to </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMe=nu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"104" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"=></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-op=en">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mj=x-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo=class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <= /mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inli=ne"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <= mi>O</mi>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>N</mi>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <m= o class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-c= ontainer>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">asymptotically. Matrix=E2=80=93vector mul=
                                tiplications can be performed in asymptotically linear
                                time as well.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Performance of the algorithms is a=
                                ssessed in light of 2D simulations of space
                                fractional diffusion
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equation with constant diffusivity=
                                . Attention is focused on smooth particle
                                approximation of the
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">governing equations, which lead to=
                                discrete operators involving explicit radial
                                kernels. The algorithms
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are first tested using the fundame=
                                ntal solution of the unforced space fractional
                                diffusion equation in
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an unbounded domain, and then for =
                                the steady, forced, fractional diffusion
                                equation in a bounded
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">domain. Both matrix-inverse and ps=
                                eudo-transient solution approaches are
                                considered in the latter
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">case. Our experiments show that th=
                                e construction of the fractional diffusion
                                matrix, the matrix=E2=80=93vector
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication, and the generation=
                                of an approximate inverse pre-conditioner all
                                perform very well on a
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">single GPU on 2D problems with </s= pan><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"105" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                            <= /mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                        x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mi>N</mi>
                                            </= math>
                                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">in the range </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"106" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:
                                    relative;">
                                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space==3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n M=athClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-= mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30="></mjx-c><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c36"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assisti= ve-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.=org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mn>105</mn>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass=-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                                    <mn>106</mn>
                                                    <= /math>
                                                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In addition, the tests also showed=
                            that, for the entire range of parameters and
                            fractional orders
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">considered, results obtained using=
                            the </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"107" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c210B TEX-SC="></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mn class=3D" mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                            </mjx-c=>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inli=ne"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <= mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=E2=84=8B</mi>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                </math></mjx-assisti= ve-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">approximations were in close agreement wi=
                            th results obtained using dense operators, and
                            exhibited the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">same spatial order of convergence.=
                            Overall, the present experiences showed that
                            the </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"108" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c210B TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c32"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" d=isplay=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" displa=y=3D"inline">
                                    <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=E2=84=8B</mi>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                    </math=>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix framework promises to provide prac=
                            tical means to handle large-scale space
                            fractional diffusion
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">models in several space dimensions=
                            , at a computational cost that is
                            asymptotically similar to the cost
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of handling classical diffusion eq=
                            uations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bramas2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-70" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bramas, B=C3=A9renger and Alain Ketterlin. =
            =E2=80=9CImproving parallel executions by increasing task
            granularity in task-based runtime systems using acyclic DAG clu=
            stering=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">PeerJ
                Computer
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Science </span>6 (Jan. 2020), e2=
            47. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: =
            10.7717/peerj-cs.247.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The task-based approach is a
                parallelization paradigm in which an algorithm is transforme=
                d
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">into a direct acyclic graph of tas=
                    ks: the vertices are computational elements
                    extracted from the original
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm and the edges are depend=
                    encies between those. During the execution, the
                    management of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the dependencies adds an overhead =
                    that can become significant when the
                    computational cost of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tasks is low. A possibility to red=
                    uce the makespan is to aggregate the tasks to
                    make them heavier, while
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">having fewer of them, with the obj=
                    ective of mitigating the importance of the
                    overhead. In this paper,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we study an existing clustering/pa=
                    rtitioning strategy to speed up the parallel
                    execution of a task-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application. We provide two additi=
                    onal heuristics to this algorithm and perform
                    an in-depth study on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a large graph set. In addition, we=
                    propose a new model to estimate the execution
                    duration and use
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it to choose the proper granularit=
                    y. We show that this strategy allows speeding
                    up a real numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application by a factor of 7 on a =
                    multi-core system.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Brayford2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-71" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Brayford, David et al. =E2=80=9CAnalyzing Pe=
            rformance Properties Collected by the PerSyst Scalable
            HPC Monitoring Tool=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv: 2009.060=
            61 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The ability to understand how a
                scientific application is executed on a large HPC system
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is of great importance in allocati=
                    ng resources within the HPC data center. In
                    this paper, we describe
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">how we used system performance dat=
                    a to identify: execution patterns, possible
                    code optimizations and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improvements to the system monitor=
                    ing. We also identify candidates for employing
                    machine learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques to predict the performa=
                    nce of similar scientific codes.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Brock2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-72" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Brock, Benjamin, Aydin Buluc, et al. =E2=80=
            =9CConsiderations for a Distributed GraphBLAS API=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 IEEE Intern=
                ational Parallel and Distributed Processing
                Symposium
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Workshops</span>. IPDPSW =E2=80=
            =9920. IEEE, May 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                    doi</span></span>: 10.1109/ipdpsw50202.2020.00048.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The GraphBLAS emerged from an
                international effort to standardize linear-algebraic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">building blocks for computing on g=
                    raphs and graph-structured data. The GraphBLAS
                    is expressed

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as a C API and has paved the way f=
                    or multiple implementations. The GraphBLAS C
                    API, however,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">does not define how distributed-me=
                    mory parallelism should be handled. This paper
                    reviews various
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches for a GraphBLAS API for=
                    distributed computing. This work is guided by
                    our experience
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with existing distributed memory l=
                    ibraries. Our goal for this paper is to
                    highlight the pros and cons
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of different approaches rather tha=
                    n to advocate for one particular choice.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Brock2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-73" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Brock, Benjamin, Ayd=C4=B1n Bulu=C3=A7, et a=
            l. <span class=3D"cmti-10">A Roadmap for the GraphBLAS C++
                API</span>. Tech. rep.
            U.C. Berkeley, 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The GraphBLAS is an API for graph
                algorithms expressed in terms of linear algebra.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The current GraphBLAS specificatio=
                    n is for the C Programming Language.
                    Implementations of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GraphBLAS exposed a number of limi=
                    tations due to C that restrict both the
                    expressiveness and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the performance of the GraphBLAS. =
                    The C++ language=E2=80=99s first-class support for
                    generics, including
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">template metaprogramming, addresse=
                    s these limitations, yielding a simpler
                    GraphBLAS API that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">should deliver better performance =
                    especially for methods based on user-defined
                    types and operators.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">When combined with the pervasivene=
                    ss of C++ across many domains as well as within
                    largescale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed codes, we see a compel=
                    ling argument to define a GraphBLAS C++ API.
                    This paper presents
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a roadmap for the development of a=
                    GraphBLAS C++ API with a focus on the open
                    issues we must
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">resolve before completing the spec=
                    ification. Our goal is to foster discussion
                    within the GraphBLAS
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">user community and receive feedbac=
                    k on the directions we are taking with the
                    GraphBLAS C++ API.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Brown2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-74" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Brown, Cade et al. <span class=3D"cmti-10">D=
                esign, Optimization, and Benchmarking of Dense
                Linear
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Algebra Algorithms on AMD GPUs</= span>. Tech. rep. University of Tennessee,
                2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</= span></span>:
                    <a href=3D"https://www.icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2020/icl-=utk-1405-2020.pdf"
                        class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.icl.ut=
                            k.edu/files/publications/2020/icl-utk-1405-2020.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Dense linear algebra (DLA) has
                historically been in the vanguard of software that must be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adapted first to hardware changes.=
                    This is because DLA is both critical to the
                    accuracy and performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of so many different types of appl=
                    ications, and because they have proved to be
                    outstanding vehicles
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for finding and implementing solut=
                    ions to the problems that novel architectures
                    pose. Therefore, in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this paper we investigate the port=
                    ability of the MAGMA DLA library to the latest
                    AMD GPUs. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">use auto tools to convert the CUDA=
                    code in MAGMA to the HeterogeneousComputing
                    Interface for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Portability (HIP) language. MAGMA =
                    provides LAPACK for GPUs and benchmarks for
                    fundamental
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DLA routines ranging from BLAS to =
                    dense factorizations, linear systems and
                    eigen-problem solvers.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We port these routines to HIP and =
                    quantify currently achievable performance
                    through the MAGMA
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benchmarks for the main workload a=
                    lgorithms on MI25 and MI50 AMD GPUs. Comparison
                    with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance roofline models and th=
                    eoretical expectations are used to identify
                    current limitations and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">directions for future improvements=
                    .</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Brunie2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-75" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Brunie, H. et al. =E2=80=9CTuning Floating-P=
            oint Precision Using Dynamic Program Information
            and Temporal Locality=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Pro=
                ceedings of the 2020 International Conference for High
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Performance Computing, Networkin=
                g, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC =E2=80=9920. Los
            Alamitos, CA, USA:
            IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 694=E2=80=93707. <span cl=ass=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1109/SC41=
            405.2020.00054. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</sp= an></span>:
                <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.=2020.00054" class=3D"url"><span
                        class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputers=
                        ociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00054</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a methodology for
                precision tuning of full applications. These techniquesmust
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">select a search space composed of =
                    either variables or instructions and provide a
                    scalable search
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategy. In full application sett=
                    ings one cannot assume compiler support for
                    practical reasons. Thus,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an additional important challenge =
                    is enabling code refactoring. We argue for an
                    instruction-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">search space and we show: 1) how t=
                    o exploit dynamic program information based on
                    call stacks;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and 2) how to exploit the iterativ=
                    e nature of scientific codes, combined with
                    temporal locality. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applied the methodology to tune th=
                    e implementation of scientific codes written in
                    a combination of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Python, CUDA, C++ and Fortran, tun=
                    ing calls to math exp library functions. The
                    iterative search
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">refinement always reduces the sear=
                    ch complexity and the number of steps to
                    solution. Dynamic program

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">information increases search effic=
                    acy. Using this approach, we obtain application
                    runtime performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improvements up to 27%.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Brunie2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-76" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CTuning Floating-Point Pre=
            cision Using Dynamic Program Information and Temporal
            Locality=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of t=
                he 2020 International Conference for High Performance
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computing, Networking, Storage a=
                nd Analysis</span>. SC =E2=80=9920. Los Alamitos, CA,
            USA: IEEE
            Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 694=E2=80=93707. <span class==3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/SC41405.2020.00054. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span clas=s=3D"small-caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.=2020.00054" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputers=
                    ociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00054</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a methodology for
                precision tuning of full applications. These techniquesmust
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">select a search space composed of =
                    either variables or instructions and provide a
                    scalable search
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategy. In full application sett=
                    ings one cannot assume compiler support for
                    practical reasons. Thus,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an additional important challenge =
                    is enabling code refactoring. We argue for an
                    instruction-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">search space and we show: 1) how t=
                    o exploit dynamic program information based on
                    call stacks;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and 2) how to exploit the iterativ=
                    e nature of scientific codes, combined with
                    temporal locality. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applied the methodology to tune th=
                    e implementation of scientific codes written in
                    a combination of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Python, CUDA, C++ and Fortran, tun=
                    ing calls to math exp library functions. The
                    iterative search
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">refinement always reduces the sear=
                    ch complexity and the number of steps to
                    solution. Dynamic program
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">information increases search effic=
                    acy. Using this approach, we obtain application
                    runtime performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improvements up to 27%.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Brust2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-77" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Brust, J. J., S. Leyffer, and C. G. Petra. =
            =E2=80=9CCompact Representations of Structured BFGS
            Matrices=E2=80=9D. In: (2020). eprint: ANL/MCS-P9279-0120.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">For general large-scale
                optimization problems compact representations exist in which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">recursive quasi-Newton update form=
                    ulas are represented as compact matrix
                    factorizations. For
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems in which the objective fu=
                    nction contains additional structure, so-called
                    structured
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quasiNewton methods exploit availa=
                    ble second-derivative information and
                    approximate unavailable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">second derivatives. This article d=
                    evelops the compact representations of two
                    structured
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Broyden-FletcherGoldfarb-Shanno up=
                    date formulas. The compact representations
                    enable efficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">limited memory and initialization =
                    strategies. Two limited memory line search
                    algorithms are described
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and tested on a collection of prob=
                    lems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bueler2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-78" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bueler, Ed. <span class=3D"cmti-10">PETSc fo=
                r Partial Differential Equations: Numerical
                Solutions in C and Python</span>.
            Society for Industrial and Applied Mathematics, Jan. 2020. <spa= n class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1137/=
                1.9781611976311.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Portable, Extensible Toolkit
                for Scientific Computation (PETSc) is an open-source library=
                of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">advanced data structures and metho=
                    ds for solving linear and nonlinear equations
                    and for managing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">discretizations. This book uses th=
                    ese modern numerical tools to demonstrate how
                    to solve nonlinear partial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">differential equations (PDEs) in p=
                    arallel. It starts from key mathematical
                    concepts, such as Krylov space
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods, preconditioning, multigri=
                    d, and Newton=E2=80=99s method. In PETSc these
                    components are composed at run
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time into fast solvers.</span><br=class=3D"newline"><span
                    class=3D"cmr-9">Discretizations are introduced from=
                    the beginning, with an emphasis on finite difference
                    and finite element
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methodologies. The example C progr=
                    ams of the first 12 chapters, listed on the
                    inside front cover,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solve (mostly) elliptic and parabo=
                    lic PDE problems. Discretization leads to
                    large, sparse, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generally nonlinear systems of alg=
                    ebraic equations. For such problems,
                    mathematical solver concepts
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are explained and illustrated thro=
                    ugh the examples, with sufficient context to
                    speed further
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">development.</span><br class=3D"ne=wline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">PETSc for
                    Partial
                    Differential Equations</span>
        </p>
        <ul class=3D"itemize1">
            <li class=3D"itemize"><span class=3D"cmr-9">addresses both disc=
                    retization and fast solvers for PDEs;</span>
            </li>
            <li class=3D"itemize"><span class=3D"cmr-9">emphasizes practice=
                    more than theory;</span>
            </li>
            <li class=3D"itemize"><span class=3D"cmr-9">contains well-struc=
                    tured examples, with advice on run-time solver
                    choices;</span>

            </li>
            <li class=3D"itemize"><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrates how to=
                    achieve high performance and parallel
                    scalability; and</span>
            </li>
            <li class=3D"itemize"><span class=3D"cmr-9">builds on the reade=
                    r=E2=80=99s understanding of fast solver concepts when
                    applying the Firedrake</span></li>
        </ul>
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmr-9">Python finite element =
                solver library in the last two
                chapters.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">=
                This textbook, the first to cover PETSc
                programming for nonlinear PDEs, provides an on-ramp for
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graduate students and researchers =
                to a major area of high-performance computing
                for science and
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">engineering. It is suitable as a s=
                upplement for courses in scientific computing
                or numerical methods
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for differential equations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Bullins2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-79" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Bullins, Brian and Kevin A. Lai. =E2=80=9CHi=
            gher-order methods for convex-concave min-max
            optimization and monotone variational inequalities=E2=80=9D. In=
            : (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.04528v1
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We provide improved convergence
                rates for constrained convex-concave min-max problems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and monotone variational inequalit=
                    ies with higher-order smoothness. In min-max
                    settings where the</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"109" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c210E TEX-I">
                                    </m= jx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"=inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inlin=e">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">pth</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-cont= ainer><span class=3D"cmr-9">-order
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">derivatives are Lipschitz continuo=
                        us, we give an algorithm HigherOrderMirrorProx
                        that achieves an
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteration complexity of </span>
                    <!-= - l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTM=L" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"110" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posi=tion: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi c=lass=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow= space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=28 TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-bin" space==3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mj=x-i" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D447 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mf= rac>
                                            <mjx-frac>
                                                <mjx-num>
                                                    <mjx-nstrut></mjx-nstrut>
                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                        <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mi>
                                                            <mjx-m= o class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                                <= mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow=>
                                </mjx-num>
                                <mjx-dbox>
                                    <mjx-dtable>
                                        <mjx-line></mjx-line>
                                        <mjx-row>
                                            <mjx-den>
                                                <mj= x-dstrut>
                                                    </mjx-dstrut>
                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                            <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mn>
                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                            </mjx-den>
                                        </mjx-row>
                                        </mjx-dtab= le>
                                </mjx-dbox>
                                </mjx-frac>
                                </mjx-mfrac>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop Ma=thClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>=
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                <math=xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>O</mi>
                                    <= mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathCl=ass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                            <mi>T</mi>
                                            <mfrac>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mi>p</mi>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathCl=ass-bin">+</mo>
                                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                                </mrow>
                                            </mfrac>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo c=lass=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-cont= ainer>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">when given access to an oracle for findin=
                                g a fixed point of a </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"111" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c210E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D="on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"=display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">pth</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-order
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equation. We give analogous rates =
                                for the weak monotone variational inequality
                                problem. For </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"Ma=thJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D="112" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"M=JX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1=D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D="4">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3E"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space==3D"4">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>p</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel"=>&gt;</mo>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class==3D"cmr-9">,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our results improve upon the itera=
                                tion complexity of the first-order Mirror Prox
                                method of Nemirovski
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">[2004] and the second-order method=
                                of Monteiro and Svaiter [2012]. We further
                                instantiate our entire
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm in the unconstrained </s= pan><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"113" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                            <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D">
                                                    <= /mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mo>
                                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                                            <mjx-c
                                                                class=3D"mjx-c32="></mjx-c></mjx-mn></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"
                                                                on" displa=y=3D"inline"><math
                                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                    display=3D"=inline">
                                                                    <mi>p</mi>
                                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                                                </math></mj= x-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">case.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Burke2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-80" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Burke, James V. et al. =E2=80=9CInexact Sequ=
            ential Quadratic Optimization with Penalty Parameter
            Updates within the QP Solver=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-=10">SIAM Journal on Optimization </span>30.3
            (Jan.
            2020),
            pp. 1822=E2=80=931849. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"=small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1137/18m1176488.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper focuses on the design
                of sequential quadratic optimization (commonly known
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as SQP) methods for solving large-=
                    scale nonlinear optimization problems. The most
                    computationally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demanding aspect of such an approa=
                    ch is the computation of the search direction
                    during each iteration,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for which we consider the use of m=
                    atrix-free methods. In particular, we develop a
                    method that requires
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an inexact solve of a single QP su=
                    bproblem to establish the convergence of the
                    overall SQP method. It
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is known that SQP methods can be p=
                    lagued by poor behavior of the global
                    convergence mechanism. To
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">confront this issue, we propose th=
                    e use of an exact penalty function with a
                    dynamic penalty parameter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">updating strategy to be employed w=
                    ithin the subproblem solver in such a way that
                    the resulting search
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">direction predicts progress toward=
                    both feasibility and optimality. We present
                    our parameter updating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategy and prove that, under rea=
                    sonable assumptions, the strategy does not
                    modify the penalty
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parameter unnecessarily. We close =
                    the paper with a discussion of the results of
                    numerical experiments
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that illustrate the benefits of ou=
                    r proposed techniques.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Buttari2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-81" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Buttari, Alfredo et al. <span class=3D"cmti-=10">Block Low Rank Single Precision Coarse
                Grid
                Solvers forExtreme Scale
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Multigrid Methods</span>. Tech. =
            rep. Institut de recherche en informatique de
            Toulouse (IRIT), 2020.
            HAL: 02528532. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-0252=8532" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://hal.archives-ouvertes.f=
                    r/hal-02528532</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Extreme scale simulation requires
                fast and scalable algorithms, such as multigrid methods.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">To achieve asymptotically optimal =
                    complexity it is essential to employ a
                    hierarchy of grids. The cost to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solve the coarsest grid system can=
                    often be neglected in sequential computings,
                    but cannot be ignored
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in massively parallel executions. =
                    In this case, the coarsest grid can be large
                    and its efficient solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">becomes a challenging task. We pro=
                    pose solving the coarse grid system using
                    modern, approximate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse direct methods and investig=
                    ate the expected gains compared with
                    traditional iterative methods.

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Since the coarse grid system only =
                    requires an approximate solution, we show that
                    we can leverage
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">block low-rank techniques, combine=
                    d with the use of single precision arithmetic,
                    to significantly reduce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the computational requirements of =
                    the direct solver. In the case of extreme scale
                    computing, the coarse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">grid system is too large for a seq=
                    uential solution, but too small to permit
                    massively parallel efficiency.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We show that the agglomeration of =
                    the coarse grid system to a subset of
                    processors is necessary for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse direct solver to achieve pe=
                    rformance. We demonstrate the efficiency of the
                    proposed method on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a Stokes-type saddle point system.=
                    We employ a monolithic Uzawa multigrid method.
                    In particular,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we show that the use of an approxi=
                    mate sparse direct solver for the coarse grid
                    system can outperform
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that of a preconditioned minimal r=
                    esidual iterative method. This is demonstrated
                    for the multigrid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution of systems of order up to=
                    1+e11 degrees of freedom on a petascale
                    supercomputer using 43
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">200 processes.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cai2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-82" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cai, Yunfeng and Ping Li. =E2=80=9CSolving t=
            he Robust Matrix Completion Problem via a System of
            Nonlinear Equations=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.1099=
            2v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ML]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We consider the problem of robust
                matrix completion, which aims to recover a low rank
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-c= ontainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"114" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">=
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-= mrow>
                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                            <m= jx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                                                </mj= x-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unsel=ectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Mat=h/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>L</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class==3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </msub>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    <= /mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">and a sparse matrix </span><!-- l. 30 -->=
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"115" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-msub>
                                    <mjx-mrow>=
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D446 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>=
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em; margin-left: -0.03=2em;">
                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-script>
                                </mjx-msub>
                                </mjx-= math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <msub>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>=
                                                    S</mi>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                            </mrow>
                                        </msub>=
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">from incomplete observations of their sum=
                        </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"116" style=3D"font-size: 1=17.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true="><mjx-mi class=3D" mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-m= i>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D="></mjx-c></mjx-mo><mjx-msub space=3D" 4">
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-script s=tyle=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx=-n MathClass-bin">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </= mjx-script>
                    </mjx-msub>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                        <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c2B">
                            </mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    <mjx-msub space=3D"3">
                        <mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx= -mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D446 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mj= x-mrow>
                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em; margin-left:
                                    -0.032em;="><mjx-mrow size=3D" s">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-script>
                    </mjx-msub>
                    <mjx-mo=class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2208">
                            </mjx-= c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c211D TE=X-A"></mjx-c>
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    <mjx-mo cla=ss=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </m= jx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"=></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"i=nline">
                                <mi>M</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                <msub>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>L</mi>=
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </msub>
                                <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                <msub>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>S</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D="MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </msub>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=
                                    =88=88</mo>
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">=E2=84=9Dm</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass=-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                <mi>n</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span= class=3D"cmr-9">.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Algorithmically, the robust matrix=
                                completion problem is transformed into a
                                problem of solving a
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">system of nonlinear equations, and=
                                the alternative direction method is then used
                                to solve the nonlinear
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations. In addition, the algori=
                                thm is highly parallelizable and suitable for
                                large scale problems.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Theoretically, we characterize the=
                                sufficient conditions for when </span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"117" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                    <mjx-msub>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43=F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -=0.15em;">
                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                    <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                        </mjx-script>
                                    </mjx-msub>
                                    </m= jx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <msub>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <m= i>L</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                                </mrow>
                                                </msu= b>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">can be approximated by a low rank approxi=
                                mation of the observed </span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"118" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                    <mjx-msub>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44=0 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -=0.15em; margin-left: -0.081em;">
                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-bin">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                            </mj= x-script>
                                    </mjx-msub>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                                        <msub>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>M</mi>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=
                                                    =88=97</mo>
                                            </mrow>
                                        </msub>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span c=lass=3D"cmr-9">.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">And under proper assumptions, it i=
                                s shown that the algorithm converges to the
                                true solution linearly.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Numerical simulations show that th=
                                e simple method works as expected and is
                                comparable with
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art methods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Calandra2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-83" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Calandra, H. et al. =E2=80=9COn a multilevel=
            Levenberg=E2=80=93Marquardt method for the training
            of artificial neural networks and its application to the soluti=
            on of partial differential
            equations=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Optimization Me=
                thods and Software </span>(June 2020), pp. 1=E2=80=9326. <span class=3D"cmc=sc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1080/10556788.2020.1775828.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we propose a new
                multilevel Levenberg=E2=80=93Marquardt optimizer for the tra=
                ining of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">artificial neural networks with qu=
                    adratic loss function. This setting allows us
                    to get further insight into
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the potential of multilevel optimi=
                    zation methods. Indeed, when the least squares
                    problem arises from
