                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the training of artificial neural =
                    networks, the variables subject to optimization
                    are not related by any
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">geometrical constraints and the st=
                    andard interpolation and restriction operators
                    cannot be employed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">any longer. A heuristic, inspired =
                    by algebraic multigrid methods, is then
                    proposed to construct the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multilevel transfer operators. We =
                    test the new optimizer on an important
                    application: the approximate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution of partial differential e=
                    quations by means of artificial neural
                    networks. The learning problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is formulated as a least squares p=
                    roblem, choosing the nonlinear residual of the
                    equation as a loss
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">function, whereas the multilevel m=
                    ethod is employed as a training method.
                    Numerical experiments
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show encouraging results related t=
                    o the efficiency of the new multilevel
                    optimization method compared
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to the corresponding one-level pro=
                    cedure in this context.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Campos2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-84" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Campos, Juan S., Ruth Misener, and Panos Par=
            pas. =E2=80=9CPartial Lasserre relaxation for sparse
            Max-Cut=E2=80=9D. In: (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A common approach to solve or
                find bounds of polynomial optimization problems like
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Max-Cut is to use the first level =
                    of the Lasserre hierarchy. Higher levels of the
                    Lasserre hierarchy provide
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tighter bounds, but solving these =
                    relaxations is usually computationally
                    intractable. We propose to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strengthen the first level relaxat=
                    ion for sparse Max-Cut problems using
                    constraints from the second
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">order Lasserre hierarchy. We explo=
                    re a variety of approaches for adding a subset
                    of the positive

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">semidefinite constraints of the se=
                    cond order sparse relaxation obtained by using
                    the maximum cliques
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the graph=E2=80=99s chordal ext=
                    ension. We apply this idea to sparse graphs of
                    different sizes and densities,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and provide evidence of its streng=
                    ths and limitations when compared to the
                    state-of-the-art Max-Cut
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solver BiqCrunch and the alternati=
                    ve sparse relaxation CS-TSSOS.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cao2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-85" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cao, Qinglei et al. =E2=80=9CLeveraging PaRS=
            EC Runtime Support to Tackle Challenging 3D
            Data-Sparse Matrix Problems=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-1=0">Proceedings of the 35th IEEE
                International Parallel
                &amp;
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Distributed Processing Symposium=
            </span>. IPDPS =E2=80=9921. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The task-based programming model
                associated with dynamic runtime systems has gained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">popularity for challenging problem=
                    s because of workload imbalance, heterogeneous
                    resources, or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extreme concurrency. During the la=
                    st decade, lowrank matrix approximations, where
                    the main
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">idea consists of exploiting data s=
                    parsity typically by compressing off-diagonal
                    tiles up to an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application-specific accuracy thre=
                    shold, have been adopted to address the curse
                    of dimensionality
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at extreme scale. In this paper, w=
                    e create a bridge between the runtime and the
                    linear algebra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by communicating knowledge of the =
                    data sparsity to the runtime. We design and
                    implement this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">synergistic approach with high use=
                    r productivity in mind, in the context of the
                    PaRSEC runtime
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">system and the HiCMA numerical lib=
                    rary. This requires to extend PaRSEC with new
                    features to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integrate rank information into th=
                    e dataflow so that proper decisions can be
                    taken at runtime. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">focus on the tile low-rank (TLR) C=
                    holesky factorization for solving 3D
                    data-sparse covariance matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems arising in environmental =
                    applications. In particular, we employ the 3D
                    exponential model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of Matern matrix kernel, which exh=
                    ibits challenging nonuniform high ranks in
                    off-diagonal tiles. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">first provide a dynamic data struc=
                    ture management driven by a performance model
                    to reduce extra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating-point operations. Next, w=
                    e optimize the memory footprint of the
                    application by relying on a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dynamic memory allocator, and supp=
                    orted by a rank-aware data distribution to cope
                    with the workload
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">imbalance. Finally, we expose furt=
                    her parallelism using kernel recursive
                    formulations to shorten the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">critical path. Our resulting high-=
                    performance implementation outperforms existing
                    data-sparse TLR
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Cholesky factorization by up to 7-=
                    fold on a large-scale distributed-memory
                    system, while minimizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the memory footprint up to a 44-fo=
                    ld factor. This multidisciplinary work
                    highlights the need to empower
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">runtime systems beyond their origi=
                    nal duty of task scheduling for servicing
                    next-generation low-rank
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix algebra libraries.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cao2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-86" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cao, Xuanyu and K. J. Ray Liu. =E2=80=9CDist=
            ributed Newton=E2=80=99s Method for Network Cost
            Minimization=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE Transac=
                tions on Automatic Control </span>(2020), pp. 1=E2=80=931.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1109/tac.2020.2989266.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this work, we examine a novel
                generic network cost minimization problem, in which every
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">node has a local decision vector t=
                    o optimize. Each node incurs a cost associated
                    with its decision vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">while each link incurs a cost rela=
                    ted to the decision vectors of its two end
                    nodes. All nodes collaborate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to minimize the overall network co=
                    st. The formulated network cost minimization
                    problem has broad
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications in distributed signal=
                    processing and control, in which the notion of
                    link costs often arises.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">To solve this problem in a decentr=
                    alized manner, we develop a distributed variant
                    of the Newton=E2=80=99s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method, which possesses faster con=
                    vergence than alternative first order
                    optimization methods such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as gradient descent and alternatin=
                    g direction method of multipliers. The proposed
                    method is based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on an appropriate splitting of the=
                    Hessian matrix and an approximation of its
                    inverse, which is used
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to determine the Newton step. Glob=
                    al linear convergence of the proposed algorithm
                    is established
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">under several standard technical a=
                    ssumptions on the local cost functions.
                    Furthermore, analogous to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">classical centralized Newton=E2=80=
                    =99s method, a quadratic convergence phase of the
                    algorithm over a certain
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time interval is identified. Final=
                    ly, numerical simulations are conducted to
                    validate the effectiveness
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the proposed algorithm and its =
                    superiority over other first order methods,
                    especially when the cost
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">functions are ill-conditioned. Com=
                    plexity issues of the proposed distributed
                    Newton=E2=80=99s method and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">alternative first order methods ar=
                    e also discussed.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Carmon2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-87" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Carmon, Yair and John C. Duchi. =E2=80=9CFir=
            st-Order Methods for Nonconvex Quadratic
            Minimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.04546 <span= class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We consider minimization of
                indefinite quadratics with either trust-region (norm) constr=
                aints
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or cubic regularization. Despite t=
                    he nonconvexity of these problems we prove
                    that, under mild
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">assumptions, gradient descent conv=
                    erges to their global solutions, and give a
                    non-asymptotic rate of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence for the cubic variant.=
                    We also consider Krylov subspace solutions and
                    establish sharp
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence guarantees to the solu=
                    tions of both trust-region and
                    cubic-regularized problems. Our rates
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mirror the behavior of these metho=
                    ds on convex quadratics and eigenvector
                    problems, highlighting
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">their scalability. When we use Kry=
                    lov subspace solutions to approximate the
                    cubic-regularized Newton
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">step, our results recover the stro=
                    ngest known convergence guarantees to
                    approximate second-order
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stationary points of general smoot=
                    h nonconvex functions.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Carratala2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-88" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Carratal=C3=A1-S=C3=A1ez, Roc=C3=ADo et al. =
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Tiled Algorithms for Efficient
                Task-Parallel H-Matrix Solvers</span>.
            Tech. rep. INRIA, 2020. eprint: hal-02489269. <span class=3D"cm=csc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">url</span></span>: <a href=3D"https://ha=l.inria.fr/hal-02489269"
                class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://hal.=
                    inria.fr/hal-02489269</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we describe and
                evaluate an extension of the Chameleon library to operate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with hierarchical matrices (H-Matr=
                    ices) and hierarchical arithmetic
                    (H-Arithmetic), producing efficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solvers for linear systems arising=
                    in Boundary Element Methods (BEM). Our
                    approach builds
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">upon an open-source H-Matrices lib=
                    rary from Airbus, named Hmat-oss, that collects
                    sequential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical kernels for both hierarc=
                    hical and low-rank structures; the tiled
                    algorithms and task-parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">decompositions available in Chamel=
                    eon for the solution of linear systems; and the
                    StarPU runtime
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">system to orchestrate an efficient=
                    task-parallel (multi-threaded) execution on a
                    multicore architecture.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Using an application producing mat=
                    rices with features close to real industrial
                    applications, we present
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">shared-memory results that demonst=
                    rate a fair level of performance, close to (and
                    sometimes better
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">than) the one offered by a pure H-=
                    Matrix approach, as proposed by Airbus Hmat
                    proprietary (and non
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">open-source) library. Hence, this =
                    combination Chameleon + Hmat-oss proposes the
                    most efficient fully
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">open-source software stack to solv=
                    e dense compressible linear systems on shared
                    memory architectures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(distributed memory is under devel=
                    opment).</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Carson2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-89" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Carson, Erin and Tom=C3=A1=C5=A1 Gergelits. =
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Quarter I Report: Initial
                Exploration of the Use of Mixed
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Precision in Iterative Solvers</= span>. Tech. rep. LLNL-Charles University,
                2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The first quarter of the project
                was primarily spent identifying potential projects at the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intersection of finite precision a=
                    nalysis, mixed precision computation, and
                    Krylov subspace methods.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We summarize our findings in the r=
                    emainder of the document. Other activities
                    include attending
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">biweekly xSDK meetings as well as =
                    contributing material to the technical report
                    and journal versions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the multiprecision landscape pa=
                    per.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">The subsequent quart=
                    er will be spent selecting a subset of the described projects to focus
                    on, performing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">initial numerical experiments to e=
                    valuate the potential for the use of mixed
                    precision, and developing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">initial theoretical analysis.</spa= n>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Carson2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-90" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Carson, Erin, Nicholas J. Higham, and Srikar=
            a Pranesh. =E2=80=9CThree-Precision GMRES-Based
            Iterative Refinement for Least Squares Problems=E2=80=9D. In: (=
            2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">issn</span></spa= n>: 1749-9097. MIMS EPrint:
                MIMS EPrint:2020.5. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sma=ll-caps">url</span></span>: <a
                    href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.u=k/2745/1/paper.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                        class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.ma=
                        ths.manchester.ac.uk/2745/1/paper.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The standard iterative refinement
                procedure for improving an approximate solution to the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">least squares problem </span>
                <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"119" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <= mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c6D"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c69"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=6E"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mtext>
                                </mjx-mstyle>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertic=al-align: -0.15em;">
                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-script>
                                </mjx-msub=>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C"></mjx-c>
                                    </m= jx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C">
                                            </mjx-= c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-I">
                                            </mjx-= c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I">
                                            </mjx-= c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C">
                                            <= /mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-msub>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                                <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C">
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-script style=3D"ve=rtical-align: -0.285em;">
                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                    <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                                        </mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mn>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                        </mjx-script>
                                    </mjx-msub>
                                    </m= jx-math>

                                                    </mr= ow>
                                            </msub>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">|</mo>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">|
                            </= mo>
                            <mi>b</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"i=talic">Ax</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">|</mo>
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-rel">|</mo>
                                </mrow>

                                </mrow>
                            </msub></math></mjx-assist= ive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"120" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2208"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi=class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c211D TEX-A"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-bin" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <= /mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>A</mi>
                                    <m= o class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=88=88</mo>
                                        <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">=E2=84=
                                            =9Dm</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                        <mi>n</mi>
                                </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">with </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container=class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu=_counter=3D"121" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math= class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-re=l" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2265"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D="mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mj= x-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmln=s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>m</mi>
                                                <mo c=lass=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A5</mo>
                                                <mi>n</mi>
                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        <= /mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">has full rank, is based on solving the
                                        </= span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"122" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <= mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                                </= mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">=
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"=3">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                </m= jx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathC=lass-open">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                        <mjx-mo clas=s=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                                                            </mj= x-mo>
                                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">=
                                                                </mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mi>
                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml= unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/19=98/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo=>
                                                            <mrow>
                                                                <mi>m</mi>
                                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                                                <mi>n</mi>
                                                            </mrow>
                                                            <mo cla=ss=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                                    </mrow>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>=
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi>m</mi>
                                                            <mo class=3D"MathC=lass-bin">+</mo>
                                                            <mi>n</mi>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                                        </mrow=>
                                                </math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">augmented system with the aid of a QR fac=
                                torization. In order to exploit multiprecision
                                arithmetic,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iterative refinement can be formul=
                                ated to use three precisions, but the resulting
                                algorithm converges
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">only for a limited range of proble=
                                ms. We build an iterative refinement algorithm
                                called GMRES-LSIR,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analogous to the GMRES-IR algorith=
                                m developed for linear systems [SIAM J. Sci.
                                Comput., 40 (2019),

                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">pp. A817-A847], that solves the au=
                                gmented system using GMRES preconditioned by a
                                matrix based
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the computed QR factors. We exp=
                                lore two left preconditioners; the first has
                                full off-diagonal blocks
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the second is block diagonal a=
                                nd can be applied in either left-sided or split
                                form. We prove
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that for a wide range of problems =
                                the first preconditioner yields backward and
                                forward errors for the
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">augmented system of order the work=
                                ing precision under suitable assumptions on the
                                precisions and
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the problem conditioning. Our proo=
                                f does not extend to the block diagonal
                                preconditioner, but our
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical experiments show that wi=
                                th this preconditioner the algorithm performs
                                about as well in
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">practice.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Carson2020c" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-91" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Carson, Erin, Kathryn Lund, et al. =E2=80=9C=
            An overview of block Gram-Schmidt methods and their
            stability properties=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.120=
            58 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Block Gram-Schmidt algorithms
                comprise essential kernels in many scientific computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications, but for many commonl=
                    y used variants, a rigorous treatment of their
                    stability properties
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">remains open. This survey provides=
                    a comprehensive categorization of block
                    Gram-Schmidt algorithms,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">especially those used in Krylov su=
                    bspace methods to build orthonormal bases one
                    block vector at a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time. All known stability results =
                    are assembled, and new results are summarized
                    or conjectured for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">important communication-reducing v=
                    ariants. A diverse array of numerical
                    illustrations are presented,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">along with the MATLAB code for rep=
                    roducing the results in a publicly available at
                    repository
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">https://github.com/katlund/BlockSt=
                    ab. A number of open problems are discussed,
                    and an appendix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">containing all algorithms type-set=
                    in a uniform fashion is provided.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Carson2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-92" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Carson, Erin and Zden=C4=9Bk Strako=C5=A1. =
            =E2=80=9COn the cost of iterative computations=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D="cmti-10">Philosophical
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Transactions of the Royal Societ=
                y A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering
                Sciences</span>
            378.2166 (Jan. 2020), p. 20190050. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <sp= an class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>:
            10.1098/rsta.2019.0050.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">With exascale-level computation
                on the horizon, the art of predicting the cost of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computations has acquired a renewe=
                    d focus. This task is especially challenging in
                    the case of iterative
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods, for which convergence beh=
                    aviour often cannot be determined with
                    certainty a priori (unless
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we are satisfied with potentially =
                    outrageous overestimates) and which typically
                    suffer from performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bottlenecks at scale due to synchr=
                    onization cost. Moreover, the amplification of
                    rounding errors can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">substantially affect the practical=
                    performance, in particular for methods with
                    short recurrences. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this article, we focus on what we =
                    consider to be key points which are crucial to
                    understanding the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cost of iteratively solving linear=
                    algebraic systems. This naturally leads us to
                    questions on the place of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical analysis in relation to =
                    mathematics, computer science and sciences, in
                    general.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cartis2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-93" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cartis, C., N. I. M. Gould, and Ph. L. Toint=
            . =E2=80=9CStrong Evaluation Complexity of An Inexact
            Trust-Region Algorithm for Arbitrary-Order Unconstrained Noncon=
            vex Optimization=E2=80=9D. In:
            (Nov. 2020). arXiv: 2011.00854 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC=
                ]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A trust-region algorithm using
                inexact function and derivatives values is introduced for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving unconstrained smooth optim=
                    ization problems. This algorithm uses
                    high-order Taylor models
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and allows the search of strong ap=
                    proximate minimizers of arbitrary order. The
                    evaluation complexity of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">finding a </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mj= x-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D="0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"123" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative=;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i"=>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assisti= ve-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.=org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>q</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-m= ml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-th
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approximate minimizer using this a=
                            lgorithm is then shown, under standard
                            conditions, to be </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class==3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_coun=ter=3D"124" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                                    <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC">
                                        </mjx-c>

                                <mjx-mstyle style=3D"font-=size: 119%;">
                                    <mjx-mfenced>

                                            </mjx-c>

                                <mjx-msub>=

                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6D"></mjx-c>=

                                        </mjx-mi>=
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2061"></mjx-c>
                </mj= x-mo>
                </mjx-mrow>

                <mjx= -assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http:/=/www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-sc=ript">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                        <mstyle mathsize=3D"1.19em">

                                        <mi class=3D"qopname">=E2=80=

                            </mrow>
                        </msub>
                        <mo class=3D"MathCla=ss-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                        <mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                            <mrow>
                                <mi>q<= /mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathCla=ss-close">)</mo>
                        </mrow>
                        <mstyle mathsize=3D"1.19em">
                            <mfenced open=3D")" sepa=rators=3D"" close=3D"">
                                <mrow></mrow>
                            </mfenced>
                        </mstyle>
                    </math></mjx-assisti= ve-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">where the </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"125" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-msub>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi cl=ass=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow=>
                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                            <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D457 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-= mrow>
                                    </mjx-script>
                            </mjx-msub>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"=on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" d=isplay=3D"inline">
                                    <msub>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>j</mi>
                                            <= /mrow>
                                    </msub>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">are the order-dependent requested accurac=
                                y thresholds. Remarkably, this order is
                                identical to that of
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">classical trust-region methods usi=
                                ng exact information.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cartis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-94" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cartis, Coralia, Nick Gould, and Philippe L.=
            Toint. =E2=80=9CStrong Evaluation Complexity Bounds
            for Arbitrary-Order Optimization of Nonconvex Nonsmooth Composi=
            te Functions=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan.
            2020). arXiv: 2001.10802 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</spa= n>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce the concept of
                strong high-order approximate minimizers for nonconvex optim=
                ization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems. These apply in both stan=
                    dard smooth and composite non-smooth settings,
                    and additionally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">allow convex or inexpensive constr=
                    aints. An adaptive regularization algorithm is
                    then proposed to find
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such approximate minimizers. Under=
                    suitable Lipschitz continuity assumptions,
                    whenever the feasible
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">set is convex, it is shown that us=
                    ing a model of degree </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"126" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>p=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this algorithm will find a strong =
                    approximate q-th-order minimizer in at most
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"127" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                                            </mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </m= jx-mi>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                    <= /mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mfenced>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" dis=play=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>

                                        </math></mjx-as= sistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluations. If the problem is composite,=
                                    and either </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"

                                            <mi>q</mi>

                                            <mn>1<= /mn>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">or the feasible set is not convex, the bo=
                                    und is then </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"134" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                        </mjx-mi>

                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>

                                                <mjx-msub>

                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D457 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mi>
                                                            <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">

                                                                </mjx-mi>

                                                                </mjx-script>
                                                </mjx-msub>
                                                <mjx-msub>
                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I">
                                                                </mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-script sty=le=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">

                                                        </mjx-scrip= t>

                                                </mjx-mo>

                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-= mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mi>
                                                            <mjx-= mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B">
                                                                    </mjx= -c>
                                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                                                            </= mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mn>
                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                        <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx=-n">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mfenced>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx= -assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http:/=/www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-sc=ript">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                                            <mfenced open=3D"(" separators=3D"" close=3D")">
                                                <mro= w>
                                                    <msub>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi class=3D"qopname">max</mi>
                                                            <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        <mrow>=
                                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A4</mo>
                                                            <mi>j</mi>
                                                            <mo class=3D"=MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A4</mo>
                                                            <mi>q</mi>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                    </msub>
                                                    <msub>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi>=F0=9D=
                                                                =9C=96</mi>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi>j</mi>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                    </msub>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin"=>=E2=88=92</mo>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi>q</mi>
                                                            <mo= class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                                                <mn>1</mn>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-clos=e">)</mo>
                                                    </mrow>
                                                    </mrow>
                                            </mfenced>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container=>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluations. These results not only provi=
                                            de, to our knowledge, the first known bound for
                                            (unconstrained
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or inexpensively-constrained) comp=
                                            osite problems for optimality orders exceeding
                                            one, but also give the
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">first sharp bounds for high-order =
                                            strong approximate </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"135" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45E TEX-=I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                                                    <mi>q</mi>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container><span class==3D"cmr-9">-th
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">order minimizers of standard (unco=
                                            nstrained and inexpensively constrained) smooth
                                            problems, thereby
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complementing known results for we=
                                            ak minimizers.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Champion2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-95" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Champion, Camille et al. =E2=80=9CRobust spe=
            ctral clustering using LASSO regularization=E2=80=9D. In: (Apr.
            2020). arXiv: 2004.03845v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ML]</s= pan>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Cluster structure detection is a
                fundamental task for the analysis of graphs, in order to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">understand and to visualize their =
                    functional characteristics. Among the different
                    cluster structure
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detection methods, spectral cluste=
                    ring is currently one of the most widely used
                    due to its speed and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simplicity. Yet, there are few the=
                    oretical guarantee to recover the underlying
                    partitions of the graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for general models. This paper the=
                    refore presents a variant of spectral
                    clustering, called 1-spectral
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">clustering, performed on a new ran=
                    dom model closely related to stochastic block
                    model. Its goal is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to promote a sparse eigenbasis sol=
                    ution of a 1 minimization problem revealing the
                    natural structure
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the graph. The effectiveness an=
                    d the robustness to small noise perturbations
                    of our technique is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">confirmed through a collection of =
                    simulated and real data examples.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-96" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chang, Tyler H. =E2=80=9CMathematical Softwa=
            re for Multiobjective Optimization Problems=E2=80=9D. PhD
            thesis. Virginia Polytechnic Institute and State University, 20=
            20.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this thesis, two distinct
                problems in data-driven computational science are considered=
                .
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The main problem of interest is th=
                    e multiobjective optimization problem, where
                    the tradeoff surface
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(called the Pareto front) between =
                    multiple conflicting objectives must be
                    approximated in order
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to identify designs that balance r=
                    eal-world tradeoffs. In order to solve
                    multiobjective optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems that are derived from com=
                    putationally expensive blackbox functions, such
                    as engineering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">design optimization problems, seve=
                    ral methodologies are combined, including
                    surrogate modeling,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">trust region methods, and adaptive=
                    weighting. The result is a numerical software
                    package that finds
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approximately Pareto optimal solut=
                    ions that are evenly distributed across the
                    Pareto front, using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">minimal cost function evaluations.=
                    The second problem of interest is the closely
                    related problem of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multivariate interpolation, where =
                    an unknown response surface representing an
                    underlying phenomenon
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is approximated by finding a funct=
                    ion that exactly matches available data. To
                    solve the interpolation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem, a novel algorithm is prop=
                    osed for computing only a sparse subset of the
                    elements in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Delaunay triangulation, as needed =
                    to compute the Delaunay interpolant. For
                    high-dimensional data,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this reduces the time and space co=
                    mplexity of Delaunay interpolation from
                    exponential time to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">polynomial time in practice. For e=
                    ach of the above problems, both serial and
                    parallel implementations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are described. Additionally, both =
                    solutions are demonstrated on real-world
                    problems in computer
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">system performance modeling.</span=>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chatzidimitriou2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-97" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chatzidimitriou, A. and D. Gizopoulos. =E2=
            =80=9CrACE: Reverse-Order Processor Reliability
            Analysis=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 Design, Aut=
                omation Test in Europe Conference
                Exhibition</span>. DATE
            2020. Mar. 2020, pp. 1115=E2=80=931120. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10="><span class=3D"
                small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.23919/DATE48585.2020.9116355.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Modern microprocessors suffer
                from increased error rates that come along with fabrication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">technology scaling. Processor desi=
                    gns continuously become more prone to hardware
                    faults that lead to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">execution errors and system failur=
                    es, which raise the requirement of protection
                    mechanisms. However,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">error mitigation strategies have t=
                    o be applied diligently, as they impose
                    significant power, area, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance overheads. Early and a=
                    ccurate reliability estimation of a
                    microprocessor design is essential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in order to determine the most vul=
                    nerable hardware structures and the most
                    efficient protection
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">schemes. One of the most commonly =
                    used techniques for reliability estimation is
                    Architecturally Correct
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Execution (ACE) analysis.ACE analy=
                    sis can be applied at different abstraction
                    models, including
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">microarchitecture and RTL and ofte=
                    n requires a single or few simulations to
                    report the Architectural
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Vulnerability Factor (AVF) of the =
                    processor structures. However, ACE analysis
                    overestimates the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vulnerability of structures becaus=
                    e of its pessimistic, worst-case nature.
                    Moreover, it only delivers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">coarse-grain vulnerability reports=
                    and no details about the expected result of
                    hardware faults (silent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data corruptions, crashes). In thi=
                    s paper, we present reverse ACE (rACE), a
                    methodology that (a)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improves the accuracy of ACE analy=
                    sis and (b) delivers fine-grain error outcome
                    reports. Using a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reverse-order tracing flow, rACE a=
                    nalysis associates portions of the simulated
                    execution of a program
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with the actual output and the con=
                    trol flow, delivering finer accuracy and
                    results classification. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">findings show that rACE reports an=
                    average 1.45</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_=Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"136" style=3D"=font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hi=dden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7="></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"
                                on" displa=y=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                    display=3D"=inline">
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                <= /mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">overestimation, compared to Statistical F=
                                        ault Injection, for different sizes of the
                                        register file of an
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">out-of-order CPU core (executing b=
                                        oth ARM and x86 binaries), when a baseline ACE
                                        analysis reports
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">2.3</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"137" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-bin">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive= -mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.or=g/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</= mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">overestimation and even refined versions =
                    of ACE analysis report an average of 1.8</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"138" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">overestimation.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chen2020f" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-98" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chen, Chao, Tianyu Liang, and George Biros. =
            =E2=80=9CRCHOL: Randomized Cholesky Factorization
            for Solving SDD Linear Systems=E2=80=9D. In: (Nov. 2020). arXiv=
            : 2011.07769 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce a randomized
                algorithm, namely </span><span class=3D"cmtt-9">rchol</span>=
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">, to construct an
                    approximate Cholesky
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization for a given sparse L=
                    aplacian matrix (a.k.a., graph Laplacian). The
                    (exact) Cholesky
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization for the matrix intro=
                    duces a clique in the associated graph after
                    eliminating every row/column.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">By randomization, </span><span cla=ss=3D"cmtt-9">rchol </span><span
                    class=3D"cmr-9">samples a subset of the ed=
                    ges in the clique. We prove </span><span class=3D"cmtt-9">rchol
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is breakdown
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">free and apply it to solving linea=
                    r systems with symmetric diagonally-dominant
                    matrices. In addition,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we parallelize </span><span class==3D"cmtt-9">rchol </span><span
                    class=3D"cmr-9">based
                    on the nested dissection ordering for shared-memory machines=
                    . Numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments demonstrated the robus=
                    tness and the scalability of </span><span class=3D"cmtt-9">rchol</span>
                <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">. For example, our parallel code
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scaled up to 64 threads on a singl=
                        e node for solving the 3D Poisson equation,
                        discretized with the 7-point
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stencil on a </span><!-- l. 30 -->=
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"139" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx=-n">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                    </mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                            <mjx-= mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                    </mjx= -c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                            </= mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c34">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin=" space=3D" 3">
