                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mj=x-n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30">=
                                        </mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                                        <= /mjx-mn>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline="><math xmlns=3D"
                            http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn=>1024</mn>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                <mn>1024</mn>
                                <mo class=3D"=MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                <mn>1024</mn></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-con= tainer>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">grid, or </span><span class=3D"cmbx-9">on=
                            e billion </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unknowns.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chen2020d" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-99" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chen, Long, Xiaozhe Hu, and Huiwen Wu. =E2=
            =80=9CRandomized Fast Subspace Descent Methods=E2=80=9D.
            In: (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.06589v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[m=
                ath.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Randomized Fast Subspace Descent
                (RFASD) Methods are developed and analyzed for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">smooth and non-constraint convex o=
                    ptimization problems. The efficiency of the
                    method relies on a space
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">decomposition which is stable in <= /span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" ja=x=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"140" style=3D"font-size: 117=.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">=
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>=
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                <math=xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>A</mi>
                                    <= /math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-norm,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and meanwhile, the condition numbe=
                            r </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"141" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tru=e">
                                <mjx-msub>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D705 T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.1=53em;">
                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D=434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-script>
                                </mjx-msub>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <= mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <msub>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>=CE=BA<= /mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>A</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </msub>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-c= ontainer>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">measured in </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-con= tainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    c=txtmenu_counter=3D"142" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <m= jx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mm= l unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1=998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>A</mi>
                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </= mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-norm
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is small. At each iteration, the s=
                                ubspace is chosen randomly either uniformly or
                                by a probability
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proportional to the local Lipschit=
                                z constants. Then in each chosen subspace, a
                                preconditioned gradient
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">descent method is applied. RFASD c=
                                onverges sublinearly for convex functions and
                                linearly for strongly
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex functions. Comparing with t=
                                he randomized block coordinate descent methods,
                                the convergence

                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of RFASD is faster provided </span=><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"=CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"143" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;=position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-= msub>
                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D705 TEX-I">
                    </= mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-mrow>
                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.153em;">
                        <= mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-=I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-script>
                    </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=CE=BA</mi>
                                    </mr= ow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>A</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                            </msub>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container=>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">is small and the subspace decomposition i=
                            s </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"144" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tru=e">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-= mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                <ma= th xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>A</m= i></math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-stable.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This improvement is supported by c=
                            onsidering a multilevel space decomposition for
                            Nesterov=E2=80=99s =E2=80=98worst=E2=80=99
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chen2020e" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-100" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chen, Shixiang et al. =E2=80=9CAn Alternatin=
            g Manifold Proximal Gradient Method for Sparse
            Principal
            Component Analysis and Sparse Canonical Correlation Analysis=E2=
            =80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">INFORMS Journal on
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Optimization </span>(July 2020),=
            ijoo.2019.0032. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</s= pan></span>:
                10.1287/ijoo.2019.0032.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse principal component
                analysis and sparse canonical correlation analysis are two
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">essential techniques from high-dim=
                    ensional statistics and machine learning for
                    analyzing large-scale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data. Both problems can be formula=
                    ted as an optimization problem with nonsmooth
                    objective and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nonconvex constraints. Because non=
                    smoothness and nonconvexity bring numerical
                    difficulties, most
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms suggested in the litera=
                    ture either solve some relaxations of them or
                    are heuristic and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lack convergence guarantees. In th=
                    is paper, we propose a new alternating manifold
                    proximal gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method to solve these two high-dim=
                    ensional problems and provide a unified
                    convergence analysis.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Numerical experimental results are=
                    reported to demonstrate the advantages of our
                    algorithm.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chen2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-101" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chen, Tong et al. =E2=80=9CPolynomial Optimi=
            zation for Bounding Lipschitz Constants of Deep
            Networks=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.03657 <span cla=ss=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Lipschitz constant of a
                network plays an important role in many applications of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">deep learning, such as robustness =
                    certification and Wasserstein Generative
                    Adversarial Network. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">introduce a semidefinite programmi=
                    ng hierarchy to estimate the global and local
                    Lipschitz constant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a multiple layer deep neural ne=
                    twork. The novelty is to combine a polynomial
                    lifting for ReLU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">functions derivatives with a weak =
                    generalization of Putinar=E2=80=99s positivity
                    certificate. This idea could also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">apply to other, nearly sparse, pol=
                    ynomial optimization problems in machine
                    learning. We empirically
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate that our method not on=
                    ly runs faster than state-of-the-art linear
                    programming based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method, but also provides sharper =
                    bounds.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chen2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-102" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chen, Yongyong et al. =E2=80=9CGraph-regular=
            ized least squares regression for multi-view
            subspace clustering=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Knowl=
                edge-Based Systems </span>(Jan. 2020), p. 105482. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">=
                <span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.knosys.2020.105482.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many works have proven that the
                consistency and differences in multi-view subspace
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">clustering make the clustering res=
                    ults better than the single-view clustering.
                    Therefore, this paper
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">studies the multi-view clustering =
                    problem, which aims to divide data points into
                    several groups using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiple features. However, existi=
                    ng multi-view clustering methods fail to
                    capturing the grouping
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effect and local geometrical struc=
                    ture of the multiple features. In order to
                    solve these problems, this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paper proposes a novel multi-view =
                    subspace clustering model called
                    graph-regularized least squares
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regression (GLSR), which uses not =
                    only the least squares regression instead of
                    the nuclear norm to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generate grouping effect, but also=
                    the manifold constraint to preserve the local
                    geometrical structure
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of multiple features. Specifically=
                    , the proposed GLSR method adopts the least
                    squares regression to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learn the globally consensus infor=
                    mation shared by multiple views and the
                    column-sparsity norm to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">measure the residual information. =
                    Under the alternating direction method of
                    multipliers framework,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an effective method is developed b=
                    y iteratively update all variables. Numerical
                    studies on eight real
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">databases demonstrate the effectiv=
                    eness and superior performance of the proposed
                    GLSR over eleven
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art methods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chen2020c" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-103" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chen, Yuedan, Guoqing Xiao, M. Tamer Ozsu, e=
            t al. =E2=80=9CaeSpTV: An Adaptive and Efficient
            Framework for Sparse Tensor-Vector Product Kernel on a High-Per=
            formance Computing
            Platform=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE Transaction=
                s on Parallel and Distributed Systems </span>(2020),
            pp. 1=E2=80=931. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-=caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/tpds.2020.2990429.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Multi-dimensional, large-scale,
                and sparse data, which can be neatly represented by sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensors, are increasingly used in =
                    various applications such as data analysis and
                    machine learning. A
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high-performance sparse tensor-vec=
                    tor product (SpTV), one of the most fundamental
                    operations of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processing sparse tensors, is nece=
                    ssary for improving efficiency of related
                    applications. In this paper, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">propose aeSpTV, an adaptive and ef=
                    ficient SpTV framework on Sunway TaihuLight
                    supercomputer,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to solve several challenges of opt=
                    imizing SpTV on high-performance computing
                    platforms. First, to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">map SpTV to Sunway architecture an=
                    d tame expensive memory access latency and
                    parallel writing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conflict due to the intrinsic irre=
                    gularity of SpTV, we introduce an adaptive SpTV
                    parallelization.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Second, to co-execute with the par=
                    allelization design while still ensuring high
                    efficiency, we design
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a sparse tensor data structure nam=
                    ed CSSoCR. Third, based on the adaptive SpTV
                    parallelization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with the novel tensor data structu=
                    re, we present an auto-tuner that chooses the
                    most befitting tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">partitioning method for aeSpTV usi=
                    ng the variance analysis theory of mathematical
                    statistics to achieve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">load balance. Fourth, to further l=
                    everage the computing power of Sunway, we
                    propose customized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimizations for aeSpTV. Experime=
                    ntal results show that aeSpTV yields good
                    sacalability on both
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">thread-level and process-level par=
                    allelism of Sunway. It achieves a maximum
                    GFLOPS of 195.69 on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">128 processes. Additionally, it is=
                    proved that optimization effects of the
                    partitioning auto-tuner and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization techniques are remark=
                    able.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chen2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-104" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chen, Yuedan, Guoqing Xiao, Fan Wu, et al. =
            =E2=80=9CtpSpMV: A Two-Phase Large-scale Sparse
            Matrix-Vector Multiplication Kernel for Manycore Architectures=
            =E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Information
                Sciences</span>
            (Mar. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps=">doi</span></span>: 10.1016/j.ins.2020.03.020.
         </p><!-- l. 30 -->
         <p class=3D" noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse matrix-vector
                        multiplication (SpMV) is one of the important subroutines in=
                        numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear algebras widely used in lot=
                    s of large-scale applications. Accelerating
                    SpMV on multicore and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">manycore architectures based on Co=
                    mpressed Sparse Row (CSR) format via row-wise
                    parallelization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is one of the most popular directi=
                    ons. However, there are three main challenges
                    in optimizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel CSR-based SpMV: (a) limit=
                    ed local memory of each computing unit can be
                    overwhelmed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by assignments to long rows of lar=
                    ge-scale sparse matrices; (b) irregular
                    accesses to the input vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">result in expensive memory access =
                    latency; (c) sparse data structure leads to low
                    bandwidth usage.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This paper proposes a two-phase la=
                    rge-scale SpMV, called tpSpMV, based on the
                    memory structure
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and computing architecture of mult=
                    icore and manycore architectures to alleviate
                    the three main
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">difficulties. First, we propose th=
                    e two-phase parallel execution technique for
                    tpSpMV that performs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel CSR-based SpMV into two s=
                    eparate phases to overcome the computational
                    scale limitation.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Second, we respectively propose th=
                    e adaptive partitioning methods and
                    parallelization designs using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the local memory caching technique=
                    for the two phases to exploit the
                    architectural advantages of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the high-performance computing pla=
                    tforms and alleviate the problem of high memory
                    access latency.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Third, we design several optimizat=
                    ions, such as data reduction, aligned memory
                    accessing, and pipeline
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">technique, to improve bandwidth us=
                    age and optimize tpSpMV=E2=80=99s performance.
                    Experimental results on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SW26010 CPUs of the Sunway TaihuLi=
                    ght supercomputer prove that tpSpMV achieves up
                    to 28.61
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">speedups and yields the performanc=
                    e improvement of 13.16% over the
                    state-of-the-art work on average.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cheng2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-105" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cheng, Yu, Debmalya Panigrahi, and Kevin Sun=
            . =E2=80=9CSparsification of Balanced Directed Graphs=E2=80=9D.
            In: (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.01975v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[c=
                s.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparsification, where the cut
                values of an input graph are approximately preserved by a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse graph (called a cut sparsif=
                    ier) or a succinct data structure (called a cut
                    sketch), has been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an influential tool in graph algor=
                    ithms. But, this tool is restricted to
                    undirected graphs, because
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">some directed graphs are known to =
                    not admit sparsification. Such examples,
                    however, are structurally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">very dissimilar to undirected grap=
                    hs in that they exhibit highly unbalanced cuts.
                    This motivates
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">us to ask: can we sparsify a balan=
                    ced digraph? To make this question concrete, we
                    define balance</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"145" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FD TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>=
                                =CE=B2</mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">of a digraph as the maximum ratio of the =
                    cut value in the two directions (Ene et al.,
                    STOC 2016). We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show the following results: For-Al=
                    l Sparsification: If all cut values need to be
                    simultaneously preserved
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(cf. Bencz=C3=BAr and Karger, STOC=
                    1996), then we show that the size of the sparsifier
                    (or even cut sketch)

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">must scale linearly with </span>
                <!= -- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHT=ML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"146" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; pos=ition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi=class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FD TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-ma= th>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>=CE=B2</mi>
                                    </ma= th>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The upper bound is a simple extens=
                        ion of sparsification of undirected graphs
                        (formally stated recently
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in Ikeda and Tanigawa (WAOA 2018))=
                        , so our main contribution here is to show a
                        matching lower
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bound. For-Each Sparsification: If=
                        each cut value needs to be individually
                        preserved (Andoni et al.,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ITCS 2016), then the situation is =
                        more interesting. Here, we give a cut sketch
                        whose size scales with</span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"147" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-msqrt>
                                <mjx-sqrt>
                                    <mjx-surd>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop">
                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c221A TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-surd>
                                    <mjx-box style=3D"padding=-top: 0.158em;">
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6F=D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-box>
                                </mjx-sqrt>
                            </mjx-msqrt>
                            </mjx= -math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <msqrt>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi=>=CE=B2</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </msqrt>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span= class=3D"cmr-9">,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">thereby beating the linear lower b=
                            ound above. We also show that this result is
                            tight by exhibiting a
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matching lower bound of </span>
                        <!-= - l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTM=L" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"148" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posi=tion: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-msqr= t>
                                        <mjx-sqrt>
                                            <mjx-surd>
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c221A T=EX-S1"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-surd>
                                            <mjx-box style=3D"padding-top: 0.158em;"=>
                                                <mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FD TEX-I">
                                                            </mjx-= c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                            </mjx-box>
                                        </mjx-sqrt>
                                        </mjx-msqrt>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <msqrt>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>=CE=B2</mi>
                                                </m= row>
                                        </msqrt>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">on =E2=80=9Dfor-each=E2=80=9D cut sketche=
                                s. Our upper bounds work for general weighted graphs, while the
                                lower bounds
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">even hold for unweighted graphs wi=
                                th no parallel edges.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chennupati2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-106" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chennupati, Gopinath et al. =E2=80=9CMachine=
            Learning Enabled Scalable Performance Prediction of
            Scientific Codes=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.04212 <= span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.PF]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present the Analytical Memory
                Model with Pipelines (AMMP) of the Performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Prediction Toolkit (PPT). PPT-AMMP=
                    takes high-level source code and hardware
                    architecture
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parameters as input, predicts runt=
                    ime of that code on the target hardware
                    platform, which is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">defined in the input parameters. P=
                    PT-AMMP transforms the code to an
                    (architecture-independent)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intermediate representation, then =
                    (i) analyzes the basic block structure of the
                    code, (ii) processes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architecture-independent virtual m=
                    emory access patterns that it uses to build
                    memory reuse distance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distribution models for each basic=
                    block, (iii) runs detailed basic-block level
                    simulations to determine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware pipeline usage. PPT-AMMP =
                    uses machine learning and regression techniques
                    to build the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prediction models based on small i=
                    nstances of the input code, then integrates
                    into a higher-order
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">discrete-event simulation model of=
                    PPT running on Simian PDES engine. We validate
                    PPT-AMMP
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on four standard computational phy=
                    sics benchmarks, finally present a use case of
                    hardware parameter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sensitivity analysis to identify b=
                    ottleneck hardware resources on different code
                    inputs. We further
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extend PPT-AMMP to predict the per=
                    formance of scientific application (radiation
                    transport), SNAP.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We analyze the application of mult=
                    i-variate regression models that accurately
                    predict the reuse profiles
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the basic block counts. The pr=
                    edicted runtimes of SNAP when compared to that
                    of actual times
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are accurate.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cheramangalath2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-107" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cheramangalath, Unnikrishnan, Rupesh Nasre, =
            and Y. N. Srikant. =E2=80=9CGraph Analytics
            Frameworks=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Distributed Gr=
                aph Analytics</span>. Springer International
            Publishing, 2020,
            pp. 99=E2=80=93122. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sma=ll-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-41886-1=CB=994.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Frameworks take away the drudgery
                of routine tasks in programming graph analytic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications. This chapter describ=
                    es in some detail, the different models of
                    execution that are used in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph analytics, such as BSP, Map-=
                    Reduce, asynchronous execution, GAS,
                    Inspector-Executor, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Advance-Filter-Compute. It also pr=
                    ovides a glimpse of different existing
                    frameworks on multi-core
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CPUs, GPUs, and distributed system=
                    s.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cherubin2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-108" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cherubin, Stefano et al. =E2=80=9CDynamic Pr=
            ecision Autotuning with TAFFO=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">ACM Tra=
                nsactions
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">on Architecture and Code Optimiz=
                ation </span>17.2 (May 2020), pp. 1=E2=80=9326. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <s= pan class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>: 10.1145/3388785. <span class=3D="cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://dl.acm.org/doi/pdf/10.1145/3388785" class=3D="url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://dl.acm.org/doi/pdf/10.1145/3388785</s= pan></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many classes of applications,
                both in the embedded and high performance domains, can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">trade off the accuracy of the comp=
                    uted results for computation performance. One
                    way to achieve such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a trade-off is precision tuning=E2=
                    =80=94that is, to modify the data types used for the
                    computation by reducing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the bit width, or by changing the =
                    representation from floating point to fixed
                    point. We present a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methodology for high-accuracy dyna=
                    mic precision tuning based on the
                    identification of input classes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(i.e., classes of input datasets t=
                    hat benefit from similar optimizations). When a
                    new input region is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detected, the application kernels =
                    are re-compiled on the fly with the appropriate
                    selection of parameters.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this way, we obtain a continuou=
                    s optimization approach that enables the
                    exploitation of the reduced
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">precision computation while progre=
                    ssively exploring the solution space, thus
                    reducing the time required

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by compilation overheads. We provi=
                    de tools to support the automation of the
                    runtime part of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution, leaving to the user only=
                    the task of identifying the input classes. Our
                    approach provides
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a significant performance boost (u=
                    p to 320%) on the typical approximate computing
                    benchmarks,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">without meaningfully affecting the=
                    accuracy of the result, since the error
                    remains always below 3%.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chevalier2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-109" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chevalier, C=C3=A9dric, Franck Ledoux, and S=
            =C3=A9bastien Morais. =E2=80=9CA Multilevel Mesh Partitioning
            Algorithm Driven by Memory Constraints=E2=80=9D. In: <span clas=s=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the SIAM
                Workshop on
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Combinatorial Scientific Computi=
                ng</span>. Society for Industrial and Applied
            Mathematics, Jan.
            2020, pp. 85=E2=80=9395. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1137/1.9781611976229.9.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Running numerical simulations on
                HPC architectures requires distributing data to be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processed over the various availab=
                    le processing units. This task is usually done
                    by partitioning tools,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">whose primary goal is to balance t=
                    he workload while minimizing inter-process
                    communication. However,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">they do not take the memory load a=
                    nd memory capacity of the processing units into
                    account. As this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can lead to memory overflow, we pr=
                    opose a new approach to address mesh
                    partitioning by including
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ghost cells in the memory usage an=
                    d by considering memory capacity as a strong
                    constraint to abide. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model the problem using a bipartit=
                    e graph and present a new greedy algorithm that
                    aims at producing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a partition according to the memor=
                    y capacity. This algorithm focuses on memory
                    consumption, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we use it in a multi-level approac=
                    h to improving the quality of the returned
                    solutions during the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">refinement phase. The experimental=
                    results obtained from our benchmarks show that
                    our approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can yield solutions respecting mem=
                    ory constraints for instances where traditional
                    partitioning tools
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fail.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chieu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-110" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chieu, Nguyen Huy, Le Van Hien, and Nguyen T=
            hi Quynh Trang. =E2=80=9CTilt Stability for Quadratic
            Programs with One or Two Quadratic Inequality Constraints=E2=80=
            =9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Acta Mathematica
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Vietnamica </span>45.2 (June 202=
            0), pp. 477=E2=80=93499. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps=">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/s40306-020-00372-4.
         </p><!-- l. 30 -->
         <p class=3D" noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper examines tilt
                        stability for quadratic programs with one or two quadratic i=
                        nequality
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">constraints. Exploiting specific f=
                    eatures of these problems and using some known
                    results on tilt stability
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in nonlinear programming, we estab=
                    lish quite simple characterizations of
                    tilt-stable local minimizers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for quadratic programs with one qu=
                    adratic inequality constraint under metric
                    subregularity constraint
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">qualification. By the same way, we=
                    also derive various tilt stability conditions
                    for quadratic programs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with two quadratic inequality cons=
                    traints and satisfying certain suitable
                    assumptions. Especially, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">obtained results show that some ti=
                    lt stability conditions only known to be
                    sufficient in nonlinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programming become the necessary o=
                    nes when the considered problems are quadratic
                    programs with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">one or two quadratic inequality co=
                    nstraints.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chilukuri2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-111" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chilukuri, Aditya, Josh Milthorpe, and Beau =
            Johnston. =E2=80=9CCharacterizing Optimizations to
            Memory Access Patterns using Architecture-Independent Program F=
            eatures=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020).
            arXiv: 2003.06064v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">High-performance computing
                developers are faced with the challenge of optimizing the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance of OpenCL workloads on=
                    diverse architectures. The
                    Architecture-Independent Workload
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Characterization (AIWC) tool is a =
                    plugin for the Oclgrind OpenCL simulator that
                    gathers metrics of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">OpenCL programs that can be used t=
                    o understand and predict program performance on
                    an arbitrary
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">given hardware architecture. Howev=
                    er, AIWC metrics are not always easily
                    interpreted and do not
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reflect some important memory acce=
                    ss patterns affecting efficiency across
                    architectures. We propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a new metric of parallel spatial l=
                    ocality =E2=80=93 the closeness of memory accesses
                    simultaneously issued
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by OpenCL work-items (threads). We=
                    implement the parallel spatial locality metric
                    in the AIWC
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">framework, and analyse gathered re=
                    sults on matrix multiply and the Extended
                    OpenDwarfs OpenCL
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benchmarks. The differences in the=
                    observed parallel spatial locality metric
                    across implementations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of matrix multiply reflect the opt=
                    imizations performed. The new metric can be
                    used to distinguish
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">between the OpenDwarfs benchmarks =
                    based on the memory access patterns affecting
                    their performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on various architectures. The impr=
                    ovements suggested to AIWC will help HPC
                    developers better

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">understand memory access patterns =
                    of complex codes and guide optimization of
                    codes for arbitrary
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware targets.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Choi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-112" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Choi,
            Jaemin, David F. Richards, and Laxmikant V. Kale. =E2=80=9CAchi=
            eving Computation-Communication
            Overlapwith Overdecomposition on GPU Systems=E2=80=9D. In: <spa= n class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020
                IEEE/ACM
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">nternational Workshop on Extreme=
                    Scale Programming Models and
                    Middleware</span>. ESPM2 =E2=80=9920.
                2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The landscape of high performance
                computing is shifting towards a collection of multi-GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nodes, widening the gap between on=
                    -node compute and off-node communication
                    capabilities.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Consequently, the ability to toler=
                    ate communication latencies and maximize
                    utilization of the compute
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware are becoming increasingly=
                    important in achieving high performance.
                    Overdecomposition has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">been successfully adopted on tradi=
                    tional CPU-based systems to achieve
                    computation-communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overlap, significantly reducing th=
                    e impact of communication on application
                    performance. However,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it has been unclear whether overde=
                    composition can provide the same benefits on
                    modern GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. In this work, we address =
                    the challenges in achieving
                    computation-communication overlap with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overdecomposition on GPU systems u=
                    sing the Charm++ parallel programming system.
                    By prioritizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication with CUDA streams in=
                    the application and supporting asynchronous
                    progress of GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operations in the Charm++ runtime =
                    system, we obtain improvements in overall
                    performance of up to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">50% and 47% with proxy application=
                    s Jacobi3D and MiniMD, respectively.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Choi2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-113" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CAchieving Computation-Com=
            munication Overlapwith Overdecomposition on GPU Systems=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the IEEE/ACM 5th Int=
                ernational Workshop on Extreme Scale
                Programming
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Models and Middleware</span>. ES=
            PM2 2020. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</sp= an></span>: <a
                    href=3D"https://conferences.computer.org/scwpub/pdfs/ESPM220=20-3LkQkprM0X1ItzzieZeAlw/107400a001/107400a001.pdf"
                    class=3D"url"><span cl=ass=3D"cmtt-10">https://conferences.computer.org/scwpub/pdfs/ESPM22020-3LkQ=
                        kprM0X1ItzzieZeAlw/107400a001/107400a001.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The landscape of high performance
                computing is shifting towards a collection of multi-GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nodes, widening the gap between on=
                    -node compute and off-node communication
                    capabilities.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Consequently, the ability to toler=
                    ate communication latencies and maximize
                    utilization of the compute
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware are becoming increasingly=
                    important in achieving high performance.
                    Overdecomposition has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">been successfully adopted on tradi=
                    tional CPU-based systems to achieve
                    computation-communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overlap, significantly reducing th=
                    e impact of communication on application
                    performance. However,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it has been unclear whether overde=
                    composition can provide the same benefits on
                    modern GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. In this work, we address =
                    the challenges in achieving
                    computation-communication overlap with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overdecomposition on GPU systems u=
                    sing the Charm++ parallel programming system.
                    By prioritizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication with CUDA streams in=
                    the application and supporting asynchronous
                    progress of GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operations in the Charm++ runtime =
                    system, we obtain improvements in overall
                    performance of up to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">50% and 47% with proxy application=
                    s Jacobi3D and MiniMD, respectively.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chou2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-114" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chou, Stephen, Fredrik Kjolstad, and Saman A=
            marasinghe. =E2=80=9CAutomatic Generation of Efficient
            Sparse Tensor Format Conversion Routines=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 20=
            20). arXiv: 2001.02609 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper shows how to generate
                code that efficiently converts sparse tensors between
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">disparate storage formats (data la=
                    youts) like CSR, DIA, ELL, and many others. We
                    decompose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse tensor conversion into thre=
                    e logical phases: coordinate remapping,
                    analysis, and assembly. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">then develop a language that preci=
                    sely describes how different formats group
                    together and order a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensor=E2=80=99s nonzeros in memor=
                    y. This enables a compiler to emit code that performs
                    complex reorderings
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(remappings) of nonzeros when conv=
                    erting between formats. We additionally develop
                    a query language
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that can extract complex statistic=
                    s about sparse tensors, and we show how to emit
                    efficient analysis code
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that computes such queries. Finall=
                    y, we define an abstract interface that
                    captures how data structures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for storing a tensor can be effici=
                    ently assembled given specific statistics about
                    the tensor. Disparate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formats can implement this common =
                    interface, thus letting a compiler emit
                    optimized sparse tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conversion code for arbitrary comb=
                    inations of a wide range of formats without
                    hard-coding for any

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">specific one. Our evaluation shows=
                    that our technique generates sparse tensor
                    conversion routines with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance between 0.99 and 2.2</= span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"149" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx= -mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                            <m= ath xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo cla=ss=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">that of hand-optimized implementations in=
                        two widely used sparse linear algebra
                        libraries, SPARSKIT
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and Intel MKL. By emitting code th=
                        at avoids materializing temporaries, our
                        technique also outperforms
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both libraries by between 1.4 and =
                        3.4</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0=" jax=3D" CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"150" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>=
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inlin=e"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <m= o class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-conta= iner>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">for CSC/COO to DIA/ELL conversion.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Chowdhury2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-115" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Chowdhury, Agniva et al. =E2=80=9CSpeeding u=
            p Linear Programming using Randomized Linear
            Algebra=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.08072v1 <span cl=ass=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Linear programming (LP) is an
                extremely useful tool and has been successfully applied to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solve various problems in a wide r=
                    ange of areas, including operations research,
                    engineering, economics,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or even more abstract mathematical=
                    areas such as combinatorics. It is also used
                    in many machine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learning applications, such as </s= pan><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"151" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <= mjx-msub>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2195">
                                            </mjx= -c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                    <mjx-= mrow size=3D"s">
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            </= mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                            </mjx-msub>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml uns=electable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/M=ath/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <msub>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>=E2=86=95</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <= mn>1</mn>
                                    </mrow>
                                </msub>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span cla=ss=3D"cmr-9">-regularized
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SVMs, basis pursuit, nonnegative m=
                        atrix factorization, etc. Interior Point
                        Methods (IPMs) are one of
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the most popular methods to solve =
                        LPs both in theory and in practice. Their
                        underlying complexity
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is dominated by the cost of solvin=
                        g a system of linear equations at each
                        iteration. In this paper, we
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consider </span><span class=3D"cmt=i-9">infeasible </span><span
                        class=3D"cmr-9">IPMs
                        for the special case where the number of variables is much l=
                        arger than the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number of constraints. Using tools=
                        from Randomized Linear Algebra, we present a
                        preconditioning
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">technique that, when combined with=
                        the Conjugate Gradient iterative solver,
                        provably guarantees that
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">infeasible IPM algorithms (suitabl=
                        y modified to account for the error incurred by
                        the approximate
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solver), converge to a feasible, a=
                        pproximately optimal solution, without
                        increasing their iteration
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complexity. Our empirical evaluati=
                        ons verify our theoretical results on both
                        real-world and synthetic
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Christlieb2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-116" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Christlieb, Andrew J. et al. =E2=80=9CParall=
            el Algorithms for Successive Convolution=E2=80=9D. In: (July
            2020).
            arXiv: 2007.03041v2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[physics.comp-ph]</= span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this work, we consider
                alternative discretizations for PDEs which use expansions in=
                volving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integral operators to approximate =
                    spatial derivatives. These constructions use
                    explicit information
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">within the integral terms, but tre=
                    at boundary data implicitly, which contributes
                    to the overall speed of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the method. This approach is prova=
                    bly unconditionally stable for linear problems
                    and stability has been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrated experimentally for no=
                    nlinear problems. Additionally, it is
                    matrix-free in the sense that it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is not necessary to invert linear =
                    systems and iteration is not required for
                    nonlinear terms. Moreover, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scheme employs a fast summation al=
                    gorithm that yields a method with a
                    computational complexity of</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"152" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-S=C"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-m= o class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo=>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"in=line"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"=>
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathCl=ass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>N</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>=
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"153" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>N</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            <= /mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the number of mesh points along a dire=
                                    ction. While much work has been done to explore
                                    the theory
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">behind these methods, their practi=
                                    cality in large scale computing environments is
                                    a largely unexplored
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">topic. In this work, we explore th=
                                    e performance of these methods by developing a
                                    domain decomposition
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm suitable for distributed=
                                    memory systems along with shared memory
                                    algorithms. As a first
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">pass, we derive an artificial CFL =
                                    condition that enforces a nearest-neighbor
                                    communication pattern
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and briefly discuss possible gener=
                                    alizations. We also analyze several approaches
                                    for implementing the
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel algorithms by optimizing =
                                    predominant loop structures and maximizing data
                                    reuse. Using a
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hybrid design that employs MPI and=
                                    Kokkos for the distributed and shared memory
                                    components of
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the algorithms, respectively, we s=
                                    how that our methods are efficient and can
                                    sustain an update rate</span>
                                <!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"154" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3E">
                                                </mjx-= c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                                </m= jx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c38"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" displ=ay=3D"inline">
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">&gt;</mo>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"M=athClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                            <mn>108</mn>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-conta= iner>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">DOF/node/s. We provide results that demon=
                                        strate the scalability and versatility of our
                                        algorithms
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using several different PDE test p=
                                        roblems, including a nonlinear example, which
                                        employs an adaptive
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time-stepping rule.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cojean2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-117" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cojean, Terry, Yu-Hsiang =E2=80=9DMike=E2=80=
            =9D Tsai, and Hartwig Anzt. =E2=80=9CGinkgo =E2=80=93 A Math Library design=
            ed
            for Platform Portability=E2=80=9D. In: (Nov. 2020). arXiv: 2011=
            .08879 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The first associations to
                software sustainability might be the existence of a continuo=
                us
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integration (CI) framework; the ex=
                    istence of a testing framework composed of unit
                    tests, integration
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tests, and end-to-end tests; and a=
                    lso the existence of software documentation.
                    However, when asking
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">what is a common deathblow for a s=
                    cientific software product, it is often the
                    lack of platform and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance portability. Against t=
                    his background, we designed the Ginkgo library
                    with the primary
