                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">focus on platform portability and =
                    the ability to not only port to new hardware
                    architectures, but also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">achieve good performance. In this =
                    paper we present the Ginkgo library design,
                    radically separating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms from hardware-specific =
                    kernels forming the distinct hardware
                    executors, and report our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experience when adding execution b=
                    ackends for NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel GPUs. We
                    also comment
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the different levels of perform=
                    ance portability, and the performance we
                    achieved on the distinct
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware backends.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Connolly2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-118" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Connolly, Michael P., Nicholas J. Higham, an=
            d Theo Mary. =E2=80=9CStochastic Rounding and its
            Probabilistic Backward Error Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: (2020). MIM=
            S EPrint: MIMS EPrint:2020.12. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"smal=l-caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/2763/1/paper.p=df" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths.manchester.a=
                    c.uk/2763/1/paper.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Stochastic rounding rounds a real
                number to the next larger or smaller floatingpoint number
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with probabilities 1 minus the rel=
                    ative distances to those numbers. It is gaining
                    attention in deep
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learning because it can improve th=
                    e accuracy of the computations. We compare
                    basic properties of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stochastic rounding with those for=
                    round to nearest, finding properties in common
                    as well as significant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">differences. We prove that for sto=
                    chastic rounding the rounding errors are mean
                    independent random
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variables with zero mean. We deriv=
                    e a new version of our probabilistic error
                    analysis theorem from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">[SIAM J. Sci. Comput., 41 (2019), =
                    pp. A2815=E2=80=93A2835], weakening the assumption of
                    independence of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the random variables to mean indep=
                    endence. These results imply that for a wide
                    range of linear algebra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computations the backward error fo=
                    r stochastic rounding is unconditionally
                    bounded by a multiple of</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"155" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-msqrt>
                            <mjx-sqrt>
                                <mjx-surd>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c221A"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-surd>
                                <mjx-box style=3D"padding-top: 0=.281em;">
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I="></mjx-c></mjx-mi></mjx-mrow></mjx-box></mjx-sqrt></mjx-msqrt><mjx-mi clas=
s=3D" mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <= mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msqrt>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>n</mi>=
                                </mrow>
                            </msqrt>
                            <mi>u</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">to first order, with a certain probabilit=
                    y, where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Atta=ched_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"156" style=3D"font=-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden==3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c=>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inli=ne"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <= mi>n</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the problem size and </span>
                <!-- l. 30=
 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabi=ndex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"157" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: r=elative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D="mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>u</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the unit roundoff. This is the first s=
                    cenario where the rule of thumb that one can
                    replace nu by</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"158" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-msqrt>
                            <mjx-sqrt>
                                <mjx-surd>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c221A"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-surd>
                                <mjx-box style=3D"padding-top: 0=.281em;">
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I="></mjx-c></mjx-mi></mjx-mrow></mjx-box></mjx-sqrt></mjx-msqrt><mjx-mi clas=
s=3D" mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <= mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msqrt>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>n</mi>=
                                </mrow>
                            </msqrt>
                            <mi>u</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">in a rounding error bound has been shown =
                    to hold without any additional assumptions on
                    the rounding
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">errors. We also explain how stocha=
                    stic rounding avoids the phenomenon of
                    stagnation in sums, whereby
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">small addends are obliterated by r=
                    ound to nearest when they are too small
                    relative to the sum.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Constantinides2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-119" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Constantinides, G. A. =E2=80=9CRethinking ar=
            ithmetic for deep neural networks=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Phi=
                losophical
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Transactions of the Royal Societ=
                y A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering
                Sciences</span>
            378.2166 (Jan. 2020), p. 20190051. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <sp= an class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>:
            10.1098/rsta.2019.0051.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We consider efficiency in the
                implementation of deep neural networks. Hardware accelerator=
                s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are gaining interest as machine le=
                    arning becomes one of the drivers of
                    high-performance computing.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In these accelerators, the directe=
                    d graph describing a neural network can be
                    implemented as a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">directed graph describing a Boolea=
                    n circuit. We make this observation precise,
                    leading naturally to an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">understanding of practical neural =
                    networks as discrete functions, and show that
                    the so-called binarized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">neural networks are functionally c=
                    omplete. In general, our results suggest that
                    it is valuable to consider
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Boolean circuits as neural network=
                    s, leading to the question of which circuit
                    topologies are promising.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We argue that continuity is centra=
                    l to generalization in learning, explore the
                    interaction between data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">coding, network topology, and node=
                    functionality for continuity and pose some
                    open questions for future
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">research. As a first step to bridg=
                    ing the gap between continuous and Boolean
                    views of neural network
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accelerators, we present some rece=
                    nt results from our work on LUTNet, a novel
                    Field-Programmable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Gate Array inference approach. Fin=
                    ally, we conclude with additional possible
                    fruitful avenues for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">research bridging the continuous a=
                    nd discrete views of neural networks.
                </span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">This article=
                    is part of a discussion meeting issue
                    =E2=80=9DNumerical algorithms for high-performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational science=E2=80=9D.</s= pan>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cornelis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-120" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cornelis, Jeffrey and Wim Vanroose. =E2=80=
            =9CProjected Newton method for noise constrained
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"159"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-msub>
                        <mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2113="></mjx-c></mjx-mi></mjx-mrow><mjx-script style=3D"
                                    vertical-align: -0.15em;="><mjx-mrow size=3D" s">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-script>
                    </mjx-msub>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-a= ssistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://w=ww.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <msub>
                            <mrow>
                                <mi>=E2=84=93</m= i>
                            </mrow>
                            <mrow>
                                <mi>p</mi>
                            </mrow>
                        </msub>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer>
                    regularization=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.02687v1 <s= pan class=3D"cmtt-10">
                        [math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Choosing an appropriate
                regularization term is necessary to obtain a meaningful solu=
                tion to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an ill-posed linear inverse proble=
                    m contaminated with measurement errors or
                    noise. A regularization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">term in the the </span>
                <!-- l. 30 =
-->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabin=dex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"160" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: re=lative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mr= ow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2113"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mj= x-mrow>
                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">=
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>=
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=E2=84=93</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>p</m= i>
                                </mrow>
                            </msub>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">norm with </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"161" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2265"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mn cla=ss=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        </mjx-ma= th>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>p</mi>
                                <mo class==3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A5</mo>
                                <mn>1</mn>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx= -container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">covers a wide range of choices since its =
                        behavior critically depends on the choice of
                    </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"162" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I">
                                    </mj= x-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline="><mi>p</mi></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D" cmr-9">and since it can easily be combined with =
                                a suitable regularization matrix. We develop an
                                efficient
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm that simultaneously dete=
                                    rmines the regularization parameter and
                                    corresponding </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"M=athJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter==3D"163" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class==3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-msub>
                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-=i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2113"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-script style==3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                            </mjx-script>=
                                        </mjx-msub>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                                            <msub>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mi>=E2=84=93</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mi>p</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                            </msub>
                                        </math>
                                        <= /mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">regularized solution such that the discre=
                                    pancy principle is satisfied. We project the
                                    problem on a
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">low-dimensional Generalized Krylov=
                                    subspace and compute the Newton direction for
                                    this much smaller
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem. We illustrate some intere=
                                    sting properties of the algorithm and compare
                                    its performance with
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">other state-of-the-art approaches =
                                    using a number of numerical experiments, with a
                                    special focus of the
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparsity inducing </span>
                                <!-- l. 3=
0 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tab=index=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"164" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:=relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-msub>
                                            <mjx-= mrow>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2113"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                </= mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s="><mjx-mn class=3D" mjx-n">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mn>
                                                        </mjx-m= row>
                                                </mjx-script>
                                        </mjx-msub>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"o=n" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" di=splay=3D"inline">
                                            <msub>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mi>=E2=84=93</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                                    </mro= w>
                                            </msub>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">norm and edge-preserving total variation =
                                    regularization.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cortiella2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-121" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cortiella, Alexandre, Kwang-Chun Park, and A=
            lireza Doostan. =E2=80=9CSparse Identification of
            Nonlinear
            Dynamical Systems via Reweighted
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"165"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-msub>
                        <mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2113="></mjx-c></mjx-mi></mjx-mrow><mjx-script style=3D"
                                    vertical-align: -0.15em;="><mjx-mrow size=3D" s">
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                            </m= jx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-script>
                    </mjx-msub>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <msub>
                            <mrow>
                                <mi>=E2=84=93</mi>
                            </mrow>=
                            <mrow>
                                <mn>1</mn>
                            </mrow>
                        </msub>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container>-r=
            egularized
            Least Squares=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.13232v1 <sp= an class=3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ML]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This work proposes an iterative
                sparse-regularized regression method to recover governing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations of nonlinear dynamical s=
                    ystems from noisy state measurements. The
                    method is inspired by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Sparse Identification of Nonli=
                    near Dynamics (SINDy) approach of </span><span class=3D"cmti-9">[Brunton et=
                    al., PNAS, 113 (15)
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-9">(2016) 3932-3937]</span><span cla=ss=3D"cmr-9">, which relies on two main
                    assumptions: the state variables are known </span><span clas=s=3D"cmti-9">a priori </span><span
                    class=3D"cmr-9">and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the governing equations lend thems=
                    elves to sparse, linear expansions in a
                    (nonlinear) basis of the state
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variables. The aim of this work is=
                    to improve the accuracy and robustness of
                    SINDy in the presence of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state measurement noise. To this e=
                    nd, a reweighted </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtM=enu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"166" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2113"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-al=ign: -0.15em;">
                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=E2=84=
                                        =93</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                </mrow>
                            </msub>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </m= jx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-regularized
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">least squares solver is developed,=
                        wherein the regularization parameter is
                        selected from the corner point
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a Pareto curve. The idea behind=
                        using weighted </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMe=nu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"167" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-msub>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2113"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-al=ign: -0.15em;">
                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-script>
                            </mjx-msub>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <msub>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>=E2=84=
                                            =93</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                    </mrow>
                                </msub>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </m= jx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-norm
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for regularization =E2=80=93 inste=
                            ad of the standard </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"168" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-msub>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c2113"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-a=lign: -0.15em;">
                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-script>
                                </mjx-msub>
                                </mjx-math=>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"h=ttp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <msub>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>=E2=
                                                    =84=93</mi>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mn>1</mn>
                                            </mrow>
                                        </msub>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>=
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-norm
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">=E2=80=93 is to better promote spa=
                            rsity in the recovery of the governing equations and,
                            in turn, mitigate the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effect of noise in the state varia=
                            bles. We also present a method to recover
                            single physical constraints
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from state measurements. Through s=
                            everal examples of well-known nonlinear
                            dynamical systems, we
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate empirically the accura=
                            cy and robustness of the reweighted </span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"169" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                <mjx-msub>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c
                                                class=3D"mjx-c2113="></mjx-c></mjx-mi></mjx-mrow><mjx-script style=3D"
                                                vertical-align: -0.15em;="><mjx-mrow size=3D" s">
                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                                        </m= jx-c>
                                                </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    </mjx-script>
                                </mjx-msub>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <msub>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>=E2=84=93</mi>
                                        </mrow>=
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </msub>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <s= pan class=3D"cmr-9">-regularized
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">least squares strategy with respec=
                                t to state measurement noise, thus illustrating
                                its viability for a wide
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">range of potential applications.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Crane2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-122" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Crane, Rixon and Fred Roosta. =E2=80=9CDINO:=
            Distributed Newton-Type Optimization Method=E2=80=9D. In:
            (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.03694v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.=
                OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a novel
                communication-efficient Newton-type algorithm for finite-sum
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization over a distributed co=
                    mputing environment. Our method, named DINO,
                    overcomes both
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">theoretical and practical shortcom=
                    ings of similar existing methods. Under minimal
                    assumptions,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we guarantee global sub-linear con=
                    vergence of DINO to a first-order stationary
                    point for general
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-convex functions and arbitrary=
                    data distribution over the network.
                    Furthermore, for functions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">satisfying Polyak-Lojasiewicz (PL)=
                    inequality, we show that DINO enjoys a linear
                    convergence rate.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Our proposed algorithm is practica=
                    lly parameter free, in that it will converge
                    regardless of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">selected hyper-parameters, which a=
                    re easy to tune. Additionally, its sub-problems
                    are simple linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">least-squares, for which efficient=
                    solvers exist. Numerical simulations
                    demonstrate the efficiency of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DINO as compared with similar alte=
                    rnatives.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Criscitiello2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-123" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Criscitiello, Chris and Nicolas Boumal. =E2=
            =80=9CAn accelerated first-order method for non-convex
            optimization on manifolds=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). arXiv: 200=
            8.02252v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We describe the first gradient
                methods on Riemannian manifolds to achieve accelerated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rates in the non-convex case. Unde=
                    r Lipschitz assumptions on the Riemannian
                    gradient and Hessian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the cost function, these method=
                    s find approximate first-order critical points
                    strictly faster than
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regular gradient descent. A random=
                    ized version also finds approximate
                    second-order critical points.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Both the algorithms and their anal=
                    yses build extensively on existing work in the
                    Euclidean case. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">basic operation consists in runnin=
                    g the Euclidean accelerated gradient descent
                    method (appropriately
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">safe-guarded against non-convexity=
                    ) in the current tangent space, then moving
                    back to the manifold
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and repeating. This requires lifti=
                    ng the cost function from the manifold to the
                    tangent space, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be done for example through th=
                    e Riemannian exponential map. For this approach
                    to succeed, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lifted cost function (called the p=
                    ullback) must retain certain Lipschitz
                    properties. As a contribution of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">independent interest, we prove pre=
                    cise claims to that effect, with explicit
                    constants. Those claims are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">affected by the Riemannian curvatu=
                    re of the manifold, which in turn affects the
                    worst-case complexity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bounds for our optimization algori=
                    thms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Croci2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-124" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Croci, Matteo and Michael Bryce Giles. =E2=
            =80=9CEffects of round-to-nearest and stochastic rounding
            in
            the numerical solution of the heat equation in low precision=E2=
            =80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.16225
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Motivated by the advent of
                machine learning, the last few years saw the return of hardw=
                are-supported
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">low-precision computing. Computati=
                    ons with fewer digits are faster and more
                    memory and energy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficient, but can be extremely su=
                    sceptible to rounding errors. An application
                    that can largely benefit
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from the advantages of low-precisi=
                    on computing is the numerical solution of
                    partial differential equations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(PDEs), but a careful implementati=
                    on and rounding error analysis are required to
                    ensure that sensible
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results can still be obtained.</sp= an><br class=3D"newline"><span
                        class=3D"cmr-9">In
                        this paper we study the accumulation of rounding errors in t=
                        he solution of the heat equation, a
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proxy for parabolic PDEs, via Rung=
                        e-Kutta finite difference methods using
                        round-to-nearest (RtN) and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stochastic rounding (SR). We demon=
                        strate how to implement the scheme to reduce
                        rounding errors and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we derive </span><span class=3D"cm=ti-9">a priori </span><span
                        class=3D"cmr-9">estimates for local and global =
                        rounding errors. Let </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"170" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" dis=play=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                                <mi>u</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">be the roundoff unit. While the worst-cas=
                        e local errors are </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"171" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-=I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-m= o class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo=>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"in=line"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"=>
                                <mi>O</mi>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>u</mi>
                                    </mrow>=
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx= -container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">with respect to the discretization parame=
                            ters, the RtN and SR error behavior is
                            substantially different.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We prove that the RtN solution is =
                            discretization, initial condition and precision
                            dependent, and always
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stagnates for small enough </span>=
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"C=HTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"172" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; p=osition: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6E5 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselecta=ble=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Ma=thML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">=CE=94t</mi>
                                </math></mj= x-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Until stagnation, the global error=
                            grows like </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"173" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I">
                                        </mj= x-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                            </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6E5 TEX-I"=></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                            </m= jx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>=
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselec=table=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/=MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>O</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</= mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">u=CE=94t</mi>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin=">=E2=88=92</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mo class=3D"
                                                    MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                                </mrow=>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In contrast, we show that the lead=
                            ing order errors introduced by SR are
                            zero-mean, independent in
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">space and mean-independent in time=
                            , making SR resilient to stagnation and
                            rounding error accumulation.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In fact, we prove that for SR the =
                            global rounding errors are only </span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"174" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </m= jx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6E5 TEX-I">
                                                    </mjx-= c>
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mj= x-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mn>=
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F">=
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3=4"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mn>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>O</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClas=s-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">u=CE=94t</mi>
                                            <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95<= /mo>
                                                    <mn>4</mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mj= x-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">in 1D and are essentially bounded (up to =
                            logarithmic factors) in higher
                            dimensions.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Cummins2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-125" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Cummins, Chris et al. =E2=80=9CProGraML: Gra=
            ph-based Deep Learning for Program Optimization
            and Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.10536v1 <sp= an class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The increasing complexity of
                computing systems places a tremendous burden on optimizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compilers, requiring ever more acc=
                    urate and aggressive optimizations. Machine
                    learning offers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significant benefits for construct=
                    ing optimization heuristics but there remains a
                    gap between what
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art methods achieve a=
                    nd the performance of an optimal heuristic.
                    Closing this gap
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">requires improvements in two key a=
                    reas: a representation that accurately captures
                    the semantics of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programs, and a model architecture=
                    with sufficient expressiveness to reason about
                    this representation.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce ProGraML - Program Gr=
                    aphs for Machine Learning - a novel graph-based
                    program
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">representation using a low level, =
                    language agnostic, and portable format; and
                    machine learning models
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">capable of performing complex down=
                    stream tasks over these graphs. The ProGraML
                    representation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is a directed attributed multigrap=
                    h that captures control, data, and call
                    relations, and summarizes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instruction and operand types and =
                    ordering. Message Passing Neural Networks
                    propagate information

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through this structured representa=
                    tion, enabling whole-program or per-vertex
                    classification tasks.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ProGraML provides a general-purpos=
                    e program representation that equips learnable
                    models to perform
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the types of program analysis that=
                    are fundamental to optimization. To this end,
                    we evaluate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the performance of our approach fi=
                    rst on a suite of traditional compiler analysis
                    tasks: control
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">flow reachability, dominator trees=
                    , data dependencies, variable liveness, and
                    common subexpression
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detection. On a benchmark dataset =
                    of 250k LLVM-IR files covering six source
                    programming languages,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ProGraML achieves an average 94.0 =
                    F1 score, significantly outperforming the
                    state-of-the-art
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches. We then apply our appr=
                    oach to two high-level tasks - heterogeneous
                    device mapping and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">program classification - setting n=
                    ew state-of-the-art performance in both.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Curtis2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-126" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Curtis, Frank E., Yutong Dai, and Daniel P. =
            Robinson. =E2=80=9CA Subspace Acceleration Method
            for Minimization Involving a Group Sparsity-Inducing Regularize=
            r=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv:
            2007.14951v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We consider the problem of
                minimizing an objective function that is the sum of a convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">function and a group sparsity-indu=
                    cing regularizer. Problems that integrate such
                    regularizers arise in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">modern machine learning applicatio=
                    ns, often for the purpose of obtaining models
                    that are easier to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">interpret and that have higher pre=
                    dictive accuracy. We present a new method for
                    solving such problems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that utilize subspace acceleration=
                    , domain decomposition, and support
                    identification. Our analysis
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">shows, under common assumptions, t=
                    hat the iterate sequence generated by our
                    framework is globally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergent, converges to an </span=><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"=CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"175" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;=position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-= mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx= -math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96=
                                        </mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-appr=
                        oximate
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution in at most </span>
                    <!-- l.=

                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" t=abindex=3D"0"

                    <= /mjx-math>

                                <mi>O</mi>

30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" ta=bindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"178" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:=relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">

                            </m= ath>

                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">bounded above and large enough (respectiv=
                            ely, all </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Atta=ched_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"179" style=3D"font=-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden==3D"true">

                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I">
                                        </mjx-c=>
                                </mjx-mi>
