                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inli=ne"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <= mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">bounded above) where </span>
                        <!-- l. 30 --=
><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabinde=
x=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"180" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rela=
tive;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mi class=3D"mj=
x-i"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mo class=3D"mj=
x-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3E"></mjx-c></mjx-mo><m=
jx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c></mjx-mn=
></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math=
 xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"><mi>p</mi>=
<mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">&gt;</mo><mn>0</mn></math></mjx-assistive-mml><=
/mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is an algorithm parameter, and exhibits s=
uperlinear local convergence. Preliminary
               numerical results
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for the task of binary classificat=
ion based on regularized logistic regression
               show that our approach is
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficient and robust, with the abi=
lity to outperform a state-of-the-art
               method.</span>
         </p>
      </dd>
      <dt id=3D"X0-Curtis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
      </dt>
      <dd id=3D"bib-127" class=3D"thebibliography">
         <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <p class=3D"noindent">Curtis, Frank E. and Katya Scheinberg. =E2=
                            =80=9CAdaptive Stochastic Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020).
                            arXiv: 2001.06699 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
                        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Optimization lies at the heart of
                                machine learning and signal processing. Contemporary
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches based on the stochastic=
                    gradient method are non-adaptive in the sense
                    that their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation employs prescribed =
                    parameter values that need to be tuned for each
                    application. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">article summarizes recent research=
                    and motivates future work on adaptive
                    stochastic optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods, which have the potential =
                    to offer significant computational savings when
                    training large-scale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-DAmbra2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-128" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">D=E2=80=99Ambra, Pasqua, Fabio Durastante, a=
            nd Salvatore Filippone. =E2=80=9CAMG preconditioners for
            Linear Solvers towards Extreme Scale=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2020).=
            arXiv: 2006.16147v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Linear solvers for large and
                sparse systems are a key element of scientific applications,=
                and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">their efficient implementation is =
                    necessary to harness the computational power of
                    current computers.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Algebraic Multigrid (AMG) Precondi=
                    tioners are a popular ingredient of such linear
                    solvers; this is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the motivation for the present wor=
                    k where we examine some recent developments in
                    a package of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">AMG preconditioners to improve eff=
                    iciency, scalability, and robustness on
                    extreme-scale problems.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The main novelty is the design and=
                    implementation of a new parallel coarsening
                    algorithm based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on aggregation of unknowns employi=
                    ng weighted graph matching techniques; this is
                    a completely
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automated procedure, requiring no =
                    information from the user, and applicable to
                    general symmetric
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">positive definite (s.p.d.) matrice=
                    s. The new coarsening algorithm improves in
                    terms of numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalability at low operator comple=
                    xity over decoupled aggregation algorithms
                    available in previous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">releases of the package. The preco=
                    nditioners package is built on the parallel
                    software framework

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PSBLAS, which has also been update=
                    d to progress towards exascale. We present weak
                    scalability
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results on two of the most powerfu=
                    l supercomputers in Europe, for linear systems
                    with sizes up to</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"181" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-= mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-m= o>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                            display=3D"inline="><mi>O</mi><mrow><mo class=3D" MathClass-open">(</mo>
                            <mrow>
                                <mn>1010</mn>
                                </m= row>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    <= /mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">unknowns.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Das2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-129" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Das, A. et al. =E2=80=9CScalable yet
            Rigorous Floating-Point Error Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <span clas=s=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020
                International
                Conference
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">for High Performance Computing, =
                Networking, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC
            =E2=80=9920. Los Alamitos,
            CA, USA: IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 708=E2=80=93721.=
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.=
            1109/SC41405.2020.00055.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020=.00055"
                        class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputersocie=
                            ty.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00055</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Automated techniques for rigorous
                floating-point round-off error analysis are a prerequisite
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to placing important activities in=
                    HPC such as precision allocation,
                    verification, and code optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on a formal footing. Yet existing =
                    techniques cannot provide tight bounds for
                    expressions beyond a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">few dozen operators =E2=80=93 bare=
                    ly enough for HPC. In this work, we offer an approach
                    embedded in a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new tool called SATIRE that scales=
                    error analysis by four orders of magnitude
                    compared to today=E2=80=99s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">best-of-class tools. We explain ho=
                    w three key ideas underlying SATIRE helps it
                    attain such scale: path
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strength reduction, bound optimiza=
                    tion, and abstraction. SATIRE provides tight
                    bounds and rigorous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">guarantees on significantly larger=
                    expressions with well over a hundred thousand
                    operators, covering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">important examples including FFT, =
                    matrix multiplication, and PDE stencils.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Das2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-130" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CScalable yet
            Rigorous Floating-Point Error Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <span clas=s=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020
                International
                Conference
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">for High Performance Computing, =
                Networking, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC
            =E2=80=9920. Los Alamitos,
            CA, USA: IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 708=E2=80=93721.=
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.=
            1109/SC41405.2020.00055.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020=.00055"
                        class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputersocie=
                            ty.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00055</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Automated techniques for rigorous
                floating-point round-off error analysis are a prerequisite
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to placing important activities in=
                    HPC such as precision allocation,
                    verification, and code optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on a formal footing. Yet existing =
                    techniques cannot provide tight bounds for
                    expressions beyond a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">few dozen operators =E2=80=93 bare=
                    ly enough for HPC. In this work, we offer an approach
                    embedded in a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new tool called SATIRE that scales=
                    error analysis by four orders of magnitude
                    compared to today=E2=80=99s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">best-of-class tools. We explain ho=
                    w three key ideas underlying SATIRE helps it
                    attain such scale: path
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strength reduction, bound optimiza=
                    tion, and abstraction. SATIRE provides tight
                    bounds and rigorous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">guarantees on significantly larger=
                    expressions with well over a hundred thousand
                    operators, covering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">important examples including FFT, =
                    matrix multiplication, and PDE stencils.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Das2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-131" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Das, Arnab et al. =E2=80=9CAn Abstraction-gu=
            ided Approach to Scalable and Rigorous Floating-Point
            Error Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: (Apr. 2020). arXiv: 2004.11960v1 <= span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.PL]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Automated techniques for rigorous
                floating-point round-off error analysis are important in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">areas including formal verificatio=
                    n of correctness and precision tuning. Existing
                    tools and techniques,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">while providing tight bounds, fail=
                    to analyze expressions with more than a few
                    hundred operators,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">thus unable to cover important pra=
                    ctical problems. In this work, we present
                    Satire, a new tool
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that sheds light on how scalabilit=
                    y and bound-tightness can be attained through a
                    combination of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">incremental analysis, abstraction,=
                    and judicious use of concrete and symbolic
                    evaluation. Satire has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">handled problems exceeding 200K op=
                    erators. We present Satire=E2=80=99s underlying error
                    analysis approach,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">information-theoretic abstraction =
                    heuristics, and a wide range of case studies,
                    with evaluation covering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">FFT, Lorenz system of equations, a=
                    nd various PDE stencil types. Our results
                    demonstrate the tightness
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of Satire=E2=80=99s bounds, its ac=
                    ceptable runtime, and valuable insights
                    provided.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Davis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-132" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Davis, Timothy A. et al. =E2=80=9CAlgorithm =
            1003: Mongoose, A Graph Coarsening and
            Partitioning Library=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">ACM =
                Transactions on Mathematical Software </span>46.7 (1
            2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi<= /span></span>:
                10.1145/3387915.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Partitioning graphs is a common
                and useful operation in many areas, from parallel computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to VLSI design to sparse matrix al=
                    gorithms. In this paper, we introduce Mongoose,
                    a multilevel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hybrid graph partitioning algorith=
                    m and library. Building on previous work in
                    multilevel partitioning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">frameworks and combinatoric approa=
                    ches, we introduce novel stall-reducing and
                    stall-free coarsening
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategies, as well as an efficien=
                    t hybrid algorithm leveraging 1) traditional
                    combinatoric methods and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">2) continuous quadratic programmin=
                    g formulations. We demonstrate how this new
                    hybrid algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">outperforms either strategy in iso=
                    lation, and we also compare Mongoose to METIS
                    and demonstrate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its effectiveness on large and soc=
                    ial networking (power law) graphs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-DeFreez2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-133" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">DeFreez, Daniel et al. =E2=80=9CDetecting an=
            d reproducing error-code propagation bugs in MPI
            implementations=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedin=
                gs of the 25th ACM SIGPLAN Symposium on Principles and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Practice of Parallel Programming=
            </span>. PPoPP =E2=80=9920. ACM, Feb. 2020. <span
                class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span=class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1145/3332466.3374515.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present an approach to
                automatically detect and reproduce error code propagation bu=
                gs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in MPI implementations. Specifical=
                    ly, we combine static analysis and program
                    repair for bug detection,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and apply fault injection to repro=
                    duce error propagation bugs found in MPI
                    libraries written in C.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We demonstrate our approach on the=
                    MPICH library, one of the most popular
                    implementations of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">MPI, and the MPICH-based implement=
                    ation MVAPICH, uncovering 447 previously
                    unknown bugs.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We discovered that 31 of these bug=
                    s result in program crashes, and 60% of the
                    MPICH test suite is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">susceptible to crashing due to fai=
                    lures to propagate error codes. Moreover, 95
                    bugs produce undesirable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">behavior that has been confirmed d=
                    ynamically, causing tests to fail, hanging
                    processes, or simply
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dropping error codes before reachi=
                    ng user applications.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Demidov2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-134" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Demidov, Denis. =E2=80=9CAMGCL&nbsp;=E2=80=
            =94A C++ library for efficient solution of large sparse linear
            systems=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Software Impacts =
            </span>6 (Nov. 2020), p. 100037. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.simpa.2020.100037.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">AMGCL is a header-only C++
                library for the solution of large sparse linear systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with algebraic multigrid. The meth=
                    od may be used as a black-box solver for
                    computational
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems in various fields, since =
                    it does not require any information about the
                    underlying geometry.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">AMGCL provides an efficient, flexi=
                    ble, and extensible implementation of several
                    iterative solvers and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preconditioners on top of differen=
                    t backends allowing the acceleration of the
                    solution with the help of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">OpenMP, OpenCL, or CUDA technologi=
                    es. Most algorithms have both shared memory and
                    distributed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory implementations. The librar=
                    y is published under a permissive MIT
                    license.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Demirci2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-135" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Demirci, Gunduz Vehbi and Cevdet Aykanat. =
            =E2=80=9CCartesian Partitioning Models for 2D and 3D
            Parallel SpGEMM Algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10=">IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed
               Systems</span>
            31.12 (Dec. 2020), pp. 2763=E2=80=932775. <span class=3D" cmcsc-=10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/tpds.2020.3000708.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The focus is distributed-memory
                parallelization of sparse-general-matrix-multiplication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(SpGEMM). Parallel SpGEMM algorith=
                    ms are classified under one-dimensional (1D),
                    2D, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">3D categories denoting the number =
                    of dimensions by which the 3D sparse workcube
                    representing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the iteration space of SpGEMM is p=
                    artitioned. Recently proposed successful 2D-
                    and 3D-parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SpGEMM algorithms benefit from upp=
                    er bounds on communication overheads enforced
                    by 2D and 3D
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cartesian partitioning of the work=
                    cube on 2D and 3D virtual processor grids,
                    respectively. However,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">these methods are based on random =
                    cartesian partitioning and do not utilize
                    sparsity patterns
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of SpGEMM instances for reducing t=
                    he communication overheads. We propose
                    hypergraph models
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for 2D and 3D cartesian partitioni=
                    ng of the workcube for further reducing the
                    communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overheads of these 2D- and 3D- par=
                    allel SpGEMM algorithms. The proposed models
                    utilize two- and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">three-phase partitioning that expl=
                    oit multi-constraint hypergraph partitioning
                    formulations. Extensive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experimentation performed on 20 Sp=
                    GEMM instances by using upto 900 processors
                    demonstrate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that proposed partitioning models =
                    significantly improve the scalability of 2D and
                    3D algorithms.

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">For example, in 2D-parallel SpGEMM=
                    algorithm on 900 processors, the proposed
                    partitioning model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">respectively achieves 85 and 42 pe=
                    rcent decrease in total volume and total number
                    of messages, leading
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to 1.63 times higher speedup compa=
                    red to random partitioning, on average.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Demmel2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-136" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Demmel, James et al. <span class=3D"cmti-10"=>Prospectus for the
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Next LAPACK and ScaLAPACK Librar=
                ies:Basic ALgebra LIbraries for Sustainable
                Technology
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">withInterdisciplinary Collaborat=
                ion (BALLISTIC)</span>. Tech. rep. University
            of Tennessee, 2020.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://www.icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2020/icl-utk-=1391-2020.pdf"
                        class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.icl.utk.ed=
                            u/files/publications/2020/icl-utk-1391-2020.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The convergence of several
                unprecedented changes, including formidable new system desig=
                n
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">constraints and revolutionary leve=
                    ls of heterogeneity, has made it clear that
                    much of the essential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">software infrastructure of computa=
                    tional science and engineering is, or will soon
                    be, obsolete. Math
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">libraries have historically been i=
                    n the vanguard of software that must be adapted
                    first to such changes,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both because these low-level workh=
                    orses are so critical to the accuracy and
                    performance of so many
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different types of applications, a=
                    nd because they have proved to be outstanding
                    vehicles for finding
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and implementing solutions to the =
                    problems that novel architectures pose. Under
                    the Basic ALgebra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">LIbraries for Sustainable Technolo=
                    gy with Interdisciplinary Collaboration
                    (BALLISTIC) project, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">principal designers of the Linear =
                    Algebra PACKage (LAPACK) and the Scalable
                    Linear Algebra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PACKage (ScaLAPACK), the combinati=
                    on of which is abbreviated Sca/LAPACK, aim to
                    enhance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and update these libraries for the=
                    ongoing revolution in processor architecture,
                    system design, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application requirements by incorp=
                    orating them into a layered package of software
                    components =E2=80=93 the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">BALLISTIC ecosystem =E2=80=93 that=
                    provides users seamless access to state-of-the-art
                    solver implementations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through familiar and improved Sca/=
                    LAPACK interfaces.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">The se=
                    t of innovations and improvements that will be made available
                    through BALLISTIC is the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">result of a combination of inputs =
                    from a variety of sources: the authors=E2=80=99 own
                    algorithmic and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">software research, which attacks t=
                    he challenges of multi-core, hybrid, and
                    extreme-scale system
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">designs; extensive interactions wi=
                    th users, vendors, and the management of large
                    high-performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing (HPC) facilities to help=
                    anticipate the demands and opportunities of
                    new architectures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and programming languages; and, fi=
                    nally, the enthusiastic participation of the
                    research community
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in developing and offering enhance=
                    d versions of existing dense linear algebra
                    software components.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Aiming to help applications run po=
                    rtably at all levels of the platform pyramid,
                    including in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cloud-based systems, BALLISTIC=E2=
                    =80=99s technical agenda includes: (1) adding new
                    functionality requested
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by stakeholder communities; (2) in=
                    corporating vastly improved numerical methods
                    and algorithms;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(3) leveraging successful research=
                    results to transition Sca/LAPACK (interfaces)
                    to multi-core and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accelerator-enabled versions; (4) =
                    providing user-controllable autotuning for the
                    deployed software; (5)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">introducing new interfaces and dat=
                    a structures to increase ease of use; (6)
                    enhancing engineering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for evolution via standards and co=
                    mmunity engagement; and (7) continuing to
                    expand application
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">community outreach. Enhanced engin=
                    eering will also help keep the reference
                    implementation for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Sca/LAPACK efficient, maintainable=
                    , and testable at reasonable cost in the
                    future.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">Th=
                    e Sca/LAPACK libraries are the community
                    standard for dense linear algebra. They have been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adopted and/or supported by a larg=
                    e community of users, computing centers, and
                    HPC vendors.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Learning to use them is a basic pa=
                    rt of the education of a computational
                    scientist or engineer in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many fields and at many academic i=
                    nstitutions. No other numerical library can
                    claim this breadth of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integration with the community. Co=
                    nsequently, enhancing these libraries with
                    state-of-the-art methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and algorithms and adapting them f=
                    or new and emerging platforms (reaching up to
                    extreme scale and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">including cloud-based environments=
                    ) is set to have a correspondingly large impact
                    on the research and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">education community, government la=
                    boratories, and private industry.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Deng2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-137" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Deng, Xiaoge et al. =E2=80=9CA Nonconvex Imp=
            lementation of Sparse Subspace Clustering:
            Algorithm and Convergence Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D="cmti-10">IEEE Access </span>8 (2020), pp.
            54741=E2=80=9354750.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1109/access.2020.2981740.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Subspace clustering has been
                widely applied to detect meaningful clusters in high-dimensi=
                onal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data spaces. And the sparse subspa=
                    ce clustering (SSC) obtains superior clustering
                    performance by

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving a relaxed </span>
                <!-- l. 3=

            <span class=3D"cmr-9">one can make the object function convex, =
it causes large errors on large coefficients in
               some cases.
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we study the sparse=
 subspace clustering algorithm based on a
               nonconvex modeling
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formulation. Specifically, we intr=
oduce a nonconvex pseudo-norm that makes a
               better approximation to
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-cont= ainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ct=xtmenu_counter=3D"186" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mj= x-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi c=lass=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mro= w>
                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                        <mjx-= mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    </m= jx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" disp=lay=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                                <msub>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mn>0</mn>
                                    </mrow>=
                                </msub>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-mi=
                            nimization
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">than the traditional </span>
                        <!-- l=
. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"187" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positio=n: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-msub>
                                    <m= jx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            <= /mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                                    <mjx-mrow= size=3D"s">
                                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-= mn>
                                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                                </mjx-script>
                                </mjx-msub>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselec=table=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/=MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <msub>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <m= n>1</mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </msub>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span clas=s=3D"cmr-9">-minimization
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">framework and consequently finds a=
                            better affinity matrix. However, this
                            formulation makes the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization task challenging due =
                            to that the traditional alternating direction
                            method of multipliers
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(ADMM) encounters troubles in solv=
                            ing the nonconvex subproblems. In view of this,
                            the reweighted
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques are employed in making =
                            these subproblems convex and easily solvable.
                            We provide several
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">guarantees to derive the convergen=
                            ce results, which proves that the nonconvex
                            algorithm is globally
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergent to a critical point. Ex=
                            periments on two real-world problems of motion
                            segmentation and
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">face clustering show that our meth=
                            od outperforms state-of-the-art
                            techniques.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Derezinski2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-138" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Derezi=C5=84ski, Micha=C5=82 and Michael W. =
            Mahoney. =E2=80=9CDeterminantal Point Processes in Randomized
            Numerical Linear Algebra=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.=
            03185v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Randomized Numerical Linear
                Algebra (RandNLA) uses randomness to develop improved
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms for matrix problems tha=
                    t arise in scientific computing, data science,
                    machine learning, etc.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Determinantal Point Processes (DPP=
                    s), a seemingly unrelated topic in pure and
                    applied mathematics,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is a class of stochastic point pro=
                    cesses with probability distribution
                    characterized by sub-determinants
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a kernel matrix. Recent work ha=
                    s uncovered deep and fruitful connections
                    between DPPs and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">RandNLA which lead to new guarante=
                    es and improved algorithms that are of interest
                    to both areas.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We provide an overview of this exc=
                    iting new line of research, including brief
                    introductions to RandNLA
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and DPPs, as well as applications =
                    of DPPs to classical linear algebra tasks such
                    as least squares
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regression, low-rank approximation=
                    and the Nystr=C3=B6m method. For example, random
                    sampling with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a DPP leads to new kinds of unbias=
                    ed estimators for least squares, enabling more
                    refined statistical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and inferential understanding of t=
                    hese algorithms; a DPP is, in some sense, an
                    optimal randomized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm for the Nystr=C3=B6m met=
                    hod; and a RandNLA technique called leverage score
                    sampling can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be derived as the marginal distrib=
                    ution of a DPP. We also discuss recent
                    algorithmic developments,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">illustrating that, while not quite=
                    as efficient as standard RandNLA techniques,
                    DPP-based algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are only moderately more expensive=
                    .</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Devarakonda2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-139" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Devarakonda, Aditya and James Demmel. =E2=80=
            =9CAvoiding Communication in Logistic Regression=E2=80=9D.
            In: (Nov. 2020). arXiv: 2011.08281 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.=
                LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Stochastic gradient descent (SGD)
                is one of the most widely used optimization methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for solving various machine learni=
                    ng problems. SGD solves an optimization problem
                    by iteratively
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sampling a few data points from th=
                    e input data, computing gradients for the
                    selected data points, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">updating the solution. However, in=
                    a parallel setting, SGD requires interprocess
                    communication at every
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteration. We introduce a new comm=
                    unication-avoiding technique for solving the
                    logistic regression
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem using SGD. This technique =
                    re-organizes the SGD computations into a form
                    that communicates
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">every </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"188" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>s</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            <= /mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">iterations instead of every iteration, wh=
                                    ere </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_=0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"189" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mj= x-mi>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                        <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>s<= /mi></math>
                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is a tuning parameter. We prove theoretic=
                                    al flops, bandwidth, and latency upper bounds
                                    for SGD and
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its new communication-avoiding var=
                                    iant. Furthermore, we show experimental results
                                    that illustrate that
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the new Communication-Avoiding SGD=
                                    (CA-SGD) method can achieve speedups of up to
                                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"190" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx= -mn>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2E">
                                                    </mjx-c=>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c39">
                                                    </mj= x-c>
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c37"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-bin">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.o=rg/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mn>4</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-pu=nc">.</mo>
                                            <mn>97</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                        </math></mjx-as= sistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">on a high-performance Infiniband cluster =
                                    without altering the convergence behavior or
                                    accuracy.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Devine2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-140" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Devine, Karen and Grey Ballard. <span class==3D"cmti-10">GentenMPI: Distributed Memory
                Sparse
                Tensor
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Decomposition</span>. Tech. rep.=
            Sandia National Laboratories, 2020. eprint:
            SAND2020-8515. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1656940" class=3D="url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1656940</s= pan></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">GentenMPl is a toolkit of sparse
                canonical polyadic (CP) tensor decomposition algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that is designed to run effectivel=
                    y on distributed-memory high-performance
                    computers. Its use of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed-memory parallelism ena=
                    bles it to efficiently decompose tensors that
                    are too large for a single
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compute node=E2=80=99s memory. Gen=
                    tenMPl leverages Sandia=E2=80=99s decades-long investment in
                    the Trilinos solver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">framework for much of its parallel=
                    -computation capability. Trilinos contains
                    numerical algorithms and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear algebra classes that have b=
                    een optimized for parallel simulation of
                    complex physical phenomena.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This work applies these tools to t=
                    he data science problem of sparse tensor
                    decomposition. In this report,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we describe the use of Trilinos in=
                    GentenMPl, extensions needed for sparse tensor
                    decomposition, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementations of the CP-ALS (CP =
                    via alternating least squares [4, 7]) and
                    GCP-SGD (generalized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CP via stochastic gradient descent=
                    [11, 12, 17]) sparse tensor decomposition
                    algorithms. We show
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that GentenMPl can decompose spars=
                    e tensors of extreme size, e.g., a
                    12.6-terabyte tensor on 8192
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computer cores. We demonstrate tha=
                    t the Trilinos backbone provides good strong
                    and weak scaling of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the tensor decomposition algorithm=
                    s.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Dinda2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-141" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Dinda, Peter, Alex Bernat, and Conor Hetland=
            . =E2=80=9CSpying on the Floating Point Behavior
            ofExisting, Unmodified Scientific Applications=E2=80=9D. In: <s= pan class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the
                29th
                International
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Symposium on High-Performance Pa=
                    rallel and Distributed Computing</span>. HPDC=E2=80=99
                20. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">u=
                        rl</span></span>:
                <a href=3D"http://pdinda.org/Papers/hpdc20.pdf" class=3D"url">
                    <= span class=3D"cmtt-10">http://pdinda.org/Papers/hpdc20.pdf</span>
                </a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Scientific (and other)
                applications are critically dependent on calculations done u=
                sing IEEE
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating point arithmetic. A numbe=
                    r of concerns have been raised about
                    correctness in such applications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">given the numerous gotchas the IEE=
                    E standard presents for developers, as well as
                    the complexity of its
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation at the hardware and=
                    compiler levels. The standard and its
                    implementations do provide
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mechanisms for analyzing floating =
                    point arithmetic as it executes, making it
                    possible to find and track
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problematic operations. However, t=
                    his capability is seldom used in practice. In
                    response, we have
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developed FPSpy, a tool that provi=
                    des this capability when operating underneath
                    existing, unmodified
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">x64 application binaries on Linux,=
                    including those using thread- and
                    process-level parallelism. FPSpy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can observe application behavior w=
                    ithout any cooperation from the application or
                    developer, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can potentially be deployed as par=
                    t of a job launch process. We present the
                    design, implementation,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and performance evaluation of FPSp=
                    y. FPSpy operates conservatively, getting out
                    of the way if the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application itself begins to use a=
                    ny of the OS or hardware features that FPSpy
                    depends on. Its overhead
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be throttled, allowing a trade=
                    off between which and how many unusual events
                    are to be captured,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the slowdown incurred by the a=
                    pplication, with the low point providing
                    virtually zero slowdown.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We evaluated FPSpy by using it to =
                    methodically study seven widely-used
                    applications/frameworks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from a range of domains (five of w=
                    hich are in the NSF XSEDE top-20), as well as
                    the NAS and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PARSEC benchmark suites. All told,=
                    these comprise about 7.5 million lines of
                    source code in a wide
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">range of languages, and parallelis=
                    m models (including OpenMP and MPI). FPSpy was
                    able to produce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">trace information for all of them.=
                    The traces show that problematic floating
                    point events occur in both
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the applications and the benchmark=
                    s. Analysis of the rounding behavior captured
                    in our traces also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">suggests the feasibility of an app=
                    roach to adding adaptive precision underneath
                    existing, unmodified
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">binaries.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ding2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-142" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ding, Nan et al. =E2=80=9CLeveraging One-Sid=
            ed Communication for Sparse Triangular Solvers=E2=80=9D. In:
            (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we implement and
                evaluate a one-sided communication-based distributed-memory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse triangular solve (SpTRSV). =
                    SpTRSV is used in conjunction with Sparse LU to
                    affect preconditioning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in linear solvers. One-sided commu=
                    nication paradigms enjoy higher effective
                    network bandwidth and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lower synchronization costs compar=
                    ed to their two-sided counterparts. We use a
                    passive target mode
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in one-sided communication to impl=
                    ement a synchronizationfree task queue to
                    manage the messaging
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">between producerconsumer pairs. Wh=
                    ereas some numerical methods lend themselves to
                    simple performance

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis, the DAG-based computatio=
                    nal graph of SpTRSV demands we construct a
                    critical path
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance model in order to asse=
                    ss our observed performance relative to machine
                    capabilities. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">alignment with our model, our foMP=
                    I-based one-sided implementation of SpTRSV
                    reduces communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time by 1.5</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"191" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx=-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx= -assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http:/=/www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin=">=C3=97</mo></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D" cmr-9">to 2.5</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container= class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmen=u_counter=3D"192" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-mat= h class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-b=in">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml= unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/19=98/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                                <= /math>
                                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                    </mjx-container>
                                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and improves SpTRSV solver performance by=
                                                        up to 2.4</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Att=ached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                        tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"193" style=3D"fon=t-size:
                                                        117.4%; position: relative;">
                                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidde=n=3D"true">
                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                                                    <= /mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mo>
                                                        </mjx-math>
                                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                display=3D"i=nline">
                                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                </= mjx-container>
                                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">compared to the SuperLU DIST=E2=80=99s tw=
                                                    o-sided MPI implementation running on 64 to 4,096
                                                    processes on
                                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Cray supercomputers.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Dinh2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-143" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Dinh, Grace and James Demmel. =E2=80=9CCommu=
            nication-Optimal Tilings for Projective Nested
            Loops with Arbitrary Bounds=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2=
            003.00119 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Reducing communication - either
                between levels of a memory hierarchy or between processors
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">over a network - is a key componen=
                    t of performance optimization (in both time and
                    energy) for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many problems, including dense lin=
                    ear algebra, particle interactions, and machine
                    learning. For these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems, which can be represented=
                    as nested-loop computations, previous tiling
                    based approaches
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have been used to find both lower =
                    bounds on the communication required to execute
                    them and optimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rearrangements, or blockings, to a=
                    ttain such lower bounds. However, such general
                    approaches have
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">typically assumed the problem size=
                    s are large, an assumption that is often not
                    met in practice. For
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instance, the classical </span>
                <!-= - l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTM=L" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"194" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posi=tion: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mrow=>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </= mjx-mo>

                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c69"></mjx-c>

                                                        </= mjx-c>

                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n M=athClass-bin" space=3D"3">

                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mr= ow space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                                <mjx-mtext class==3D"mjx-n">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c63"></mjx-c>
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c61">
                                                        </mjx-= c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c63"></mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c68"></mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c65"></mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cA0"></mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c73"></mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c69"></mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7A">=
                                                        </mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c65"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mtext>
                                            </mjx-mstyle>
                                            </mjx-mro= w>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>=
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                                </mjx-= c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mstyl= e class=3D"text">
                                            <mtext>num&nbsp;arithmetic&nbsp;operations</mtext>
                                            </mstyle=>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-b=in">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mstyle cl=ass=3D"text">
                                            <mtext>cache&nbsp;size</mtext>
                                        </mstyle>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                                <mn>1</mn>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95<= /mo>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">lower bound for matrix multiplication is =
                        not tight for matrix-vector multiplications,
                        which must read in
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at least </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx= -container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"=0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"195" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;="><mjx-math class=3D"
                        MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <m= jx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx= -mo>
                    <mjx-mrow>
                        <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                            <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c6E"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c75"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c6D"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cA0"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c61">=
                                </mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c72"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c69"></mjx-c>
                                <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c74">
                                    </mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c68"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c6D"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c65"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c74"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c69"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c63">
                                        </m= jx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cA0"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c70">
                                    </mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c65"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c72"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c61"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=74"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c69"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6F">
                                        </mjx= -c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6E"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c73"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-= mtext>
                        </mjx-mstyle>
                    </mjx-mrow>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                        <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                            </mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass=-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                        <mtext>num&nbsp;arithmetic&nbsp;o=
                                            perations</mtext>
                                    </mstyle>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow=>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">words of memory; similar issues occur for=
                            almost all convolutions in machine learning
                            applications,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which use extremely small filter s=
                            izes (and therefore, loop bounds). In this
                            paper, we provide an efficient
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">way to both find and obtain, via a=
                            n appropriate, efficiently constructible
                            blocking, communication lower
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bounds and matching tilings which =
                            attain these lower bounds for nested loop
                            programs with arbitrary
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">loop bounds that operate on multid=
                            imensional arrays in the projective case, where
                            the array indices
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are subsets of the loop indices. O=
                            ur approach works on all such problems,
                            regardless of dimensionality,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">size, memory access patterns, or n=
                            umber of arrays, and directly applies to (among
                            other examples)
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix multiplication and similar =
                            dense linear algebra operations, tensor
                            contractions, n-body pairwise
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">interactions, pointwise convolutio=
                            ns, and fully connected layers.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Dixit2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-144" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Dixit, Rishabh and Waheed U. Bajwa. =E2=80=
            =9CExit Time Analysis for Approximations of Gradient
            Descent Trajectories Around Saddle Points=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2=
            020). arXiv: 2006.01106v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper considers the problem
