                of understanding the exit time for trajectories
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of gradient-related first-order me=
                    thods from saddle neighborhoods under some
                    initial boundary
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conditions. Given the =E2=80=98fla=
                    t=E2=80=99 geometry around saddle points, first-order methods
                    can struggle in escaping
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">these regions in a fast manner due=
                    to the small magnitudes of gradients
                    encountered. In particular,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">while it is known that gradient-re=
                    lated first-order methods escape strict-saddle
                    neighborhoods, existing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">literature does not explicitly lev=
                    erage the local geometry around saddle points
                    in order to control
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">behavior of gradient trajectories.=
                    It is in this context that this paper puts
                    forth a rigorous geometric
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis of the gradient-descent m=
                    ethod around strict-saddle neighborhoods using
                    matrix perturbation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">theory. In doing so, it provides a=
                    key result that can be used to generate an
                    approximate gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">trajectory for any given initial c=
                    onditions. In addition, the analysis leads to a
                    linear exit-time solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for gradient-descent method under =
                    certain necessary initial conditions for a
                    class of strict-saddle
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">functions.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Doikov2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-145" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Doikov, Nikita and Yurii Nesterov. =E2=80=9C=
            Affine-invariant contracting-point methods for Convex
            Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv: 2009.08894 <spa= n class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we develop new
                affine-invariant algorithms for solving composite convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">minimization problems with bounded=
                    domain. We present a general framework of
                    Contracting-Point
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods, which solve at each itera=
                    tion an auxiliary subproblem restricting the
                    smooth part of the

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">objective function onto contractio=
                    n of the initial domain. This framework
                    provides us with a systematic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">way for developing optimization me=
                    thods of different order, endowed with the
                    global complexity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bounds. We show that using an appr=
                    opriate affine-invariant smoothness condition,
                    it is possible to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implement one iteration of the Con=
                    tracting-Point method by one step of the pure
                    tensor method of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">degree </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-c= ontainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"196" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">=
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Mat=hClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2265"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mn= class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                            </mj= x-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmln=s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>p</mi>
                                    <mo c=lass=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A5</mo>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            <= /mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The resulting global rate of conve=
                                    rgence in functional residual is then </span>
                                <!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"197" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-S=C"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=open">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"=mjx-n">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mn>
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Mat=hClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mi c=lass=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I">
                                                        </mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mj=x-n MathClass-close">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <= /mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mi mathvari=ant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(
                                        </= mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                            <mi mathvarian=t=3D"italic">kp</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                        </mat= h>
                                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"198" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:
                                    relative;">
                                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>k</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        <= /mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the iteration counter. It is important=
                                that all constants in our bounds are
                                affine-invariant. For</span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"199" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </m= jx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c3D"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" disp=lay=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>p</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                        </ma= th>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our scheme recovers well-known Fra=
                                nk-Wolfe algorithm, providing it with a new
                                interpretation by a
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">general perspective of tensor meth=
                                ods. Finally, within our framework, we present
                                efficient implementation
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and total complexity analysis of t=
                                he inexact second-order scheme </span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"200" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space==3D"4">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" sp=ace=3D"4">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo clas=s=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx= -mrow>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mrow=>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>p</mi>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-=rel">=3D</mo>
                                                <mn>2</mn>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                            </mrow>
                        </= math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">called Contracting Newton method. =
                            It can be seen as a proper implementation of
                            the trust-region idea.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Preliminary numerical results conf=
                            irm its good practical performance both in the
                            number of iterations,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and in computational time.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Doikov2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-146" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CConvex optimization based=
            on global lower second-order models=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                CORE Discussion
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Papers ; 2020/23 (2020) 22 pages=
                http://hdl.handle.net/2078.1/230370
            </span>(June 2020). arXiv:
            2006.08518v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we present new
                second-order algorithms for composite convex optimization,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">called Contracting-domain Newton m=
                    ethods. These algorithms are affine-invariant
                    and based on global
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">second-order lower approximation f=
                    or the smooth component of the objective. Our
                    approach has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an interpretation both as a second=
                    -order generalization of the conditional
                    gradient method, or as a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variant of trust-region scheme. Un=
                    der the assumption, that the problem domain is
                    bounded, we prove</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"201" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-S=C"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"=mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Mat=hClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi c=lass=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-= mi>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-= mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                                            </mjx= -c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" di=splay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo clas=s=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=
                                        =88=95</mo>
                                    <mi>k</mi>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                <= /mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">global rate of convergence in functional =
                    residual, where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMe=nu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"202" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"=></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"i=nline">
                            <mi>k</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the iteration counter, minimizing conv=
                    ex functions with Lipschitz continuous Hessian.
                    This significantly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improves the previously known boun=
                    d </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"203" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tru=e">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC">=
                            </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-ope=n">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx=-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>=
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"=></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"=on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" d=isplay=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <m= o class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin=">=E2=88=95</mo><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo class=3D"
                                            MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow=>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">for this type of algorithms. Additionally=
                    , we propose a stochastic extension of our
                    method, and present
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational results for solving =
                    empirical risk minimization problem.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Doikov2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-147" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CInexact Tensor Methods wi=
            th Dynamic Accuracies=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.09403
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we study inexact
                high-order Tensor Methods for solving convex optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems with composite objective.=
                    At every step of such methods, we use
                    approximate solution of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">auxiliary problem, defined by the =
                    bound for the residual in function value. We
                    propose two dynamic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategies for choosing the inner =
                    accuracy: the first one is decreasing as
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"204" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx= -mn>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=2F"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>=
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B">=
                                </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3=1"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                            <mn>1</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                            <mi mathvarian=t=3D"italic">kp</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                            <mn>1</mn>
                        </math></mj= x-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"205" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2265"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mn=class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        </mjx= -math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>p</mi>
                                <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A5</mo>
                                <mn>1</mn>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </= mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the order of the method and </span>
                    <!-= - l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTM=L" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"206" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posi=tion: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi c=lass=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mat= h>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"=http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>k</mi>
                                    </math></m= jx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the iteration counter, and the second =
                            approach is using for the inner accuracy the
                            last progress in the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">target objective. We show that ine=
                            xact Tensor Methods with these strategies
                            achieve the same global
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rate as in the error-f=
                            ree case. For the second approach we also
                            establish local superlinear
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rates (for </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"207" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx=-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2265"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <= mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-m= n>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                <mat= h xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>p</mi=>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=89=A5</mo>
                                        <mn>2</mn></math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">),
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and propose the accelerated scheme=
                                            . Lastly, we present computational results on a
                                            variety of machine
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learning problems for several meth=
                                            ods and different accuracy policies.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-DomenechAsensi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-148" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Domenech-Asensi, Gines and Tom J. Kazmierski=
            . =E2=80=9CStability and Efficiency of Explicit
            Integration in Interconnect Analysis on GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: <spa= n class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020
                IEEE
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">International Symposium on Circu=
                    its and Systems</span>. ISCAS 2020. IEEE, Oct.
                2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</= span></span>:
                    10.1109/iscas45731.2020.9181157.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper presents a new
                high-performance technique to parallelise numeric integratio=
                n
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of large VLSI interconnect analog =
                    models on a general purpose GPU. The technique
                    is based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the combination of space-state =
                    formulation with an explicit integration method
                    based on the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Adams-Bashforth second order formu=
                    la. The paper studies the stability of the
                    variable step explicit
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method and proposes a technique to=
                    guarantee integration stability specifically
                    for interconnect
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. Although explicit methods=
                    require smaller integration steps compared to
                    those of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">traditional implicit techniques, t=
                    hey avoid the complex calculations inherent to
                    implicit integration.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The proposed approach is demonstra=
                    ted using an RC VLSI interconnect model and
                    results are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compared to those achieved by Ngsp=
                    ice, a state-of-the-art implicit integration
                    solver, implemented on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the same parallel hardware. The re=
                    sults show that the speed-up of the
                    parallelised explicit solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reaches one order of magnitude for=
                    large systems and is increasing with the
                    circuit size.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Domingos2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-149" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Domingos, Jo=C3=A3o and Jos=C3=A9 M. F. Mour=
            a. =E2=80=9CGraph Fourier Transform: A Stable
            Approximation=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). arXiv: 2001.05042 <spa= n class=3D"cmtt-10">[eess.SP]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In Graph Signal Processing (GSP),
                data dependencies are represented by a graph whose nodes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">label the data and the edges captu=
                    re dependencies among nodes. The graph is
                    represented by a weighted
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adjacency matrix </span>
                <!-- l. 30=
 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabi=ndex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"208" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: r=elative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D="mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>A</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">that, in GSP, generalizes the Discrete Si=
                    gnal Processing (DSP) shift operator </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"209" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D467 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c2212"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D="on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"=display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>z</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                            <mn>=
                                1</mn>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The (right) eigenvectors of the sh=
                    ift </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_=0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"210" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mj= x-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>A<= /mi></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">(graph spectral components) diagonalize <= /span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" ja=x=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"211" style=3D"font-size: 117=.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">=
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>=
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                <math=xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>A</mi>
                                    <= /math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">and lead to a graph Fourier basis </span>=
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"C=HTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"212" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; p=osition: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-= math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>F</mi>
                                            </math=>
                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">that provides a graph spectral representa=
                            tion of the graph signal. The inverse of the
                            (matrix of the)
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph Fourier basis </span>
                        <!-- l.=
 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" t=abindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"213" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position=: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>F</mi>
                                </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the Graph Fourier transform (GFT), </s= pan><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"214" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"=></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=31"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" disp=lay=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                                        <mi>F</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                        <= /math>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Often, including in real world exa=
                                mples, this diagonalization is numerically
                                unstable. This paper
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">develops an approach to compute an=
                                accurate approximation to </span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"215" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </m= jx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>F=
                                        </mi>
                                    </math>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"216" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin=" space=3D" 3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"=mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mj= x-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http:=//www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>F</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-conta= iner><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">while insuring their numerical sta=
                                        bility, by means of solving a non convex
                                        optimization problem. To
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">address the non-convexity, we prop=
                                        ose an algorithm, the stable graph Fourier
                                        basis algorithm (SGFA)
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that we prove to exponentially inc=
                                        rease the accuracy of the approximating </span>
                                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"217" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </m= jx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                            <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>F=
                                                </mi>
                                            </math>
                                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">per iteration. Likewise, we can apply SGF=
                                        A to </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"218" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43B TEX-I">
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">AH</mi>
                                                </ma= th>
                                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and, hence, approximate the stable left e=
                                        igenvectors for the graph shift </span>
                                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"219" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </m= jx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                            <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>A=
                                                </mi>
                                            </math>
                                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and directly compute the GFT. We evaluate=
                                        empirically the quality of SGFA by applying it
                                        to graph
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">shifts </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-c= ontainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                        tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"220" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:
                                        relative;">=
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I">
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>A</mi>
                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>=
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">drawn from two real world problems, the 2=
                                004 US political blogs graph and the Manhattan
                                road map,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">carrying out a comprehensive study=
                                on tradeoffs between different SGFA
                                parameters. We also confirm
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our conclusions by applying SGFA o=
                                n very sparse and very dense directed
                                Erd=C5=91s-R=C3=A9nyi graphs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Dong2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-150" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Dong, Jing and Xin T. Tong. =E2=80=9CReplica=
            Exchange for Non-Convex Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan.
            2020). arXiv: 2001.08356 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</spa= n>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Gradient descent (GD) is known to
                converge quickly for convex objective functions, but it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be trapped at local minimums. =
                    On the other hand, Langevin dynamics (LD) can
                    explore the state
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">space and find global minimums, bu=
                    t in order to give accurate estimates, LD needs
                    to run with small
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">discretization stepsize and weak s=
                    tochastic force, which in general slow down its
                    convergence. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paper shows that these two algorit=
                    hms can =E2=80=9Dcollaborate=E2=80=9D through a simple exchange
                    mechanism, in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which they swap their current posi=
                    tions if LD yields a lower objective function.
                    This idea can be seen
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as the singular limit of the repli=
                    ca exchange technique from the sampling
                    literature. We show that this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new algorithm converges to the glo=
                    bal minimum linearly with high probability,
                    assuming the objective
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">function is strongly convex in a n=
                    eighborhood of the unique global minimum. By
                    replacing gradients
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with stochastic gradients, and add=
                    ing a proper threshold to the exchange
                    mechanism, our algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can also be used in online setting=
                    s. We further verify our theoretical results
                    through some numerical

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments, and observe superior =
                    performance of the proposed algorithm over
                    running GD or LD
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">alone.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Dongarra2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-151" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Dongarra, Jack et al. <span class=3D"cmti-10=">Translational
            </span><span class=3D" cmti-10">Process: Mathematical Software P=
                erspective</span>. Tech. rep. University of
            Tennessee, 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-=caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://www.icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2020/icl-=utk-1404-2020.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.icl.ut=
                    k.edu/files/publications/2020/icl-utk-1404-2020.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Each successive generation of
                computer architecture has brought new challenges to achievin=
                g
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high performance mathematical solv=
                    ers, necessitating development and analysis of
                    new algorithms,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which are then embodied in softwar=
                    e libraries. These libraries hide architectural
                    details from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications, allowing them to ach=
                    ieve a level of portability across platforms
                    from desktops to worldclass
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high performance computing (HPC) s=
                    ystems. Thus there has been an informal
                    translational computer
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">science process of developing algo=
                    rithms and distributing them in open source
                    software libraries for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adoption by applications and vendo=
                    rs. With the move to exascale, increasing
                    intentionality about this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">process will benefit the long-term=
                    sustainability of the scientific software
                    stack.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Duff2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-152" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Duff, Iain, Jonathan Hogg, and Florent Lopez=
            . =E2=80=9CA New Sparse
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"221"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I">
                            </mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I">
                                </= mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D447 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-as= sistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://ww=w.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">LD=
                            LT</mi>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container>
            Solver Using A Posteriori Threshold Pivoting=E2=80=9D. In: <spa= n class=3D"cmti-10">SIAM Journal on
                Scientific
                Computing </span>42.2
                (Jan. 2020), pp. C23=E2=80=93C42. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                    <spa= n class=3D"small-caps">doi
                </span></span>:
                10.1137/18m1225963.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The factorization of sparse
                symmetric indefinite systems is particularly challenging sin=
                ce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">pivoting is required to maintain s=
                    tability of the factorization. Pivoting
                    techniques generally offer
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">limited parallelism and are associ=
                    ated with significant data movement hindering
                    the scalability of these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods. Variants of the threshold=
                    partial pivoting (TPP) algorithm, for example,
                    have often been used
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">because of its numerical robustnes=
                    s but standard implementations exhibit poor
                    parallel performance.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">On the other hand, some methods tr=
                    ade stability for performance on parallel
                    architectures such as the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">supernode Bunch=E2=80=93Kaufman us=
                    ed in the PARDISO solver. In this case, however, the
                    factors obtained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">might not be used to accurately co=
                    mpute the solution of the system. For this
                    reason we have designed a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">task-based </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"222" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c1D447 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">LDLT</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization algorithm based on a new pi=
                            voting strategy called a posteriori threshold
                            pivoting (APTP)
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that is much more suitable for mod=
                            ern multicore architectures and has the same
                            numerical robustness
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as the TPP strategy. We implemente=
                            d our algorithm in a new version of the SPRAL
                            sparse symmetric
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">indefinite direct solver, which in=
                            itially supported GPU-only factorization. We
                            have used OpenMP 4 task
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">features to implement a multifront=
                            al algorithm with dense factorizations using
                            the novel APTP, and
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we show that it performs favorably=
                            compared to the state-of-the-art solvers
                            HSL_MA86, HSL_MA97
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and PARDISO both in terms of perfo=
                            rmance on a multicore machine and in terms of
                            numerical
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">robustness. Finally we show that t=
                            his new solver is able to make use of GPU
                            devices for accelerating
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the factorization on heterogeneous=
                            architectures.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Duff2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-153" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Duff, Iain, Philippe Leleux, et al. =E2=80=
            =9CImproving the Scalability of the ABCD Solver with
            a Combination of New Load Balancing and Communication Minimizat=
            ion Techniques=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Advances in Parallel Computing </sp= an>36.Parallel Computing: Technology Trends
                (2020),
                pp. 277=E2=80=93286. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">issn</span></span>: 0927-5452.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span cla=ss=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.3233/APC200052.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The hybrid scheme block
                row-projection method implemented in the ABCD Solver is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">designed for solving large sparse =
                    unsymmetric systems of equations on distributed
                    memory parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computers. The method implements a=
                    block Cimmino iterative scheme, accelerated
                    with a stabilized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">block conjugate gradient algorithm=
                    . An augmented pseudo-direct variant has also
                    been developed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to overcome convergence issues. Bo=
                    th methods are included in the ABCD solver with
                    a hybrid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelization scheme. The parall=
                    el performance of the ABCD Solver is improved
                    in the first non-beta
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">release, version 1.0, which we pre=
                    sent in this paper. Novel algorithms for the
                    distribution of partitions to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processes are introduced to minimi=
                    ze communication as well as to balance the
                    workload. Furthermore,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the master-slave approach on each =
                    subsystem is also improved in order to achieve
                    higher scalability
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through run-time placement of proc=
                    esses. We illustrate the improved parallel
                    scalability of the ABCD

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Solver on a distributed memory arc=
                    hitecture by solving several problems from the
                    SuiteSparse Matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Collection.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Dvurechensky2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-154" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Dvurechensky, Pavel et al. =E2=80=9CSelf-con=
            cordant analysis of Frank-Wolfe algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb.
            2020). arXiv: 2002.04320 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</spa= n>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Projection-free optimization via
                different variants of the Frank-Wolfe (FW) method has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">become one of the cornerstones in =
                    optimization for machine learning since in many
                    cases the linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">minimization oracle is much cheape=
                    r to implement than projections and some
                    sparsity needs to be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preserved. In a number of applicat=
                    ions, e.g. Poisson inverse problems or quantum
                    state tomography,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the loss is given by a self-concor=
                    dant (SC) function having unbounded curvature,
                    implying absence of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">theoretical guarantees for the exi=
                    sting FW methods. We use the theory of SC
                    functions to provide a new
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adaptive step size for FW methods =
                    and prove global convergence rate </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"223" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S1">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mfrac>
                                        <mjx-frac>
                                            <= mjx-num>
                                                <mjx-nstrut></mjx-nstrut>
                                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-=n">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mn>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                </mjx-num>
                                                <mjx-dbox=>
                                                    <mjx-dtable>
                                                        <mjx-line></mjx-line>
                                                        <mjx-row>
                                                            <mjx-den>
                                                                <mjx-dstrut>
                                                                    </mjx-dstru= t>
                                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                            </mjx-den>
                                                        </mjx-row>
                                                    </mjx-dtable>
                                                    </mjx-db= ox>
                                        </mjx-frac>
                                    </mjx-mfrac>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-clo=se">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>=
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"ht=tp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mfrac>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>=
                                                k</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </mfrac>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                            </ma= th>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"224" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>k=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">being the iteration counter. If the probl=
                    em can be represented by a local linear
                    minimization oracle,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we are the first to propose a FW m=
                    ethod with linear convergence rate without
                    assuming neither strong
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convexity nor a Lipschitz continuo=
                    us gradient.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Edwars2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-155" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Edwars, James Alexander. =E2=80=9CStudy of f=
            ine-grained, irregular parallel applications on a
            many-core
            processor=E2=80=9D. PhD thesis. University of Maryland, Institu=
            te for Advanced Computer Studies,
            Department of Electrical, and Computer Engineering, 2020. <span= class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">url</span></span>: <a
                    href==3D"https://drum.lib.umd.edu/bitstream/handle/1903/26626/Edwards_umd_0117E_=21139.pdf"
                    class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://drum.lib.umd.edu/b=
                        itstream/handle/1903/26626/Edwards_umd_0117E_21139.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This dissertation demonstrates
                the possibility of obtaining strong speedups for a variety
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of parallel applications versus th=
                    e best serial and parallel implementations on
                    commodity platforms.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">These results were obtained using =
                    the PRAM-inspired Explicit Multi-Threading
                    (XMT) many-core
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing platform, which is desig=
                    ned to efficiently support execution of both
                    serial and parallel code
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and switching between the two.</sp= an>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ek2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-156" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ek, David and Anders Forsgren. =E2=80=9CAppr=
            oximate solution of system of equations arising
            in interior-point methods for bound-constrained optimization=E2=
            =80=9D. In: (Apr. 2020). arXiv:
            2004.04057v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The focus in this paper is
                interior-point methods for bound-constrained nonlinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization where the system of n=
                    onlinear equations that arise are solved with
                    Newton=E2=80=99s method.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">There is a trade-off between solvi=
                    ng Newton systems directly, which give high
                    quality solutions, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving many approximate Newton sy=
                    stems which are computationally less expensive
                    but give lower
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quality solutions. We propose part=
                    ial and full approximate solutions to the
                    Newton systems, which in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">general involves solving a reduced=
                    system of linear equations. The specific
                    approximate solution and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">size of the reduced system that ne=
                    eds to be solved at each iteration are
                    determined by estimates of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">active and inactive constraints at=
                    the solution. These sets are at each iteration
                    estimated by a simple
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">heuristic. In addition, we motivat=
                    e and suggest two modified-Newton approaches
                    which are based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on an intermediate step that consi=
                    sts of the partial approximate solutions. The
                    theoretical setting is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">introduced and asymptotic error bo=
                    unds are given along with numerical results for
                    bound-constrained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex quadratic optimization prob=
                    lems, both random and from the CUTEst test
                    collection.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Elekes2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
