    <dd id=3D"bib-157" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Elekes, Marton et al. =E2=80=9CA GraphBLAS s=
            olution to the SIGMOD 2014 Programming Contest
            using multi-source BFS=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Pr=
                oceedings of the 2020 IEEE High Performance Extreme
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computing Conference</span>. HPE=
            C 2020. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The GraphBLAS standard defines a
                set of fundamental building blocks for formulating graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms in the language of line=
                    ar algebra. Since its first release in 2017,
                    the expressivity of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GraphBLAS API and the performance =
                    of its implementations (such as
                    SuiteSparse:GraphBLAS) have
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">been studied on a number of textbo=
                    ok graph algorithms such as BFS, single-source
                    shortest path, and

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">connected components. However, les=
                    s attention was devoted to other aspects of
                    graph processing such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as handling typed and attributed g=
                    raphs (also known as property graphs), and
                    making use of complex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph query techniques (handling p=
                    aths, aggregation, and filtering). To study
                    these problems in more
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detail, we have used GraphBLAS to =
                    solve the case study of the 2014 SIGMOD
                    Programming Contest,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which defines complex graph proces=
                    sing tasks that require a diverse set of
                    operations. Our solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">makes heavy use of multi-source BF=
                    S algorithms expressed as sparse matrix-matrix
                    multiplications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">along with other GraphBLAS techniq=
                    ues such as masking and submatrix extraction.
                    While the queries
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be formulated in GraphBLAS con=
                    cisely, our performance evaluation shows mixed
                    results. For some
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">queries and data sets, the perform=
                    ance is competitive with the hand-optimized top
                    solutions submitted
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to the contest, however, in some c=
                    ases, it is currently outperformed by orders of
                    magnitude.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ellis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-158" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ellis, Marquita May. =E2=80=9CParallelizing =
            Irregular Applications for Distributed Memory
            Scalability:
            Case Studies from
            Genomics=E2=80=9D. PhD thesis. University of California, Berkel=
            ey, 2020. eprint: EECS-2020-133. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2020/EE=CS-2020-133.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www2.eecs.be=
                    rkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2020/EECS-2020-133.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Generalizable approaches, models,
                and frameworks for irregular application scalability is an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">old yet open area in parallel and =
                    distributed computing research. Irregular
                    applications are particularly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hard to parallelize and distribute=
                    because, by definition, the pattern of
                    computation is dependent upon
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the input data. With the prolifera=
                    tion of data-driven and data-intensive
                    applications from the realm of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Big Data, and the increasing deman=
                    d for and availability of large-scale computing
                    resources through
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">HPC-Cloud convergence, the importa=
                    nce of generalized approaches to achieving
                    irregular application
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalability is only growing.</span=><br class=3D"newline"><span
                        class=3D"cmr-9">Rather than offering another s=
                        oftware language or framework, this dissertation argues we
                        first need to
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">understand application scalability=
                        , especially irregular application scalability,
                        and more closely examine
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">patterns of computation, data shar=
                        ing, and dependencies. As it stands,
                        predominant performance
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">models and tools from parallel and=
                        distributed computing focus on applications
                        that are divided into
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distinct communication and computa=
                        tion phases, and ignore issues related to
                        memory utilization.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">While time-tested and valuable, th=
                        ese models are not always sucient for
                        understanding full application
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalability, particularly, the sca=
                        lability of data-intensive irregular
                        applications. We present application
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">case studies from genomics, highli=
                        ghting the interdependencies of communication,
                        computation, and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory capacities and performance.=
                    </span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">The genomics application=
                        s we will examine offer a particularly useful and practical vantage
                        point
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for this analysis, as they are dat=
                        a-intensive irregular application targets for
                        both HPC and cloud
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing. Further, they present a=
                        n extreme for both domains. For HPC, they are
                        less akin to
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">traditional, well-studied and well=
                        -supported scientific simulations and more akin
                        to text and document
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis applications. For cloud c=
                        omputing, they are an extreme in that they
                        require frequent random
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">global access to memory and data, =
                        stressing interconnection network latency and
                        bandwidth and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">co-scheduled processors for tightl=
                        y orchestrated computation.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-=9">We show how
                        common patterns of irregular all-to-all computation can
                        be managed eciently, comparing
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bulk-synchronous approaches built =
                        on collective communication and asynchronous
                        approaches based on
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">one-sided communication. For the f=
                        ormer, our work is based on the popular Message
                        Passing Interface
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(MPI) and makes heavy use of globa=
                        lly collective communication operations that
                        exchange data across
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processors in a single step or, to=
                        save memory use, in a set of irregular steps.
                        For the latter, we build
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the UPC++ programming framework=
                        , which provides lightweight RPC mechanisms, to
                        transfer
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both data and computational work b=
                        etween processors. We present performance
                        results across multiple
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">platforms including several modern=
                        HPC systems and, at least in one case, a cloud
                        computing platform.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">With these application case studie=
                        s, we seek not only to contribute to
                        discussions around parallel
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm and data structure desig=
                        n, programming systems, and performance
                        modeling within the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel computing community, but =
                        also to contribute to broader work in genomics
                        through software
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">development and analysis. Thus, we=
                        develop and present the first distributed
                        memory scalable software
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for analyzing data sets from the l=
                        atest generation of sequencing technologies,
                        known as long read data
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sets. Specifically, we present sca=
                        lable solutions to the problem of many-to-many
                        long read overlap and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">alignment, the computational bottl=
                        eneck to long read assembly, error correction,
                        and direct analysis.

                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Through cross-architectural empiri=
                        cal analysis, we identify the key components to
                        ecient scalability,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and highlight the priorities for a=
                        ny future optimization with analytical
                        models.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Engelmann2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-159" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Engelmann, Alexander et al. =E2=80=9CALADIN-
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"225"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FC TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </m= jx-mi>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>=
                            =CE=B1</mi>
                    </math>
                </mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container>
            =E2=80=93 An open-source MATLAB toolbox for distributed non-con=
            vex optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2020).
            arXiv: 2006.01866v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[eess.SY]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper introduces an
                open-source software for distributed and decentralized non-c=
                onvex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization named ALADIN-</span>
                <= !-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CH=TML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"226" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; po=sition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi= class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FC TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-m= ath>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>=CE=B1</mi>
                                        <= /math>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ALADIN-</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-c= ontainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                        tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"227" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">=
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FC TEX-I">
                                    </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>=CE=B1</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is a MATLAB implementation of the Augment=
                                ed Lagrangian Alternating Direction Inexact
                                Newton
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(ALADIN) algorithm, which is tailo=
                                red towards rapid prototyping for non-convex
                                distributed optimization.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">An improved version of the recentl=
                                y proposed bi-level variant of ALADIN is
                                included enabling decentralized
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-convex optimization. A collect=
                                ion of application examples from different
                                applications fields including
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">chemical engineering, robotics, an=
                                d power systems underpins the application
                                potential of ALADIN-</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container clas=s=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_cou=nter=3D"228" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math cla=ss=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c1D6FC TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselecta=ble=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Ma=thML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>=CE=B1</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-co= ntainer><span class=3D"cmr-9">.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Eswar2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-160" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Eswar, S. et al. =E2=80=9CDistributed-Memory=
            Parallel Symmetric Nonnegative Matrix Factorization=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 Internation=
                al Conference for
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">High Performance Computing, Netw=
                orking, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC =E2=80=9920.
            Los Alamitos, CA,
            USA: IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 1041=E2=80=931054. <= span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.11=
                09/SC41405.2020.00078.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                    <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020=.00078"
                            class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputersocie=
                                ty.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00078</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We develop the first
                distributed-memory parallel implementation of Symmetric Nonn=
                egative
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Matrix Factorization (SymNMF), a k=
                    ey data analytics kernel for clustering and
                    dimensionality
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reduction. Our implementation incl=
                    udes two different algorithms for SymNMF, which
                    give comparable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results in terms of time and accur=
                    acy. The first algorithm is a parallelization
                    of an existing sequential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approach that uses solvers for non=
                    symmetric NMF. The second algorithm is a novel
                    approach based on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Gauss-Newton method. It exploi=
                    ts second-order information without incurring
                    large computational
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and memory costs. We evaluate the =
                    scalability of our algorithms on the Summit
                    system at Oak
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Ridge National Laboratory, scaling=
                    up to 128 nodes (4096 cores) with 70%
                    efficiency. Additionally, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate our software on an ima=
                    ge segmentation task.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Facca2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-161" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Facca, Enrico and Michele Benzi. =E2=80=9CFa=
            st Iterative Solution of the Optimal Transport Problem
            on Graphs=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv: 2009.13478 <span c=lass=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we address the
                numerical solution of the Optimal Transport Problem on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">undirected weighted graphs, taking=
                    the shortest path distance as transport cost.
                    The optimal solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is obtained from the long-time lim=
                    it of the gradient descent dynamics. Among
                    different time stepping
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">procedures for the discretization =
                    of this dynamics, a backward Euler time
                    stepping scheme combined
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with the inexact Newton-Raphson me=
                    thod results in a robust and accurate approach
                    for the solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the Optimal Transport Problem o=
                    n graphs. It is found experimentally that the
                    algorithm requires
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving between </span>
                <!-- l. 30 =
-->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabin=dex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"229" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: re=lative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mj= x-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c29">
                                </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script"=>=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>1</mn>=
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mm= l>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"230" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i"=>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">=
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </m= jx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 TEX-I">
                                            <= /mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                        </= mjx-mn>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2E">
                                                </mj= x-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33">
                                                <= /mjx-c>
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c36"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-= mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi ma=thvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-ope=n">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>M</mi>
                                    <mn>0</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">.</mo>
                                    <mn>3=
                                        6</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow></math></mjx-assist= ive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">linear systems involving weighted Laplaci=
                    an matrices, where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"231" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 TEX-=I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                            <mi>M</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the number of edges. These linear syst=
                    ems are solved via algebraic multigrid methods,
                    resulting in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an efficient solver for the Optima=
                    l Transport Problem on graphs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Fan2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-162" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Fan, Tiffany et al. =E2=80=9CSpectrum-Adapte=
            d Polynomial Approximation for Matrix Functions with
            Applications in Graph Signal Processing=E2=80=9D. In: <span cla=ss=3D"cmti-10">Algorithms </span>13.11 (Nov.
            2020), p.
            295. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</s= pan></span>:
                10.3390/a13110295.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">e propose and investigate two new
                methods to approximate </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"232" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mr= ow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-= mi>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=29"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D44F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>f</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <m= row>
                                    <mi>A</mi>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                            <mi>b</mi>
                            <= /math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">for large, sparse, Hermitian matrices </s= pan><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"233" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <= /mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>A</mi>
                                </= math>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Computations of this form play an =
                        important role in numerous signal processing
                        and machine learning

                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tasks. The main idea behind both m=
                        ethods is to first estimate the spectral
                        density of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"Math=Jax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"2=34" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX=-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4=34 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on=" display=3D" inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" dis=play=3D"inline">
                                <mi>A</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span=class=3D"cmr-9">,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and then find polynomials of a fix=
                        ed order that better approximate the function f
                        on areas of the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">spectrum with a higher density of =
                        eigenvalues. Compared to state-of-the-art
                        methods such as the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Lanczos method and truncated Cheby=
                        shev expansion, the proposed methods tend to
                        provide more
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accurate approximations of </span>=
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"C=HTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"235" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; p=osition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-m= row space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>f</mi>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo clas=s=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>A</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-cl=ose">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                                <mi>b</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">at lower polynomial orders, and for matri=
                        ces </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_=0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"236" style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mj= x-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                            <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>A<= /mi></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">with a large number of distinct interior =
                        eigenvalues and a small spectral width. We also
                        explore the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application of these techniques to=
                        (i) fast estimation of the norms of localized
                        graph spectral filter
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dictionary atoms, and (ii) fast fi=
                        ltering of time-vertex signals.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Fang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-163" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Fang, Jianbin et al. =E2=80=9CParallel Progr=
            amming Models for Heterogeneous Many-Cores : A Survey=E2=80=9D.
            In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.04094v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs=
                .DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Heterogeneous many-cores are now
                an integral part of modern computing systems ranging
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from embedding systems to supercom=
                    puters. While heterogeneous many-core design
                    offers the potential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for energy-efficient high-performa=
                    nce, such potential can only be unlocked if the
                    application programs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are suitably parallel and can be m=
                    ade to match the underlying heterogeneous
                    platform. In this article,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we provide a comprehensive survey =
                    for parallel programming models for
                    heterogeneous many-core
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architectures and review the compi=
                    ling techniques of improving programmability
                    and portability. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">examine various software optimizat=
                    ion techniques for minimizing the communicating
                    overhead between
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">heterogeneous computing devices. W=
                    e provide a road map for a wide variety of
                    different research areas.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We conclude with a discussion on o=
                    pen issues in the area and potential research
                    directions. This article
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provides both an accessible introd=
                    uction to the fast-moving area of heterogeneous
                    programming and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a detailed bibliography of its mai=
                    n achievements.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Fang2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-164" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Fang, Zhuangyan et al. =E2=80=9CLow Rank Dir=
            ected Acyclic Graphs and Causal Structure Learning=E2=80=9D.
            In: (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.05691v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[c=
                s.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Despite several important
                advances in recent years, learning causal structures represe=
                nted
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) =
                    remains a challenging task in high dimensional
                    settings when
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the graphs to be learned are not s=
                    parse. In particular, the recent formulation of
                    structure learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as a continuous optimization probl=
                    em proved to have considerable advantages over
                    the traditional
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">combinatorial formulation, but the=
                    performance of the resulting algorithms is
                    still wanting when the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">target graph is relatively large a=
                    nd dense. In this paper we propose a novel
                    approach to mitigate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this problem, by exploiting a low =
                    rank assumption regarding the (weighted)
                    adjacency matrix of a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DAG causal model. We establish sev=
                    eral useful results relating interpretable
                    graphical conditions to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the low rank assumption, and show =
                    how to adapt existing methods for causal
                    structure learning to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">take advantage of this assumption.=
                    We also provide empirical evidence for the
                    utility of our low rank
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms, especially on graphs t=
                    hat are not sparse. Not only do they outperform
                    state-of-the-art
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms when the low rank condi=
                    tion is satisfied, the performance on randomly
                    generated scale-free
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graphs is also very competitive ev=
                    en though the true ranks may not be as low as
                    is assumed.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Farhan2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-165" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Farhan, Mohammed Al et al. =E2=80=9CMAGMA te=
            mplates for scalable linear algebra on emerging
            architectures=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">The Interna=
                tional Journal of High Performance Computing
                Applications</span>
            (July 2020), p. 109434202093842. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <span= class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>:
            10.1177/1094342020938421.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">With the acquisition and
                widespread use of more resources that rely on accelerator/wi=
                de
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vector-based computing, there has =
                    been a strong demand for science and
                    engineering applications to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">take advantage of these latest ass=
                    ets. This, however, has been extremely
                    challenging due to the diversity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of systems to support their extrem=
                    e concurrency, complex memory hierarchies,
                    costly data movement,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and heterogeneous node architectur=
                    es. To address these challenges, we design a
                    programming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model and describe its ease of use=
                    in the development of a new MAGMA Templates
                    library that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">delivers high-performance scalable=
                    linear algebra portable on current and
                    emerging architectures.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">MAGMA Templates derives its perfor=
                    mance and portability by (1) building on
                    existing state-of-the-art

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear algebra libraries, like MAG=
                    MA, SLATE, Trilinos, and vendor-optimized math
                    libraries, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(2) providing access (seamlessly t=
                    o the users) to the latest algorithms and
                    architecture-specific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimizations through a single, ea=
                    sy-to-use C++-based API.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Fasi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-166" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Fasi, Massimiliano and Nicholas Higham. =E2=
            =80=9CGenerating extreme-scale matrices with specified
            singular values or condition numbers=E2=80=9D. In: (2020). <spa= n class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">issn</span></span>: 1749-90=
                97. MIMS EPrint: MIMS
                EPrint:2020.8. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">url</span></span>: <a
                    href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/275=5/1/fahi20.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                        class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths.=
                        manchester.ac.uk/2755/1/fahi20.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A widely used form of test matrix
                is the randsvd matrix constructed as the product </span>
                <!--=
 l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML=" tabindex=3D" 0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"237" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posit=ion: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi cl=ass=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                            </= mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D448 TEX-I="></mjx-c><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c1D6F4 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D=449 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>A</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                            <mi m=athvariant=3D"italic">U=CE=A3V</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</m= o>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"238" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D448 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>U</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            <= /mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"239" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D449 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>V</mi>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-cont= ainer>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">are random orthogonal or unitary matrices=
                                        from the Haar distribution and </span>
                                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"240" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6F4 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </m= jx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                            <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>=
                                                    =CE=A3</mi>
                                            </math>
                                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">is a diagonal matrix of singular values. =
                                        Such matrices are random but have a specified
                                        singular value
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distribution. The cost of forming =
                                        an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0=" jax=3D" CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"241" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx= -mi>
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=D7"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>m</mi>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                                <mi>=
                                                    n</mi>
                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">randsvd matrix is </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"242" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx=-n">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>=
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            </mjx-mat= h>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"=http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mi>m</mi>
                                                    <mn>3</mn>=
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                                    <mi>n</mi>
                                                    <mn>3</mn>
                                                </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">flops, which is prohibitively expensive a=
                                    t extreme scale; moreover, the randsvd
                                    construction requires a
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significant amount of communicatio=
                                    n, making it unsuitable for distributed memory
                                    environments. By
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dropping the requirement that </sp= an><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"243" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                            <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D448 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                <= /mjx-math>
                                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                            x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                            display=3D"inline">
                                                            <mi>U</mi>
                                                </= math>
                                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"244" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D449 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>V</mi>
                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-cont= ainer>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">be Haar distributed and that both be rand=
                                            om, we derive new algorithms for forming
                                        </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"245" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I">
                                                        </mj= x-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline="><mi>A</mi></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D" cmr-9">that have cost linear in the number of ma=
                                                    trix elements and require a low amount of
                                                    communication and
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">synchronization. We specialize the=
                                    se algorithms to generating matrices with
                                    specified 2-norm condition
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number. Numerical experiments show=
                                    that the algorithms have excellent efficiency
                                    and scalability.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Fasi2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-167" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Fasi, Massimiliano and Nicholas J. Higham. =
            =E2=80=9CMatrices with Tunable Infinity-Norm Condition
            Number and No Need for Pivoting in LU Factorization=E2=80=9D. I=
            n: (2020). MIMS EPrint: MIMS
            EPrint:2020.17. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-c=aps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/id=/eprint/2775" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths.ma=
                    nchester.ac.uk/id/eprint/2775</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We propose a two-parameter family
                of nonsymmetric dense </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container cl=ass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_c=ounter=3D"246" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math c=lass=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin=" space=3D" 3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mj=x-i" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-m= ath>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>n</mi>
                                <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                <mi>n</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx= -container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-contai= ner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxt=menu_counter=3D"247" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-= math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo c=lass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mj= x-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FC TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </= mjx-mi>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C">
                                    </mj= x-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FD=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close"=>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assis= tive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w=3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>A</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=CE=B1</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>=
                                    <mi>=CE=B2</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></m= jx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">for which LU factorization without pivoti=
                    ng is numerically stable, and we show how to
                    choose </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax=CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"248"=style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX=" aria-hidden=3D" true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FC T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" di=splay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi>=CE=B1</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"249" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D6FD TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>=CE=B2</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx= -container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">to achieve any value of the </span>
                    <!-- l=
. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"250" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positio=n: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c221E"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-ass= istive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www=.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>=E2=88=9E</mi>
                            </math></mjx= -assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-norm
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">condition number. The matrix
            </spa= n><!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D="CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"251"
                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;=position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                    <mjx= -mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx= -mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c1D6FC TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                    <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"2"=>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FD TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo clas=s=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx= -mrow>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>A=
                        </mi>
                        <mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                            <mrow>
                                <mi>=CE=B1</mi>
                                <mo clas=s=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                <mi>=CE=B2</mi>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-clo=se">)</mo>
                        </mrow>
                    </math>
                </mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">can be formed from a simple formula in </= span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"252" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <= mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-c=lose">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class==3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>n</mi>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">flops. The matrix is suitable for use in =
                    the HPL-AI Mixed-Precision Benchmark, which
                    requires an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extreme scale test matrix (dimensi=
                    on </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0=" jax=3D" CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"253" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx= -mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=3E"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c31"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c37">
                                    </= mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D="inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inli=ne">
                            <mi>n</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">&gt;</mo>
                            <mn>107</mn>
                        </math></mjx= -assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that has a controlled condition nu=
                    mber and can be safely used in LU factorization
                    without pivoting.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">It is also of interest as a genera=
                    l-purpose test matrix.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Fasi2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-168" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Fasi, Massimiliano, Nicholas J. Higham,
            et al. =E2=80=9CNumerical Behavior of the NVIDIA Tensor Cores=
            =E2=80=9D. In: (2020). MIMS EPrint: MIMS
            EPrint:2020.10. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-c=aps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/27=61/1/fhms20.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths=
                    .manchester.ac.uk/2761/1/fhms20.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We explore the floating-point
                arithmetic used by the NVIDIA Volta tensor cores, which are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware accelerators for mixed-pr=
                    ecision matrix multiplication. We investigate
                    what precision is used
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for intermediate results, whether =
                    subnormal numbers are supported, what rounding
                    mode is used, in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which order the operations in the =
                    dot products arising in the matrix
                    multiplication are performed,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and whether partial sums are norma=
                    lized. These aspects are not documented by
                    NVIDIA, and we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gain insight by running carefully =
                    designed numerical experiments on these
                    hardware accelerators.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Knowing the answers to these quest=
                    ions is important if one wishes to: 1) build
                    hardware that computes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a matrix-matrix product matching t=
                    he results of NVIDIA tensor cores; 2) achieve
                    bit-reproducible
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results when designing on conventi=
                    onal hardware with IEEE 754 floating point
                    arithmetic code meant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to run on NVIDIA tensor cores; and=
                    3) understand the differences between results
                    produced by code

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that utilizes tensor cores and cod=
                    e that uses only IEEE 754-compliant arithmetic
                    operations. As an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">additional result, we point out a =
                    non-monotonicity issue that arises in
                    floating-point multi-operand
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">addition without the normalization=
                    of the intermediate results.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Favaro2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-169" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Favaro, Federico et al. =E2=80=9CExploring F=
            PGA Optimizations to Compute Sparse Numerical Linear
            Algebra Kernels=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Applied R=
                econfigurable Computing.
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Architectures, Tools, and Applic=
                ations</span>. Springer International
            Publishing, 2020, pp. 258=E2=80=93268.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1007/978-3-030-44534-8=CB=9920.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The solution of sparse triangular
                linear systems (sptrsv) is the bottleneck of many numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods. Thus, it is crucial to co=
