                    unt with efficient implementations of such
                    kernel, at least for commonly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">used platforms. In this sense, Fie=
                    ld=E2=80=93Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) have
                    evolved greatly in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the last years, entering the HPC h=
                    ardware ecosystem largely due to their superior
                    energy=E2=80=93efficiency
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">relative to more established accel=
                    erators. Up until recently, the design for
                    FPGAs implied the use of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">low=E2=80=93level Hardware Descrip=
                    tion Languages (HDL) such as VHDL or Verilog. Nowadays,
                    manufacturers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are making a large effort to adopt=
                    High=E2=80=93Level Synthesis languages like C/C++ or
                    OpenCL, but
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the gap between their performance =
                    and that of HDLs is not yet fully studied. This
                    work focuses
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the performance offered by FPGA=
                    s to compute the sptrsv using OpenCL. For this
                    purpose,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we implement different parallel va=
                    riants of this kernel and experimentally
                    evaluate several setups,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">varying among others the work=E2=
                    =80=93group size, the number of compute units, the
                    unroll=E2=80=93factor and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vectorization=E2=80=93factor.</spa= n>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Fegaras2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-170" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Fegaras, Leonidas and Md Hasanuzzaman Noor. =
            =E2=80=9CTranslation of Array-Based Loops to
            Distributed Data-Parallel Programs=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). a=
            rXiv: 2003.09769v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DB]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Large volumes of data generated
                by scientific experiments and simulations come in the form
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of arrays, while programs that ana=
                    lyze these data are frequently expressed in
                    terms of array operations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in an imperative, loop-based langu=
                    age. But, as datasets grow larger, new
                    frameworks in distributed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Big Data analytics have become ess=
                    ential tools to large-scale scientific
                    computing. Scientists, who
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are typically comfortable with num=
                    erical analysis tools but are not familiar with
                    the intricacies of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Big Data analytics, must now learn=
                    to convert their loop-based programs to
                    distributed data-parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programs. We present a novel frame=
                    work for translating programs expressed as
                    array-based loops to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed data parallel programs=
                    that is more general and efficient than
                    related work. Although our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">translations are over sparse array=
                    s, we extend our framework to handle packed
                    arrays, such as tiled
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, without sacrificing perf=
                    ormance. We report on a prototype
                    implementation on top of Spark
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and evaluate the performance of ou=
                    r system relative to hand-written
                    programs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Feng2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-171" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Feng, Z. =E2=80=9CGRASS: GRAph Spectral Spar=
            sification Leveraging Scalable Spectral Perturbation
            Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE Transaction=
                s on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Systems </span>(2020), pp. 1=E2=
            =80=931. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">issn</span></s= pan>: 1937-4151. <span
                    class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</s= pan></span>: 10.1109/TCAD.2020.2968543.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Spectral graph sparsification
                aims to find ultra-sparse subgraphs whose Laplacian matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can well approximate the original =
                    Laplacian eigenvalues and eigenvectors. In
                    recent years, spectral
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparsification techniques have bee=
                    n extensively studied for accelerating various
                    numerical and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph-related applications. Prior =
                    nearly-linear-time spectral sparsification
                    methods first extract
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">low-stretch spanning tree from the=
                    original graph to form the backbone of the
                    sparsifier, and then
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">recover small portions of spectral=
                    ly-critical off-tree edges to the spanning tree
                    to significantly improve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the approximation quality. However=
                    , it is not clear how many off-tree edges
                    should be recovered for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">achieving a desired spectral simil=
                    arity level within the sparsifier. Motivated by
                    recent graph signal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processing techniques, this paper =
                    proposes a similarity-aware spectral graph
                    sparsification framework
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that leverages efficient spectral =
                    off-tree edge embedding and filtering schemes
                    to construct spectral
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparsifiers with guaranteed spectr=
                    al similarity (relative condition number)
                    level. An iterative graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">densification scheme is also intro=
                    duced to facilitate efficient and effective
                    filtering of off-tree edges
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for highly ill-conditioned problem=
                    s. The proposed method has been validated using
                    various kinds of

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graphs obtained from public domain=
                    sparse matrix collections relevant to VLSI
                    CAD, finite element
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis, as well as social and da=
                    ta networks frequently studied in many machine
                    learning and data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mining applications. For instance,=
                    a sparse SDD matrix with 40 million unknowns
                    and 180 million
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nonzeros can be solved (1E-3 accur=
                    acy level) within two minutes using a single
                    CPU core and about
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">6GB memory.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Frandsen2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-172" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Frandsen, Abraham and Rong Ge. =E2=80=9COpti=
            mization landscape of Tucker decomposition=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Mathematical Programming </span>(June 2=
            020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>=
            : 10.1007/s10107-020-01531-z.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Tucker decomposition is a popular
                technique for many data analysis and machine learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications. Finding a Tucker dec=
                    omposition is a nonconvex optimization problem.
                    As the scale of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems increases, local search a=
                    lgorithms such as stochastic gradient descent
                    have become popular
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in practice. In this paper, we cha=
                    racterize the optimization landscape of the
                    Tucker decomposition
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem. In particular, we show th=
                    at if the tensor has an exact Tucker
                    decomposition, for a standard
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nonconvex objective of Tucker deco=
                    mposition, all local minima are also globally
                    optimal. We also give
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a local search algorithm that can =
                    find an approximate local (and global) optimal
                    solution in polynomial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gaihre2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-173" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gaihre, Anil, Xiaoye S. Li, and Hang Liu. =
            =E2=80=9CGSoFa: Scalable Sparse LU Symbolic Factorization
            on GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.00840v2 <span cl=ass=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Decomposing a matrix A into a
                lower matrix L and an upper matrix U, which is also known
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as LU decomposition, is an importa=
                    nt operation in numerical linear algebra. For a
                    sparse matrix, LU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">decomposition often introduces mor=
                    e nonzero entries in the L and U factors than
                    the original matrix.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Symbolic factorization step is nee=
                    ded to identify the nonzero structures of L and
                    U matrices. Attracted
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by the enormous potentials of Grap=
                    hics Processing Units (GPUs), an array of
                    efforts has surged to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">deploy various steps of LU factori=
                    zation on GPUs except, to the best of our
                    knowledge, symbolic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization.This paper introduce=
                    s GSoFa, a GPU based Symbolic factorization
                    design with the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">following three optimizations to e=
                    nable scalable LU symbolic factorization for
                    nonsymmetric pattern
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse matrices on GPUs. First, we=
                    introduce a novel fine-grained parallel
                    symbolic factorization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm that is well suited for =
                    the Single Instruction Multiple Thread (SIMT)
                    architecture of GPUs.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Second, we propose multi-source co=
                    ncurrent symbolic factorization to improve the
                    utilization of GPUs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with focus on balancing the worklo=
                    ad. Third, we introduce a three-pronged
                    optimization to reduce the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">excessive space requirement faced =
                    by multi-source concurrent symbolic
                    factorization. Taken together,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this work scales LU symbolic facto=
                    rization towards 1,000 GPUs with superior
                    performance over the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art CPU algorithm.</s= pan>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gao2020c" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-174" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gao, G. et al. =E2=80=9CDistributed
            Quasi-Newton Derivative-Free Optimization Method for Optimizati=
            on Problems with Multiple
            Local Optima=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Conference P=
                roceedings, ECMOR</span>. ECMOR XVII. 2020, pp. 1=E2=80=9322.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>:
                    <a href=3D"https://www.earthdoc.org/content/papers/10.3997/2214=-4609.202035131" class=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.earthdoc=
                            .org/content/papers/10.3997/2214-4609.202035131</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">For highly nonlinear problems,
                the objective function f(x) may have multiple local optima
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and it is desired to locate all of=
                    them. Analytical or adjoint-based derivatives
                    may not be available for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">most real optimization problems, e=
                    specially, when responses of a system are
                    predicted by numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simulations. The distributed-Gauss=
                    -Newton (DGN) optimization method performs
                    quite efficiently
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and robustly for history-matching =
                    problems with multiple best matches. However,
                    this method is not
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applicable for generic optimizatio=
                    n problems, e.g., life-cycle production
                    optimization or well location
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization.</span><br class=3D"n=ewline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this
                    paper, we
                    generalized the distribution techniques of the DGN optimizat=
                    ion method and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developed a new distributed quasi-=
                    Newton (DQN) optimization method that is
                    applicable to generic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization problems. It can hand=
                    le generalized objective functions </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"254" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-punc">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" s=pace=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D466 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mrow spa=ce=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                                </= mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close"=>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </m= jx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mr= ow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-= mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>F</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <m= row>
                                    <mi>x</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                    <mi>y</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class==3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>x</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-clo=se">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo clas=s=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                            <mi>f</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(<= /mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>x</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                            </mat= h>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">with both explicit variables x and implic=
                    it variables, i.e., simulated responses,
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"255" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D466 TEX-I">
                                </mj= x-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                    </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-= mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>y<= /mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>x</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo cla=ss=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-contai= ner><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The partial derivatives of </span>=
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"C=HTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"256" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; p=osition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-m= row space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                            <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c2C">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"2">
                                            <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D466 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-= mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                            <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>F<= /mi>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>x</mi>
                                                <mo class=3D"M=athClass-punc">,</mo>
                                                <mi>y</mi>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                            <= /mrow></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>

                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">with respect to both x and y can be compu=
                        ted analytically, whereas the partial
                        derivatives of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"=MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter==3D"257" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class==3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-c1D466 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mj=x-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mj= x-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </m= jx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                                                </= mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" disp=lay=3D"inline">
                                <mi>y</mi>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi=>x</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </math></mjx-assis= tive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">with respect to x (sensitivity matrix) is=
                        estimated by applying the same efficient
                        information sharing
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mechanism implemented in the DGN o=
                        ptimization method. An ensemble of quasi-Newton
                        optimization
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tasks is distributed among multipl=
                        e high-performance-computing (HPC) cluster
                        nodes. The simulation
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results generated from one optimiz=
                        ation task are shared with others by updating a
                        common set of
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">training data points, which record=
                        s simulated responses of all simulation jobs.
                        The sensitivity matrix at
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the current best solution of each =
                        optimization task is approximated by either the
                        linear-interpolation
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(LI) method or the support-vector-=
                        regression (SVR) method, using some or all
                        training data points.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The gradient of the objective func=
                        tion is then analytically computed using its
                        partial derivatives with
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">respect to x and y and the estimat=
                        ed sensitivities of y with respect to </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"258" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </m= jx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                            <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>x=
                                </mi>
                            </math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The Hessian is updated using the q=
                        uasi-Newton formulation. A new search point for
                        each distributed
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization task is generated by =
                        solving a quasi-Newton trust-region subproblem
                        for the next iteration.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span cl=ass=3D"cmr-9">The proposed DQN
                        method is first
                        validated on a synthetic history matching problem and its pe=
                        rformance
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is found to be comparable with the=
                        DGN optimizer. Then, the DQN method is tested
                        on different
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization problems. For all tes=
                        t problems, the DQN method can find multiple
                        optima of the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">objective function with reasonably=
                        small numbers of iterations (30 to 50).
                        Compared to sequential
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model-based derivative-free optimi=
                        zation methods, the DQN method can reduce the
                        computational
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cost, in terms of the number of si=
                        mulations required for convergence, by a factor
                        of 3 to 10.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gao2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-175" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gao, Jianhua et al. =E2=80=9CA Systematic Su=
            rvey of General Sparse Matrix-Matrix Multiplication=E2=80=9D.
            In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.11273 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.=
                DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">SpGEMM (General Sparse
                Matrix-Matrix Multiplication) has attracted much attention f=
                rom
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">researchers in fields of multigrid=
                    methods and graph analysis. Many optimization
                    techniques have been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developed for certain application =
                    fields and computing architecture over the
                    decades. The objective
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of this paper is to provide a stru=
                    ctured and comprehensive overview of the
                    research on SpGEMM.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Existing optimization techniques h=
                    ave been grouped into different categories
                    based on their target
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems and architectures. Covere=
                    d topics include SpGEMM applications, size
                    prediction of result
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix, matrix partitioning and lo=
                    ad balancing, result accumulating, and target
                    architecture-oriented
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization. The rationales of di=
                    fferent algorithms in each category are
                    analyzed, and a wide range of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SpGEMM algorithms are summarized. =
                    This survey sufficiently reveals the latest
                    progress and research
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">status of SpGEMM optimization from=
                    1977 to 2019. More specifically, an
                    experimentally comparative
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">study of existing implementations =
                    on CPU and GPU is presented. Based on our
                    findings, we highlight
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">future research directions and how=
                    future studies can leverage our findings to
                    encourage better design
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and implementation.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gao2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-176" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gao, Tianxiang et al. =E2=80=9CRandomized Br=
            egman Coordinate Descent Methods for Non-Lipschitz
            Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). arXiv: 2001.05202 <span= class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We propose a new </span><span class=3D"cmti-9">randomized=
                    Bregman (block) coordinate descent </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(RBCD) met=
                    hod for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">minimizing a composite problem, wh=
                    ere the objective function could be either
                    convex or nonconvex, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the smooth part are freed from the=
                    global Lipschitz-continuous (partial) gradient
                    assumption. Under
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the notion of relative smoothness =
                    based on the Bregman distance, we prove that
                    every limit point of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generated sequence is a stationary=
                    point. Further, we show that the iteration
                    complexity of the proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method is </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mj= x-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D="0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"259" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative=;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i"=>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                </mj= x-mo>
                <mjx-mrow>
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                            </= mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D700 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"m=jx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo=>
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx= -mn>
                </mjx-mrow>
                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c29"></mjx-c>
                </mjx-mo>
                </mjx-mrow>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>O</mi>
                        <mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                            <m= row>
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">n=F0=9D=9C=80</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin=">=E2=88=92</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mo class=3D"
                                    MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow=>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">to achieve </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-cont= ainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ct=xtmenu_counter=3D"260" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mj= x-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml= unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/19=98/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assist= ive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-stationary
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">point, where </span><!-- l. 30 -->=
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"261" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>n</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the number of blocks of coordinates. I=
                                f the objective is assumed to be convex, the
                                iteration complexity
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is improved to </span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -=
-><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabind=
ex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"262" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rel=
ative;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mi class=3D"m=
jx-i"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mrow space=3D=
"2"><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c=
></mjx-mo><mjx-mrow><mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-=
I"></mjx-c><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mo class=

jx-mo><mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>=
</mjx-mn></mjx-mrow><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close"><mjx-c class=3D=
"mjx-c29"></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-mrow></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselec=
table=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/=
MathML" display=3D"inline"><mi>O</mi><mrow><mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</=
mo><mrow><mi mathvariant=3D"italic">n=F0=9D=9C=96</mi><mo class=3D"MathClas=
s-bin">=E2=88=92</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo><=
/mrow></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">If, in addition, the objective is =
strongly convex (relative to the reference
               function), the global linear
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rate is recovered. We =
also present the accelerated version of the
               RBCD method, which
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">attains an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"263" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mrow space=3D"2="><mjx-mo class=3D" mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        <= /mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"=></mjx-c>
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D700 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>=
                            </mjx-mn>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D6FE TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Ma=thClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-m= ath>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>O</mi>
                                    <mrow>=
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">n=F0=9D=
                                                =9C=80</mi>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D="MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                            <mi>=CE=B3</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-=close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">iteration complexity for the convex case,=
                                where the scalar </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctxt=Menu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"264" styl=e=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ar=ia-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FE TEX-I="></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mo class=3D" mjx-n
                                            MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c2208">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5B"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mr= ow>
                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mn>
                                                <mjx-m= o class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>=
                                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-= mn>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=5D"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>=CE=B3</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=88=88<= /mo>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">[</mo>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                                        <mo class=3D"M=athClass-punc">,</mo>
                                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                                    </mrow>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">]</mo>
                                                    <= /mrow>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is determined by the </span><span class==3D"cmti-9">generalized
                                translation variant
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the Bregman distance. Convergen=
                                ce analysis

                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">without assuming the global Lipsch=
                                itz-continuous (partial) gradient sets our
                                results apart from the
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">existing works in the composite pr=
                                oblems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gao2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-177" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gao, Weiguo, Yingzhou Li, and Bichen Lu. =E2=
            =80=9CTriangularized Orthogonalization-free Method for
            Solving Extreme Eigenvalue Problems=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). a=
            rXiv: 2005.12161v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A novel orthogonalization-free
                method together with two specific algorithms are proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to solve extreme eigenvalue proble=
                    ms. On top of gradient-based algorithms, the
                    proposed algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">modify the multi-column gradient s=
                    uch that earlier columns are decoupled from
                    later ones. Global
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence to eigenvectors instea=
                    d of eigenspace is guaranteed almost surely.
                    Locally, algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">converge linearly with convergence=
                    rate depending on eigengaps. Momentum
                    acceleration, exact
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linesearch, and column locking are=
                    incorporated to further accelerate both
                    algorithms and reduce their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational costs. We demonstrat=
                    e the efficiency of both algorithms on several
                    random matrices
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with different spectrum distributi=
                    on and matrices from practice.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-GarciaGasulla2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-178" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Garcia-Gasulla, Marta
            et al. =E2=80=9CA generic performance analysis technique applie=
            dto different CFD methods for HPC=E2=80=9D.
            In: (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps"=>url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"https://perso.limsi.fr/tenaud/Files/cfd-in-h=pc_2020_accepted.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://perso.l=
                    imsi.fr/tenaud/Files/cfd-in-hpc_2020_accepted.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">For complex engineering and
                scientific applications, Computational Fluid Dynamics
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simulations (CFD) require a huge a=
                    mount of computational power. As such, it is of
                    paramount
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">importance to carefully assess the=
                    performance of CFD codes and to study them in
                    depth for enabling
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization and portability. In t=
                    his paper we study three complex CFD codes,
                    OpenFOAM, Alya
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and CHORUS representing two numeri=
                    cal methods, namely the finite volume and
                    finite element
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods, on both structured and un=
                    structured meshes. To all codes we apply a
                    generic performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis method based on a set of =
                    metrics helping the code developer in spotting
                    the critical points
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that can potentially limit the sca=
                    lability of a parallel application. We show the
                    root cause of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance bottlenecks studying t=
                    he three applications on the MareNostrum4
                    supercomputer. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conclude providing hints for impro=
                    ving the performance and the scalability of
                    each application.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Garmanjani2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-179" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Garmanjani, R. =E2=80=9CA note on the worst-=
            case complexity of nonlinear stepsize control methods
            for convex smooth unconstrained optimization=E2=80=9D. In: <spa= n class=3D"cmti-10">Optimization
                </span>(Oct. 2020),
                pp. 1=E2=80=9311. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>:
                10.1080/02331934.2020.1830088.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we analyse the
                worst-case complexity of nonlinear stepsize control (NSC)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms for solving convex smoo=
                    th unconstrained optimization problems. We show
                    that, to drive the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">norm of the gradient below some gi=
                    ven positive </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_=Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"265" style=3D"=font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hi=dden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I">
                                </m= jx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"=inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inlin=e">
                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span cl=ass=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such methods take at most </span>
                <= !-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CH=TML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"266" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; po=sition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi= class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mr= ow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space==3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n"=space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </m= jx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline="><math xmlns=3D"
                            http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi=>O</mi>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>=
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">iterations, which shows that the complexi=
                        ty bound for these methods is in parity with
                        that of gradient
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">descent methods for the same class=
                        of problems. As NSC algorithm is a
                        generalization of several
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods such as trust-region and a=
                        daptive cubic with regularization methods, such
                        bound holds
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automatically for these methods as=
                        well.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Georgakoudis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-180" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Georgakoudis, Giorgis et al. =E2=80=9CFAROS:=
            A Framework to Analyze OpenMP Compilation Through
            Benchmarking and Compiler Optimization Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <= span class=3D"cmti-10">OpenMP: Portable
                Multi-Level
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Parallelism on Modern Systems</s= pan>. Springer International
                    Publishing, 2020,
                    pp. 3=E2=80=9317. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>:
                    10.1007/978-3-030-58144-2=CB=991.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Compilers optimize OpenMP
                programs differently than their serial elision. Early outlin=
                ing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of parallel regions and invocation=
                    of parallel code via OpenMP runtime functions
                    are two of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">most profound differences. Underst=
                    anding the interplay between compiler
                    optimizations, OpenMP
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compilation, and application perfo=
                    rmance is hard and usually requires specialized
                    benchmarks and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compilation analysis tools.</span>=
                <br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">To
                    this end, we present FAROS, an extensible framework to autom=
                    ate and structure the analysis

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of compiler optimization of OpenMP=
                    programs. FAROS provides a generic
                    configuration interface to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">profile and analyze OpenMP applica=
                    tions with their native build configurations.
                    Using FAROS on a set
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of 39 OpenMP programs, including H=
                    PC applications and kernels, we show that
                    OpenMP compilation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hinders optimization for the major=
                    ity of programs. Comparing single-threaded
                    OpenMP execution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to its sequential counterpart, we =
                    observed slowdowns as much as 135.23%. In some
                    cases, however,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">OpenMP compilation speeds up execu=
                    tion as much as 25.48% when OpenMP semantics
                    help compiler
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization. Following analysis o=
                    n compiler optimization reports enables us to
                    pinpoint the reasons
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">without in-depth knowledge of the =
                    compiler. The information can be used to
                    improve compilers and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">also to bring performance on par t=
                    hrough manual code refactoring.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ginsbach2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-181" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ginsbach, Philip, Bruce Collie, and Michael =
            F. P. O=E2=80=99Boyle. =E2=80=9CAutomatically Harnessing Sparse
            Acceleration=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10="><span class=3D"
                small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1145/3377555.3377893. arXiv: 2001.07938 <span class=3D"cmtt-=10">[cs.PF]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse linear algebra is central
                to many scientific programs, yet compilers fail to optimize =
                it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">well. High-performance libraries a=
                    re available, but adoption costs are
                    significant. Moreover, libraries
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tie programs into vendor-specific =
                    software and hardware ecosystems, creating
                    non-portable code. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this paper, we develop a new appro=
                    ach based on our specification Language for
                    implementers of Linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Algebra Computations (LiLAC). Rath=
                    er than requiring the application developer to
                    (re)write every
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">program for a given library, the b=
                    urden is shifted to a one-off description by
                    the library implementer.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The LiLAC-enabled compiler uses th=
                    is to insert appropriate library routines
                    without source code
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">changes. LiLAC provides automatic =
                    data marshaling, maintaining state between
                    calls and minimizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data transfers. Appropriate places=
                    for library insertion are detected in compiler
                    intermediate representation,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">independent of source languages. W=
                    e evaluated on large-scale scientific
                    applications written in FORTRAN;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">standard C/C++ and FORTRAN benchma=
                    rks; and C++ graph analytics kernels. Across
                    heterogeneous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">platforms, applications and data s=
                    ets we show speedups of 1.1</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"267" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">to over 10</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"268" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive= -mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.or=g/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</= mo>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">without user intervention.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Goebel2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-182" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Goebel, Fritz et al. =E2=80=9CMultiprecision=
            Block-Jacobi for Iterative Triangular Solves=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Euro-Par 2020: Parallel Processing</spa= n>. Springer International Publishing,
                2020,
                pp. 546=E2=80=93560. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">doi</span></span>:
                10.1007/978-3-030-57675-2=CB=9934.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Recent research efforts have
                shown that Jacobi and block-Jacobi relaxation methods can be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">used as an effective and highly pa=
                    rallel approach for the solution of sparse
                    triangular linear systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arising in the application of ILU-=
                    type preconditioners. Simultaneously, a few
                    independent works
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have focused on designing efficien=
                    t high performance adaptive-precision
                    block-Jacobi preconditioning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(block-diagonal scaling), in the c=
                    ontext of the iterative solution of sparse
                    linear systems, on manycore
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architectures. In this paper, we b=
                    ridge the gap between relaxation methods based
                    on regular
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">splittings and preconditioners by =
                    demonstrating that iterative refinement can be
                    leveraged to construct
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a relaxation method from the preco=
                    nditioner. In addition, we exploit this insight
                    to construct
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a highly-efficient sparse triangul=
                    ar system solver for graphics processors that
                    combines iterative
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">refinement with the block-Jacobi p=
                    reconditioner available in the Ginkgo
                    library.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Goik2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-183" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Goik, Damian and Krzysztof Bana=C5=9B. =E2=
            =80=9CA Block Preconditioner for Scalable Large Scale Finite
            Element Incompressible Flow Simulations=E2=80=9D. In: <span cla=ss=3D"cmti-10">Lecture Notes in Computer
                Science</span>. Springer
            International Publishing, 2020, pp. 199=E2=80=93211. <span clas=s=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/978-3-=
            030-50420-5=CB=9915.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a block
                preconditioner, based on the algebraic multigrid method, for=
                solving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems of linear equations, that =
                    arise in incompressible flow simulations
                    performed by the stabilized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">finite element method. We select a=
                    set of adjustable parameters for the
                    preconditioner and show how
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to tune the parameters in order to=
                    obtain fast convergence of the standard GMRES
                    solver in which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the preconditioner is employed. Ad=
                    ditionally, we show some details of the
                    parallel implementation of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the preconditioner and the achieve=
                    d scalability of the solver in large scale
                    parallel incompressible flow
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simulations.</span>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Goli2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-184" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Goli, Mehdi et al. =E2=80=9CTowards Cross-Pl=
            atform Performance Portability ofDNN Models using
            SYCL=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2=
                020 IEEE/ACM International Workshop on Performance,
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Portability and Productivity in =
                HPC</span>. P3HPC =E2=80=9920 (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The incoming deployment of
                Exascale platforms with a myriad of different architectures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and co-processors have prompted th=
                    e need to provide a software ecosystem based on
                    open standards
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that can simplify maintaining HPC =
                    applications on different hardware.
                    Applications written for a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">particular platform should be port=
                    able to a different one, ensuring performance
                    is as close to the peak
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as possible. However, it is not ex=
                    pected that key performance routines on
                    relevant HPC applications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">will be performance portable as is=
                    , especially for common building blocks such as
                    BLAS or DNN.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The oneAPI the initiative aims to =
                    tackle this problem by combining a programming
                    model, SYCL,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with a set of interfaces for commo=
                    n building blocks that can be optimized for
                    different hardware
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vendors. In particular, oneAPI inc=
                    ludes the oneDNN performance library, which
                    contains building
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">blocks for deep learning applicati=
                    ons and frameworks. By using the SYCL
                    programming model, it can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integrate easily with existing SYC=
                    L and C++ applications, sharing data and
                    executing collaboratively
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on devices with the rest of the ap=
                    plication. In this paper, we introduce a cuDNN
                    backend for oneDNN,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which allows running oneAPI applic=
                    ations on NVIDIA hardware taking advantage of
                    existing building
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">blocks from the CUDA ecosystem. We=
                    implement relevant neural networks (ResNet-50
                    and VGG16) on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">native CUDA and also a version of =
                    oneAPI with a CUDA backend, and demonstrate
                    that performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">portability can be achieved by lev=
                    eraging existing building blocks for the target
                    hardware.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-GomezCrespo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-185" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">G=C3=B3mez Crespo, Constantino et al. <span=class=3D"cmti-10">Optimizing sparse
                matrix-vector
                multiplication in
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">NEC SX-Aurora vector engine</spa= n>. Tech. rep. Barcelona Supercomputing
                Centre,
                2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</= span></span>:
                    <a href=3D"http://hdl.handle.net/2117/192586" class=3D"url">
                        <sp= an class=3D"cmtt-10">http://hdl.handle.net/2117/192586</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse Matrix-Vector
                multiplication (SpMV) is an essential piece of code used in =
                many
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">High Performance Computing (HPC) a=
                    pplications. As previous literature shows,
                    achieving efficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vectorization and performance in m=
                    odern multi-core systems is nothing
                    straightforward. It is important
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">then to revisit the current stateo=
                    f-the-art matrix formats and optimizations to
                    be able to deliver deliver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high performance in long vector ar=
                    chitectures. In this tech-report, we describe
                    how to develop an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficient implementation that achi=
                    eves high throughput in the NEC Vector Engine:
                    a 256 element-long
