                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vector architecture. Combining sev=
                    eral pre-processing and kernel optimizations we
                    obtain an average
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">12% improvement over a base SELLC-=
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" j=ax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"269" style=3D"font-size: 11=7.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"=>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D70E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi=>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <math= xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>=CF=83=
                            </mi></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation on a heterogeneous set of =
                    24 matrices.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Goncalves2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-186" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Goncalves, M. M. et al. =E2=80=9CImproving S=
            elective Fault Tolerance in GPU Register Files by
            Relaxing
            Application Accuracy=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE=
                Transactions on Nuclear Science </span>(2020), pp.
            1=E2=80=931. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps=">issn</span></span>:
            1558-1578. <span class=3D" cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                        doi</span></span>: 10.1109/TNS.2020.2982162.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The high computing power of GPUs
                makes them attractive for safety-critical applications,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where reliability is a major conce=
                    rn. This paper uses an approximate computing
                    perspective to relax
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application accuracy in order to i=
                    mprove selective fault tolerance techniques.
                    Our approach first assesses
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the vulnerability of a Kepler GPU =
                    to transient effects through a neutron beam
                    experiment. Then, it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performs a fault injection campaig=
                    n to identify the most critical registers and
                    relaxes result accuracy.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Finally, it uses acquired data to =
                    improve selective fault tolerance techniques in
                    terms of occupation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and performance. Results show that=
                    it was possible to improve the GPU register
                    file=E2=80=99s reliability in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an average of 71.6% by relaxing ap=
                    plication accuracy and, when compared to
                    selective hardening
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques, it was able to reduce =
                    replicated registers by an average of 41.4%,
                    while maintaining 100%
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fault coverage.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gottesbueren2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-187" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gottesb=C3=BCren, Lars et al. =E2=80=9CScala=
            ble Shared-Memory Hypergraph Partitioning=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020).
            arXiv: 2010.10272 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Hypergraph partitioning is an
                important preprocessing step for optimizing data placement
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and minimizing communication volum=
                    es in high-performance computing applications.
                    To cope with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ever growing problem sizes, it has=
                    become increasingly important to develop fast
                    parallel partitioning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms whose solution quality =
                    is competitive with existing sequential
                    algorithms. To this end,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we present Mt-KaHyPar, the first s=
                    hared-memory multilevel hypergraph partitioner
                    with parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementations of many techniques=
                    used by the sequential, high-quality
                    partitioning systems: a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel coarsening algorithm that=
                    uses parallel community detection as guidance,
                    initial partitioning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">via parallel recursive bipartition=
                    ing with work-stealing, a scalable label
                    propagation refinement algorithm,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the first fully-parallel direc=
                    t </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"270" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tru=e">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-= mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <ma= th xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>k</m= i></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-way
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formulation of the classical FM al=
                    gorithm. Experiments performed on a large
                    benchmark set of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instances from various application=
                    domains demonstrate the scalability and
                    effectiveness of our approach.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">With 64 cores, we observe self-rel=
                    ative speedups of up to 51 and a harmonic mean
                    speedup of 23.5.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In terms of solution quality, we o=
                    utperform the distributed hypergraph
                    partitioner Zoltan on 95% of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the instances while also being a f=
                    actor of 2.1 faster. With just four
                    cores,Mt-KaHyPar is also slightly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">faster than the fastest sequential=
                    multilevel partitioner PaToH while producing
                    better solutions on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">83% of all instances. The sequenti=
                    al high-quality partitioner KaHyPar still finds
                    better solutions than
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our parallel approach, especially =
                    when using max-flow-based refinement. This,
                    however, comes at the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cost of considerably longer runnin=
                    g times.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gou2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-188" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gou, Changjiang et al. =E2=80=9CImproving Ma=
            pping for Sparse Direct&nbsp;Solvers=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                Euro-Par
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">2020: Parallel Processing</span>=
            . Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp.
            167=E2=80=93182. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-=caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-57675-2=CB=9911.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In order to express parallelism,
                parallel sparse direct solvers take advantage of the elimina=
                tion
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tree to exhibit tree-shaped task g=
                    raphs, where nodes represent computational
                    tasks and edges represent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data dependencies. One of the pre-=
                    processing stages of sparse direct solvers
                    consists of mapping
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational resources (processor=
                    s) to these tasks. The objective is to minimize
                    the factorization time
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by exhibiting good data locality a=
                    nd load balancing. The proportional mapping
                    technique is a widely
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">used approach to solve this resour=
                    ce-allocation problem. It achieves good data
                    locality by assigning the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">same processors to large parts of =
                    the elimination tree. However, it may limit
                    load balancing in some
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cases. In this paper, we propose a=
                    dynamic mapping algorithm based on
                    proportional mapping. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new approach, named Steal, relaxes=
                    the data locality criterion to improve load
                    balancing. In order
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to validate the newly introduced m=
                    ethod, we perform extensive experiments on the
                    PaStiX sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">direct solver. It demonstrates tha=
                    t our algorithm enables better static
                    scheduling of the numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization while keeping good d=
                    ata locality.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gower2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-189" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gower, Robert M., Mark Schmidt, et al. =E2=
            =80=9CVariance-Reduced Methods for Machine Learning=E2=80=9D.
            In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.00892 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.=
                LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Stochastic optimization lies at
                the heart of machine learning, and its cornerstone is stocha=
                stic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient descent (SGD), a method i=
                    ntroduced over 60 years ago. The last 8 years
                    have seen an exciting
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new development: variance reductio=
                    n (VR) for stochastic optimization methods.
                    These VR methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">excel in settings where more than =
                    one pass through the training data is allowed,
                    achieving a faster
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence than SGD in theory as =
                    well as practice. These speedups underline the
                    surge of interest in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">VR methods and the fast-growing bo=
                    dy of work on this topic. This review covers
                    the key principles
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and main developments behind VR me=
                    thods for optimization with finite data sets
                    and is aimed at
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-expert readers. We focus mainl=
                    y on the convex setting, and leave pointers to
                    readers interested in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extensions for minimizing non-conv=
                    ex functions.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gower2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-190" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gower, Robert M., Othmane Sebbouh, and Nicol=
            as Loizou. =E2=80=9CSGD for Structured Nonconvex
            Functions: Learning Rates, Minibatching and Interpolation=E2=80=
            =9D. In: (June 2020). arXiv:
            2006.10311v2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We provide several convergence
                theorems for SGD for two large classes of structured
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-convex functions: (i) the Quas=
                    ar (Strongly) Convex functions and (ii) the
                    functions satisfying the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Polyak-Lojasiewicz condition. Our =
                    analysis relies on the Expected Residual
                    condition which we show is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a strictly weaker assumption as co=
                    mpared to previously used growth conditions,
                    expected smoothness
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or bounded variance assumptions. W=
                    e provide theoretical guarantees for the
                    convergence of SGD for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different step size selections inc=
                    luding constant, decreasing and the recently
                    proposed stochastic Polyak
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">step size. In addition, all of our=
                    analysis holds for the arbitrary sampling
                    paradigm, and as such, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are able to give insights into the=
                    complexity of minibatching and determine an
                    optimal minibatch size.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In particular we recover the best =
                    known convergence rates of full gradient
                    descent and single element
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sampling SGD as a special case. Fi=
                    nally, we show that for models that interpolate
                    the training data,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we can dispense of our Expected Re=
                    sidual condition and give state-of-the-art
                    results in this setting.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gratien2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-191" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gratien, Jean-Marc. =E2=80=9CIntroducing mul=
            ti-level parallelism, at coarse, fineand instruction
            level
            to enhance the performance ofiterative solvers for large sparse=
            linear systems on Multi- and
            Many-core architecture=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Pr=
                oceedings of the 2020 IEEE/ACM 6th Workshop on the LLVM
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Compiler Infrastructure in HPC (=
                LLVM-HPC) and Workshop on
                HierarchicalParallelism for
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Exascale Computing</span>. HiPar=
            =E2=80=9920. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">With the evolution of High
                Performance Computing, multi-core and many-core systems are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">now a common feature of new hardwa=
                    re architectures. The introduction of very
                    large number of cores
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at the processor level is challeng=
                    ing because it requires to handle multi level
                    parallelism at various levels
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">either coarse or fine to fully tak=
                    e advantage of the offered computing power. The
                    induced programming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effort can be fixed with parallel =
                    programming models based on the data flow model
                    and the task
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programming paradigm [1]. To do so=
                    many of the standard numerical algorithms must
                    be revisited as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">they cannot be easily parallelized=
                    at the finest levels. Iterative linear solvers
                    are a key part of petroleum
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reservoir simulation as they can r=
                    epresent up to 80% of the total computing time.
                    In these algorithms,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the standard preconditioning metho=
                    ds for large, sparse and unstructured matrices
                    =E2=80=93 such as Incomplete
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">LU Factorization (ILU) or Algebrai=
                    c Multigrid (AMG) =E2=80=93 fail to scale on
                    shared-memory architectures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with large number of cores. In thi=
                    s paper we reconsider preconditioning
                    algorithms to better introduce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multi-level parallelism at both co=
                    arse level with MPI, fine level with threads
                    and at the instruction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">level to enable SIMD optimizations=
                    . This paper illustrates how we enhance the
                    implementation of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preconditioners like the multileve=
                    l domain decomposition (DDML) preconditioners
                    [2], based on the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">popular Additive Schwartz Method (=
                    ASM), or the classical ILU0 preconditioner with
                    the fine grained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel fixed point variant prese=
                    nted in [3]. Our approach is validated on
                    linear systems extracted from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">realistic petroleum reservoir simu=
                    lations. The robustness of the preconditioners
                    is tested with respect
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to the data heterogeneities of the=
                    study cases. We evaluate the extensibility of
                    our implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regarding the model sizes and its =
                    scalability regarding the large number of cores
                    provided by new KNL
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processors or multi-nodes clusters=
                    .</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gratton2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-192" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gratton, Serge et al. =E2=80=9CMinimizing co=
            nvex quadratics with variable precision conjugate
            gradients=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Numerical Linea=
                r Algebra with Applications </span>(Oct. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <s= pan class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>: 10.1002/nla.2337.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We investigate the method of
                conjugate gradients, exploiting inaccurate matrix-vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">products, for the solution of conv=
                    ex quadratic optimization problems. Theoretical
                    performance bounds
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are derived, and the necessary qua=
                    ntities occurring in the theoretical bounds
                    estimated, leading to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a practical algorithm. Numerical e=
                    xperiments suggest that this approach has
                    significant potential,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">including in the steadily more imp=
                    ortant context of multiprecision
                    computations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gross2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-193" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gross, James C. and Geoffrey T. Parks. =E2=
            =80=9COptimization by moving ridge functions:
            Derivative-free optimization for computationally intensive func=
            tions=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv:
            2007.04893v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A novel derivative-free
                algorithm, optimization by moving ridge functions (OMoRF), f=
                or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unconstrained and bound-constraine=
                    d optimization is presented. This algorithm
                    couples trust region

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methodologies with output-based di=
                    mension reduction to accelerate convergence of
                    model-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization strategies. The dimen=
                    sion-reducing subspace is updated as the trust
                    region moves through
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the design space, allowing OMoRF t=
                    o be applied to functions with no known global
                    low-dimensional
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">structure. Furthermore, its low co=
                    mputational requirement allows it to make rapid
                    progress when
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimizing high-dimensional functi=
                    ons. Its performance is examined on a set of
                    test problems of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">moderate to high dimension and a h=
                    igh-dimensional design optimization problem.
                    The results show
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that OMoRF compares favourably to =
                    other common derivative-free optimization
                    methods, particularly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">when very few function evaluations=
                    are available.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gruetzmacher2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-194" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gr=C3=BCtzmacher, Thomas et al. =E2=80=9CAcc=
            eleration of PageRank with Customized Precision Based
            on Mantissa Segmentation=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                ACM Transactions on Parallel Computing </span>7.1 (Mar.
            2020),
            pp. 1=E2=80=9319. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1145/3380934.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We describe the application of a
                communication-reduction technique for the PageRank
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm that dynamically adapts =
                    the precision of the data access to the
                    numerical requirements of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the algorithm as the iteration con=
                    verges. Our variable-precision strategy, using
                    a customized precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">format based on mantissa segmentat=
                    ion (CPMS), abandons the IEEE 754 single- and
                    double-precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number representation formats empl=
                    oyed in the standard implementation of
                    PageRank, and instead
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">handles the data in memory using a=
                    customized floating-point format. The
                    customized format enables
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fast data access in different accu=
                    racy, prevents overflow/underflow by preserving
                    the IEEE 754
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">double-precision exponent, and eff=
                    iciently avoids data duplication, since all
                    bits of the original IEEE
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">754 double-precision mantissa are =
                    preserved in memory, but re-organized for
                    efficient reduced precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">access. With this approach, the tr=
                    uncated values (omitting significand bits), as
                    well as the original
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">IEEE double-precision values, can =
                    be retrieved without duplicating the data in
                    different formats.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class==3D"cmr-9">Our numerical experiments on
                    an NVIDIA
                    V100 GPU (Volta architecture) and a server equipped
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with two Intel Xeon Platinum 8168 =
                    CPUs (48 cores in total) expose that, compared
                    with a standard
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">IEEE double-precision implementati=
                    on, the CPMS-based PageRank completes about 10%
                    faster if
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high-accuracy output is needed, an=
                    d about 30% faster if reduced output accuracy
                    is acceptable.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gryazin2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-195" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gryazin, Yury A. and Rick B. Spielman. =E2=
            =80=9CParallel Direct Regularized Solver for Power
            Circuit
            Applications=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Advances in =
                Intelligent Systems and Computing</span>. Springer
            International
            Publishing, Nov. 2020, pp. 193=E2=80=93204. <span class=3D"cmcs=c-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-63089-8=CB=9912.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, a new direct
                parallel linear solver for a variety of pulsed power applica=
                tions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is presented. This algorithm is th=
                    e core of the electrical circuit simulator
                    Screamer considered in our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">previous publications. The main id=
                    ea of the underlying algorithm is to use graph
                    partitioning to break
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the problem tree into a series of =
                    branches that can be solved in parallel. Then
                    this division is used
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in the recursive elimination proce=
                    ss to guarantee the high efficiency of the
                    direct method. The graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">partitioning in this problem is st=
                    raightforward since it corresponds to the
                    branch structure of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">underlying circuit. The partitioni=
                    ng method naturally leads to the parallel
                    implementation of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization and solution steps. =
                    The numerical results of test problems confirm
                    the high efficiency of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the suggested direct algorithm.</s= pan>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-196" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gu, Zhixiang et al. =E2=80=9CBandwidth-Optim=
            ized Parallel Algorithms for Sparse Matrix-Matrix
            Multiplication using Propagation Blocking=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2=
            020). arXiv: 2002.11302 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse matrix-matrix
                multiplication (SpGEMM) is a widely used kernel in various
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph, scientific computing and ma=
                    chine learning algorithms. It is well known
                    that SpGEMM is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a memory-bound operation, and its =
                    peak performance is expected to be bound by the
                    memory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bandwidth. Yet, existing algorithm=
                    s fail to saturate the memory bandwidth,
                    resulting in suboptimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance under the Roofline mod=
                    el. In this paper we characterize existing
                    SpGEMM algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based on their memory access patte=
                    rns and develop practical lower and upper
                    bounds for SpGEMM
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance. We then develop an Sp=
                    GEMM algorithm based on outer product matrix
                    multiplication.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The newly developed algorithm call=
                    ed PB-SpGEMM saturates memory bandwidth by
                    using the

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">propagation blocking technique and=
                    by performing in-cache sorting and merging.
                    For many practical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, PB-SpGEMM runs 20%-50% f=
                    aster than the state-of-the-art heap and hash
                    SpGEMM
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms on modern multicore pro=
                    cessors. Most importantly, PB-SpGEMM attains
                    performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">predicted by the Roofline model, a=
                    nd its performance remains stable with respect
                    to matrix size and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparsity.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Guidi2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-197" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Guidi, Giulia, Marquita Ellis, et al. =E2=80=
            =9C10 Years Later: Cloud Computing is Closing the
            Performance Gap=E2=80=9D. In: (Nov. 2020). arXiv: 2011.00656 <s= pan class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Large scale modeling and
                simulation problems, from nanoscale materials to universe-sc=
                ale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cosmology, have in the past used t=
                    he massive computing resources of
                    High-Performance Computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(HPC) systems. Over the last decad=
                    e, cloud computing has gained popularity for
                    business applications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and increasingly for computational=
                    ly intensive machine learning problems. Despite
                    the prolific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">literature, the question remains o=
                    pen whether cloud computing can provide
                    HPC-competitive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance for a wide range of sc=
                    ientific applications.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">Th=
                    e answer to this question is crucial in guiding the design of
                    future systems and providing access
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to high-performance resources to a=
                    broadened community. Here we present a
                    multi-level approach to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">identifying the performance gap be=
                    tween HPC and cloud computing and to isolate
                    several variables
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that contribute to this gap by div=
                    iding our experiments into (i) hardware and
                    system microbenchmarks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and (ii) user applications. </span=><br class=3D"newline"><span
                        class=3D"cmr-9">Our
                        results show that today=E2=80=99s high-end cloud computing c=
                        an deliver HPC-like performance - at least
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at modest scales - not only for co=
                        mputationally intensive applications, but also
                        for memory- and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication-intensive applicatio=
                        ns, thanks to the high-speed memory systems and
                        interconnects
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and dedicated batch scheduling now=
                        available on some cloud platforms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Guidi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-198" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Guidi, Giulia, Oguz Selvitopi, et al. =E2=80=
            =9CParallel String Graph Construction and Transitive
            Reduction for De Novo Genome Assembly=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020)=
            . arXiv: 2010.10055 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">One of the most computationally
                intensive tasks in computational biology is de novo genome
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">assembly, the decoding of the sequ=
                    ence of an unknown genome from redundant and
                    erroneous short
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sequences. A common assembly parad=
                    igm identifies overlapping sequences,
                    simplifies their layout,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and creates consensus. Despite man=
                    y algorithms developed in the literature, the
                    efficient assembly of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large genomes is still an open pro=
                    blem. In this work, we introduce new
                    distributed-memory parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms for overlap detection a=
                    nd layout simplification steps of de novo
                    genome assembly, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implement them in the diBELLA 2D p=
                    ipeline. Our distributed memory algorithms for
                    both overlap
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detection and layout simplificatio=
                    n are based on linear-algebra operations over
                    semirings using 2D
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed sparse matrices. Our l=
                    ayout step consists of performing a transitive
                    reduction from the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overlap graph to a string graph. W=
                    e provide a detailed communication analysis of
                    the main stages of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our new algorithms. diBELLA 2D ach=
                    ieves near linear scaling with over 80%
                    parallel efficiency for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">human genome, reducing the runtime=
                    for overlap detection by 1.2-1.3</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"271" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">for the human genome and 1.5-1.9</span>
                    <!= -- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHT=ML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"272" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; pos=ition: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mo=class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </m= jx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mo class=3D"M=athClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">for C. elegans compared to the state-of-t=
                            he-art. Our transitive reduction algorithm
                            outperforms an
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">existing distributed-memory implem=
                            entation by 10.5-13.3</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"273" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on=" display=3D" inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" dis=play=3D"inline">
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                </math></mjx-assisti= ve-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">for the human genome and 18-29</span>
                        <!--=
 l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML=" tabindex=3D" 0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"274" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posit=ion: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx= -math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">for the C. elegans. Our work paves the wa=
                            y for efficient de novo assembly of large
                            genomes using long
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reads in distributed memory.</span=>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Guler2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-199" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Guler, Basak, Salman Avestimehr, and Antonio=
            Ortega. =E2=80=9CTACC: Topology-Aware Coded
            Computing for Distributed Graph Processing=E2=80=9D. In: <span=class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE Transactions on Signal
                and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Information Processing over Netw=
                orks </span>(2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi=
                </span></span>: 10.1109/tsipn.2020.2998223.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper proposes a coded
                distributed graph processing framework to alleviate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication bottleneck in large-=
                    scale distributed graph processing. In
                    particular, we propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a topology-aware coded computing (=
                    TACC) algorithm that has two novel salient
                    features: (i) a

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">topology-aware graph allocation st=
                    rategy, and (ii) a coded aggregation scheme
                    that combines the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intermediate computations for grap=
                    h processes while constructing coded messages.
                    The proposed setup
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results in a trade-off between com=
                    putation and communication, in that increasing
                    the computation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">load at the distributed parties ca=
                    n in turn reduce the communication load. We
                    demonstrate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effectiveness of the TACC algorith=
                    m by comparing the communication load with
                    existing setups on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both Erdos-Renyi and Barabasi-Albe=
                    rt type random graphs, as well as real-world
                    Google web graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for PageRank computations. In part=
                    icular, we show that the proposed coding
                    strategy can lead to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">up to 82% reduction in communicati=
                    on load and up to 46% reduction overall
                    execution time, when
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compared to the state-of-the-art a=
                    nd implemented on the Amazon EC2 cloud compute
                    platform.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Guo2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-200" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Guo, H., I. Laguna, and C. Rubio-Gonz=C3=A1l=
            ez. =E2=80=9CpLiner: Isolating Lines of Floating-Point Code
            for Compiler-Induced Variability=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"c=mti-10">Proceedings of the 2020
                International
                Conference for
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">High Performance Computing, Netw=
                orking, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC=E2=80=99 20.
            Los Alamitos, CA,
            USA: IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 680=E2=80=93693. <sp= an class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1109=
                /SC41405.2020.00053.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                    <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020=.00053"
                            class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputersocie=
                                ty.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00053</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Scientific applications are often
                impacted by numerical inconsistencies when using different
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compilers or when a compiler is us=
                    ed with different optimization levels; such
                    inconsistencies hinder
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reproducibility and can be hard to=
                    diagnose. We present PLINER, a tool to
                    automatically pinpoint
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">code lines that trigger compiler-i=
                    nduced variability. PLINER uses a novel
                    approach to enhance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating-point precision at differ=
                    ent levels of code granularity, and performs a
                    guided search to identify
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">locations affected by numerical in=
                    consistencies. We demonstrate PLINER on a
                    real-world numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inconsistency that required weeks =
                    to diagnose, which PLINER isolates in minutes.
                    We also evaluate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PLINER on 100 synthetic programs, =
                    and the NAS Parallel Benchmarks (NPB). On the
                    synthetic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programs, PLINER detects the affec=
                    ted lines of code 87% of the time while the
                    stateof- the-art approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">only detects the affected lines 6%=
                    of the time. Furthermore, PLINER successfully
                    isolates all numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inconsistencies found in the NPB.<= /span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Guo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-201" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Guo, Hui and Cindy Rubio-Gonz=C3=A1lez. =E2=
            =80=9CEfficient Generation of Error-Inducing Floating-Point
            Inputsvia Symbolic Execution=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-=10">Proceedings of
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">the 42nd IEEE/ACM International =
                Conference on Software Engineering</span>.
            ICSE=E2=80=9920. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"=small-caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://hguo15.github.io/huiguo.github.io/files/fpge=n-icse20.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://hguo15.github.i=
                    o/huiguo.github.io/files/fpgen-icse20.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Floating point is widely used in
                software to emulate arithmetic over reals. Unfortunately,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating point leads to rounding e=
                    rrors that propagate and accumulate during
                    execution. Generating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inputs to maximize the numerical e=
                    rror is critical when evaluating the accuracy
                    of floating-point
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">code. In this paper, we formulate =
                    the problem of generating high error-inducing
                    floating-point inputs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as a code coverage maximization pr=
                    oblem solved using symbolic execution.
                    Specifically, we define
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inaccuracy checks to detect large =
                    precision loss and cancellation. We inject
                    these checks at strategic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">program locations to construct spe=
                    cialized branches that, when covered by a given
                    input, are likely to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lead to large errors in the result=
                    . We apply symbolic execution to generate
                    inputs that exercise these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">specialized branches, and describe=
                    optimizations that make our approach
                    practical. We implement a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tool named FPGen and present an ev=
                    aluation on 21 numerical programs including
                    matrix computation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and statistics libraries. We show =
                    that FPGen exposes errors for 20 of these
                    programs and triggers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">errors that are, on average, over =
                    2 orders of magnitude larger than the state of
                    the art.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Guo2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-202" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Guo, Mengyu and Shaowen Wang. =E2=80=9CQuant=
            um Computing for Solving Spatial Optimization
            Problems=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Geotechnologies =
                and the Environment</span>. Springer International
            Publishing, 2020,
            pp. 97=E2=80=93113. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sma=ll-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-47998-5=CB=996.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Ever since Shor=E2=80=9D=E2=80=99s quantum
                factoring algorithm was developed, quantum computing has bee=
                n
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">pursued as a promising and powerfu=
                    l approach to solving many computationally
                    complex problems such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as combinatorial optimization and =
                    machine learning. As an important quantum
                    computing approach,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quantum annealing (QA) has receive=
                    d considerable attention. Extensive research
                    has shown that QA,

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exploiting quantum-mechanical effe=
                    cts such as tunneling, entanglement and
                    superposition, could be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">much more efficient in solving har=
                    d combinatorial optimization problems than its
                    classical counterpart =E2=80=93
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simulated annealing. Recent advanc=
                    es in quantum annealing hardware open the
                    possibility of empirical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">testing of QA against the most cha=
                    llenging computational problems arising in
                    geospatial applications.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This chapter demonstrates how to e=
                    mploy QA to solve NP-hard spatial optimization
                    problems through
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an illustrative example of program=
                    ming a p-median model and a case study on
                    spatial supply chain
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization. The research finding=
                    s also address the short- and long-term
                    potential of quantum
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing in the future developmen=
                    t of high-performance computing for geospatial
                    applications.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Gusmeroli2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-203" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Gusmeroli, Nicol=C3=B2 et al. =E2=80=9CBiqBi=
            n: a parallel branch-and-bound solver for binary quadratic
            problems with linear constraints=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). ar=
            Xiv: 2009.06240 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present BiqBin, an exact
                solver for linearly constrained binary quadratic problems. O=
                ur
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approach is based on an exact pena=
                    lty method to first efficiently transform the
                    original problem into an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instance of Max-Cut, and then to s=
                    olve the Max-Cut problem by a branch-and-bound
                    algorithm. All
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the main ingredients are carefully=
                    developed using new semidefinite programming
                    relaxations obtained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by strengthening the existing rela=
                    xations with a set of hypermetric inequalities,
                    applying the bundle
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method as the bounding routine and=
                    using new strategies for exploring the
                    branch-and-bound tree.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Furthermore, an efficient C implem=
                    entation of a sequential and a parallel
                    branch-and-bound algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is presented. The latter is based =
                    on a load coordinator-worker scheme using MPI
                    for multi-node
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelization and is evaluated o=
                    n a high-performance computer. The new solver
                    is benchmarked
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">against BiqCrunch, GUROBI, and SCI=
                    P on four families of (linearly constrained)
                    binary quadratic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems. Numerical results demons=
                    trate that BiqBin is a highly competitive
                    solver. The serial version
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">outperforms the other three solver=
                    s on the majority of the benchmark instances.
                    We also evaluate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel solver and show that it h=
                    as good scaling properties. The general
                    audience can use it as an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on-line service available at http:=
                    //www.biqbin.eu.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Guettel2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-204" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">G=C3=BCttel, Stefan, Daniel Kressner, and Ka=
            thryn Lund. =E2=80=9CLimited-memory polynomial methods
            for large-scale matrix functions=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arX=
            iv: 2002.01682 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Matrix functions are a central
                topic of linear algebra, and problems requiring their numeri=
                cal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approximation appear increasingly =
                    often in scientific computing. We review
                    various limited-memory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods for the approximation of t=
                    he action of a large-scale matrix function on a
                    vector. Emphasis is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">put on polynomial methods, whose m=
                    emory requirements are known or prescribed a
                    priori. Methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based on explicit polynomial appro=
                    ximation or interpolation, as well as restarted
                    Arnoldi methods, are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">treated in detail. An overview of =
                    existing software is also given, as well as a
                    discussion of challenging
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">open problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Haidar2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-205" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Haidar, Azzam et al. =E2=80=9CMixed-Precisio=
            n IterativeRefinement using TensorCores on GPUs to
            AccelerateSolution of Linear Systems=E2=80=9D. In: <span class==3D"cmti-10">Philosophical Transactions of
                the Royal
                Society A:
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Mathematical, Physical and Engin=
                eering Sciences </span>(2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Double-precision floating-point
                arithmetic (FP64) has been the de facto standard for enginee=
                ring
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and scientific simulations for sev=
                    eral decades. Problem complexity and the sheer
                    volume of data coming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from various instruments and senso=
                    rs motivate researchers to mix and match
                    various approaches
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to optimize compute resources, inc=
                    luding different levels of floating-point
                    precision. In recent years,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">machine learning has motivated har=
                    dware support for halfprecision floating-point
                    arithmetic. A primary
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">challenge in high-performance comp=
                    uting is to leverage reducedprecision and
                    mixed-precision hardware.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We show how the FP16/FP32 Tensor C=
                    ores on NVIDIA GPUs can be exploited to
                    accelerate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution of linear systems of equa=
                    tions </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attache=d_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"275" style=3D"font-si=ze: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D="true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Mat=hClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi c=lass=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-= mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <ma= th xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi math=variant=3D"italic">Ax</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                            <mi>b</mi>
                            </m= ath>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">without sacrificing numerical stability. =
                    On the NVIDIA Quadro GV100 (Volta) GPU, we
                    achieve a</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"276" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mj= x-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-m= o>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <mat= h xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>4</mn=>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-co= ntainer>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">=E2=80=93 </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"277" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c35">
                                    </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                    </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unse=lectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Ma=th/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mn>5</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97<= /mo>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">performance increase and </span>
                <!-- l. 3=
0 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tab=index=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"278" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:=relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class==3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-a= ssistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://w=ww.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>5</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathC=lass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>

                <span class=3D"cmr-9">better energy efficiency versus the stand=
                    ard FP64 implementation while maintaining an
                    FP64 level of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical stability.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hamilton2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-206" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hamilton, Kathleen E. et al. =E2=80=9CAccele=
            rating Scientific Computing in the Post-Moore=E2=80=99s Era=E2=80=9D.
            In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">ACM Transactions on Parallel Computing =
            </span>7.1 (Mar. 2020), pp. 1=E2=80=9331. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    cl=ass=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1145/3380940.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Novel uses of graphical
                processing units for accelerated computation revolutionized =
                the field
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of high-performance scientific com=
                    puting by providing specialized workflows
                    tailored to algorithmic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">requirements. As the era of Moore=
                    =E2=80=99s law draws to a close, many new non=E2=80=93von
                    Neumann processors
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are emerging as potential computat=
                    ional accelerators, including those based on
                    the principles of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">neuromorphic computing, tensor alg=
                    ebra, and quantum information. While
                    development of these new
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processors is continuing to mature=
                    , the potential impact on accelerated computing
                    is anticipated to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be profound. We discuss how differ=
                    ent processing models can advance computing in
                    key scientific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paradigms: machine learning and co=
                    nstraint satisfaction. Significantly, each of
                    these new processor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">types utilizes a fundamentally dif=
                    ferent model of computation, and this raises
                    questions about how to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">best use such processors in the de=
                    sign and implementation of applications. While
                    many processors are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">being developed with a specific do=
                    main target, the ubiquity of spin-glass models
                    and neural networks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provides an avenue for multi-funct=
                    ional applications. This also hints at the
                    infrastructure needed to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integrate next-generation processi=
                    ng units into future high-performance computing
                    systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Han2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-207" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Han, Jingoo et al. =E2=80=9CMARBLE: A Multi-=
            GPU Aware Job Scheduler for Deep Learning on HPC
            Systems=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of th=
                e 20th IEEE/ACM International Symposium on Cluster,
                Cloud
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">and Internet Computing</span>. C=
            CGRID =E2=80=9920. IEEE, May 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"=small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/ccgrid49817.2020.00-66.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Deep learning (DL) has become a
                key tool for solving complex scientific problems. However,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">managing the multi-dimensional lar=
                    ge-scale data associated with DL, especially
                    atop extant multiple
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graphics processing units (GPUs) i=
                    n modern supercomputers poses significant
                    challenges. Moreover, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">latest high-performance computing =
                    (HPC) architectures bring different performance
                    trends in training
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">throughput compared to the existin=
                    g studies. Existing DL optimizations such as
                    larger batch size and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU locality-aware scheduling have=
                    little effect on improving DL training
                    throughput performance due
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to fast CPU-to-GPU connections. Ad=
                    ditionally, DL training on multiple GPUs scales
                    sublinearly. Thus,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simply adding more GPUs to a syste=
                    m is ineffective. To this end, we design
                    MARBLE, a first-of-its-kind
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">job scheduler, which considers the=
                    non-linear scalability of GPUs at the
                    intra-node level to schedule an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">appropriate number of GPUs per nod=
                    e for a job. By sharing the GPU resources on a
                    node with multiple
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DL jobs, MARBLE avoids low GPU uti=
                    lization in current multi-GPU DL training on
                    HPC systems.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Our comprehensive evaluation in th=
                    e Summit supercomputer shows that MARBLE is
                    able to improve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DL training performance by up to 4=
                    8.3% compared to the popular Platform Load
                    Sharing Facility
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(LSF) scheduler. Compared to the s=
                    tate-of-the-art of DL scheduler, Optimus,
                    MARBLE reduces the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">job completion time by up to 47%.<= /span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Han2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-208" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Han, Qingchang et al. =E2=80=9CTowards effic=
            ient tile low-rank GEMM computation on
            sunway many-core processors=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-1=0">The Journal of Supercomputing
            </span>(Oct. 2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1007/s11227-020-03444-2.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Tile low-rank general matrix
                multiplication (TLR GEMM) is a novel method of matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication on large data-spars=
                    e matrices, which can significantly reduce
                    storage footprint and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arithmetic complexity under given =
                    accuracy. To implement high-performance TLR
                    GEMM on Sunway
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many-core processor, the following=
                    challenges remain to be addressed: 1) design
                    an efficient parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scheme; 2) provide an efficient ke=
                    rnel library of math functions commonly used in
                    TLR GEMM. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paper proposes swTLR GEMM, an effi=
                    cient implementation of TLR GEMM. We assign LR
                    GEMM
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computation to a single computing =
                    processing element (CPE) and use grouped task
                    queue to process
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different data tiles of the TLR ma=
                    trix. Moreover, we implement an efficient
                    kernel library (swLR
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Kernels) for low-rank matrix opera=
                    tions. To scale to massive (CGs), we organize
                    the CGs into the CG
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">grid and partition the matrices in=
                    to blocks accordingly. We also apply Cannon=E2=80=99s
                    algorithm to enable

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficient communication when proce=
                    ssing the matrix blocks across CGs
                    simultaneously. The experiment
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results show that the DGEMM kernel=
                    in swLR Kernels achieves 102</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"279" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">speedup on average. In terms of overall p=
                        erformance, swTLR GEMM-LLD and swTLR GEMM-LLL
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">achieve 91</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mj= x-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D="0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"280" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative=;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-as= sistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://ww=w.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=
                                    =C3=97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
