                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and 20.1</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-contain= er class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtm=enu_counter=3D"281" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-m= ath class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo=>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">speedup on average, respectively. In addi=
                                tion, our implementation of swTLR GEMM exhibits
                                good
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalability when running on 1,024 =
                                CGs of Sunway processors (66,560 cores in
                                total).</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hanford2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-209" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hanford, Nathan et al. =E2=80=9CChallenges o=
            f GPU-aware Communication in MPI=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proc=
                eedings
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">of the 2020 Workshop on Exascale=
                MPI</span>. ExaMPI =E2=80=9920. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">GPUs are increasingly popular in
                HPC systems and applications. However, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication bottleneck between G=
                    PUs, distributed across HPC nodes within a
                    cluster, has limited
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">achievable scalability of GPU-cent=
                    ric applications. Advances in inter-node GPU
                    communication such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as NVIDIA=E2=80=99s GPUDirect have=
                    made great strides in addressing this issue. The added
                    software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">development complexity has been ad=
                    dressed by simplified GPU programming paradigms
                    such as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Unified or Managed Memory. To unde=
                    rstand the performance of these new features,
                    new benchmarks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">were developed. Unfortunately, the=
                    se benchmark efforts do not include correctness
                    checking and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">certain messaging patterns used in=
                    applications. In this paper we highlight
                    important gaps in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication benchmarks and motiv=
                    ate a methodology to help application
                    developers understand
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the performance tradeoffs of diffe=
                    rent data movement options. Furthermore, we
                    share systems tuning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and deployment experiences across =
                    different GPU-aware MPI implementations. In
                    particular, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate correctness testing is=
                    needed along with performance testing through
                    modifications to an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">existing benchmark. In addition, w=
                    e present a case study where existing
                    benchmarks fail to characterize
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">how data is moved within SW4, a se=
                    ismic wave application, and create a benchmark
                    to model this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">behavior. Finally, we motivate the=
                    need for an applicationinspired benchmark
                    methodology to assess
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">system performance and guide appli=
                    cation programmers on how to use the system
                    more efficiently.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hanzely2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-210" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hanzely, Filip. =E2=80=9COptimization for Su=
            pervised Machine Learning: Randomized Algorithms for
            Data and Parameters=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). <span class=3D"c=mcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.25781/KAUST-4F2DH. arXiv: 2008.11824
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many key problems in machine
                learning and data science are routinely modeled as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization problems and solved v=
                    ia optimization algorithms. With the increase
                    of the volume of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data and the size and complexity o=
                    f the statistical models used to formulate
                    these often ill-conditioned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization tasks, there is a nee=
                    d for new efficient algorithms able to cope
                    with these challenges. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this thesis, we deal with each of =
                    these sources of difficulty in a different way.
                    To efficiently address the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">big data issue, we develop new met=
                    hods which in each iteration examine a small
                    random subset of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">training data only. To handle the =
                    big model issue, we develop methods which in
                    each iteration update a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">random subset of the model paramet=
                    ers only. Finally, to deal with ill-conditioned
                    problems, we devise
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods that incorporate either hi=
                    gher-order information or Nesterov=E2=80=99s
                    acceleration/momentum. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">all cases, randomness is viewed as=
                    a powerful algorithmic tool that we tune, both
                    in theory and in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments, to achieve the best r=
                    esults. Our algorithms have their primary
                    application in training
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">supervised machine learning models=
                    via regularized empirical risk minimization,
                    which is the dominant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paradigm for training such models.=
                    However, due to their generality, our methods
                    can be applied in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many other fields, including but n=
                    ot limited to data science, engineering,
                    scientific computing, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">statistics.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hanzely2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-211" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hanzely, Filip et al. =E2=80=9CStochastic Su=
            bspace Cubic Newton Method=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv:
            2002.09526 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we propose a new
                randomized second-order optimization algorithm=E2=80=94Stoch=
                astic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Subspace Cubic Newton (SSCN)=E2=80=
                    =94for minimizing a high dimensional convex function
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"282" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I">
                                </mj= x-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                            display=3D"inline="><mi>f</mi></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container><span class=3D"
                            cmr-9=">.
            </span><span class=3D" cmr-9">Our method can be seen both as a <= /span><span class=3D"cmti-9">stochastic
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extension of the cubically-regular=
                                    ized Newton method

                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of Nesterov and Polyak (2006), and=
                                    a </span><span class=3D"cmti-9">second-order
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">enhancement of stochastic subspace=
                                    descent of
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Kozak et al. (2019). We prove that=
                                    as we vary the minibatch size, the global
                                    convergence rate of
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SSCN interpolates between the rate=
                                    of stochastic coordinate descent (CD) and the
                                    rate of cubic
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regularized Newton, thus giving ne=
                                    w insights into the connection between first
                                    and second-order
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods. Remarkably, the local con=
                                    vergence rate of SSCN matches the rate of
                                    stochastic subspace
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">descent applied to the problem of =
                                    minimizing the quadratic function </span>
                                <!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"283" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                        <mjx-mfrac>
                                            <mjx-frac>
                                                <mjx-num>
                                                    <mjx-nstrut></mjx-nstrut>
                                                    <mjx-mrow size==3D"s">
                                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mn>
                            </= mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-num>
                            <mjx-dbox>
                                <mjx-dtable>
                                    <mjx-line></mjx-line>

                                        <mjx= -den>
                                            <mjx-dstrut></mjx-dstrut>
                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">

                                        0.235em;">=F0=9D=9B=BB</mjx-utext>

                                        </= mjx-c>

                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-bin">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo cla=ss=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </mj= x-mrow>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"=><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mfr= ac>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                                </mrow>
                                                </mfrac>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mo class=3D="MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mi>x</mi>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=
                                                            =92</mo>
                                                        <mi>x</mi>
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                                    </mrow>
                                                    <mo clas=s=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mi class=3D"MathClass-op">=E2=8A=A4</mi=>
                                                    <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                                    <mi class=3D"MathClass-op">=E2=88=87</mi>
                                                    <mo>=E2=81=A1</= mo>
                                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                                        <mi>f</mi>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                            <mrow>
                                                                <mi>x=
                                                                </mi>
                                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                                            </mrow>
                                                            <mo class=3D"MathClas=s-close">)</mo>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                            <mrow>
                                                                <mi>x<= /mi>
                                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                                                        <mi>x</mi>
                                                                        <mo class=3D"MathCl=ass-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                                                            </mrow>
                                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        </= math>
                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"284" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2217"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>x</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClas=s-bin">=E2=88=97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the minimizer of </span><!-- l. 30 -->=
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"285" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>f</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and hence depends on the propertie=
                                s of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"286" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </m= jx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>f=
                                        </mi>
                                    </math>
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">at the optimum only. Our numerical experi=
                                ments show that SSCN outperforms
                                non-accelerated first-order
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CD algorithms while being competit=
                                ive to their accelerated variants.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Harris2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-212" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Harris, Steven, Roger D. Chamberlain, and Ch=
            ristopher Gill. =E2=80=9COpenCL Performance
            on the Intel Heterogeneous Architecture Research Platform=E2=80=
            =9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE High-Performance Extreme Co=
                mputing Conference</span>. HPEC 2020. 2020.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>:
                    <a href=3D"https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~roger/papers/hcg20.pdf" c=lass=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~roger/paper=
                            s/hcg20.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The fundamental operation of
                matrix multiplication is ubiquitous across a myriad of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">disciplines. Yet, the identificati=
                    on of new optimizations for matrix
                    multiplication remains relevant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for emerging hardware architecture=
                    s and heterogeneous systems. Frameworks such as
                    OpenCL enable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computation orchestration on exist=
                    ing systems, and its availability using the
                    Intel High Level Synthesis
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compiler allows users to architect=
                    new designs for reconfigurable hardware using
                    C/C++. Using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the HARPv2 as a vehicle for explor=
                    ation, we investigate the utility of several
                    traditional matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication optimizations to be=
                    tter understand the performance portability of
                    OpenCL and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implications for such optimization=
                    s on cache coherent heterogeneous
                    architectures. Our results give
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">targeted insights into the applica=
                    bility of best practices that were designed for
                    existing architectures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">when used on emerging heterogeneou=
                    s systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-AlHarthi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-213" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Al-Harthi, Noha et al. =E2=80=9CSolving Acou=
            stic Boundary Integral Equations Using High Performance
            Tile Low-Rank LU Factorization=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmt=i-10">Proceedings of ISC High Performance
                2020</span>.
            ISC =E2=80=9920.
            June 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                    url</span></span>: <a href=3D"http://hdl.handle.net/10754/663212" class=3D"=url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://hdl.handle.net/10754/663212</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We design and develop a new high
                performance implementation of a fast direct LU-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solver using low-rank approximatio=
                    ns on massively parallel systems. The LU
                    factorization is the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">most time-consuming step in solvin=
                    g systems of linear equations in the context of
                    analyzing acoustic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scattering from large 3D objects. =
                    The matrix equation is obtained by discretizing
                    the boundary integral
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the exterior Helmholtz problem =
                    using a higher-order Nystr=C3=B6m scheme. The main
                    idea is to exploit
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the inherent data sparsity of the =
                    matrix operator by performing local
                    tile-centric approximations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">while still capturing the most sig=
                    nificant information. In particular, the
                    proposed LU-based solver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">leverages the Tile Low-Rank (TLR) =
                    data compression format as implemented in the
                    Hierarchical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Computations on Manycore Architect=
                    ures (HiCMA) library to decrease the complexity
                    of =E2=80=9Cclassical=E2=80=9D
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dense direct solvers from cubic to=
                    quadratic order. We taskify the underlying
                    boundary integral
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels to expose fine-grained com=
                    putations. We then employ the dynamic runtime
                    system StarPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to orchestrate the scheduling of c=
                    omputational tasks on shared and
                    distributed-memory systems.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The resulting asynchronous executi=
                    on permits to compensate for the load imbalance
                    due to the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">heterogeneous ranks, while mitigat=
                    ing the overhead of data motion. We assess the
                    robustness of our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">TLR LU-based solver and study the =
                    qualitative impact when using different
                    numerical accuracies. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new TLR LU factorization outperfor=
                    ms the state-of-the-art dense factorizations by
                    up to an order of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">magnitude on various parallel syst=
                    ems, for analysis of scattering from
                    large-scale 3D synthetic and real
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">geometries.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-He2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-214" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">He, Guanlin, St=C3=A9phane Vialle, and Marc =
            Baboulin. =E2=80=9CParallelization of the
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"287"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </m= jx-mi>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>k=
                        </mi>
                    </math>
                </mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container>-means
            Algorithm in a Spectral Clustering Chain on CPU-GPU Platforms=
            =E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the

            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Conference on Algorithms, Models=
                and Tools for Parallel Computing on
                Heterogeneous Platforms</span>.
            HeteroPar 2020 (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"=small-caps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"https://www.lri.fr/~baboulin/hete=ropar2020.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.lri.fr/~ba=
                    boulin/heteropar2020.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <!--=
 l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML=" tabindex=3D" 0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"288"
                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posit=ion: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                    <mjx-mi cl=ass=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math=>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"h=ttp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>k</mi>
                        </math></mj= x-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-means
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is a standard algorithm for cluste=
                ring data. It constitutes generally the final
                step in a more complex
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">chain of high quality spectral clu=
                stering. However this chain suffers from lack
                of scalability when
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">addressing large datasets. This ca=
                n be overcome by applying also the </span>
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"289"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </m= jx-mi>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>k=
                        </mi>
                    </math>
                </mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-mean=
                s
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm as a pre-processing task=
                to reduce the input data instances. We
                describe parallel optimization
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques for the </span>
            <!-- l. =
30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" ta=bindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"290"
                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:=relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                    <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                </mjx-math>
                <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>k</mi>
                    </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-means
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm on CPU and GPU. Experime=
                ntal results on synthetic dataset illustrate
                the numerical
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accuracy and performance of our im=
                plementations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-He2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-215" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">He, Xiao et al. =E2=80=9CTesting high perfor=
            mance numerical simulation programs: experience,
            lessons
            learned, and open issues=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                Proceedings of the 29th ACM SIGSOFT International
                Symposium
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">on Software Testing and Analysis=
            </span>. ACM, July 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps=">doi</span></span>: 10.1145/3395363.3397382.
         </p><!-- l. 30 -->
         <p class=3D" noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">High performance numerical
                        simulation programs are widely used to simulate actual physi=
                        cal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processes on high performance comp=
                    uters for the analysis of various physical and
                    engineering problems.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">They are usually regarded as non-t=
                    estable due to their high complexity. This
                    paper reports our real
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experience and lessons learned fro=
                    m testing five simulation programs that will be
                    used to design and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analyze nuclear power plants. We a=
                    pplied five testing approaches and found 33
                    bugs. We found that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">property-based testing and metamor=
                    phic testing are two effective methods.
                    Nevertheless, we suffered
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from the lack of domain knowledge,=
                    the high test costs, the shortage of test
                    cases, severe oracle issues,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and inadequate automation support.=
                    Consequently, the five programs are not
                    exhaustively tested from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the perspective of software testin=
                    g, and many existing software testing
                    techniques and tools are not fully
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applicable due to scalability and =
                    portability issues. We need more collaboration
                    and communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with other communities to promote =
                    the research and application of software
                    testing techniques.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-He2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-216" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">He, Xin et al. =E2=80=9CSparse-TPU: Adapting=
            Systolic Arrays for Sparse Matrices=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the International Confer=
                ence on Supercomputing</span>. ICS =E2=80=9920. 2020.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>:
                    <a href=3D"https://web.eecs.umich.edu/~subh/publication/stpu-ic=s20/stpu-ics20.pdf"
                        class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://web.eecs.=
                            umich.edu/~subh/publication/stpu-ics20/stpu-ics20.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">While systolic arrays are widely
                used for dense-matrix operations, they are seldom used for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse-matrix operations. In this =
                    paper, we show how a systolic array of
                    Multiply-and-Accumulate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(MAC) units, similar to Google=E2=
                    =80=99s Tensor Processing Unit (TPU), can be adapted to
                    efficiently handle
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse matrices. TPU-like accelera=
                    tors are built upon a 2D array of MAC units and
                    have demonstrated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high throughput and efficiency for=
                    dense matrix multiplication, which is a key
                    kernel in machine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learning algorithms and is the tar=
                    get of the TPU. In this work, we employ a
                    co-designed approach of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">first developing a packing techniq=
                    ue to condense a sparse matrix and then propose
                    a systolic array
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based system, Sparse-TPU, abbrevia=
                    ted to STPU, to accommodate the matrix
                    computations for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">packed denser matrix counterparts.=
                    To demonstrate the efficacy of our co-designed
                    approach, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluate sparse matrix-vector mult=
                    iplication on a broad set of synthetic and
                    real-world sparse matrices.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Experimental results show that STP=
                    U delivers 16.08</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMe=nu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"291" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" di=splay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-m= ml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">higher performance while consuming 4.39</= span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"292" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx= -mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                            <m= ath xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo cla=ss=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and 19.79</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-contai= ner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxt=menu_counter=3D"293" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-= math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</m= o>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">lower energy for integer (int8) and float=
                                    ing point (float32) implementations,
                                    respectively, over a TPU
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">baseline. Meanwhile, STPU has 12.9=
                                    3% area overhead and an average of 4.14%
                                    increase in dynamic
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">energy over the TPU baseline for t=
                                    he float32 implementation.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hebling2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-217" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hebling, Gustavo M. et al. =E2=80=9CSparse a=
            nd numerically stable implementation of a distribution
            system state estimation based on Multifrontal QR factorization=
            =E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Electric Power
                Systems
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Research </span>189 (Dec. 2020),=
            p. 106734. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1016/j.epsr.2020.106734.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Enhancing situational awareness
                of distribution networks is a requirement of Smart Grids.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In order to fulfill this requireme=
                    nt, specialized algorithms have been developed
                    to perform Distribution

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">System State Estimation (DSSE). Du=
                    e to the particularities of such networks,
                    those algorithms often
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rely on simplifications and approx=
                    imations of the measurement model which make
                    difficult to generalize
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">their results. This paper presents=
                    a sparse and numerically stable implementation
                    of an algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for DSSE, which does not require a=
                    ny additional assumption from the traditional
                    state estimation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formulation. The numerical stabili=
                    ty is guaranteed by using Multifrontal QR
                    factorization and an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimal ordering technique is eval=
                    uated to reduce fill-in. Simulation results are
                    carried out with IEEE
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">three-phase unbalanced test feeder=
                    s to evaluate the algorithm.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Herault2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-218" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Herault, Thomas et al. <span class=3D"cmti-1=0">Distributed-memory multi-GPU block-sparse
                tensor contraction for
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">electronic structure</span>. Tec=
            h. rep. Institut de recherche en informatique
            de Toulouse (IRIT), 2020.
            HAL: 02872813. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"https://hal.inria.fr/hal-02872813/documen=t" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://hal.inria.fr/hal-02872813/=
                    document</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many domains of scientific
                simulation (chemistry, condensed matter physics, data scienc=
                e)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">increasingly eschew dense tensors =
                    for block-sparse tensors, sometimes with
                    additional structure
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(recursive hierarchy, rank sparsit=
                    y, etc.). Distributed-memory parallel
                    computation with block-sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensorial data is paramount to min=
                    imize the time-tosolution (e.g., to study
                    dynamical problems or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for real-time analysis) and to acc=
                    ommodate problems of realistic size that are
                    too large to fit into
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the host/device memory of a single=
                    node equipped with accelerators.
                    Unfortunately, computation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with such irregular data structure=
                    s is a poor match to the dominant imperative,
                    bulk-synchronous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel programming model. In thi=
                    s paper, we focus on the critical element of
                    block-sparse tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algebra, namely binary tensor cont=
                    raction, and report on an efficient and
                    scalable implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using the task-focused PaRSEC runt=
                    ime. High performance of the block-sparse
                    tensor contraction on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Summit supercomputer is demons=
                    trated for synthetic data as well as for real
                    data involved in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">electronic structure simulations o=
                    f unprecedented size.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Herholz2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-219" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Herholz, Philipp and Olga Sorkine-Hornung. =
            =E2=80=9CSparse Cholesky Updates for Interactive Mesh
            Parameterization=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">ACM Tran=
                saction son Computer Graphics </span>39.6 (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">=
                <span class=3D"small-caps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"https://igl.ethz.c=h/projects/sparse-cholesky-update/sparse-cholesky-update-paper.pdf"
                class==3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://igl.ethz.ch/projects/sparse-choles=
                    ky-update/sparse-cholesky-update-paper.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a novel linear solver
                for interactive parameterization tasks. Our method is based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the observation that quasi-conf=
                    ormal parameterizations of a triangle mesh are
                    largely determined
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by boundary conditions. These boun=
                    dary conditions are typically constructed
                    interactively by users,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">who have to take several artistic =
                    and geometric constraints into account while
                    introducing cuts on the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">geometry. Commonly, the main compu=
                    tational burden in these methods is solving a
                    linear system every
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time new boundary conditions are i=
                    mposed. The core of our solver is a novel
                    approach to efficiently
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">update the Cholesky factorization =
                    of the linear system to reflect new boundary
                    conditions, thereby
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">enabling a seamless and interactiv=
                    e workflow even for large meshes consisting of
                    several millions of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vertices</span>.
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hermans2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-220" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hermans, Ben, Andreas Themelis, and Panagiot=
            is Patrinos. =E2=80=9CQPALM: A Proximal Augmented
            Lagrangian Method for Nonconvex Quadratic Programs=E2=80=9D. In=
            : (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.02653
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We propose QPALM, a nonconvex
                quadratic programming (QP) solver based on the proximal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">augmented Lagrangian method. This =
                    method solves a sequence of inner subproblems
                    which can be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">enforced to be strongly convex and=
                    which therefore admit of a unique solution.
                    The resulting steps are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">shown to be equivalent to inexact =
                    proximal point iterations on the
                    extended-real-valued cost function.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Furthermore, we prove global conve=
                    rgence of such iterations to a stationary point
                    at an R-linear rate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in the specific case of a (possibl=
                    y nonconvex) QP. The QPALM algorithm solves the
                    subproblems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteratively using semismooth Newto=
                    n directions and an exact linesearch. The
                    former can be computed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiently in most iterations by =
                    making use of suitable factorization update
                    routines, while the latter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">requires the zero of a monotone, p=
                    iecewise affine function. QPALM is implemented
                    in open-source
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">C code, with tailored linear algeb=
                    ra routines for the factorization in a
                    self-written package LADEL.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The resulting implementation is sh=
                    own to be extremely robust in numerical
                    simulations, solving all of

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Maros-Meszaros problems and fi=
                    nding a stationary point for most of the
                    nonconvex QPs in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Cutest test set. Furthermore, it i=
                    s shown to be competitive against
                    state-of-the-art convex QP solvers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in typical QPs arising from applic=
                    ation domains such as portfolio optimization
                    and model predictive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">control. As such, QPALM strikes a =
                    unique balance between solving both easy and
                    hard problems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiently.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hey2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-221" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hey, Tony et al. =E2=80=9CMachine learning a=
            nd big scientific data=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Philosophical =
                Transactions of
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">the Royal Society A: Mathematica=
                l, Physical and Engineering Sciences
            </span>378.2166 (Jan. 2020),
            p. 20190054. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps=">doi</span></span>: 10.1098/rsta.2019.0054.
         </p><!-- l. 30 -->
         <p class=3D" noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper reviews some of the
                        challenges posed by the huge growth of experimental data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generated by the new generation of=
                    large-scale experiments at UK national
                    facilities at the Rutherford
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Appleton Laboratory (RAL) site at =
                    Harwell near Oxford. Such =E2=80=98Big Scientific Data=E2=80=99
                    comes from the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Diamond Light Source and Electron =
                    Microscopy Facilities, the ISIS Neutron and
                    Muon Facility and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the UK=E2=80=99s Central Laser Fac=
                    ility. Increasingly, scientists are now required to use
                    advanced machine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learning and other AI technologies=
                    both to automate parts of the data pipeline
                    and to help find
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new scientific discoveries in the =
                    analysis of their data. For commercially
                    important applications,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such as object recognition, natura=
                    l language processing and automatic
                    translation, deep learning has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">made dramatic breakthroughs. Googl=
                    e=E2=80=99s DeepMind has now used the deep learning
                    technology to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">develop their AlphaFold tool to ma=
                    ke predictions for protein folding. Remarkably,
                    it has been able
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to achieve some spectacular result=
                    s for this specific scientific problem. Can
                    deep learning be similarly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">transformative for other scientifi=
                    c problems? After a brief review of some
                    initial applications of machine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learning at the RAL, we focus on c=
                    hallenges and opportunities for AI in advancing
                    materials science.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Finally, we discuss the importance=
                    of developing some realistic machine learning
                    benchmarks using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Big Scientific Data coming from se=
                    veral different scientific domains. We conclude
                    with some initial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">examples of our =E2=80=98scientifi=
                    c machine learning=E2=80=99 benchmark suite and of the research
                    challenges these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benchmarks will enable. </span>
                <br= class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">This
                        article is part of a discussion meeting issue =E2=80=98Numer=
                        ical algorithms for high-performance
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational science=E2=80=99.</s= pan>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Higham2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-222" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Higham, Desmond J., Nicholas J. Higham, and =
            Srikara Pranesh. =E2=80=9CRandom Matrices Generating
            Large Growth in LU Factorization with Pivoting=E2=80=9D. In: (2=
            020). MIMS EPrint: MIMS
            EPrint:2020.13. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-c=aps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/27=64/1/paper.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths.=
                    manchester.ac.uk/2764/1/paper.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We identify a class of random,
                dense </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"294" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">=
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.o=rg/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>n</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bi=n">=C3=97</mo>
                            <mi>n</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices for which LU factorization with =
                    any form of pivoting produces a growth factor
                    of at least</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"295" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c2F"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mn class==3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mstyle class=3D"t=ext">
                                        <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c67"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mtext>
                                        </mjx-m= style>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                            </m= jx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>n</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo=>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mn>4</mn>
                                        <mstyle class=3D"=text">
                                            <mtext>log</mtext>
                                        </mstyle>
                                        <mi>n</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-cl=ose">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">for large n with high probability. The co=
                    ndition number of the matrices can be
                    arbitrarily chosen and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large growth also happens for the =
                    transpose. No previous matrices with all these
                    properties were known.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The matrices can be generated by t=
                    he MATLAB function </span><span class=3D"cmtt-9">gallery(=E2=80=99randsvd=
                    =E2=80=99,..)</span><span class=3D"cmr-9">, and they are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formed as the product of two rando=
                    m orthogonal matrices from the Haar
                    distribution with a diagonal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix having only one diagonal en=
                    try different from 1, which lies between 0 and
                    1 (the =E2=80=9Done small
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">singular value=E2=80=9D case). Our=
                    explanation for the large growth uses the fact that
                    the maximum absolute
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">value of any element of a Haar dis=
                    tributed orthogonal matrix tends to be
                    relatively small for large</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"296" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>n=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We verify the behavior numerically=
                    , finding that for partial pivoting the actual
                    growth is significantly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">larger than the lower bound, and m=
                    uch larger than the growth observed for random
                    matrices with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">elements from the uniform </span>
                <= !-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CH=TML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"297" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; po=sition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mr= ow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5B"></mjx-c>=
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30">
                                            </mjx-c=>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C">
                                            <= /mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31="></mjx-c></mjx-mn></mjx-mrow><mjx-mo class=3D" mjx-n
                                            MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c5D">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">[
                </= mo>
                <mrow>
                    <mn>0</mn>
                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                    <mn>1</mn>
                </mrow>
                <mo=class=3D"MathClass-close">]</mo>
                </mrow></math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-con= tainer>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">or standard normal distributions. We show=
                    more generally that a rank-1 perturbation to
                    an orthogonal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix producing large growth for =
                    any form of pivoting also generates large
                    growth under reasonable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">assumptions. Finally, we demonstra=
                    te that GMRES-based iterative refinement can
                    provide stable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solutions to </span><!-- l. 30 -->=
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"298" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c3D">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">=
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.o=rg/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">Ax</mi>
                            <= mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                <mi>b</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </m= jx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">when large growth occurs in low precision=
                            LU factors, even when standard iterative
                            refinement cannot.</span>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Higham2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-223" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Higham, Nicholas J. and Xiaobo Liu. =E2=80=
            =9CA Multiprecision Derivative-Free Schur-Parlett
            Algorithm for Computing Matrix Functions=E2=80=9D. In: (2020). =
            MIMS EPrint: MIMS EPrint:2020.19.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/2781/1/paper.pdf"=class=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk=
                            /2781/1/paper.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Schur=E2=80=93Parlett algorithm,
                implemented in MATLAB as </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container= class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmen=u_counter=3D"299" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-mat= h class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I">=
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display==3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"i=nline">
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">funm</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx= -container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computes a function </span>
                        <!-- l.=
 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" t=abindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"300" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position=: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow spa=ce=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                            </= mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close"=>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assis= tive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w=3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>f</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"Mat=hClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>A</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</= mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">of an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container= class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmen=u_counter=3D"301" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-mat= h class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-b=in" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx= -math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>n</mi>
                                        <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                        <mi>n</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx= -container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-containe= r class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtme=nu_counter=3D"302" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-ma= th class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml uns=electable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/M=ath/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>A</mi>
                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-c= ontainer>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">by using the Schur decomposition and a bl=
                                            ock recurrence of Parlett. The algorithm
                                            requires the ability
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to compute </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <m= jx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex==3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"303" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relat=ive;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mi>f</mi>
                                                </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                                                </mjx-container>
                                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and its derivatives, and it requires that=
                                                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
