                                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                    tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"304" style=3D"font-size: 1=17.4%;
                                                    position: relative;">
                                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true="><mjx-mi class=3D"
                                                        mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-m= i>
                                                    </mjx-math>
                                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mat= h xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                            display=3D"inline">
                                                            <mi>f</mi=></math>
                                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                </mjx-container>
                                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">has a Taylor series expansion with a suit=
                                                    ably large radius of convergence. We develop a
                                                    version of
                                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Schur=E2=80=93Parlett algorith=
                                                    m that requires only function values and uses higher
                                                    precision arithmetic to
                                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluate </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                <mjx= -container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                    tabindex=3D"=0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"305" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                                    position: relative;="><mjx-math class=3D" MJX-TEX"
                                                    aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">=
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                    </mjx-math>
                                                    <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.o=rg/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                            display=3D"inline">
                                                            <mi>f</mi>
                                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mm= l>
                                                        </mjx-container>
                                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">on the diagonal blocks of order greater t=
                                                            han </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_=0"
                                                            jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"306"
                                                            style=3D"font-size=: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"t=rue">
                                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mn>
                                                                </mj= x-math>
                                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"
                                                                    display=3D"inline"><math
                                                                        xmln=s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                                                        </mat= h>
                                                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                        </mjx-container>
                                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">(if there are any) of the reordered and b=
                                                            locked Schur form. The key idea is to compute
                                                            by diagonalization
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the function of a small random dia=
                                                            gonal perturbation of each triangular block,
                                                            where the perturbation
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ensures that diagonalization will =
                                                            succeed. This multiprecision Schur=E2=80=93Parlett
                                                            algorithm is applicable to
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arbitrary functions </span>
                                                        <!-- l.=
 30 -->
                                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                                            jax=3D"CHTML" t=abindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"307"
                                                            style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position=: relative;">
                                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                            </mjx-math>
                                                            <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                                                <math xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                    display=3D"inline">
                                                                    <mi>f</mi>
                                                                </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
                                                        </mjx-container>
                                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">and, like the original Schur=E2=80=93Parl=
                                                            ett algorithm, it generally behaves in a numerically
                                                            stable fashion.
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Our algorithm is inspired by Davie=
                                                            s=E2=80=99s randomized approximate diagonalization
                                                            method, but we explain
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">why that is not a reliable numeric=
                                                            al method for computing matrix functions. We
                                                            apply our algorithm
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to the matrix Mittag=E2=80=93Leffl=
                                                            er function and show that it yields results of accuracy
                                                            similar to, and in
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">some cases much greater than, the =
                                                            state of the art algorithm for this function.
                                                            The algorithm will be
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">useful for evaluating any matrix f=
                                                            unction for which the derivatives of the
                                                            underlying function are not
                                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">readily available or accurately co=
                                                            mputable.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Higham2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-224" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Higham, Nicholas J. and Theo Mary. =E2=80=9C=
            Sharper Probabilistic Backward Error Analysis for Basic
            Linear Algebra Kernels with Random Data=E2=80=9D. In: (2020). <= span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">issn</span></span>: 1749=
                -9097. MIMS EPrint: MIMS
                EPrint:2020.4. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">url</span></span>: <a
                    href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/274=3/1/paper.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                        class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths.m=
                        anchester.ac.uk/2743/1/paper.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Standard backward error analyses
                for numerical linear algebra algorithms provide worst-case
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bounds that can significantly over=
                    estimate the backward error. Our recent
                    probabilistic error analysis,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which assumes rounding errors to b=
                    e independent random variables [SIAM J. Sci.
                    Comput., 41 (2019),
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">pp. A2815=E2=80=93A2835], contains=
                    smaller constants but its bounds can still be
                    pessimistic. We perform a new
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">probabilistic error analysis that =
                    assumes both the data and the rounding errors
                    to be random variables
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and assumes only mean independence=
                    . We prove that for data with zero or small
                    mean we can relax the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">existing probabilistic bounds of o=
                    rder </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"308" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45E TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45F TEX-I">
                                    </= mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-mat= h>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"=http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"i=talic">sqrtnu</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">to much sharper bounds of order </span>
                <!= -- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHT=ML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"309" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; pos=ition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi=class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D462 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-ma= th>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>u</mi>
                                </math></= mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which are independent of </span>
                    <!= -- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHT=ML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"310" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; pos=ition: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi=class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-ma= th>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>n</mi>
                                    </math></= mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Our fundamental result is for summ=
                            ation and we use it to derive results for inner
                            products, matrix=E2=80=93vector
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">products, and matrix=E2=80=93matri=
                            x products. The analysis answers the open question of
                            why random data
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed on </span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -=
-><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabind=
ex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"311" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rel=
ative;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mrow><mjx-mo =
class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5B"></mjx-c></mjx-mo><m=
jx-mrow><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></=
mjx-c></mjx-mo><mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c></m=
jx-mn><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C"></mjx=
-c></mjx-mo><mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></=
mjx-c></mjx-mn></mjx-mrow><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close"><mjx-c cl=
ass=3D"mjx-c5D"></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-mrow></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml u=
nselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998=
/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"><mrow><mo class=3D"MathClass-open">[</mo><=
mrow><mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo><mn>1</mn><mo class=3D"MathC=
lass-punc">,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mo class=3D"MathClass-close">]</mo></mro=
w></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">leads to smaller error growth for these k=
ernels than random data distributed on </span>
            <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"312" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c5B"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c30"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                            <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5D"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathC=lass-open">[</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mn>0</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                            <mn>1</= mn>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">]</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We also propose a new algorithm fo=
                            r multiplying two matrices that transforms the
                            rows of the first
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix to have zero mean and we sh=
                            ow that it can achieve significantly more
                            accurate results than
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">standard matrix multiplication.</s= pan>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hrga2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-225" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hrga, Timotej and Janez Povh. =E2=80=9CMADAM=
            : A parallel exact solver for Max-Cut based on
            semidefinite programming and ADMM=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). ar=
            Xiv: 2010.07839 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present MADAM, a parallel
                semidefinite based exact solver for Max-Cut, a problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of finding the cut with maximum we=
                    ight in a given graph. The algorithm uses
                    branch and bound

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paradigm that applies alternating =
                    direction method of multipliers as the bounding
                    routine to solve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the basic semidefinite relaxation =
                    strengthened by a subset of hypermetric
                    inequalities. The benefit
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the new approach is less comput=
                    ationally expensive update rule for the dual
                    variable with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">respect to the inequality constrai=
                    nts. We provide theoretical convergence of the
                    algorithm, as well
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as extensive computational experim=
                    ents with this method, to show that our
                    algorithm outperformes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">current state-of-the-art approache=
                    s. Furthermore, by combining algorithmic
                    ingredients from the serial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm we develop an efficient =
                    distributed parallel solver based on
                    MPI.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-226" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hu, Jonathan et al. <span class=3D"cmti-10">=
                Compare linear-system solver and preconditioner
                stacks with emphasis on
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">GPU performance and propose phas=
                e-2 NGP solvervelopment pathway</span>. Tech.
            rep. Office of
            Advanced Scientific Computing Research, Office of Science, US D=
            epartment of Energy, 2020.
            eprint: ECP-Q2-FY20. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">url</span></span>: <a
                href=3D"https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/=1630801" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.osti.gov/servlet=
                    s/purl/1630801</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The goal of the ExaWind project
                is to enable predictive simulations of wind farms comprised
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of many megawatt-scale turbines si=
                    tuated in complex terrain. Predictive
                    simulations will require
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational fluid dynamics (CFD)=
                    simulations for which the mesh resolves the
                    geometry of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the turbines and captures the rota=
                    tion and large deflections of blades. Whereas
                    such simulations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for a single turbine are arguably =
                    petascale class, multi-turbine wind farm
                    simulations will require
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exascale-class resources. The prim=
                    ary physics codes in the ExaWind project are
                    Nalu-Wind, which is an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unstructured-grid solver for the a=
                    coustically incompressible Navier-Stokes
                    equations, and OpenFAST,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which is a whole-turbine simulatio=
                    n code. The Nalu-Wind model consists of the
                    mass-continuity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Poisson-type equation for pressure=
                    and a momentum equation for the velocity. For
                    such modeling
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches, simulation times are d=
                    ominated by linear-system setup and solution
                    for the continuity and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">momentum systems. For the ExaWind =
                    challenge problem, the moving meshes greatly
                    affect overall
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solver costs as reinitialization o=
                    f matrices and recomputation of preconditioners
                    is required at every
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time step.</span><br class=3D"newl=ine"><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this
                    report we
                    evaluated GPU-performance baselines for the linear solvers i=
                    n the Trilinos and hypre
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solver stacks using two representa=
                    tive Nalu-Wind simulations: an atmospheric
                    boundary layer precursor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simulation on a structured mesh, a=
                    nd a fixed-wing simulation using unstructured
                    overset meshes.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Both strong-scaling and weak-scali=
                    ng experiments were conducted on the OLCF
                    supercomputer
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Summit and similar proxy clusters.=
                    We focused on the performance of
                    multi-threaded Gauss-Seidel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and two-stage Gauss-Seidel that ar=
                    e extensions of classical Gauss-Seidel; of
                    one-reduce GMRES,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a communication-reducing variant o=
                    f the Krylov GMRES; and algebraic multigrid
                    methods that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">incorporate the afore-mentioned me=
                    thods. The team has established that AMG
                    methods are capable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of solving linear systems arising =
                    from the fixed-wing overset meshes on CPU, a
                    critical intermediate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">result for ExaWind FY20 Q3 and Q4 =
                    milestones. For the fixed-wing strong-scaling
                    study (model with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">3M grid-points), the team identifi=
                    ed that Nalu-Wind simulations with the new
                    Trilinos and hypre
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solvers scale to modest GPU counts=
                    , maintaining above 70% efficiency up to 6
                    GPUs. However, there
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">still remain significant bottlenec=
                    ks to performance: matrix assembly (hypre), AMG
                    setup (hypre and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Trilinos) In the weak-scaling expe=
                    riments (going from 0.4M to 211M gridpoints),
                    it=E2=80=99s shown that the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solver apply phases are faster on =
                    GPUs, but that Nalu-Wind simulation times grow,
                    primarily due to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the multigrid-setup process.</span=><br class=3D"newline"><span
                        class=3D"cmr-9">Finally, based on the report o=
                        utcomes, we propose a linear solver path-forward for the
                        remainder of the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ExaWind project. Near term, the NR=
                        EL team will continue their work on GPU-based
                        linear-system
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">assembly. They will also investiga=
                        te how the use of alternatives to the NVIDIA
                        UVM (unified virtual
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory) paradigm affects performan=
                        ce. Longer term, the NREL team will evaluate
                        algorithmic
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance on other types of acce=
                        lerators and merge their improvements back to
                        the main hypre
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">repository branch. Near term, the =
                        Trilinos team will address performance
                        bottlenecks identified in this
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">milestone, such as implementing a =
                        GPU-based segregated momentum solve and reusing
                        matrix graphs
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">across linear-system assembly phas=
                        es. Longer term, the Trilinos team will do
                        detailed analysis and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization of multigrid setup.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hu2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-227" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hu, Xiaozhe, Kaiyi Wu, and Ludmil T. Zikatan=
            ov. =E2=80=9CA Posteriori Error Estimates for
            Multilevel
            Methods for Graph Laplacians=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: =
            2007.00189v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we study a
                posteriori error estimators which aid multilevel iterative s=
                olvers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for linear systems of graph Laplac=
                    ians. In earlier works such estimates were
                    computed by solving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a perturbed global optimization pr=
                    oblem, which could be computationally
                    expensive. We propose a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">novel strategy to compute these es=
                    timates by constructing a Helmholtz
                    decomposition on the graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based on a spanning tree and the c=
                    orresponding cycle space. To compute the error
                    estimator, we solve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiently a linear system on the=
                    spanning tree and then a least-squares problem
                    on the cycle space.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">As we show, such estimator has a n=
                    early-linear computational complexity for
                    sparse graphs under
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">certain assumptions. Numerical exp=
                    eriments are presented to demonstrate the
                    efficacy of the proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Huang2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-228" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Huang, Kevin, Junyu Zhang, and Shuzhong Zhan=
            g. =E2=80=9CCubic Regularized Newton Method for
            Saddle Point Models: a Global and Local Convergence Analysis=E2=
            =80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). arXiv:
            2008.09919v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we propose a cubic
                regularized Newton (CRN) method for solving convex-concave
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">saddle point problems (SPP). At ea=
                    ch iteration, a cubic regularized saddle point
                    subproblem is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">constructed and solved, which prov=
                    ides a search direction for the iterate. With
                    properly chosen
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stepsizes, the method is shown to =
                    converge to the saddle point with global linear
                    and local superlinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rates, if the saddle p=
                    oint function is gradient Lipschitz and
                    strongly-convex-strongly-concave.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In the case that the function is m=
                    erely convex-concave, we propose a homotopy
                    continuation (or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">path-following) method. Under a Li=
                    pschitz-type error bound condition, we present
                    an iteration complexity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bound of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx= -container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"=0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"313" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;="><mjx-math class=3D"
                    MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc=mjx-b mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    <mjx-mfenc= ed>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-m= row>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-n qopname">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c6E">

                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">

                        </mjx-container=>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">to reach an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-con= tainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            c=txtmenu_counter=3D"314" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through a homotopy continuation ap=

                                                <mjx-frac>

                                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>

                                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D703 TEX-I">=
                                                                            </mjx-c>
                                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>

                                                                    =88=95</mo>

                                                            <mrow>

                                                                <mo= class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>

                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">under a H=C3=B6lderian-type error bound c=
                                        ondition involving a parameter </span>

                                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"316" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">

                            </m= jx-mi>

                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>=
                                        =F0=9D=9C=83</mi>
                                </math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">(</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container clas=s=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_cou=nter=3D"317" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math cla=ss=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c3C">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space==3D"4">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D703 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"=mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3C"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>=
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">

                                        </mjx-= mn>

                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                    <ma= th xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mn>0</m= n>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">&lt;</mo>
                                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=83</mi>

                            </mjx-container>=
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">).</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Huang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-229" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Huang, Xunpeng et al. =E2=80=9CSPAN: A Stoch=
            astic Projected Approximate Newton Method=E2=80=9D. In:
            (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.03687 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC=
                ]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Second-order optimization methods
                have desirable convergence properties. However, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exact Newton method requires expen=
                    sive computation for the Hessian and its
                    inverse. In this paper,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we propose SPAN, a novel approxima=
                    te and fast Newton method. SPAN computes the
                    inverse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the Hessian matrix via low-rank=
                    approximation and stochastic Hessian-vector
                    products. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments on multiple benchmark =
                    datasets demonstrate that SPAN outperforms
                    existing first-order
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and second-order optimization meth=
                    ods in terms of the convergence wall-clock
                    time. Furthermore,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we provide a theoretical analysis =
                    of the per-iteration complexity, the
                    approximation error, and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rate. Both the theoret=
                    ical analysis and experimental results show
                    that our proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method achieves a better trade-off=
                    between the convergence rate and the
                    per-iteration efficiency.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Huo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-230" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Huo, Zenan et al. =E2=80=9CDesigning an effi=
            cient parallel spectral clustering algorithm on
            multi-core
            processors in Julia=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journ=
                al of Parallel and Distributed Computing </span>138
            (Apr. 2020),
            pp. 211=E2=80=93221. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.jpdc.2020.01.003.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Spectral clustering is widely
                used in data mining, machine learning and other fields. It
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can identify the arbitrary shape o=
                    f a sample space and converge to the global
                    optimal solution.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Compared with the traditional k-me=
                    ans algorithm, the spectral clustering
                    algorithm has stronger
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adaptability to data and better cl=
                    ustering results. However, the computation of
                    the algorithm is quite
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">expensive. In this paper, an effic=
                    ient parallel spectral clustering algorithm on
                    multi-core processors in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Julia language is proposed, an=
                    d we refer to it as juPSC. The Julia language
                    is a high-performance,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">open-source programming language. =
                    The juPSC is composed of three procedures: (1)
                    calculating the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">affinity matrix, (2) calculating t=
                    he eigenvectors, and (3) conducting k-means
                    clustering. Procedures

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(1) and (3) are computed by the ef=
                    ficient parallel algorithm, and the COO format
                    is used to compress
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the affinity matrix. Two groups of=
                    experiments are conducted to verify the
                    accuracy and efficiency
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the juPSC. Experimental results=
                    indicate that (1) the juPSC achieves speedups
                    of approximately</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"318" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space==3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mover space=3D"3">
                            <mj= x-over style=3D"padding-bottom: 0.105em; padding-left: 0.25em; margin-botto=m:
                                -0.565em;">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n" style=3D"width: 0px; margin-left: -0.=25em;">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2DC"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-over>
                                <mjx-base>
                                    <mjx= -mrow>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                        </m= jx-mrow>
                                </mjx-base>
                        </mjx-mover>
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c38"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on=" display=3D" inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" dis=play=3D"inline">
                            <mn>14</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            <mover ac=cent=3D"true">
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo accent=3D"true">=CC=83</mo>
                                </mover=>
                                <mn>8</mn>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mm= l>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">on a 24-core CPU and that (2) the serial =
                    version of the juPSC is faster than the Python
                    version of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scikit-learn. Moreover, the struct=
                    ure and functions of the juPSC are designed
                    considering modularity,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which is convenient for combinatio=
                    n and further optimization with other parallel
                    computing platforms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Hussain2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-231" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Hussain, Md Taufique et al. =E2=80=9CCommuni=
            cation-Avoiding and Memory-Constrained Sparse
            Matrix-Matrix Multiplication at Extreme Scale=E2=80=9D. In: (Oc=
            t. 2020). arXiv: 2010.08526 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse matrix-matrix
                multiplication (SpGEMM) is a widely used kernel in various g=
                raph,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scientific computing and machine l=
                    earning algorithms. In this paper, we consider
                    SpGEMMs performed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on hundreds of thousands of proces=
                    sors generating trillions of nonzeros in the
                    output matrix.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Distributed SpGEMM at this extreme=
                    scale faces two key challenges: (1) high
                    communication cost
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and (2) inadequate memory to gener=
                    ate the output. We address these challenges
                    with an integrated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication-avoiding and memory-=
                    constrained SpGEMM algorithm that scales to
                    262,144 cores
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(more than 1 million hardware thre=
                    ads) and can multiply sparse matrices of any
                    size as long as inputs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and a fraction of output fit in th=
                    e aggregated memory. As we go from 16,384 cores
                    to 262,144 cores on a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Cray XC40 supercomputer, the new S=
                    pGEMM algorithm runs 10x faster when
                    multiplying large-scale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">protein-similarity matrices.</span=>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Iakymchuk2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-232" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Iakymchuk, Roman et al. =E2=80=9CReproducibi=
            lity of Parallel Preconditioned Conjugate
            Gradient in Hybrid Programming Environments=E2=80=9D. In: (2020=
            ). HAL: 02427795. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</= span></span>:
                <a href=3D"https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-02427795" class==3D"url"><span
                        class=3D"cmtt-10">https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-02427=
                        795</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Preconditioned Conjugate
                Gradient method is often employed for the solution of linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems of equations arising in nu=
                    merical simulations of physical phenomena.
                    While being widely used,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the solver is also known for its l=
                    ack of accuracy while computing the residual.
                    In this article, we propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">two algorithmic solutions that ori=
                    ginate from the ExBLAS project to enhance the
                    accuracy of the solver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as well as to ensure its reproduci=
                    bility in a hybrid MPI + OpenMP tasks
                    programming environment.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">One is based on ExBLAS and preserv=
                    es every bit of information until the final
                    rounding, while the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">other relies upon floating-point e=
                    xpansions and, hence, expands the intermediate
                    precision. Instead
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of converting the entire solver in=
                    to its ExBLAS-related implementation, we
                    identify those parts that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">violate reproducibility/non-associ=
                    ativity, secure them, and combine this with the
                    sequential executions.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">These algorithmic strategies are r=
                    einforced with programmability suggestions to
                    assure deterministic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">executions. Finally, we verify the=
                    se approaches on two modern HPC systems: both
                    versions deliver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reproducible number of iterations,=
                    residuals, direct errors, and vector-solutions
                    for the overhead of less
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">than 37.7 % on 768 cores.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Isotton2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-233" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Isotton, Giovanni, Carlo Janna, and Massimo =
            Bernaschi. =E2=80=9CA GPU-accelerated adaptive
            FSAI preconditioner for massively parallel simulations=E2=80=9D=
            . In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.14175
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The solution of linear systems of
                equations is a central task in a number of scientific and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">engineering applications. In many =
                    cases the solution of linear systems may take
                    most of the simulation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time thus representing a major bot=
                    tleneck in the further development of
                    scientific and technical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">software. For large scale simulati=
                    ons, nowadays accounting for several millions
                    or even billions of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unknowns, it is quite common to re=
                    sort to preconditioned iterative solvers for
                    exploiting their low
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory requirements and, at least =
                    potential, parallelism. Approximate inverses
                    have been shown
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to be robust and effective precond=
                    itioners in various contexts. In this work, we
                    show how adaptive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">FSAI, an approximate inverse chara=
                    cterized by a very high degree of parallelism,
                    can be successfully
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implemented on a distributed memor=
                    y computer equipped with GPU accelerators.
                    Taking advantage
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of GPUs in adaptive FSAI set-up is=
                    not a trivial task, nevertheless we show
                    through an extensive

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical experimentation how the =
                    proposed approach outperforms more traditional
                    preconditioners
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and results in a close-to-ideal be=
                    haviour in challenging linear algebra
                    problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Iwashita2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-234" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Iwashita, Takeshi, Kengo Suzuki, and Takeshi=
            Fukaya. =E2=80=9CAn Integer Arithmetic-Based Sparse
            Linear Solver Using a GMRES Method and Iterative Refinement=E2=
            =80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv:
            2009.07495 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we develop a
                (preconditioned) GMRES solver based on integer arithmetic,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and introduce an iterative refinem=
                    ent framework for the solver. We describe the
                    data format for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">coefficient matrix and vectors for=
                    the solver that is based on integer or
                    fixed-point numbers. To avoid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overflow in calculations, we intro=
                    duce initial scaling and logical shifts
                    (adjustments) of operands in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arithmetic operations. We present =
                    the approach for operand shifts, considering
                    the characteristics of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the GMRES algorithm. Numerical tes=
                    ts demonstrate that the integer
                    arithmetic-based solver with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iterative refinement has comparabl=
                    e solver performance in terms of convergence to
                    the standard solver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based on floating-point arithmetic=
                    . Moreover, we show that preconditioning is
                    important, not only for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improving convergence but also red=
                    ucing the risk of overflow.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Jakovetic2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-235" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Jakovetic, Dusan, Dragana Bajovic, et al. =
            =E2=80=9CPrimal-Dual Methods for Large-Scale and
            Distributed Convex Optimization and Data Analytics=E2=80=9D. In=
            : <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the IEEE
            </span>(2020),
            pp. 1=E2=80=9316. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/jproc.2020.3007395.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The augmented Lagrangian method
                (ALM) is a classical optimization tool that solves a given
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">=E2=80=9Cdifficult=E2=80=9D (const=
                    rained) problem via finding solutions of a sequence of =E2=80=9Ceasier=E2=
                    =80=9D
                    (often unconstrained)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subproblems with respect to the or=
                    iginal (primal) variable, wherein constraints
                    satisfaction is controlled
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">via the so-called dual variables. =
                    ALM is highly flexible with respect to how
                    primal subproblems can be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solved, giving rise to a plethora =
                    of different primal-dual methods. The powerful
                    ALM mechanism has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">recently proved to be very success=
                    ful in various large-scale and distributed
                    applications. In addition,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">several significant advances have =
                    appeared, primarily on precise complexity
                    results with respect to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational and communication co=
                    sts in the presence of inexact updates and
                    design and analysis of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">novel optimal methods for distribu=
                    ted consensus optimization. We provide a
                    tutorial-style introduction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to ALM and its variants for solvin=
                    g convex optimization problems in large-scale
                    and distributed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">settings. We describe control-theo=
                    retic tools for the algorithms=E2=80=99 analysis and
                    design, survey recent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results, and provide novel insight=
                    s into the context of two emerging
                    applications: federated learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and distributed energy trading.</s= pan>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Jakovetic2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-236" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Jakovetic, Dusan, Natasa Krejic, et al. =E2=
            =80=9CDistributed Fixed Point Method for Solving Systems
            of Linear Algebraic Equations=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). arXiv:=
            2001.03968 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a class of iterative
                fully distributed fixed point methods to solve a system of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear equations, such that each a=
                    gent in the network holds one of the equations
                    of the system. Under
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a generic directed, strongly conne=
                    cted network, we prove a convergence result
                    analogous to the one
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for fixed point methods in the cla=
                    ssical, centralized, framework: the proposed
                    method converges to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the solution of the system of line=
                    ar equations at a linear rate. We further
                    explicitly quantify the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rate in terms of the linear system=
                    and the network parameters. Next, we show that
                    the algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provably works under time-varying =
                    directed networks provided that the underlying
                    graph is connected
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">over bounded iteration intervals, =
                    and we establish a linear convergence rate for
                    this setting as well.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">A set of numerical results is pres=
                    ented, demonstrating practical benefits of the
                    method over existing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">alternatives.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Jalving2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-237" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Jalving, Jordan, Sungho Shin, and Victor M. =
            Zavala. =E2=80=9CA Graph-Based Modeling Abstraction
            for Optimization: Concepts and Implementation in Plasmo.jl=E2=
            =80=9D. In: (June 2020). arXiv:
            2006.05378v2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a general graph-based
                modeling abstraction for optimization that we call an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">OptiGraph. Under this abstraction,=
                    any optimization problem is treated as a
                    hierarchical hypergraph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in which nodes represent optimizat=
                    ion subproblems and edges represent
                    connectivity between such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subproblems. The abstraction enabl=
                    es the modular construction of highly complex
                    models in an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intuitive manner, facilitates the =
                    use of graph analysis tools (to perform
                    partitioning, aggregation,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and visualization tasks), and faci=
                    litates communication of structures to
                    decomposition algorithms. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provide an open-source implementat=
                    ion of the abstraction in the Julia-based
                    package Plasmo.jl. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provide tutorial examples and larg=
                    e application case studies to illustrate the
                    capabilities.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Jin2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-238" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Jin, Qiujiang and Aryan Mokhtari. =E2=80=9CN=
            on-asymptotic Superlinear Convergence of Standard
            Quasi-Newton Methods=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.136=
            07v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we study the
                non-asymptotic superlinear convergence rate of DFP and BFGS,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which are two well-known quasi-New=
                    ton methods. The asymptotic superlinear
                    convergence rate of these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quasi-Newton methods has been exte=
                    nsively studied, but their explicit finite time
                    local convergence
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rate has not been established yet.=
                    In this paper, we provide a finite time
                    (non-asymptotic) convergence
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis for BFGS and DFP methods =
                    under the assumptions that the objective
                    function is strongly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex, its gradient is Lipschitz =
                    continuous, and its Hessian is Lipschitz
                    continuous only in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">direction of the optimal solution.=
                    We show that in a local neighborhood of the
                    optimal solution, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iterates generated by both DFP and=
                    BFGS converge to the optimal solution at a
                    superlinear rate of</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"319" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-S=C"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClas=s-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mro= w>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S1">=

                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mfrac>

                                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">

jx-line><mjx-row><mjx-den><mjx-dstrut></mjx-dstrut><mjx-mrow size=3D" s">
                                                                    <mj= x-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                                        </m= jx-mrow>
                                                                        </mjx-den>
                                                                        </mjx-row>
                                                                        </mjx-dtable>
                                                                        </mjx-dbox>
                                            </mjx-frac>
                                        </mjx-mfrac>=
