                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2=9 TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-b=in" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn cl=ass=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-m= row>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S=1"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mro= w>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-ope=n">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mfrac>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mi>k</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                                </mfrac=>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mi>k</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"M=athClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">=
                                    )</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-=9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"320" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>k</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            <= /mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the number of iterations. In particula=
                                    r, for a specific choice of the local
                                    neighborhood, both DFP and
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">BFGS converge to the optimal solut=
                                    ion at the rate of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"321" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-open">
                                                <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S1">
                                                    </mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mfrac>
                                                    <mjx-frac=>
                                                        <mjx-num>
                                                            <mjx-nstrut></mjx-nstrut>
                                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mj=x-n">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mn>
                                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathC=lass-punc">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2E"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c38">
                                                                    </mjx-c>
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-m= n>
                                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                                        </mjx-num>
                                                        <mjx-dbox>
                                                            <mjx-dtable>
                                                                <mjx-line></mjx-line>
                                                                <mjx-row>=
                                                                    <mjx-den>
                                                                        <mjx-dstrut></mjx-dstrut>
                                                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I">
                                                                                </mjx-c>
                                                                            </mjx-mi>
                                                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                                                        </mjx-den=>
                                                                </mjx-row>
                                                            </mjx-dtable>
                                                        </mjx-dbox>
                                                        </mjx-frac>
                                                </mjx-mfrac>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-m= o class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-close">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S1">
                                                    </mjx-c=>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX=-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F TEX-S1">
                                                </mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" spac=e=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive= -mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.or=g/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(=
                                                </mo>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mfrac>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mn>0</mn>
                                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">.</mo>
                                                            <mn>85=
                                                            </mn>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi>k</mi>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                    </mfrac>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-cl=ose">)</mo>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mi>k</mi>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                            <mn>2=
                                            </mn>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Our theoretical guarantee is one o=
                                    f the first results that provide a
                                    non-asymptotic superlinear convergence
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rate for DFP and BFGS quasi-Newton=
                                    methods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Jung2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-239" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Jung, Yoon Mo, Joyce Jiyoung Whang, and Sang=
            woon Yun. =E2=80=9CSparse probabilistic
            K-means=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Applied Mathemati=
                cs and Computation </span>382 (Oct. 2020), p. 125328.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1016/j.amc.2020.125328.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The goal of clustering is to
                partition a set of data points into groups of similar data p=
                oints,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">called clusters. Clustering algori=
                    thms can be classified into two categories:
                    hard and soft clustering.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Hard clustering assigns each data =
                    point to one cluster exclusively. On the other
                    hand, soft clustering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">allows probabilistic assignments t=
                    o clusters. In this paper, we propose a new
                    model which combines the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benefits of these two models: clar=
                    ity of hard clustering and probabilistic
                    assignments of soft clustering.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Since the majority of data usually=
                    have a clear association, only a few points
                    may require a probabilistic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">interpretation. Thus, we apply the=
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"=jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"322" style=3D"font-size: 1=17.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true="><mjx-msub><mjx-mrow><mjx-mi class=3D" mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2195">
                            </= mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                <m= jx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                            </mjx-c=>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                            </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml=unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/199=8/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=E2=86=95</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mro= w>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                    </mrow>
                            </msub>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">norm constraint to impose sparsity on pro=
                    babilistic assignments. Moreover, we also
                    incorporate outlier
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detection in our clustering model =
                    to simultaneously detect outliers which can
                    cause serious problems in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">statistical analyses. To optimize =
                    the model, we introduce an alternating
                    minimization method and prove
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its convergence. Numerical experim=
                    ents and comparisons with existing models show
                    the soundness and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effectiveness of the proposed mode=
                    l.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Jyothi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-240" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Jyothi, R. and P. Babu. =E2=80=9CPIANO: A Fa=
            st Parallel Iterative Algorithm for Multinomial and
            Sparse Multinomial Logistic Regression=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020=
            ). arXiv: 2002.09133 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ML]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Multinomial Logistic Regression
                is a well-studied tool for classification and has been widel=
                y
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">used in fields like image processi=
                    ng, computer vision and, bioinformatics, to
                    name a few. Under a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">supervised classification scenario=
                    , a Multinomial Logistic Regression model
                    learns a weight vector to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">differentiate between any two clas=
                    ses by optimizing over the likelihood
                    objective. With the advent of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">big data, the inundation of data h=
                    as resulted in large dimensional weight vector
                    and has also given rise
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to a huge number of classes, which=
                    makes the classical methods applicable for
                    model estimation not
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computationally viable. To handle =
                    this issue, we here propose a parallel
                    iterative algorithm: Parallel

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Iterative Algorithm for MultiNomia=
                    l LOgistic Regression (PIANO) which is based on
                    the Majorization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Minimization procedure, and can pa=
                    rallely update each element of the weight
                    vectors. Further, we also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show that PIANO can be easily exte=
                    nded to solve the Sparse Multinomial Logistic
                    Regression problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">- an extensively studied problem b=
                    ecause of its attractive feature selection
                    property. In particular, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">work out the extension of PIANO to=
                    solve the Sparse Multinomial Logistic
                    Regression problem with l1
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and l0 regularizations. We also pr=
                    ove that PIANO converges to a stationary point
                    of the Multinomial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the Sparse Multinomial Logisti=
                    c Regression problems. Simulations were
                    conducted to compare
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PIANO with the existing methods, a=
                    nd it was found that the proposed algorithm
                    performs better than
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the existing methods in terms of s=
                    peed of convergence.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kahl2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-241" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kahl, Karsten and Bruno Lang. =E2=80=9COn th=
            e equivalence of the Hermitian eigenvalue problem and
            hypergraph edge elimination=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2=
            003.03145 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">It is customary to identify
                sparse matrices with the corresponding adjacency or incidenc=
                e
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph. For the solution of linear =
                    systems of equations using Gaussian
                    elimination, the representation by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its adjacency graph allows a symbo=
                    lic computation that can be used to predict
                    memory footprints and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">enables the determination of near-=
                    optimal elimination orderings based on
                    heuristics. The Hermitian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">eigenvalue problem on the other ha=
                    nd seems to evade such treatment at first
                    glance due to its inherent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iterative nature. In this paper we=
                    prove this assertion wrong by showing the
                    equivalence of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Hermitian eigenvalue problem with =
                    a symbolic edge elimination procedure. A
                    symbolic calculation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based on the incidence graph of th=
                    e matrix can be used in analogy to the symbolic
                    phase of Gaussian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">elimination to develop heuristics =
                    which reduce memory footprint and computations.
                    Yet, we also show
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that the question of an optimal el=
                    imination strategy remains NP-hard, in analogy
                    to the linear systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">case.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kamzolov2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-242" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kamzolov, Dmitry and Alexander Gasnikov. =E2=
            =80=9CNear-Optimal Hyperfast Second-Order Method
            for convex optimization and its Sliding=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 202=
            0). arXiv: 2002.09050 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we present a new
                Hyperfast Second-Order Method with convergence rate</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"323" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn clas=s=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mro= w>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>=
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>N</mi=>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                        <mn>5</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">up to a logarithmic factor for the convex=
                    function with Lipshitz </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"324" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        </m= jx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mn>3</mn>
                                </ma= th>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">rd
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">derivative. This method based on t=
                    wo ideas. The first comes from the superfast
                    second-order scheme
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of Yu. Nesterov (CORE Discussion P=
                    aper 2020/07, 2020). It allows implementing the
                    third-order
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scheme by solving subproblem using=
                    only the second-order oracle. This method
                    converges with rate</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"325" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn clas=s=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    </mjx-mro= w>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>=
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>N</mi=>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                        <mn>4</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"Ma=thClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The second idea comes from the wor=
                        k of Kamzolov et al. (arXiv:2002.01004). It is
                        the inexact near-optimal
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">third-order method. In this work, =
                        we improve its convergence and merge it with
                        the scheme of solving
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subproblem using only the second-o=
                        rder oracle. As a result, we get convergence
                        rate </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ct=xtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"326" st=yle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX=-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D="mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D="mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-= mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                                            </m= jx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>O</mi>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>=
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>N</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                        <mn>5</mn>
                                    </mrow>=
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx= -container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">up to a logarithmic factor. This converge=
                            nce rate is near-optimal and the best known up
                            to this
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">moment. Further, we investigate th=
                            e situation when there is a sum of two
                            functions and improve the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sliding framework from Kamzolov et=
                            al. (arXiv:2002.01004) for the second-order
                            methods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kamzolov2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-243" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kamzolov, Dmitry, Alexander Gasnikov, and Pa=
            vel Dvurechensky. =E2=80=9COn the Optimal
            Combination of Tensor Optimization Methods=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. =
            2020). arXiv: 2002.01004 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We consider the minimization
                problem of a sum of a number of functions having Lipshitz</s= pan>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"327" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>p=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-th
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">order derivatives with different L=
                    ipschitz constants. In this case, to accelerate
                    optimization, we propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a general framework allowing to ob=
                    tain near-optimal oracle complexity for each
                    function in the sum
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">separately, meaning, in particular=
                    , that the oracle for a function with lower
                    Lipschitz constant is called
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a smaller number of times. As a bu=
                    ilding block, we extend the current theory of
                    tensor methods and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show how to generalize near-optima=
                    l tensor methods to work with inexact tensor
                    step. Further, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">investigate the situation when the=
                    functions in the sum have Lipschitz
                    derivatives of a different order.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">For this situation, we propose a g=
                    eneric way to separate the oracle complexity
                    between the parts of

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the sum. Our method is not optimal=
                    , which leads to an open problem of the optimal
                    combination of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">oracles of a different order.
            </spa= n>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kanagasabapathi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-244" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kanagasabapathi, Somasundaram and MG Thushar=
            a. =E2=80=9CFORWARD AND BACKWARD
            STATIC ANALYSIS FOR CRITICAL NUMERICAL ACCURACY IN FLOATING POI=
            NT
            PROGRAMS=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Computer Science=
            </span>21.2 (Apr. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-cap=s">doi</span></span>:
            10.7494/csci.2020.21.2.3421.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this article, we introduce a
                new static analysis for numerical accuracy. We address
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the problem of determining the min=
                    imal accuracy on the inputs and on the
                    intermediary results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a program containing floating-p=
                    oint computations in order to ensure the
                    desired accuracy of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">outputs. The main approach is to c=
                    ombine a forward and backward static analysis,
                    done by abstract
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">interpretation. The backward analy=
                    sis computes the minimal accuracy needed for
                    the inputs and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intermediary results of the progra=
                    m in order to ensure the desired accuracy of
                    the results (as specified
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by the user). In practice, the inf=
                    ormation collected by our analysis may help
                    optimize the formats used
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to represent the values stored in =
                    the variables of the program or to select the
                    appropriate sensors. To
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">illustrate our analysis, we have s=
                    hown a prototype example with experimental
                    results.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kannan2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-245" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kannan, R. et al. =E2=80=9CScalable Knowledg=
            e
            Graph Analytics at 136 Petaflop/s=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"=cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020
                International
                Conference for
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">High Performance Computing, Netw=
                orking, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC =E2=80=9920.
            Los Alamitos, CA,
            USA: IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 61=E2=80=9373. <span= class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1109/S=
                C41405.2020.00010. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url<= /span></span>:
                    <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.=2020.00010" class=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputers=
                            ociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00010</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We are motivated by newly
                proposed methods for data mining large-scale corpora of scho=
                larly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">publications, such as the full bio=
                    medical literature, which may consist of tens
                    of millions of papers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">spanning decades of research. In t=
                    his setting, analysts seek to discover how
                    concepts relate to one
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">another. They construct graph repr=
                    esentations from annotated text databases and
                    then formulate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">relationship-mining problem as one=
                    of computing all-pairs shortest paths (APSP),
                    which becomes a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significant bottleneck. In this co=
                    ntext, we present a new high-performance
                    algorithm and implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the Floyd- Warshall algorithm f=
                    or distributed-memory parallel computers
                    accelerated by GPUs,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which we call DSNAPSHOT (Distribut=
                    ed Accelerated Semiring All-Pairs Shortest
                    Path). For our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">largest experiments, we ran DSNAPS=
                    HOT on a connected input graph with millions of
                    vertices using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">4,096 nodes (24,576 GPUs) of the O=
                    ak Ridge National Laboratory=E2=80=99s Summit
                    supercomputer system. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">find DSNAPSHOT achieves a sustaine=
                    d performance of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtM=enu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"328" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>=
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c36"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>=
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c22C5="></mjx-c></mjx-mo><mjx-mn class=3D" mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c31"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                    </mj= x-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml= unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/19=98/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>136</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=
                                =E2=8B=85</mo>
                            <mn>1015</mn>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">floating-point operations per second (136=
                    petaflop/s) at a parallel efficiency of 90%
                    under weak
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scaling and, in absolute speed, 70=
                    % of the best possible performance given our
                    computation (in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the single-precision tropical semi=
                    ring or =E2=80=9Dmin-plus=E2=80=9D algebra). Looking forward,
                    we believe this novel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">capability will enable the mining =
                    of scholarly knowledge corpora when embedded
                    and integrated into
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">artificial intelligence-driven nat=
                    ural language processing workflows at
                    scale.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kardos2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-246" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kardos, Juraj, Drosos Kourounis, and Olaf Sc=
            henk. =E2=80=9CReduced-Space Interior Point Methods
            in Power Grid Problems=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). arXiv: 2001.1=
            0815 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Due to critical environmental
                issues, the power systems have to accommodate a significant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">level of penetration of renewable =
                    generation which requires smart approaches to
                    the power grid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">control. Associated optimal contro=
                    l problems are large-scale nonlinear
                    optimization problems with up to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hundreds of millions of variables =
                    and constraints. The interior point methods
                    become computationally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intractable, mainly due to the sol=
                    ution of large linear systems. This document
                    addresses the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational bottlenecks of the i=
                    nterior point method during the solution of the
                    security constrained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimal power flow problems by app=
                    lying reduced space quasi-Newton IPM, which
                    could utilize
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high-performance computers due to =
                    the inherent parallelism in the adjoint method.
                    Reduced space
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">IPM approach and the adjoint metho=
                    d is a novel approach when it comes to solving
                    the (security
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">constrained) optimal power flow pr=
                    oblems. These were previously used in the
                    PDE-constrained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization. The presented method=
                    ology is suitable for high-performance
                    architectures due to inherent

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelism in the adjoint method =
                    during the gradient evaluation, since the
                    individual contingency
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scenarios are modeled by independe=
                    nt set of the constraints. Preliminary
                    evaluation of the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and convergence is performed to st=
                    udy the reduced space approach.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Karunakaran2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-247" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Karunakaran, Sivakumar and Lavanya Selvagane=
            sh. =E2=80=9CA novel graph matrix representation:
            sequence of neighbourhood matrices with an application=E2=80=9D=
            . In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">SN Applied Sciences
            </span>2.5 (Apr.
            2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi<= /span></span>: 10.1007/s42452-020-2635-1.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In the study of network
                optimization, fnding the shortest path minimizing time/dista=
                nce/cost
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from a source node to a destinatio=
                    n node is one of the fundamental problems. Our
                    focus here is to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fnd the shortest path between any =
                    pair of nodes in a given undirected unweighted
                    simple graph with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the help of the sequence of powers=
                    of neighbourhood matrices. The authors
                    recently introduced the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">concept of neighbourhood matrix as=
                    a novel representation of graphs using the
                    neighbourhood sets of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the vertices. In this article, an =
                    extension of the above work is presented by
                    introducing a sequence of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, referred to as the seque=
                    nce of powers of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtM=enu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"329" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c1D4A9 TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c2133 TEX-SC">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo=class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <m= jx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <= /mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselect=able=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/M=athML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=A9</mi>=
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=E2=84=B3</mi>

                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-=open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>G</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mr= ow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">It is denoted it by </span>
                <!-- l.=

                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"330" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position=: relative;">

                                        =B3</mi>

                                    <mo= class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->

                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"331" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">is called the iteration number, </span>
                        <!= -- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHT=ML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"332" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; pos=ition: relative;">

                                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">

                                                                </mjx-mn>

                                                <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">

                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6D">
                                                            <= /mjx-c>
