                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c74"></mjx-c>
                                                                <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c65">
                                                        </mjx-c>
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c72"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mtext=>
                                                </mjx-mstyle>
                                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">=
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i"=>
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mo clas=s=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    </mjx= -mrow>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c2309"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselect=able=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/M=athML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>k</mi>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</m= o>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mi>G</mi>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo cla=ss=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">=E2=8C=88
                    </= mo>
                    <mrow>
                        <msub>
                            <mrow>
                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mtext>log</mtext>
                                </mstyle>
                                </mr= ow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                </mrow>
                        </msub>
                        <mstyle class=3D"text">
                            <mtext>diameter</mt= ext>
                        </mstyle>
                        <mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                            <mrow>
                                <mi>G</mi>
                                </mro= w>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </mrow>
                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-=close">=E2=8C=89</mo>
                    </mrow></math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">As this sequence of matrices captu=
                            res the distance between the nodes profoundly,
                            we further develop
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the technique and present several =
                            characterizations. Based on the theoretical
                            results, we present an
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm to fnd the shortest path=
                            between any pair of nodes in a given graph.
                            The proposed algorithm
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the claims therein are formall=
                            y validated through simulations on synthetic
                            data and the real
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">network data from Facebook. The em=
                            pirical results are quite promising with our
                            algorithm having
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">best running time among all the ex=
                            isting well-known shortest path algorithms for
                            the considered graph
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">classes.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kedward2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-248" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kedward, Laurence, Christian B. Allen, and T=
            . Rendall. =E2=80=9CComparing Matrix-based and
            Matrix-free Discrete Adjoint Approaches to the Euler Equations=
            =E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">AIAA Scitech
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">2020 Forum</span>. American Inst=
            itute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Jan.
            2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</= span></span>:
                10.2514/6.2020-1294.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Detail is presented on the
                implementation of numerical derivatives with focus given to =
                the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">discrete adjoint equations. Two ap=
                    proaches are considered: a hybrid matrix-based
                    scheme where the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convective Jacobian is constructed=
                    explicitly; and a matrix-free method using
                    reverse-mode automatic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">differentiation. The hybrid matrix=
                    -based scheme exploits a compact convective
                    stencil using graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">colouring to evaluate the convecti=
                    ve Jacobian terms in </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"333" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mn class==3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30">
                                        </mjx-= c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unse=lectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Ma=th/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">=
                                    (</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>10</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                            </= math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">residual evaluations. Jacobian terms, gro=
                    uped by colours, are evaluated using the
                    complex step tangent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model; this approach requires no e=
                    xternal libraries or tools, minimal code
                    modification and provides
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">derivatives accurate to machine pr=
                    ecision. The remaining artificial dissipation
                    terms are trivial to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">differentiate by hand where the se=
                    nsor coefficients are held constant. The hybrid
                    matrix-based methodology
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is validated and compared with the=
                    =E2=80=98traditional=E2=80=98 matrix-free approach using
                    reverse-mode automatic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">differentiation. The adjoint equat=
                    ions using both approaches are solved using the
                    same fixed-point
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Runge-Kutta iteration accelerated =
                    by agglomeration multigrid. No loss in accuracy
                    is seen between
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the matrix-based and the matrix-fr=
                    ee methods when validated with the complex step
                    tangent model.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The hybrid matrix-based approach d=
                    emonstrates a notable runtime performance
                    advantage over the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">traditional matrix-free approach d=
                    ue to the prior calculation of Jacobian terms.
                    Moreover, the convective
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Jacobian calculation takes less th=
                    an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0=" jax=3D" CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"334" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-= mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c25 TEX-MI"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-m= ath>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mn>5</mn>
                                    <mi>%<= /mi>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">of primal runtime due to the compact sten=
                    cil used. A critical analysis of the results
                    and methodology is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consequently presented, focusing o=
                    n the general applicability of the hybrid
                    approach to more complex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kepner2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-249" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kepner, Jeremy et al. =E2=80=9CMulti-Tempora=
            l Analysis and Scaling Relations of 100,000,000,000
            Network Packets=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). arXiv: 2008.00307v1 =
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.NI]</span>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Our society has never been more
                dependent on computer networks. Effective utilization of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">networks requires a detailed under=
                    standing of the normal background behaviors of
                    network traffic.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Large-scale measurements of networ=
                    ks are computationally challenging. Building on
                    prior work
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in interactive supercomputing and =
                    GraphBLAS hypersparse hierarchical traffic
                    matrices, we have
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developed an efficient method for =
                    computing a wide variety of streaming network
                    quantities on diverse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time scales. Applying these method=
                    s to 100,000,000,000 anonymized
                    source-destination pairs collected
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at a network gateway reveals many =
                    previously unobserved scaling relationships.
                    These observations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provide new insights into normal n=
                    etwork background traffic that could be used
                    for anomaly detection,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">AI feature engineering, and testin=
                    g theoretical models of streaming
                    networks.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Keriven2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-250" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Keriven, Nicolas and Samuel Vaiter. =E2=80=
            =9CSparse and Smooth: improved guarantees for Spectral
            Clustering in the Dynamic Stochastic Block Model=E2=80=9D. In: =
            (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.02892
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ML]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we analyse
                classical variants of the Spectral Clustering (SC) algorithm=
                in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Dynamic Stochastic Block Model (DS=
                    BM). Existing results show that, in the
                    relatively sparse case
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where the expected degree grows lo=
                    garithmically with the number of nodes,
                    guarantees in the static case
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be extended to the dynamic cas=
                    e and yield improved error bounds when the DSBM
                    is sufficiently
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">smooth in time, that is, the commu=
                    nities do not change too much between two time
                    steps. We improve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">over these results by drawing a ne=
                    w link between the sparsity and the smoothness
                    of the DSBM: the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">more regular the DSBM is, the more=
                    sparse it can be, while still guaranteeing
                    consistent recovery. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">particular, a mild condition on th=
                    e smoothness allows to treat the sparse case
                    with bounded degree.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We also extend these guarantees to=
                    the normalized Laplacian, and as a by-product
                    of our analysis, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">obtain to our knowledge the best s=
                    pectral concentration bound available for the
                    normalized Laplacian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of matrices with independent Berno=
                    ulli entries.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Keyes2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-251" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Keyes, D. E., H. Ltaief, and G. Turkiyyah. =
            =E2=80=9CHierarchical algorithms on hierarchical
            architectures=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Philosophic=
                al Transactions of the Royal Society A: Mathematical,
                Physical
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">and Engineering Sciences </span>=
            378.2166 (Jan. 2020), p. 20190055. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"=small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1098/rsta.2019.0055.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A traditional goal of algorithmic
                optimality, squeezing out flops, has been superseded by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evolution in architecture. Flops n=
                    o longer serve as a reasonable proxy for all
                    aspects of complexity.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Instead, algorithms must now squee=
                    ze memory, data transfers, and
                    synchronizations, while extra
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">flops on locally cached data repre=
                    sent only small costs in time and energy.
                    Hierarchically low-rank
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices realize a rarely achieved=
                    combination of optimal storage complexity and
                    high-computational
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intensity for a wide class of form=
                    ally dense linear operators that arise in
                    applications for which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exascale computers are being const=
                    ructed. They may be regarded as algebraic
                    generalizations of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the fast multipole method. Methods=
                    based on these hierarchical data structures
                    and their simpler
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cousins, tile low-rank matrices, a=
                    re well proportioned for early exascale
                    computer architectures, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are provisioned for high processin=
                    g power relative to memory capacity and memory
                    bandwidth.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">They are ushering in a renaissance=
                    of computational linear algebra. A challenge
                    is that emerging
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hardware architecture possesses hi=
                    erarchies of its own that do not generally
                    align with those of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the algorithm. We describe modules=
                    of a software toolkit, hierarchical
                    computations on manycore
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architectures, that illustrate the=
                    se features and are intended as building blocks
                    of applications, such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as matrix-free higher-order method=
                    s in optimization and large-scale spatial
                    statistics. Some modules
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of this open-source project have b=
                    een adopted in the software libraries of major
                    vendors.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Khalifa2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-252" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Khalifa, Dorra Ben, Matthieu Martel, and Ass=
            al=C3=A9 Adj=C3=A9. =E2=80=9CPOP: A Tuning Assistant
            for Mixed-Precision Floating-Point Computations=E2=80=9D. In: <= span class=3D"cmti-10">Communications in
                Computer
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">and Information Science</span>. =
                Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp.
                77=E2=80=9394. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">doi</span></span>:
                10.1007/978-3-030-46902-3=CB=995.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this article, we describe a
                static program analysis to determine the lowest floating-poi=
                nt
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">precisions on inputs and intermedi=
                    ate results that guarantees a desired accuracy
                    of the output values.

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">A common practice used by develope=
                    rs without advanced training in computer
                    arithmetic consists
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in using the highest precision ava=
                    ilable in hardware (double precision on most
                    CPU=E2=80=99s) which can be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exorbitant in terms of energy cons=
                    umption, memory traffic, and bandwidth
                    capacity. To overcome this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">difficulty, we propose a new preci=
                    sion tuning tool for the floating-point
                    programs integrating a static
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">forward and backward analysis, don=
                    e by abstract interpretation. Next, our
                    analysis will be expressed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as a set of linear constraints eas=
                    ily checked by an SMT solver.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Khimich2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-253" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Khimich, O. M. et al. =E2=80=9CA Parallel Al=
            gorithm for Solving a Partial Eigenvalue Problem for
            Block-Diagonal Bordered Matrices=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"c=mti-10">Cybernetics and Systems Analysis
            </span>56.6
            (Nov. 2020),
            pp. 913=E2=80=93923. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/s10559-020-00311-z.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A hybrid algorithm of the
                iterative method for the solution subspace of a partial gene=
                ralized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">eigenvalue problem for symmetric p=
                    ositive definite sparse matrices of
                    block-diagonal structure with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bordering on hybrid computers with=
                    graphic processors is proposed, efficiency
                    coefficients of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm are obtained, and the al=
                    gorithm is tested against test and practical
                    problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kim2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-254" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kim, Hongjune et al. =E2=80=9CCompiler-direc=
            ted soft error resilience for lightweight GPU register
            file
            protection=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of=
                the 41st ACM SIGPLAN Conference on Programming
                Language
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Design and Implementation</span>=
            . ACM, June 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</= span></span>:
                10.1145/3385412.3386033.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper presents Penny, a
                compiler-directed resilience scheme for protecting GPU regis=
                ter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">files (RF) against soft errors. Pe=
                    nny replaces the conventional error correction
                    code (ECC) based RF
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">protection by using less expensive=
                    error detection code (EDC) along with
                    idempotence based recovery.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Compared to the ECC protection, Pe=
                    nny can achieve either the same level of RF
                    resilience yet with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significantly lower hardware costs=
                    or stronger resilience using the same ECC due
                    to its ability to detect
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multi-bit errors when it is used s=
                    olely for detection. In particular, to address
                    the lack of store buffers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in GPUs, which causes both checkpo=
                    int storage overwriting and the high cost of
                    checkpointing stores,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Penny provides several compiler op=
                    timizations such as storage coloring and
                    checkpoint pruning. Across
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">25 benchmarks, Penny causes only <= /span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" ja=x=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"335" style=3D"font-size: 117=.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">=
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2248"></mjx-c>
                    </= mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"=><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mo=class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=89=88</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-cont= ainer><span class=3D"cmr-9">3%
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">run-time overhead on average.</spa= n>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kirk2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-255" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kirk, Richard O. et al. =E2=80=9CWarwick Dat=
            a Store: A Data Structure Abstraction Library=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 IEEE/ACM Confer=
                ence on Performance Modeling, Benchmarking and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Simulation of High Performance C=
                omputer Systems</span>. PMBS =E2=80=9920. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">With the increasing complexity of
                memory architectures and scientific applications,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developing data structures that ar=
                    e performant, portable, scalable, and support
                    developer productivity,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is a challenging task. In this pap=
                    er, we present Warwick Data Store (WDS), a
                    lightweight and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extensible C++ template library de=
                    signed to manage these complexities and allow
                    rapid prototyping.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">WDS is designed to abstract detail=
                    s of the underlying data structures away from
                    the user, thus
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">easing application development and=
                    optimisation. We show that using WDS does not
                    significantly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">impact achieved performance across=
                    a variety of different scientific benchmarks
                    and proxy-applications,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compilers, and different architect=
                    ures. The overheads are largely below 30% for
                    smaller problems, with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the overhead deceasing to below 10=
                    % when using larger problems. This shows that
                    the library does
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">not significantly impact the perfo=
                    rmance, while providing additional
                    functionality to data structures,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the ability to optimise data s=
                    tructures without changing the application
                    code.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Klein2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-256" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Klein, Ole. =E2=80=9CAn Improved Conjugate G=
            radients Method for Quasi-linear Bayesian Inverse
            Problems, Tested on an Example from Hydrogeology=E2=80=9D. In: =
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Modeling, Simulation and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Optimization of Complex Processe=
                s HPSC 2018</span>. Springer International
            Publishing, Dec. 2020,
            pp. 357=E2=80=93385. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-55240-4=CB=9917.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a framework for
                high-performance quasi-linear Bayesian inverse modelling and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its application in hydrogeology; e=
                    xtensions to other domains of application are
                    straightforward due

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to generic programming and modular=
                    design choices. The central component of the
                    framework is a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">collection of specialized precondi=
                    tioned methods for nonlinear least squares: the
                    classical three-term
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">recurrence relation of Conjugate G=
                    radients and related methods is replaced by a
                    specific choice
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of six-term recurrence relation, w=
                    hich is used to reformulate the resulting
                    optimization problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and eliminate several costly matri=
                    x-vector products. We demonstrate that this
                    reformulation leads
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to improved performance, robustnes=
                    s, and accuracy for a synthetic example
                    application from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">hydrogeology. The proposed prior-p=
                    reconditioned caching CG scheme is the only one
                    among the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">considered CG methods that scales =
                    perfectly in the number of estimated
                    parameters. In the highly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">relevant case of sparse measuremen=
                    ts, the proposed method is up to two orders of
                    magnitude faster
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">than the classical CG scheme, and =
                    at least six times faster than a
                    prior-preconditioned, non-caching
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">version. It is therefore particula=
                    rly suited for the large-scale inversion of
                    sparse observations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Klockiewicz2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-257" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Klockiewicz, Bazyli et al. =E2=80=9CSecond O=
            rder Accurate Hierarchical Approximate Factorization of
            Sparse SPD Matrices=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.0078=
            9v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We describe a second-order
                accurate approach to sparsifying the off-diagonal blocks in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approximate hierarchical matrix fa=
                    ctorizations of sparse symmetric positive
                    definite matrices. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">norm of the error made by the new =
                    approach depends quadratically, not linearly,
                    on the error in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">low-rank approximation of the give=
                    n block. The analysis of the resulting
                    two-level preconditioner shows
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that the preconditioner is second-=
                    order accurate as well. We incorporate the new
                    approach into the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">recent Sparsified Nested Dissectio=
                    n algorithm [SIAM J. Matrix Anal. Appl., 41
                    (2020), pp. 715-746],
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and test it on a wide range of pro=
                    blems. The new approach halves the number of
                    Conjugate Gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iterations needed for convergence,=
                    with almost the same factorization complexity,
                    improving the total
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">runtimes of the algorithm. Our app=
                    roach can be incorporated into other
                    rank-structured methods for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving sparse linear systems.</sp= an>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kochurov2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-258" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kochurov, Max, Rasul Karimov, and Sergei Koz=
            lukov. =E2=80=9CGeoopt: Riemannian Optimization in
            PyTorch=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.02819v1 <span cla=ss=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.CG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Geoopt is a research-oriented
                modular open-source package for Riemannian Optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in PyTorch. The core of Geoopt is =
                    a standard Manifold interface which allows for
                    the generic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation of optimization alg=
                    orithms. Geoopt supports basic Riemannian SGD
                    as well as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">adaptive optimization algorithms. =
                    Geoopt also provides several algorithms and
                    arithmetic methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for supported manifolds, which all=
                    ow composing geometry-aware neural network
                    layers that can be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integrated with existing models.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kolodziej2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-259" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kolodziej, Scott P. and Timothy A. Davis. =
            =E2=80=9CGeneralized Gains for Hybrid Vertex
            Separator Algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proc=
                eedings of the SIAM Workshop on Combinatorial
                Scientific
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computing</span>. Society for In=
            dustrial and Applied Mathematics, Jan. 2020,
            pp. 96=E2=80=93105. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sma=ll-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1137/1.9781611976229.10.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we derive
                generalized vertex gains for computing vertex separators, gr=
                eatly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improving the efficiency and data =
                    reuse in hybrid graph partitioning contexts.
                    Using these generalized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gains, we design a novel algorithm=
                    for computing vertex separators in arbitrary
                    graphs and compare
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our approach to METIS, a popular g=
                    raph partitioning library. In general, our
                    hybrid algorithm scales
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">well to very large graphs with the=
                    increased information sharing that the
                    generalized gains afford.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kornowski2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-260" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kornowski, Guy and Ohad Shamir. =E2=80=9CHig=
            h-Order Oracle Complexity of Smooth and Strongly
            Convex Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.0664=
            2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this note, we consider the
                complexity of optimizing a highly smooth (Lipschitz </span>

                        vex function, via calls to a </span>

                                                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">),
                                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"343" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                                    position: relative;">
                                                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                        <mjx-msub>
                                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                                <mjx-mi= class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D707 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                                    </mjx-m= row>
                                                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                                                        <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                            <mj= x-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I">
                                                                                </mjx-c>
                                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                                                </m= jx-mrow>
                                                                    </mjx-script>
                                                        </mjx-msub>
                                                        </mjx-math>
                                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L"
                                                                display=3D"inline">
                                                                <msub>
                                                                    <mrow>
                                                                        <mi>=CE=BC</mi>
                                                                    </mrow>
                                                                    <mrow>
                                                                        <mi>k</mi>
                                                                        </m= row>
                                                                </msub>
                                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                        </mjx-container>
                                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the Lipschitz constant of the </span>
                                                        <= !-- l. 30 -->
                                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                                                jax=3D"CH=TML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"344"
                                                                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; po=sition: relative;">
                                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                                    <mjx-mi= class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                                        </mjx-m= ath>
                                                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"
                                                                            display=3D"inline"><math
                                                                                xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                                display=3D"inline">
                                                                                <mi>k</mi>
                                                                                </math=>
                                                                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-th
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">derivative, </span>
                                                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                                                            <= mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                                                jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"345"
                                                                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                                </mjx-math>
                                                                <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on"
                                                                    display=3D"inline"><math
                                                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                                        <mi>D</mi>
                                                                    </math>
                                            </mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                                            </mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the initial distance to the optimum, a=
                                                nd </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0=" jax=3D" CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"346" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%;
                                                position: relative;">
                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D706 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx= -mi>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <m= ath xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mi>=CE=
                                                            =BB</mi></math>
                                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the strong convexity parameter.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kressner2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-261" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kressner, Daniel et al. =E2=80=9CCompress-an=
            d-restart block Krylov subspace methods for Sylvester
            matrix equations=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.01506 <= span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Block Krylov subspace methods
                (KSMs) comprise building blocks in many state-of-the-art
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solvers for large-scale matrix equ=
                    ations as they arise, e.g., from the
                    discretization of partial differential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations. While extended and rati=
                    onal block Krylov subspace methods provide a
                    major reduction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in iteration counts over polynomia=
                    l block KSMs, they also require reliable
                    solvers for the coefficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, and these solvers are of=
                    ten iterative methods themselves. It is not
                    hard to devise scenarios
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in which the available memory, and=
                    consequently the dimension of the Krylov
                    subspace, is limited.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In such scenarios for linear syste=
                    ms and eigenvalue problems, restarting is a
                    well explored technique
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for mitigating memory constraints.=
                    In this work, such restarting techniques are
                    applied to polynomial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">KSMs for matrix equations with a c=
                    ompression step to control the growing rank of
                    the residual. An
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">error analysis is also performed, =
                    leading to heuristics for dynamically adjusting
                    the basis size in each
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">restart cycle. A panel of numerica=
                    l experiments demonstrates the effectiveness of
                    the new method with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">respect to extended block KSMs.</s= pan>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kreutzer2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-262" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kreutzer, Patrick, Stefan Kraus, and Michael=
            Philippsen. =E2=80=9CLanguage-Agnostic Generation
            of Compilable Test Programs=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-1=0">Proceedings of the13th IEEE International
                Conference
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">on Software Testing, Validation =
                and Verification</span>. ICST 2020. IEEE, Oct.
            2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</= span></span>:
                10.1109/icst46399.2020.00015.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Testing is an integral part of
                the development of compilers and other language processors.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">To automatically create large sets=
                    of test programs, random program generators,
                    or fuzzers, have
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">emerged. Unfortunately, existing a=
                    pproaches are either language-specific (and
                    thus require a rewrite for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">each language) or may generate pro=
                    grams that violate rules of the respective
                    programming language
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(which limits their usefulness). T=
                    his work introduces *Smith, a language-agnostic
                    framework for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generation of valid, compilable te=
                    st programs. It takes as input an abstract
                    attribute grammar that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">specifies the syntactic and semant=
                    ic rules of a programming language. It then
                    creates test programs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that satisfy all these rules. By a=
                    ggressively pruning the search space and
                    keeping the construction as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">local as possible, *Smith can gene=
                    rate huge, complex test programs in short time.
                    We present four case
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">studies covering four real-world p=
                    rogramming languages (C, Lua, SQL, and SMT-LIB
                    2) to show that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">*Smith is both efficient and effec=
                    tive, while being flexible enough to support
                    programming languages
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that differ considerably. We found=
                    bugs in all four case studies. For example,
                    *Smith detected 165
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different crashes in older version=
                    s of GCC and LLVM. *Smith and the language
                    grammars are available
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">online.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kronqvist2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-263" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kronqvist, Jan and Ruth Misener. =E2=80=9CA =
            disjunctive cut strengthening technique for convex
            MINLP=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Optimization and En=
                gineering </span>(Aug. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/s11081-020-09551-6.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Generating polyhedral outer
                approximations and solving mixed-integer linear relaxations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">remains one of the main approaches=
                    for solving convex mixed-integer nonlinear
                    programming (MINLP)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems. There are several algori=
                    thms based on this concept, and the efficiency
                    is greatly affected
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by the tightness of the outer appr=
                    oximation. In this paper, we present a new
                    framework for

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strengthening cutting planes of no=
                    nlinear convex constraints, to obtain tighter
                    outer approximations.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The strengthened cuts can give a t=
                    ighter continuous relaxation and an overall
                    tighter representation of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the nonlinear constraints. The cut=
                    s are strengthened by analyzing disjunctive
                    structures in the MINLP
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem, and we present two types =
                    of strengthened cuts. The first type of cut is
                    obtained by reducing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the right-hand side value of the o=
                    riginal cut, such that it forms the tightest
                    generally valid inequality
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for a chosen disjunction. The seco=
                    nd type of cut effectively uses individual
                    right-hand side values for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">each term of the disjunction. We p=
                    rove that both types of cuts are valid and that
                    the second type of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cut can dominate both the first ty=
                    pe and the original cut. We use the cut
                    strengthening in conjunction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with the extended supporting hyper=
                    plane algorithm, and numerical results show
                    that the strengthening
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can significantly reduce both the =
                    number of iterations and the time needed to
                    solve convex MINLP
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kurt2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-264" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kurt, S. et al. =E2=80=9CEfficient Tiled Spa=
            rse Matrix Multiplication through Matrix Signatures=E2=80=9D.
            In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 International C=
                onference for
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">High Performance Computing, Netw=
                orking, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC =E2=80=9920.
            Los Alamitos, CA,
            USA: IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 1234=E2=80=931247. <= span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.11=
                09/SC41405.2020.00091.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                    <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020=.00091"
                            class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputersocie=
                                ty.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00091</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Tiling is a key technique to
                reduce data movement in matrix computations. While
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tiling is well understood and wide=
                    ly used for dense matrix/tensor computations,
                    effective tiling of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse matrix computations remains=
                    a challenging problem. This paper proposes a
                    novel method to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiently summarize the impact o=
                    f the sparsity structure of a matrix on
                    achievable data reuse as a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">one-dimensional signature, which i=
                    s then used to build an analytical cost model
                    for tile size optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for sparse matrix computations. Th=
                    e proposed model-driven approach to sparse
                    tiling is evaluated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on two key sparse matrix kernels; =
                    Sparse Matrix-Matrix Multiplication (SpMM) and
                    Sampled Dense
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Dense Matrix Multiplication (SDDMM=
                    ). Experimental results demonstrate that
                    model-based tiled
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SpMM and SDDMM achieve high perfor=
                    mance relative to the current
                    state-of-the-art.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Kwasniewski2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-265" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Kwasniewski, Grzegorz et al. =E2=80=9COn the=
            Parallel I/O Optimality of Linear Algebra Kernels:
            Near-Optimal LU Factorization=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv:=
            2010.05975 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Dense linear algebra kernels,
                such as linear solvers or tensor contractions, are fundament=
                al
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">components of many scientific comp=
                    uting applications. In this work, we present a
                    novel method of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">deriving parallel I/O lower bounds=
                    for this broad family of programs. Based on
                    the X-partitioning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">abstraction, our method explicitly=
                    captures inter-statement dependencies.
                    Applying our analysis to LU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorization, we derive COnfLUX, =
                    an LU algorithm with the parallel I/O cost of
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"347" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I">
                                </mj= x-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx= -mn>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c2F TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lo=p MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mro= w>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D443 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-m= i>
                                        <mjx-msqrt>
                                            <mjx-sqrt>
                                                <mjx-surd>
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c221A"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                </mjx-surd>
                                                <mjx-box style=3D"padding-top: 0.166em=;">
                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 TEX-I">
                                                                </mj= x-c>
                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                </mjx-box>
                                            </mjx-sqrt>
                                        </mjx-msqrt>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo= class=3D"mjx-lop MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S2"></mjx-c>=
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                            <mi>N</mi>
                            <mn>3</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                            <mro= w>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>P</mi>
                                    <msqrt>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>M</mi=>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </msqrt>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></= mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">communicated elements per processor =E2=
                    =80=93 only </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"348" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx= -mn>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=2F"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c33"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D="on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"=display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>1</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                            <mn>=
                                3</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </m= jx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">over our established lower bound. We eval=
                        uate COnfLUX on various problem sizes,
                        demonstrating
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">empirical results that match our t=
                        heoretical analysis, communicating
                        asymptotically less than Cray
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ScaLAPACK or SLATE, and outperform=
                        ing the asymptotically-optimal CANDMC library.
                        Running on</span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"349" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                            </m= jx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                    </ma= th>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,</span>
                    <!-- l=
. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"350" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positio=n: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mn clas=s=3D"mjx-n">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                    </mjx= -c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c34"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml=unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/199=8/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mn>024</mn>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </= mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">nodes of Piz Daint, COnfLUX communicates =
                            1.6</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0=" jax=3D" CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"351" style=3D"font-size:=117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"tr=ue">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>=
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inlin=e"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <m= o class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-conta= iner>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">less than the second-best implementation =
                                and is expected to communicate 2.1</span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"352" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                            </mjx-= c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-con= tainer>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">less on a full-scale run on Summit.</span=>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Laguna2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-266" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Laguna, Ignacio. =E2=80=9CVarity: Quantifyin=
            g Floating-Point Variations in HPC Systems Through
            Randomized Testing=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">2020 I=
                EEE International Parallel and Distributed Processing
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Symposium (IPDPS)</span>. IEEE, =
            May 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></s= pan>:
                10.1109/ipdps47924.2020.00070.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Floating-point arithmetic can be
                confusing and it is sometimes misunderstood by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programmers. While numerical repro=
                    ducibility is desirable in HPC, it is often
                    unachievable due
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to the different ways compilers tr=
                    eat floating-point arithmetic and generate code
                    around it. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reproducibility problem is exacerb=
                    ated in heterogeneous HPC systems where code
                    can be executed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on different floating-point hardwa=
                    re, e.g., a host and a device architecture,
                    producing in some

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">situations different numerical res=
                    ults. We present VARITY, a tool to quantify
                    floatingpoint variations in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">heterogeneous HPC systems. Our app=
                    roach generates random test programs for
                    multiple architectures
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(host and device) using the compil=
                    ers that are available in the system. Using
                    differential testing, it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compares floating-point results an=
                    d identifies unexpected variations in the
                    program results. The results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can guide programmers in choosing =
                    the compilers that produce the most similar
                    results in a system,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which is useful when numerical rep=
                    roducibility is critical. By running 50,000
                    experiments with Varity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on a system with IBM POWER9 CPUs, =
                    NVIDIA V100 GPUs, and four compilers (gcc,
                    clang, xl, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nvcc), we identify and document se=
                    veral programs that produce significantly
                    different results for a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">given input when different compile=
                    rs or architectures are used, even when a
                    similar optimization level
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is used everywhere.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lai2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-267" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lai, Ming-Jun, Jiaxin Xie, and Zhiqiang Xu. =
            =E2=80=9CGraph Sparsification by Universal Greedy
            Algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.07161v1 <span= class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Graph sparsification is to
                approximate an arbitrary graph by a sparse graph and is usef=
                ul in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many applications, such as simplif=
                    ication of social networks, least squares
                    problems, numerical solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of symmetric positive definite lin=
                    ear systems and etc. In this paper, inspired by
                    the well-known sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">signal recovery algorithm called o=
                    rthogonal matching pursuit (OMP), we introduce
                    a deterministic,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">greedy edge selection algorithm ca=
                    lled universal greedy algorithm(UGA) for graph
                    sparsification. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">UGA algorithm can output a </span>=
                <!-- l. 30 -->

                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"353" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; p=osition: relative;">

                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">for some </span><!-- l. 30 -->

                                ctxt=menu_counter=3D"359" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-= math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D45C TEX-I"></mjx-c>

                                    <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
