                                            </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c3D">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space==3D"4">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mrow space==3D"2">
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX=-S2"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-mfrac>
                                                <mjx-frac>
                                                    <mjx-num>
                                                        <mjx-nstrut>
                                                            <= /mjx-nstrut>
                                                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-open">
                                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                                                        </mjx-mo>

                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6F">
                                                                                    <= /mjx-c>
                                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c67">
                                                                                        </mjx-c>
                                                                            </mjx-mi>
                                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n" sp=ace=3D"2">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2061"></mjx-c>
                                                                            </mjx-mo>
                                                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-=n qopname"
                                                                                space=3D"2">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c67"></mjx-c>
                                                                            </mjx-mi>
                                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mj=x-n" space=3D"2">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2061"></mjx-c>
                                                                            </mjx-mo>
                                                                            <mjx-mrow>

                                                                                    MathClass-open">
                                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                                                                    </mjx-m= o>
                                                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A
                                                                                                TEX-I">
                                                                                </mjx= -c>
                                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                                            </mjx-mo>
                                                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-close">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                                <mjx-= mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mn>
                                                                    <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                                    <mjx-mn=class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mn>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-= num>
                                <mjx-dbox>
                                    <mjx-dtable>
                                        <mjx-line></mjx-line>
                                        <mjx-row>
                                            <mjx-den>
                                                <mjx-dstru= t>
                                                    </mjx-dstrut>
                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-n qopname">
                                                            <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c67"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2061"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mo>
                                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mn>
                                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mo>
                                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=33"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mn>
                                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                                                <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c28">
                                                                    </mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mo>
                                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I">
                                                                        </mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"=mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mo>
                                                            </mjx-mrow=>
                                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                            </mjx-den>
                                        </mjx-row>
                                    </mjx-dtable>
                                </mjx-dbox>
                                </mjx-frac>
                                </mjx-mf= rac>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c29 TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml uns=electable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/M=ath/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>o</mi>
                        <mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open"=>(</mo>
                            <mrow>
                                <mn>1</mn>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                        </mrow>
                        <m= o class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-op=en">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mfrac>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                <mrow=>
                                                    <mi class=3D"qopname">log</mi>
                                                    <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                                    <mi class=3D"qopname">log<= /mi>
                                                            <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                                            <mrow>
                                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                                <mrow>
                                                                    <mi>m<= /mi>
                                                                </mrow>
                                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                                            </mrow>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"M=athClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=
                                            =95</mo>
                                        <mn>3</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi class=3D"qopname">log</mi>
                                    <mo>=E2=81=A1<= /mo>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                            <mn>3</mn>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo= class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>m</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathCla=ss-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </mrow>
                            </mfrac>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">=
                                )</mo>
                            </mrow>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-=9">.
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We shall point out that several ra=
                ndom graphs satisfy the sufficient condition
                and hence, can be
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparsified in linear time. For a g=
                eneral spectral sparsification problem, e.g.,
                positive subset selection
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem, a nonnegative UGA algorit=
                hm is proposed which needs </span>
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"360"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </m= jx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                                        </mjx-= c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-m= n>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B="></mjx-c></mjx-mo><mjx-mi class=3D" mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c33"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mn>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                        <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c2F">
                            </mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">=
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">=
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-mrow>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-close">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-mrow>
                    </mj= x-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmln=s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow=>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">mn</mi=>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                        <mi>n</mi>
                                        <mn>3</mn>
                                        <mo class==3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                        <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo=class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">time and the convergence is established.<= /span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lambert2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-268" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lambert, Matthew. =E2=80=9CAnalysis of High =
            Performance Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiplication for
            Small Finite Fields=E2=80=9D. PhD thesis. University of Delawar=
            e, 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This thesis explores the
                intricacies of obtaining high performance sparse matrixvecto=
                r
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication on modern hardware,=
                    with an emphasis on operating over data from
                    small finite
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fields. We develop and present nov=
                    el adaptations of classical, fast matrix
                    multiplication algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">over these fields and apply them i=
                    n a sparse setting. Further, we compare these
                    novel formats and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms to a wide array of stan=
                    dard sparse matrix formats. In particular, we
                    use modern code
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis tools to show how data la=
                    youts, vector/panel widths, and choice of
                    compiler all substantially
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">influence the efficiency of the un=
                    derlying arithmetic of these various sparse
                    formats. These analyses
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are performed in a data-agnostic m=
                    anner, meaning we focus solely on the
                    efficiency of the arithmetic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and not on higher-level aspects of=
                    performance such as cache access patterns. In
                    particular, we analyze
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the assembly code produced by comp=
                    ilers, removing matrix-specific intangibles
                    from the discussion
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of format. These are still importa=
                    nt considerations when considering any specific
                    matrix, but they
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">make comparing general formats to =
                    one another difficult, without exhaustive
                    benchmarking. These
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results show the theoretical peak =
                    arithmetic performance, which we discuss in
                    this abstract, analytic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">perspective. We see similar trends=
                    in synthetic performance benchmarks.
                    Ultimately, we show that the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Method of the Four Russians can be=
                    directly adapted to sparse matrix-panel (a
                    matrix with relatively

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">few columns) multiplication and th=
                    at a custom, high-performance variant can
                    achieve high performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in sparse matrix-vector multiplica=
                    tion, with potential to perform better in
                    real-world matrices.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lee2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-269" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lee, Hochan et al. =E2=80=9CA Study of APIs =
            for Graph Analytics Workloads=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceed=
                ings of the
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">2020 IEEE International Symposiu=
                m on Workload Characterization</span>. IISWC
            2020. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Traditionally, parallel graph
                analytics workloads have been implemented in systems like
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Pregel, GraphLab, Galois, and Ligr=
                    a that support graph data structures and graph
                    operations directly.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">An alternative approach is to expr=
                    ess graph workloads in terms of sparse matrix
                    kernels such as sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix-vector and matrix-matrix mu=
                    ltiplication. An API for these kernels has been
                    defined by the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GraphBLAS project. The SuiteSparse=
                    project has implemented this API on
                    shared-memory platforms,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the LAGraph project is buildin=
                    g a library of graph algorithms using this
                    API.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">How d=
                    oes the matrix-based approach perform compared
                    to the graph-based approach? Our experiments
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on a 56 core CPU show that for rep=
                    resentative graph workloads,
                    LAGraph/SuiteSparse solutions are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">5</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-contain= er class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtm=enu_counter=3D"361" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-m= ath class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo=>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">slower on the average than Galois solutio=
                                ns. We argue that this performance gap arises
                                from inherent
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">limitations of a matrix-based API:=
                                regardless of which architecture a matrixbased
                                algorithm is run on,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it is subject to the same inherent=
                                limitations of the matrix-based API.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Legat2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-270" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Legat, Benoit et al. =E2=80=9CMathOptInterfa=
            ce: a data structure for mathematical optimization
            problems=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.03447 <span cla=ss=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">JuMP is an open-source algebraic
                modeling language in the Julia language. In this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">work, we discuss a complete re-wri=
                    te of JuMP based on a novel abstract data
                    structure, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we call MathOptInterface, for repr=
                    esenting instances of mathematical optimization
                    problems.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">MathOptInterface is significantly =
                    more general than existing data structures in
                    the literature,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">encompassing, for example, a spect=
                    rum of problems classes from integer
                    programming with indicator
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">constraints to bilinear semidefini=
                    te programming. We highlight the challenges
                    that arise from this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generality, and how we overcame th=
                    em in the re-write of JuMP.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Leleux2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-271" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Leleux, Pierre et al. =E2=80=9CSparse Random=
            ized Shortest Paths Routing with Tsallis Divergence
            Regularization=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.00419v1 <= span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This work elaborates on the
                important problem of (1) designing optimal randomized routin=
                g
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">policies for reaching a target nod=
                    e t from a source note s on a weighted directed
                    graph G and (2) defining
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distance measures between nodes in=
                    terpolating between the least cost (based on
                    optimal movements)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the commute-cost (based on a r=
                    andom walk on G), depending on a temperature
                    parameter T. To
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this end, the randomized shortest =
                    path formalism (RSP, [2,99,124]) is rephrased
                    in terms of Tsallis
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">divergence regularization, instead=
                    of Kullback-Leibler divergence. The main
                    consequence of this change
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is that the resulting routing poli=
                    cy (local transition probabilities) becomes
                    sparser when T decreases,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">therefore inducing a sparse random=
                    walk on G converging to the least-cost
                    directed acyclic graph when
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">T tends to 0. Experimental compari=
                    sons on node clustering and semi-supervised
                    classification tasks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show that the derived dissimilarit=
                    y measures based on expected routing costs
                    provide state-of-the-art
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results. The sparse RSP is therefo=
                    re a promising model of movements on a graph,
                    balancing sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exploitation and exploration in an=
                    optimal way.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Leon2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-272" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Le=C3=B3n, Germ=C3=A1n et al. =E2=80=9CEvalu=
            ating the soft error sensitivity of a GPU-based
            SoC for matrix multiplication=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti=-10">Microelectronics Reliability
            </span>(Oct. 2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1016/j.microrel.2020.113856.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">System-on-Chip (SoC) devices can
                be composed of low-power multicore processors combined
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with a small graphics accelerator =
                    (or GPU) which offers a trade-off between
                    computational capacity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and low-power consumption. In this=
                    work we use the LLFI-GPU fault injection tool
                    on one of these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">devices to compare the sensitivity=
                    to soft errors of two different CUDA versions
                    of matrix multiplication

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benchmark. Specifically, we perfor=
                    m fault injection campaigns on a Jetson TK1
                    development kit, a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">board equipped with a SoC includin=
                    g an NVIDIA =E2=80=9DKepler=E2=80=9D Graphics Processing Unit
                    (GPU). We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluate the effect of modifying t=
                    he size of the problem and also the
                    thread-block size on the behaviour
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the algorithms. Our results sho=
                    w that the block version of the matrix
                    multiplication benchmark
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that leverages the shared memory o=
                    f the GPU is not only faster than the
                    element-wise version, but
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it is also much more resilient to =
                    soft errors. We also use the cuda-gdb debugger
                    to analyze the main
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">causes of the crashes in the code =
                    due to soft errors. Our experiments show that
                    most of the errors are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">due to accesses to invalid positio=
                    ns of the different memories of the GPU, which
                    causes that the block
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">version suffers a higher percentag=
                    e of this kind of errors.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Levy2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-273" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Levy, Ryan, Edgar Solomonik, and Bryan K. Cl=
            ark. =E2=80=9CDistributed-Memory DMRG via Sparse
            and Dense Parallel Tensor Contractions=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020=
            ). arXiv: 2007.05540v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Density Matrix
                Renormalization Group (DMRG) algorithm is a powerful tool fo=
                r solving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">eigenvalue problems to model quant=
                    um systems. DMRG relies on tensor contractions
                    and dense linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algebra to compute properties of c=
                    ondensed matter physics systems. However, its
                    efficient parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation is challenging due =
                    to limited concurrency, large memory footprint,
                    and tensor sparsity.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We mitigate these problems by impl=
                    ementing two new parallel approaches that
                    handle block sparsity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arising in DMRG, via Cyclops, a di=
                    stributed memory tensor contraction library. We
                    benchmark their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance on two physical system=
                    s using the Blue Waters and Stampede2
                    supercomputers. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DMRG performance is improved by up=
                    to 5.9X in runtime and 99X in processing rate
                    over ITensor, at
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">roughly comparable computational r=
                    esource use. This enables higher accuracy
                    calculations via larger
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensors for quantum state approxim=
                    ation. We demonstrate that despite having
                    limited concurrency,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DMRG is weakly scalable with the u=
                    se of efficient parallel tensor contraction
                    mechanisms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020f" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-274" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Cheng et al. =E2=80=9CP-Cloth: Interacti=
            ve Complex Cloth Simulation on Multi-GPU Systems
            using Dynamic Matrix Assembly and Pipelined Implicit Integrator=
            s=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). arXiv:
            2008.00409v2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.GR]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a novel parallel
                algorithm for cloth simulation that exploits multiple GPUs f=
                or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fast computation and the handling =
                    of very high resolution meshes. To accelerate
                    implicit integration,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we describe new parallel algorithm=
                    s for sparse matrix-vector multiplication
                    (SpMV) and for dynamic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix assembly on a multi-GPU wor=
                    kstation. Our algorithms use a novel work queue
                    generation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scheme for a fat-tree GPU intercon=
                    nect topology. Furthermore, we present a novel
                    collision handling
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scheme that uses spatial hashing f=
                    or discrete and continuous collision detection
                    along with a non-linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">impact zone solver. Our parallel s=
                    chemes can distribute the computation and
                    storage overhead among
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiple GPUs and enable us to per=
                    form almost interactive simulation on complex
                    cloth meshes, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can hardly be handled on a single =
                    GPU due to memory limitations. We have
                    evaluated the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with two multi-GPU workstations (w=
                    ith 4 and 8 GPUs, respectively) on cloth meshes
                    with 0.5-1.65M
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">triangles. Our approach can reliab=
                    ly handle the collisions and generate vivid
                    wrinkles and folds at
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">2-5 fps, which is significantly fa=
                    ster than prior cloth simulation systems. We
                    observe almost linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">speedups with respect to the numbe=
                    r of GPUs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020j" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-275" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Hao et al. =E2=80=9CSGD_Tucker: A Novel =
            Stochastic Optimization Strategy for Parallel Sparse
            Tucker Decomposition=E2=80=9D. In: (Dec. 2020). arXiv: 2012.035=
            50 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse Tucker Decomposition (STD)
                algorithms learn a core tensor and a group of factor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices to obtain an optimal low-=
                    rank representation feature for the
                </span><span class=3D"underline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">H</span>=
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">igh-</span><span class=3D"underline"><span
                        cla=ss=3D"cmr-9">O</span></span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rder, </span><span
                    class==3D"underline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">H</span></span><span class=3D"cmr-9">i=
                    gh-</span><span class=3D"underline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">D</span></span>
                <s= pan class=3D"cmr-9">imension,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><span class=3D"underlin=e"><span
                            class=3D"cmr-9">S</span></span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parse </span>=
                    <span class=3D"underline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">T</span></span><span class==3D"cmr-9">ensor
                        (HOHDST). However, existing STD algorithms face the proble=
                        m of intermediate
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variables explosion which results =
                        from the fact that the formation of those
                        variables, i.e., matrices
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Khatri-Rao product, Kronecker prod=
                        uct, and matrix-matrix multiplication, follows
                        the whole elements
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in sparse tensor. The above proble=
                        ms prevent deep fusion of efficient computation
                        and big data
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">platforms. To overcome the bottlen=
                        eck, a novel stochastic optimization strategy
                        (SGD</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"362" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5F">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mtext>
                            </mjx-mstyle>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-as= sistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://ww=w.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mtext=>_</mtext>
                                </mstyle>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class==3D"cmr-9">Tucker)
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is proposed for STD which can auto=
                        matically divide the high-dimension
                        intermediate variables into

                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">small batches of intermediate matr=
                        ices. Specifically, SGD</span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"363" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c5F"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mtext>
                            </mjx-mstyle>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml un=selectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/=Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mtext>_</mtext>
                                    </ms= tyle>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">Tucke=
                        r
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">only follows the randomly selected=
                        small samples rather than the whole elements,
                        while maintaining the
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overall accuracy and convergence r=
                        ate. In practice, SGD</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"364" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n"=>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5F"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mtext>
                            </mjx-mstyle>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mte= xt>_</mtext>
                                </mstyle>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class==3D"cmr-9">Tucker
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">features the two distinct advancem=
                        ents over the state of the art. First,
                        SGD</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctxt=Menu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"365" styl=e=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ar=ia-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c5F">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mtext>
                            </mjx-mstyle>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-ass= istive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www=.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mtext>=
                                        _</mtext>
                                </mstyle>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D="cmr-9">Tucker
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can prune the communication overhe=
                        ad for the core tensor in distributed settings.
                        Second, the low
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data-dependence of SGD</span>
                    <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"366" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mstyle= class=3D"text">
                                <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5F">
                                        </mjx-c=>
                                </mjx-mtext>
                                </mjx-mstyle>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" disp=lay=3D"inline">
                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mtext>_</mtext>
                                </mstyle>
                            </math></mjx= -assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">Tucker
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">enables fine-grained parallelizati=
                        on, which makes SGD</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"367" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5F">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mtext>
                            </mjx-mstyle>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-as= sistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://ww=w.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                    <mtext=>_</mtext>
                                </mstyle>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class==3D"cmr-9">Tucker
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">obtaining lower computational over=
                        heads with the same accuracy. Experimental
                        results show that
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SGD</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"368" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                <mj= x-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c5F"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mtext>
                                    </mjx-= mstyle>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"=><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mst= yle class=3D"text">
                                                <mtext>_</mtext>
                                                </mstyle>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mj= x-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">Tucker
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">runs at least 2</span>
                                <!-- l. 30 -=
-><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabind=
ex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"369" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rel=
ative;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mi class=3D"m=
jx-i"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44B TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi></mjx-math><mjx-as=
sistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http://ww=
w.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"><mi>X</mi></math></mjx-assist=
ive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">faster than the state of the art.</span>
         </p>
      </dd>
      <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020e" class=3D"thebibliography">
      </dt>
      <dd id=3D"bib-276" class=3D"thebibliography">
         <!-- l. 30 -->
                                <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Huan, Cong Fang, and Zhouchen Lin. =E2=
                                    =80=9CAccelerated First-Order Optimization
                                    Algorithms for Machine Learning=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cm=ti-10">Proceedings
                                        of the IEEE </span>(2020), pp. 1=E2=80=9316.
                                    <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                                        <= /span>:
                                            10.1109/jproc.2020.3007634.
                                </p><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Numerical optimization serves as
                                        one of the pillars of machine learning. To meet the demands
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of big data applications, lots of =
                                            efforts have been put on designing
                                            theoretically and practically fast
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms. This article provides =
                                            a comprehensive survey on accelerated
                                            first-order algorithms with
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a focus on stochastic algorithms. =
                                            Specifically, this article starts with
                                            reviewing the basic accelerated
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms on deterministic convex=
                                            optimization, then concentrates on their
                                            extensions to stochastic
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex optimization, and at last i=
                                            ntroduces some recent developments on
                                            acceleration for nonconvex
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization.</span>
                                </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-277" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Jiajia et al. =E2=80=9CA Parallel Sparse=
            Tensor Benchmark Suite on CPUs and GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan.
            2020). arXiv: 2001.00660 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>=
            .
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Tensor computations present
                significant performance challenges that impact a wide spectr=
                um
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of applications ranging from machi=
                    ne learning, healthcare analytics, social
                    network analysis, data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mining to quantum chemistry and si=
                    gnal processing. Efforts to improve the
                    performance of tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computations include exploring dat=
                    a layout, execution scheduling, and parallelism
                    in common tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels. This work presents a benc=
                    hmark suite for arbitrary-order sparse tensor
                    kernels using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art tensor formats: c=
                    oordinate (COO) and hierarchical coordinate
                    (HiCOO) on CPUs and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPUs. It presents a set of referen=
                    ce tensor kernel implementations that are
                    compatible with real-world
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensors and power law tensors exte=
                    nded from synthetic graph generation
                    techniques. We also propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Roofline performance models for th=
                    ese kernels to provide insights of computer
                    platforms from sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensor view.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020h" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-278" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CA Sparse Tensor Benchmark=
            Suite for CPUs and GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings =
                of the IEEE
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">International Symposium on Workl=
                oad Characterization</span>. IISWC 2020. IEEE,
            Oct. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                    doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/iiswc50251.2020.00027.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Tensor computations present
                significant performance challenges that impact a wide spectr=
                um
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of applications ranging from machi=
                    ne learning, healthcare analytics, social
                    network analysis, data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mining to quantum chemistry and si=
                    gnal processing. Efforts to improve the
                    performance of tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computations include exploring dat=
                    a layout, execution scheduling, and parallelism
                    in common tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels. This work presents a benc=
                    hmark suite for arbitrary-order sparse tensor
                    kernels using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art tensor formats: c=
                    oordinate (COO) and hierarchical coordinate
                    (HiCOO) on CPUs and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPUs. It presents a set of referen=
                    ce tensor kernel implementations that are
                    compatible with real-world
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensors and power law tensors exte=
                    nded from synthetic graph generation
                    techniques. We also propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Roofline performance models for th=
                    ese kernels to provide insights of computer
                    platforms from sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensor view. This benchmark suite =
                    along with the synthetic tensor generator is
                    publicly available.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020g" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-279" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Min, Chuanfu Xiao, and Chao Yang. =E2=80=
            =9Ca-Tucker: Input-Adaptive and Matricization-Free
            Tucker Decomposition for Dense Tensors on CPUs and GPUs=E2=80=
            =9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv:
            2010.10131 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Tucker decomposition is one of
                the most popular models for analyzing and compressing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large-scale tensorial data. Existi=
                    ng Tucker decomposition algorithms usually rely
                    on a single solver to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compute the factor matrices and co=
                    re tensor, and are not flexible enough to adapt
                    with the diversities
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the input data and the hardware=
                    . Moreover, to exploit highly efficient GEMM
                    kernels, most Tucker
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">decomposition implementations make=
                    use of explicit matricizations, which could
                    introduce extra costs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in terms of data conversion and me=
                    mory usage. In this paper, we present a-Tucker,
                    a new framework
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for input-adaptive and matricizati=
                    on-free Tucker decomposition of dense tensors.
                    A mode-wise flexible
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Tucker decomposition algorithm is =
                    proposed to enable the switch of different
                    solvers for the factor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices and core tensor, and a ma=
                    chine-learning adaptive solver selector is
                    applied to automatically
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cope with the variations of both t=
                    he input data and the hardware. To further
                    improve the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and enhance the memory efficiency,=
                    we implement a-Tucker in a fully
                    matricization-free manner without
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">any conversion between tensors and=
                    matrices. Experiments with a variety of
                    synthetic and real-world
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensors show that a-Tucker can sub=
                    stantially outperform existing works on both
                    CPUs and GPUs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020c" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-280" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Pei Heng, Taeho Lee, and Hee Yong Youn. =
            =E2=80=9CDimensionality Reduction with Sparse Locality
            for Principal Component Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"c=mti-10">Mathematical Problems in Engineering
            </span>2020 (May
            2020), pp. 1=E2=80=9312. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1155/2020/9723279.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Various dimensionality reduction
                (DR) schemes have been developed for projecting high-dimensi=
                onal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data into low-dimensional represen=
                    tation. The existing schemes usually preserve
                    either only the global
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">structure or local structure of th=
                    e original data, but not both. To resolve this
                    issue, a scheme called
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse locality for principal comp=
                    onent analysis (SLPCA) is proposed. In order to
                    effectively consider
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the trade-off between the complexi=
                    ty and efficiency, a robust </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"370" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43=F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -=0.15em;">
                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4=5D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>L</mi>
                                    </= mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                        <mi>p</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    </= msub>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-norm=
                    -based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">principal component analysis (R2P-=
                    PCA) is introduced for global DR, while sparse
                    representation-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">locality preserving projection (SR=
                    -LPP) is used for local DR. Sparse
                    representation is also employed to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">construct the weighted matrix of t=
                    he samples. Being parameter-free, this allows
                    the construction of an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intrinsic graph more robust agains=
                    t the noise. In addition, simultaneous learning
                    of projection matrix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and sparse similarity matrix is po=
                    ssible. Experimental results demonstrate that
                    the proposed scheme
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consistently outperforms the exist=
                    ing schemes in terms of clustering accuracy and
                    data reconstruction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">error.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020d" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-281" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Rui and Zeng-Qi Wang. =E2=80=9CRestricti=
            vely Preconditioned Conjugate Gradient Method for
            a Series of Constantly Augmented Least Squares Problems=E2=80=
            =9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">SIAM Journal on Matrix
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Analysis and Applications </span=>41.2 (Jan. 2020), pp. 838=E2=80=93851.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span clas=s=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1137/19m1284853.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this study, we analyze the
                real-time solution of a series of augmented least squares
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems, which are generated by a=
                    dding information to an original least squares
                    model repetitively.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Instead of solving the least squar=
                    es problems directly, we transform them into a
                    batch of saddle point
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear systems and subsequently so=
                    lve the linear systems using restrictively
                    preconditioned conjugate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient (RPCG) methods. Approxima=
                    tion of the new Schur complement is generated
                    effectively
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">based on a previously approximated=
                    Schur complement. Owing to the variations of
                    the preconditioned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conjugate gradient method, the pro=
                    posed methods generate convergence results
                    similar to the conjugate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient method and achieve a very=
                    fast convergent iterative sequence when the
                    coefficient matrix is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">well preconditioned. Numerical tes=
                    ts show that the new methods are more effective
                    than some standard
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Krylov subspace methods. Updated R=
                    PCG methods meet the requirement of real-time
                    computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">successfully for multifactor model=
                    s.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-282" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Ruipeng and Chaoyu Zhang. =E2=80=9CEffic=
            ient Parallel Implementations of Sparse Triangular
            Solves for GPU Architectures=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-=10">Proceedings of the 2020 SIAM Conference
                on Parallel

            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Processing for Scientific Comput=
                ing</span>. Society for Industrial and Applied
            Mathematics, Jan.
            2020, pp. 106=E2=80=93117. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1137/1.9781611976137.10.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The sparse triangular matrix
                solve (SpTrSV) is an important computation kernel that is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demanded by a variety of numerical=
                    methods such as the Gauss-Seidel iterations.
                    However, developing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficient parallel algorithms for =
                    SpTrSV that are suitable for GPUs remains a
                    challenging task due
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to the inherently sequential natur=
                    e in the solve. In this paper, we revisit this
                    problem by reviewing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">several parallel algorithms based =
                    on different task scheduling and different
                    sparse matrix storage
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">schemes, proposing modifications t=
                    o the existing methods that can greatly improve
                    the performance,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and describing the implementations=
                    in detail. Numerical results of Gauss-Seidel
                    iterations with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">structured and unstructured matric=
                    es make evident the superiority of the proposed
                    algorithms and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementations comparing with sta=
                    te-of-the-art methods in the literature.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020i" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-283" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Shengbo Eben et al. =E2=80=9CSynchronous=
            and asynchronous parallel computation for large-scale
            optimal control of connected vehicles=E2=80=9D. In: <span class==3D"cmti-10">Transportation Research Part C:
                Emerging
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Technologies </span>121 (Dec. 20=
            20), p. 102842. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</sp= an></span>:
                10.1016/j.trc.2020.102842.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Connected vehicles is an
                important intelligent transportation system to improve the t=
                raffic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance. This paper proposes t=
                    wo parallel computation algorithms to solve a
                    large-scale optimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">control problem in the coordinatio=
                    n of multiple connected vehicles. The
                    coordination is formulated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as a centralized optimization prob=
                    lem in the receding horizon fashion. A
                    decentralized computation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">network is designed to facilitate =
                    the development of parallel algorithms. We use
                    Taylor series to linearize
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-convex constraints, and introd=
                    uce a set of consensus constraints to transform
                    the centralized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem to a standard consensus op=
                    timization problem. A synchronous parallel
                    algorithm is firstly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proposed to solve the consensus op=
                    timization problem by applying the alternating
                    direction method
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of multipliers (ADMM). The ADMM fr=
                    amework allows us to decompose the coupling
                    constraints and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">decision variables, leading to par=
                    allel iterations for each vehicle in a
                    synchronous fashion. We then
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">propose an asynchronous version of=
                    the parallel algorithm that allows the
                    vehicles to update their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variables asynchronously in the co=
                    mputation network. The effectiveness and
                    efficiency of the proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms are validated by extens=
                    ive numerical simulations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Li2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-284" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Li, Shuang et al. =E2=80=9CThe Global Geomet=
            ry of Centralized and Distributed Low-rank Matrix
            Recovery without Regularization=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXi=
            v: 2003.10981v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Low-rank matrix recovery is a
                fundamental problem in signal processing and machine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learning. A recent very popular ap=
                    proach to recovering a low-rank matrix </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"371" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>X=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is to factorize it as a product of two sm=
                    aller matrices, i.e., </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"372" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c3D"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c1D448 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D449 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-m= i>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D447 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-m= i>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <mat= h xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>X</mi=>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">UV</mi>
                                <mi>=
                                    T</mi></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and then optimize over </span>
                <!--=
 l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML=" tabindex=3D" 0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"373" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; posit=ion: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi cl=ass=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D448 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math=>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"h=ttp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>U</mi>
                            </math></mj= x-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"374" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D449 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
