                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>V=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">instead of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-cont= ainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ct=xtmenu_counter=3D"375" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mj= x-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c1D44B TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml= unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/19=98/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>X</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </m= jx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Despite the resulting non-convexit=
                                y, recent results have shown that the
                                factorized objective functions
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have benign global geometry=E2=80=
                                =94with no spurious local minima and satisfying the
                                so-called strict saddle
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">property=E2=80=94ensuring converge=
                                nce to a global minimum for many local-search
                                algorithms. However, most
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of these results actually consider=
                                a modified cost function that includes a
                                balancing regularizer. While
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">useful for deriving theory, this b=
                                alancing regularizer does not appear to be
                                necessary in practice. In this
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">work, we close this theory-practic=
                                e gap by proving that the original factorized
                                non-convex problem,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">without the balancing regularizer,=
                                also has similar benign global geometry.
                                Moreover, we also extend
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our theoretical results to the fie=
                                ld of distributed optimization.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Liang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-285" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Liang, Ling, Defeng Sun, and Kim-Chuan Toh. =
            =E2=80=9CAn Inexact Augmented Lagrangian Method
            for Second-order Cone Programming with Applications=E2=80=9D. I=
            n: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.08772
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we adopt the
                augmented Lagrangian method (ALM) to solve convex quadratic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">second-order cone programming prob=
                    lems (SOCPs). Fruitful results on the
                    efficiency of the ALM have

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">been established in the literature=
                    . Recently, it has been shown by Cui et al.
                    (2019) that, if the quadratic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">growth condition holds at an optim=
                    al solution for the dual problem, then the KKT
                    residual converges
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to zero R-superlinearly when the A=
                    LM is applied to the primal problem. Moreover,
                    Cui et al (2017)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provided sufficient conditions for=
                    the quadratic growth condition to hold under
                    the metric subregularity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and bounded linear regularity cond=
                    itions for solving composite matrix
                    optimization problems involving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">spectral functions. Here, we adopt=
                    these recent ideas to analyze the convergence
                    properties of the ALM
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">when applied to SOCPs. To the best=
                    of our knowledge, no similar work has been
                    done for SOCPs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">so far. In our paper, we first pro=
                    vide sufficient conditions to ensure the
                    quadratic growth condition
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for SOCPs. With these elegant theo=
                    retical guarantees, we then design an SOCP
                    solver and apply
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it to solve various classes of SOC=
                    Ps such as minimal enclosing ball problems,
                    classic trust-region
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subproblems, square-root Lasso pro=
                    blems, and DIMACS Challenge problems. Numerical
                    results show
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that the proposed ALM based solver=
                    is efficient and robust compared to the
                    existing highly developed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solvers such as Mosek and SDPT3.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Liers2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-286" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Liers, Frauke et al. =E2=80=9CTowards the So=
            lution of Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Optimization Problems
            using Simultaneous Convexification=E2=80=9D. In: (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Solving mixed-integer nonlinear
                optimization problems (MINLPs) to global optimality
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is extremely challenging. An impor=
                    tant step for enabling their solution consists
                    in the design of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex relaxations of the feasible=
                    set. Known solution approaches based on
                    spatial branch-and-bound
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">become more effective the tighter =
                    the used relaxations are. Relaxations are
                    commonly established
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by convex underestimators, where e=
                    ach constraint function is considered
                    separately. Instead, a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">considerably tighter relaxation ca=
                    n be found via so-called simultaneous
                    convexification, where convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">underestimators are derived for mo=
                    re than one constraint at a time. In this work,
                    we present a global
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution approach for solving mixe=
                    d-integer nonlinear problems that uses
                    simultaneous convexification.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce a separation method f=
                    or the convex hull of constrained sets. It
                    relies on determining
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the convex envelope of linear comb=
                    inations of the constraints and on solving a
                    nonsmooth convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem. In particular, we apply t=
                    he method to quadratic absolute value functions
                    and derive their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex envelopes. The practicality=
                    of the proposed solution approach is
                    demonstrated on several test
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instances from gas network optimiz=
                    ation, where the method outperforms standard
                    approaches that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">use separate convex relaxations.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lin2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-287" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lin, Tianyi, Chi Jin, and Michael. I. Jordan=
            . =E2=80=9CNear-Optimal Algorithms for Minimax
            Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.02417 <span= class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper resolves a
                longstanding open question pertaining to the design of near-=
                optimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">first-order algorithms for smooth =
                    and strongly-convex-strongly-concave minimax
                    problems. Current
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art first-order algor=
                    ithms find an approximate Nash equilibrium
                    using </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"376" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-utext variant=3D"italic" style=3D"=font-size: 85.2%; padding: 0.88em 0px 0.235em;
                                    font-family: MJXZERO, serif;=font-style: italic;">=C3=95</mjx-utext>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx= -mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S1">
                                        </mjx-= c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                            <mjx-msqrt>
                                                <mjx-sqrt>
                                                    <mjx-surd>
                                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n"=>
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c221A"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mo>
                                                    </mjx-surd>
                                                    <mjx-box style=3D"p=adding-top: 0.141em;">
                                                        <mjx-mrow>
                                                            <mjx-msub>
                                                                <mjx-mrow>
                                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i"=>
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D705 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                                <mjx-script=style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                        <mjx-mstyle>
                                                                            <mjx-mi= class=3D"mjx-b">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D431 TEX-B">
                                                                                </mjx-c>
                                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                                                </mjx-m= style>
                                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                                </mjx-script>
                                                            </mjx-msub>
                                                            <mjx-msub>
                                                                <mjx-mrow>
                                                                    <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D705 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                                <m= jx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                        <mjx-msty= le>
                                                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-b">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D432 TEX-B">
                                                                                </mjx-c>
                                                                                </mjx-= mi>
                                                                                </mjx-mstyle>
                                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                                    </mjx-script>
                                                            </mjx-msub>
                                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                                    </mjx-box>
                                                    </m= jx-sqrt>
                                            </mjx-msqrt>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-close">
                                            <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S1">
                                    </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-a= ssistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://w=ww.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>=C3=95</mi>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo cla=ss=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <msqrt>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <msub>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mi>=CE=BA</mi>
                                                        <= /mrow>
                                                            <mrow>
                                                                <mstyle mathvariant=3D"bold">
                                                                    <mi>x</mi>
                                                                </mstyle>
                                                            </mrow>
                                                </msub>
                                                <= msub>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mi>=CE=BA</mi>
                                                    </mrow>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mstyle mathvariant=3D"bold">
                                                            <mi>y
                                                </= mi>
                                                </mstyle>
                                            </mrow>
                                            </msub>
                                    </mrow>
                                    </msqrt>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-clos=e">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient complexity, matching the lower b=
                        ound up to logarithmic factors. Our new
                        algorithm is
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">designed based on an accelerated p=
                        roximal point method and an accelerated solver
                        for minimax
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proximal steps. It can be easily e=
                        xtended to the settings of
                        strongly-convex-concave, convex-concave,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nonconvex-strongly-concave, and no=
                        nconvex-concave functions. This paper also
                        presents algorithms
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that match or outperform all exist=
                        ing methods in these settings in terms of
                        gradient complexity, up to
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">logarithmic factors.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lin2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-288" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lin, Zhouchen, Huan Li, and Cong Fang. =E2=
            =80=9CAccelerated Algorithms for Constrained Convex
            Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Accelerated =
                Optimization for Machine Learning</span>. Springer
            Singapore, 2020,
            pp. 57=E2=80=93108. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sma=ll-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-981-15-2910-8=CB=993.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This chapter reviews the
                representative accelerated algorithms for deterministic cons=
                trained
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex optimization. We overview t=
                    he accelerated penalty method, accelerated
                    Lagrange multiplier
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method, and the accelerated augmen=
                    ted Lagrange multiplier method. In particular,
                    we concentrate on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">two widely used algorithms, namely=
                    the alternating direction method of multiplier
                    (ADMM) and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">primal-dual method. For ADMM, we s=
                    tudy four scenarios, namely the generally
                    convex and nonsmooth
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">case, the strongly convex and nons=
                    mooth case, the generally convex and smooth
                    case, and the strongly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex and smooth case. We also in=
                    troduce its non-ergodic accelerated variant.
                    For the primal-dual
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method, we study three scenarios: =
                    both the two functions are generally convex,
                    both are strongly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex, and one is generally conve=
                    x, while the other is strongly convex. Finally,
                    we introduce the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Frank=E2=80=93Wolfe algorithm unde=
                    r the condition of strongly convex constraint
                    set.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lindquist2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-289" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lindquist, Neil, Piotr Luszczek, and Jack Do=
            ngarra. =E2=80=9CImproving the Performance of the
            GMRES Method using Mixed-Precision Techniques=E2=80=9D. In: (No=
            v. 2020). arXiv: 2011.01850
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The GMRES method is used to solve
                sparse, non-symmetric systems of linear equations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arising from many scientific appli=
                    cations. The solver performance within a single
                    node is memory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bound, due to the low arithmetic i=
                    ntensity of its computational kernels. To
                    reduce the amount of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data movement, and thus, to improv=
                    e performance, we investigated the effect of
                    using a mix of single
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and double precision while retaini=
                    ng double-precision accuracy. Previous efforts
                    have explored reduced
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">precision in the preconditioner, b=
                    ut the use of reduced precision in the solver
                    itself has received limited
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">attention. We found that GMRES onl=
                    y needs double precision in computing the
                    residual and updating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the approximate solution to achiev=
                    e double-precision accuracy, although it must
                    restart after each
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improvement of single-precision ac=
                    curacy. This finding holds for the tested
                    orthogonalization schemes:
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Modified Gram-Schmidt (MGS) and Cl=
                    assical Gram-Schmidt with Re-orthogonalization
                    (CGSR).
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Furthermore, our mixed-precision G=
                    MRES, when restarted at least once, performed
                    19% and 24%
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">faster on average than double-prec=
                    ision GMRES for MGS and CGSR, respectively. Our
                    implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">uses generic programming technique=
                    s to ease the burden of coding implementations
                    for different data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">types. Our use of the Kokkos libra=
                    ry allowed us to exploit parallelism and
                    optimize data management.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Additionally, KokkosKernels was us=
                    ed when producing performance results. In
                    conclusion, using a mix
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of single and double precision in =
                    GMRES can improve performance while retaining
                    double-precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accuracy.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Liu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-290" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Liu, Hongbin et al. =E2=80=9CUNAT: UNstructu=
            red Acceleration Toolkit on SW26010 many-core
            processor=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Engineering Com=
                putations </span>ahead-of-print.ahead-of-print (May
            2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi<= /span></span>:
                10.1108/ec-09-2019-0401.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The purpose of this paper is to
                provide an automatic parallelization toolkit for unstructure=
                d
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mesh-based computation. Among all =
                    kinds of mesh types, unstructured meshes are
                    dominant in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">engineering simulation scenarios a=
                    nd play an essential role in scientific
                    computations for their geometrical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">flexibility. However, the high-fid=
                    elity applications based on unstructured grids
                    are still time-consuming,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">no matter for programming or runni=
                    ng.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">This study develops a=
                    n efficient UNstructured Acceleration Toolkit (UNAT), which provides
                    friendly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">high-level programming interfaces =
                    and elaborates lower level implementation on
                    the target hardware
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to get nearly hand-optimized perfo=
                    rmance. At the present state, two efficient
                    strategies, a multi-level
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">blocks method and a row-subsection=
                    s method, are designed and implemented on
                    Sunway architecture.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Random memory access and write=E2=
                    =80=93write conflict issues of unstructured meshes have
                    been handled by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">partitioning, coloring and other h=
                    ardware-specific techniques. Moreover, a
                    data-reuse mechanism is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developed to increase the computat=
                    ional intensity and alleviate the memory
                    bandwidth bottleneck.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span clas=s=3D"cmr-9">The authors select sparse
                    matrix-vector multiplication as a performance benchmark of U=
                    NAT across
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different data layouts and differe=
                    nt matrix formats. Experimental results show
                    that the speed-ups reach

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">up to 26</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-= container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0=" ctxtmenu_counter=3D" 382" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;"=>
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Ma=thClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=
                                =97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">compared to single management processing =
                            element, and the utilization ratio tests
                            indicate the capability
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of achieving nearly hand-optimized=
                            performance. Finally, the authors adopt UNAT
                            to accelerate a
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">well-tuned unstructured solver and=
                            obtain speed-ups of 19</span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"383" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                </mjx-= c>
                </mjx-mo>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-con= tainer>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and 10</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container= class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmen=u_counter=3D"384" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-mat= h class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-b=in">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml= unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/19=98/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                <= /math>
                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">on average for main kernels and overall s=
                                        olver, respectively.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">The =
                                        authors design an unstructured mesh toolkit, UNAT, to link the
                                        hardware and numerical algorithm,
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and then, engineers can focus on t=
                                        he algorithms and solvers rather than the
                                        parallel implementation.
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">For the many-core processor SW2601=
                                        0 of the fastest supercomputer in China, UNAT
                                        yields up to
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">26</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                    <mjx-contai= ner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                        ctxt=menu_counter=3D"385" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                        <mjx-= math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-bin">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</m= o>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                </mjx-container>
                                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">speed-ups and achieves nearly hand-optimi=
                                                    zed performance.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Liu2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-291" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Liu, Yang et al. =E2=80=9CSparse Approximate=
            Multifrontal Factorization with Butterfly Compression
            for High Frequency Wave Equations=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). ar=
            Xiv: 2007.00202v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a fast and approximate
                multifrontal solver for large-scale sparse linear systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arising from finite-difference, fi=
                    nite-volume or finite-element discretization of
                    high-frequency wave
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations. The proposed solver lev=
                    erages the butterfly algorithm and its
                    hierarchical matrix extension
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for compressing and factorizing la=
                    rge frontal matrices via graph-distance guided
                    entry evaluation or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">randomized matrix-vector multiplic=
                    ation-based schemes. Complexity analysis and
                    numerical experiments
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <= mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"386" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-m= row space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-n qopname" space=3D"2"=>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c67"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c2061">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-c=lose">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-scr=ipt">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>N<= /mi>
                                            <mi class=3D"qopname">log</mi>
                                            <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                            <mn>2</mn>
                                            <mi>N</mi>
                                            </m= row>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        <= /mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">computation and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx= -container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"=0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"387" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:
                                relative;="><mjx-math class=3D" MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc=mjx-b mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow=space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"=></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D=441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-clo=se">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-as= sistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://ww=w.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-scrip=t">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mi>N</m= i>
                                            </mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-= mml>
                                    </mjx-container>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">memory complexity when applied to an </sp= an><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"388" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                    <= mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                                            <= /mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D=441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"o=n" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" di=splay=3D"inline">
                                                    <mi>N</mi>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                                    <mi>N</mi=>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse system arising from 3D high-freque=
                                            ncy Helmholtz and Maxwell problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Loe2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-292" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Loe, Jennifer A., Heidi K. Thornquist, and E=
            rik G. Boman. =E2=80=9CPolynomial Preconditioned
            GMRES in Trilinos: Practical Considerations for High-Performanc=
            e Computing=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 SIAM Conference=
                on Parallel Processing for Scientific
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computing</span>. Society for In=
            dustrial and Applied Mathematics, Jan. 2020,
            pp. 35=E2=80=9345. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"smal=l-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1137/1.9781611976137.4.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Polynomial preconditioners for
                GMRES and other Krylov solvers are well-known but are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">infrequently used in large-scale s=
                    oftware libraries or applications. This may be
                    due to stability problems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or complicated algorithms. We impl=
                    ement the GMRES polynomial as a preconditioner
                    in the software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">library Trilinos and demonstrate t=
                    hat it is stable and effective for parallel
                    computing. Trade-offs when
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">selecting a polynomial degree and =
                    combining with other preconditioners are
                    analyzed. We also discuss
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication-avoiding (CA) proper=
                    ties of the polynomial and relate these to
                    current CA-GMRES
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lopez2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-293" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lopez, Florent et al. =E2=80=9CAsynchronous =
            SGD for DNN training on Shared-memory
            Parallel Architectures=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Pr=
                oceedings of the 2020 IEEE International Parallel and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Distributed Processing Symposium=
                Workshops</span>. IPDPSW =E2=80=9920. IEEE, May 2020.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1109/ipdpsw50202.2020.00168.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a parallel
                asynchronous Stochastic Gradient Descent algorithm for share=
                d
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory architectures. Different fr=
                    om previous asynchronous algorithms, we
                    consider the case where the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient updates are not particula=
                    rly sparse. In the context of the MagmaDNN
                    framework, we compare
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the parallel efficiency of the asy=
                    nchronous implementation with that of the
                    traditional synchronous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation. Tests are performe=
                    d for training deep neural networks on
                    multicore CPUs and GPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">devices.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lourenco2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-294" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lourenco, Christopher et al. <span class=3D"=cmti-10">User Guide for SLIP LU, A Sparse
                Left-Looking
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Integer-Preserving LU Factorizat=
                ion</span>. Version&nbsp;1.0.2. 2020.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">SLIP LU is a software package
                designed to exactly solve unsymmetric sparse linear systems,=
                </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"389" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>

                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-rel" space=3D"4">

                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi cl=ass=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">

                                    ctxtmen=u_counter=3D"392" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">

                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-= math>
                                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                <mi>x</mi>
                                                <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=88=88</mo>
                                                <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">=E2=84=9An
                        </= mi>
                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        <mi>r</mi></math></mjx-assistive-= mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">. This package performs a left-looking, r=
                            oundoff-error-free (REF) LU factorization
                        </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"393" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                        </mj= x-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D444 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                    <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c3D">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">=
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I"=></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D448 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx= -assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math

                                    <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">LDU=
                                    </mi>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                            tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"394" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">

                                </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>L</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    <= /mjx-container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"395" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D448 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mi>U</mi>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-cont= ainer>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">are integer, </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"396" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                                position: relative;">
                                                <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I">
                                                            </mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                    </mjx-math>
                                                    <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML"
                                                            display=3D"inline">
                                                            <mi>D</mi>
                                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                        <= /mjx-container>
                                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is diagonal, and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                            <mj= x-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                                                jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D="0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"397"
                                                                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative=;">
                                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i"=>

                                                                    </mjx-mi>

                                                                    display=3D"inline"><math
                                                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.=org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                                        <mi>P</mi>

                                                    </mjx-assistive-m= ml>
