                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"398" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D444 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mi>Q</mi>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-cont= ainer>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">are row and column permutations, respecti=
                                                vely. Note that the matrix </span>
                                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"399" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%;
                                                position: relative;">
                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D437 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                        </m= jx-mi>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mi>D=
                                                        </mi>
                                                    </math>
                                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is never explicitly computed nor needed; =
                                                thus this package uses only the matrices
                                            </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"400" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%;
                                                position: relative;">
                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I">
                                                            </mj= x-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline="><mi>L</mi></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D" cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                        <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                                            jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"401"
                                                            style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                                            <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D448 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                            </mjx-math>
                                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                                                <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML"
                                                                    display=3D"inline">
                                                                    <mi>U</mi>
                                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                            </mjx-cont= ainer><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The theory associated with this
                                                                co=
                                                                de is the Sparse Left-looking
                                                                Integer-Preserving (SLIP) LU factorization
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">[8]. Aside from solving sparse
                                                                lin=
                                                                ear systems exactly, one of the key goals of
                                                                this package is to provide a
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">framework for other solvers to
                                                                ben=
                                                                chmark the reliability and stability of their
                                                                linear solvers, as our final
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution vector </span>
                                                            <!-- l. 30 =
-->
                                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                                                jax=3D"CHTML" tabin=dex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"402"
                                                                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: re=lative;">
                                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                                </mjx-math>
                                                                <mjx-a= ssistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"
                                                                    display=3D"inline"><math
                                                                        xmlns=3D"http://w=ww.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                                        <mi>x</mi>
                                                                    </math></mjx-assis= tive-mml>
                                                            </mjx-container>
                                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is guaranteed to be exact. In
                                                                addition, S=
                                                                LIP LU provides a wrapper class for the GNU
                                                                Multiple
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Precision Arithmetic (GMP) and
                                                                GNU=
                                                                Multiple Precision Floating Point Reliable
                                                                (MPFR) libraries
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in order to prevent memory
                                                                leaks a=
                                                                nd improve the overall stability of these
                                                                external libraries. SLIP
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">LU is written in ANSI C and is
                                                                acc=
                                                                ompanied by a MATLAB interface.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-295" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lu, Yuechao et al. =E2=80=9CReducing the Amo=
            unt of Out-of-Core Data Access for GPU-Accelerated
            Randomized SVD=E2=80=9D. In: (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We propose two acceleration
                methods, namely Fused and Gram, for reducing outof-core
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data access when performing random=
                    ized singular value decomposition (RSVD) on
                    graphics processing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">units (GPUs). Out-of-core data her=
                    e are data that are too large to fit into the
                    GPU memory at once.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Both methods accelerate GPU-enable=
                    d RSVD using the following three schemes: (1) a
                    highly tuned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">general matrix-matrix multiplicati=
                    on (GEMM) scheme for processing out-of-core
                    data on GPUs; (2) a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data-access reduction scheme based=
                    on one-dimensional (1D) data partition; and
                    (3) a first-in, first-out
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(FIFO) scheme that reduces CPU-GPU=
                    data transfer using the reverse iteration. The
                    Fused method
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">further reduces the amount of out-=
                    of-core data access by merging two GEMM
                    operations into a single
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operation. In contrast, the Gram m=
                    ethod reduces both in-core and out-of-core data
                    access by explicitly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">forming the Gram matrix. According=
                    to our experimental results, the Fused and
                    Gram methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improved the RSVD performance by u=
                    p to 1.7</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attac=hed_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"403" style=3D"font-=size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden==3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </= mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D="inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inli=ne">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx= -container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">and 5.2</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-containe= r class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtme=nu_counter=3D"404" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-ma= th class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mm= l unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1=998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>=
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">respectively, compared with a stra=
                    ightforward method that deploys schemes (1) and
                    (2) on the GPU.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In addition, we present a case stu=
                    dy of deploying the Gram method for
                    accelerating robust principal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">component analysis (RPCA), a conve=
                    x optimization problem in machine
                    learning.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lu2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-296" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lu, Zhengyang, Yuyao Niu, and Weifeng Liu. =
            =E2=80=9CEfficient Block Algorithms for Parallel Sparse
            Triangular Solve=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedi=
                ngs of the 49th International Conference on Parallel
                Processing</span>.
            ICPP 2020. ACM, Aug. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1145/3404397.3404413.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The sparse triangular solve
                (SpTRSV) kernel is an important building block for a number =
                of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear algebra routines such as sp=
                    arse direct and iterative solvers. The major
                    challenge of accelerating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SpTRSV lies in the difficulties of=
                    finding higher parallelism. Existing work
                    mainly focuses on reducing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dependencies and synchronizations =
                    in the level-set methods. However, the 2D block
                    layout of the input
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix has been largely ignored in=
                    designing more efficient SpTRSV
                    algorithms.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9=">In this paper, we implement three block
               algorithms, i.e., column block, row block and recursive
            </span><span class=3D" cmr-9">block algorithms, for parallel SpT=
                    RSV on modern GPUs, and propose an adaptive
                    approach that can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automatically select the best kern=
                    els according to input sparsity structures. By
                    testing 159 sparse

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices on two high-end NVIDIA GP=
                    Us, the experimental results demonstrate that
                    the recursive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">block algorithm has the best perfo=
                    rmance among the three block algorithms, and it
                    is on average
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">4.72</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-cont= ainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ct=xtmenu_counter=3D"405" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mj= x-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.o=rg/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97<= /mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">(up to 72.03</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-con= tainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                c=txtmenu_counter=3D"406" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <m= jx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathC=lass-bin">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assisti= ve-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.=org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97=
                                            </mo>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">)
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and 9.95</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-= container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0=" ctxtmenu_counter=3D" 407" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;"=>
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Ma=thClass-bin">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=
                                                        =97</mo>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                </mjx-container>
                                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">(up to 61.08</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                <mjx-con= tainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                    tabindex=3D"0" c=txtmenu_counter=3D"408" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                                    position: relative;">
                                                    <m= jx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathC=lass-bin">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mo>
                                                        </mjx-math>
                                                        <mjx-assisti= ve-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                                            <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.=org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                display=3D"inline">
                                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97=
                                                                </mo>
                                                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">)
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">faster than cuSPARSE v2 and
                                                                Sync-f=
                                                                ree methods, respectively. Besides, our method
                                                                merely needs
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">moderate cost for preprocessing
                                                                th=
                                                                e input matrix, thus is highly efficient for
                                                                multiple right-hand sides
                                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and iterative scenarios.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Lubbe2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-297" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Lubbe, Retief et al. =E2=80=9CAnalysis of pa=
            rallel spatial partitioning algorithms for GPU
            based DEM=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Computers and G=
                eotechnics </span>125 (Sept. 2020), p. 103708. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <sp= an class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>:
            10.1016/j.compgeo.2020.103708.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The capability of solving a
                geotechnical discrete element method (DEM) applications is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">determined by the complexity of th=
                    e simulation and its computational
                    requirements. Collision detection
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms are fundamental to reso=
                    lve the mechanical collisions between millions
                    of particles efficiently.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">These algorithms are a bottleneck =
                    for many DEM applications resulting in
                    excessive memory usage
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or poor computational performance.=
                    In particular, for GPU based DEM, there are
                    many factors for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a user to consider when deciding o=
                    n an algorithm. This study discusses a set of
                    diverse classes of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">geotechnical problems and the impa=
                    ct of algorithm choice. Four factors were
                    considered: i) the world
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">domain size, number of particles a=
                    nd particle density, ii) polydispersity in
                    size, iii) the time evolution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and iv) the particle shape. This s=
                    tudy shows that for spherical particles, the
                    choice of broad-phase
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">collision detection algorithm has =
                    the most impact on computational performance.
                    The computational
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cost for convex polyhedral particl=
                    es is dominated by the selection of the
                    particles=E2=80=99 bounding volumes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and their intersection tests over =
                    the selection of the broad-phase collision
                    detection algorithm. On
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">average for convex polyhedral part=
                    icles, the broad-phase occupies at most 1.3% of
                    the total runtime,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">while the narrow-phase collision d=
                    etection and collision response require more
                    than 87% of the runtime.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">A combination of bounding spheres =
                    and axis-aligned bounding boxes for use as
                    bounding volumes of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">particles showed the best performa=
                    nce reducing the computational cost by 20%.
                    This study serves as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a guide for further research in th=
                    e field of GPU based DEM collision detection
                    and the application in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">geotechnics.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Luo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-298" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Luo, Xiaopeng and Xin Xu. =E2=80=9CRegulariz=
            ed asymptotic descents for nonconvex optimization=E2=80=9D.
            In: (Apr. 2020). arXiv: 2004.02210v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[m=
                ath.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper we propose
                regularized asymptotic descent (RAD) methods for solving non=
                convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization problems. Our motivat=
                    ion is first to apply the regularized iteration
                    and then to use an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">explicit asymptotic formula to app=
                    roximate the solution of each regularized
                    minimization. We consider
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a class of possibly nonconvex, non=
                    smooth, or even discontinuous objectives
                    extended from strongly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convex functions with Lipschitz-co=
                    ntinuous gradients, in each of which has a
                    unique global minima
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and is continuously differentiable=
                    at the global minimizer. The main theoretical
                    result shows that the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">RAD method enjoys the global linea=
                    r convergence with high probability for such a
                    class of nonconvex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">objectives, i.e., the method will =
                    not be trapped in saddle points, local minima,
                    or even discontinuities.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Besides, the method is derivative-=
                    free and its per-iteration cost, i.e., the
                    number of function evaluations,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is bounded, so that it has a compl=
                    exity bound </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"409" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4A=A TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop M=athClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-n qopname">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c67"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo cla=ss=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2061"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-m= frac>
                                        <mjx-frac>
                                            <mjx-num>
                                                <mjx-nstrut></mjx-nstrut>
                                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                    <mjx-= mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mn>
                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                </m= jx-num>
                                                <mjx-dbox>
                                                    <mjx-dtable>
                                                        <mjx-line></mjx-line>
                                                        <mjx-row>
                                                            <mjx-den>
                                                                <mjx-ds= trut>
                                                                    </mjx-dstrut>
                                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                            </mjx-den>
                                                        </mjx-row>
                                                        </mjx= -dtable>
                                                </mjx-dbox>
                                        </mjx-frac>
                                        </mjx-mfrac>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-s=op MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S1"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-= mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi ma=thvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-ope=n">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi class=3D"qopname">log</mi>
                                    <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>
                                    <mfrac>
                                        <mrow=>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                </mfrac>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo clas=s=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-contain= er>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">for finding a point such that the optimal=
                        ity gap at this point is less than </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"410" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </m= jx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c3E"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" disp=lay=3D"inline">
                                <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">&gt;</mo>
                                <m= n>0</mn>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.<= /span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ma2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-299" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ma, Linjian, Jiayu Ye, and Edgar Solomonik. =
            =E2=80=9CAutoHOOT: Automatic High-Order
            Optimization for Tensors=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.=
            04540v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">High-order optimization methods,
                including Newton=E2=80=99s method and its variants as well a=
                s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">alternating minimization methods, =
                    dominate the optimization algorithms for tensor
                    decompositions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and tensor networks. These tensor =
                    methods are used for data analysis and
                    simulation of quantum

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. In this work, we introduc=
                    e AutoHOOT, the first automatic differentiation
                    (AD) framework
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">targeting at high-order optimizati=
                    on for tensor computations. AutoHOOT takes
                    input tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computation expressions and genera=
                    tes optimized derivative expressions. In
                    particular, AutoHOOT
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">contains a new explicit Jacobian /=
                    Hessian expression generation kernel whose
                    outputs maintain the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">input tensors=E2=80=99 granularity=
                    and are easy to optimize. The expressions are then
                    optimized by both the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">traditional compiler optimization =
                    techniques and specific tensor algebra
                    transformations. Experimental
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results show that AutoHOOT achieve=
                    s competitive performance for both tensor
                    decomposition and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensor network applications compar=
                    ed to existing AD software and other tensor
                    computation libraries
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with manually written kernels, bot=
                    h on CPU and GPU architectures. The scalability
                    of the generated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels is as good as other well-k=
                    nown high-order numerical algorithms so that it
                    can be executed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiently on distributed paralle=
                    l systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Madani2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-300" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Madani, Ramtin et al. =E2=80=9CPenalized
            Semidefinite Programming for QuadraticallyConstrained Quadratic=
            Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://www.ocf.berkeley.edu/~madani/paper/penalized_sdp=.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.ocf.berkeley.edu/~m=
                            adani/paper/penalized_sdp.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we give a new
                penalized semidefinite programming approach for non-convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quadratically-constrained quadrati=
                    c programs (QCQPs). We incorporate penalty
                    terms into the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">objective of convex relaxations in=
                    order to retrieve feasible and near-optimal
                    solutions for non-convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">QCQPs. We introduce a generalized =
                    linear independence constraint qualification
                    (GLICQ) criterion
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and prove that any GLICQ regular p=
                    oint that is sufficiently close to the feasible
                    set can be used to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">construct an appropriate penalty t=
                    erm and recover a feasible solution. As a
                    consequence, we describe a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">heuristic sequential procedure tha=
                    t preserves feasibility and aims to improve the
                    bjective value at each
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteration. Numerical experiments o=
                    n large-scale system identification problems as
                    well as benchmark
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">instances from the library of quad=
                    ratic programming (QPLIB) demonstrate the
                    ability of the proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">penalized semidefinite programs in=
                    finding near-optimal solutions for non-convex
                    QCQP.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Madsen2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-301" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Madsen, Jonathan R. et al. =E2=80=9CTimemory=
            : Modular Performance Analysis for HPC=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Lecture Notes in Computer Science</span=>. Springer International Publishing, 2020,
                pp.
                434=E2=80=93452. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-=caps">doi</span></span>:
                10.1007/978-3-030-50743-5=CB=9922.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">HPC has undergone a significant
                transition toward heterogeneous architectures. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">transition has introduced several =
                    issues in code migration to support multiple
                    frameworks for targeting
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the various architectures. In orde=
                    r to cope with these challenges, projects such
                    as Kokkos and LLVM
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">create abstractions which map a ge=
                    neric front-end API to the backend that
                    supports the targeted
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architecture. This paper presents =
                    a complementary framework for performance
                    measurement and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis. Several performance meas=
                    urement and analysis tools in existence provide
                    their capabilities
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through various methods but the co=
                    mmon theme among these tools are prohibitive
                    limitations in terms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of user-level extensions. For this=
                    reason, software developers commonly have to
                    learn multiple tools
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and valuable analysis methods, suc=
                    h as the roofline model, are frequently
                    required to be generated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">manually. The timemory framework p=
                    rovides complete modularity for performance
                    measurement and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis and eliminates all restri=
                    ctions on user-level extensions. The timemory
                    framework also provides
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a highly-efficient and intuitive m=
                    ethod for handling multiple tools/measurements
                    (i.e., =E2=80=9Dcomponents=E2=80=9D)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">concurrently. The intersection of =
                    these characteristics provide ample evidence
                    that timemory can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">serve as the common interface for =
                    existing performance measurement and analysis
                    tools. Timemory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">components are developed in C++ bu=
                    t includes multi-language support for C,
                    Fortran, and Python
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">codes. Numerous components are pro=
                    vided by the library itself =E2=80=93 including, but
                    not limited to, timers,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory usage, hardware counters, a=
                    nd FLOP and instruction roofline models.
                    Additionally, analysis
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the intrinsic overhead demonstr=
                    ates superior performance in comparison with
                    popular tools.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mai2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-302" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mai, Ngoc Hoang Anh, Victor Magron, and Jean=
            -Bernard Lasserre. =E2=80=9CA sparse version of
            Reznick=E2=80=99s Positivstellensatz=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020).=
            arXiv: 2002.05101 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.AG]</span>.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">If </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"411" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" dis=play=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi>f</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is a positive definite form, Reznick=E2=
                    =80=99s Positivstellensatz [Mathematische Zeitschrift.
                    220 (1995), pp.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">75=E2=80=9397] states that there e=
                    xists </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attache=d_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"412" style=3D"font-si=ze: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">

                                <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c2208"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mstyle space=3D"4">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-b">
                                    <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D40D TEX-B">

                            xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>k</mi>

                                <mi>N</mi>

                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">such that </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"413" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">

                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2225"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i"=space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo cla=ss=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">

                                    <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-ali=gn: -0.15em;">

                                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">can be written as a sum of forms </span>

                                                        tabindex==3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"416" style=3D"font-size:
                                                        117.4%; position: relat=ive;">

                                                                    </mjx-mi>=
                                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                                <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;
                                                                    margin-left: -0.06=em;">
                                                                    <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45=9 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                                                </mjx-script>
                                                            </mjx-msub>
                                                        </mjx-math>
                                                        <mj= x-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                                                            <math xmlns=3D"http:=//www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                display=3D"inline">

                                                    </m= row>

                                                    </mrow>
                                                    </msub>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    </mjx-containe= r>
                                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">depends on a subset of the initial variab=
                                        les, and assuming that these subsets satisfy
                                        the so-called
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">running intersection property, we =
                                        provide a sparse version of Reznick=E2=80=99s
                                        Positivstellensatz. Namely, there
                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exists </span><!-- l. 30 -->

                                        </mj= x-container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">such that </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"418" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I">

                                                </mjx-mi>

                                                            </mjx-mn>

                                                    </mjx-script>
                                                </mjx-msub>
                                                <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                <mjx-msub>
                                                    <mj= x-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D70E TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </= mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -0.15em;">
                                <mjx-mrow=size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D459 TEX-I">
                                            </mjx-= c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                            </mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-msub sp=ace=3D"3">
                                <mjx-mrow>

                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>l</mi>
                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>

                                    <mi>p</mi>
                    </math>
                    <= /mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In other words, the sparsity patte=
                rn of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attache=d_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"424"
                style=3D"font-si=ze: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D="true">
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </= mjx-mi>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"=><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi>=
                            f</mi>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
            </mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is also reflected in this sparse version =
                of Reznick=E2=80=99s certificate of positivity. We next
                use this result to also
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">obtain positivity certificates for=
                (i) polynomials nonnegative on the whole space
                and (ii) polynomials
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nonnegative on a (possibly non-com=
                pact) basic semialgebraic set, assuming that
                the input data satisfy
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the running intersection property.=
                Both are sparse versions of a positivity
                certificate due to Putinar
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and Vasilescu.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Majumdar2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-303" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Majumdar, Anirudha, Georgina
            Hall, and Amir Ali Ahmadi. =E2=80=9CRecent Scalability Improvem=
            ents for Semidefinite Programming
            with Applications in Machine Learning, Control, and Robotics=E2=
            =80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Annual Review of
                Control,
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Robotics, and Autonomous Systems=
            </span>3.1 (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi=
                </span></span>: 10.1146/annurev-control-091819-074326.
            eprint: https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-control-091819-074326.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Historically, scalability has
                been a major challenge for the successful application of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">semidefinite programming in fields=
                    such as machine learning, control, and
                    robotics. In this article,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we survey recent approaches to thi=
                    s challenge, including those that exploit
                    structure (e.g., sparsity
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and symmetry) in a problem, those =
                    that produce low-rank approximate solutions to
                    semidefinite
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programs, those that use more scal=
                    able algorithms that rely on augmented
                    Lagrangian techniques and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the alternating-direction method o=
                    f multipliers, and those that trade off
                    scalability with conservatism
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(e.g., by approximating semidefini=
                    te programs with linear and second-order cone
                    programs). For each
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">class of approaches, we provide a =
                    high-level exposition, an entry point to the
                    corresponding literature,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and examples drawn from machine le=
                    arning, control, or robotics. We also present a
                    list of software
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">packages that implement many of th=
                    e techniques discussed in the review. Our hope
                    is that this article
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">will serve as a gateway to the ric=
                    h and exciting literature on scalable
                    semidefinite programming for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both theorists and practitioners.<= /span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mangoubi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-304" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mangoubi, Oren and Nisheeth K. Vishnoi. =E2=
            =80=9CA Second-order
            Equilibrium in Nonconvex-Nonconcave Min-max Optimization: Exist=
            ence and Algorithm=E2=80=9D. In:
            (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.12363v2 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS=
                ]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Min-max optimization, with a
                nonconvex-nonconcave objective function </span>
                <!-- l. 30 --=
><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabinde=
x=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"425" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rela=
tive;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mi class=3D"mj=
x-i"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D453 TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mo class=3D"mj=
x-n MathClass-punc" space=3D"4"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3A"></mjx-c></mjx-mo><=

><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D451 TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n =
MathClass-bin" space=3D"3"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c></mjx-mo><mjx-m=
i class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c211D TEX-A"></mjx-c><mjx=

lass-rel" space=3D"4"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2192"></mjx-c></mjx-mo><mjx-mi c=

i></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><mat=

></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,

               nonconvexity-nonconcavity

                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">

                                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">

                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">points.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Manguoglu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-305" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Manguo=C4=9Flu, Murat, Eric Polizzi, and Ahm=
            ed H. Sameh. =E2=80=9CParallel Hybrid Sparse Linear
            System Solvers=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Parallel A=
                lgorithms in Computational Science and
                Engineering</span>. Springer
            International Publishing, 2020, pp. 95=E2=80=93120. <span class==3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/978-3-0=
            30-43736-7=CB=994.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this chapter, we present the
                SPIKE family of algorithms for solving banded linear systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and its multithreaded implementati=
                    on as well as direct-iterative hybrid variants
                    for solving general
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse linear system of equations.=
                </span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Marchal2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-306" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Marchal, Loris et al. <span class=3D"cmti-10=">Trading Performance for Memoryin Sparse Direct
               Solvers using Low-rank
            </span><span class=3D" cmti-10">Compression</span>. Tech. rep. I=
            nria, 2020. HAL: 02976233. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-ca=ps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"https://hal.inria.fr/hal-02976233/document" class=3D="url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://hal.inria.fr/hal-02976233/document</s= pan></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse direct solvers using Block
                Low-Rank compression have been proven efficient to solve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems arising in many real-life=
                    applications. Improving those solvers is
                    crucial for being able to 1)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solve larger problems and 2) speed=
                    up computations. A main characteristic of a
                    sparse direct solver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using low-rank compression is when=
                    compression is performed. There are two
                    distinct approaches: (1)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">all blocks are compressed before s=
                    tarting the factorization, which reduces the
                    memory as much as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">possible, or (2) each block is com=
                    pressed as late as possible, which usually
                    leads to better speedup. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">objective of this paper is to desi=
                    gn a composite approach, to speedup
                    computations while staying under
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a given memory limit. This should =
                    allow to solve large problems that cannot be
                    solved with Approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">2 while reducing the execution tim=
                    e compared to Approach 1. We propose a
                    memory-aware strategy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where each block can be compressed=
                    either at the beginning or as late as
                    possible. We first consider
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the problem of choosing when to co=
                    mpress each block, under the assumption that
                    all information on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">blocks is perfectly known, i.e., m=
                    emory requirement and execution time of a block
                    when compressed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or not. We show that this problem =
                    is a variant of the NP-complete Knapsack
                    problem, and adapt an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">existing 2-approximation algorithm=
                    for our problem. Unfortunately, the required
                    information on blocks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">depends on numerical properties an=
                    d in practice cannot be known in advance. We
                    thus introduce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">models to estimate those values. E=
                    xperiments on the PaStiX solver demonstrate
                    that our new approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can achieve an excellent trade-off=
                    between memory consumption and computational
                    cost. For instance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on matrix Geo1438, Approach 2 uses=
                    three times as much memory as Approach 1 while
                    being three
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">times faster. Our new approach lea=
                    ds to an execution time only 30% larger than
                    Approach 2 when
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">given a memory 30% larger than the=
                    one needed by Approach 1</span>.
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Marin2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-307" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Marin, Oana, Emil Constantinescu, and Barry =
            Smith. =E2=80=9CA scalable matrix-free spectral element
            approach for unsteady PDE constrainedoptimization using PETSc/T=
            AO=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computational Science </span>(Se=
            pt. 2020), p. 101207. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">d=
                    oi</span></span>: 10.1016/j.jocs.2020.101207.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We provide a new approach for the
                efficient matrix-free application of the transpose of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Jacobian for the spectral elem=
                    ent method for the adjoint-based solution of
                    partial differential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equation (PDE) constrained optimiz=
                    ation. This results in optimizations of
                    nonlinear PDEs using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">explicit integrators where the int=
                    egration of the adjoint problem is not more
                    expensive than the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">forward simulation. Solving PDE co=
                    nstrained optimization problems entails
                    combining expertise from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiple areas, including simulati=
                    on, computation of derivatives, and
                    optimization. The Portable,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Extensible Toolkit for Scientific =
                    computation (PETSc) together with its companion
                    package, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Toolkit for Advanced Optimization =
                    (TAO), is an integrated numerical software
                    library that contains
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an algorithmic/software stack for =
                    solving linear systems, nonlinear systems,
                    ordinary differential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations, differential algebraic =
                    equations, and large-scale optimization
                    problems and, as such, is an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ideal tool for performing PDE-cons=
                    trained optimization. This paper describes an
                    efficient approach in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which the software stack provided =
                    by PETSc/TAO can be used for large-scale
                    nonlinear time-dependent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems. While time integration c=
                    an involve a range of high-order methods, both
                    implicit and explicit.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The PDE-constrained optimization a=
                    lgorithm used is gradient-based and seamlessly
                    integrated with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the simulation of the physical pro=
                    blem.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Martinsson2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-308" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Martinsson, Per-Gunnar and Joel Tropp. =E2=
            =80=9CRandomized Numerical Linear Algebra: Foundations
            &amp; Algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.01387 <= span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This survey describes
                probabilistic algorithms for linear algebra computations, su=
                ch as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">factorizing matrices and solving l=
                    inear systems. It focuses on techniques that
                    have a proven track record
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for real-world problem instances. =
                    The paper treats both the theoretical
                    foundations of the subject and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the practical computational issues=
                    .</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">Topics covered include =
                    norm estimation; matrix approximation by sampling; structured and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unstructured random embeddings; li=
                    near regression problems; low-rank
                    approximation; subspace
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteration and Krylov methods; erro=
                    r estimation and adaptivity; interpolatory and
                    CUR factorizations;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Nystr=C3=B6m approximation of posi=
                    tive-semidefinite matrices; single view
                    (=E2=80=9Dstreaming=E2=80=9D) algorithms; full
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rank-revealing factorizations; sol=
                    vers for linear systems; and approximation of
                    kernel matrices that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arise in machine learning and in s=
                    cientific computing.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mei2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-309" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mei, Jincheng et al. =E2=80=9COn the Global =
            Convergence Rates of Softmax Policy Gradient Methods=E2=80=9D.
            In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.06392v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs=
                .LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We make three contributions
                toward better understanding policy gradient methods in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tabular setting. First, we show th=
                    at with the true gradient, policy gradient with
                    a softmax parametrization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">converges at a </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -=
-><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabind=
ex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"441" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rel=
ative;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mi class=3D"m=
jx-i"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi><mjx-mrow space=3D=
"2"><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c=

c></mjx-mn><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3"><mjx-c class=

ass=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c></mjx-mi></mjx-mrow><mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=
 MathClass-close"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-mrow></mj=
x-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math xmln=
s=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"><mi>O</mi><mrow=

bin">=E2=88=95</mo><mi>t</mi></mrow><mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo></m=
row></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">rate, with constants depending on the pro=
blem and initialization. This result
               significantly expands the
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">recent asymptotic convergence resu=
lts. The analysis relies on two findings: that
               the softmax policy
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient satisfies a =C5=81ojasiew=
icz inequality, and the minimum probability of an
               optimal action during
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization can be bounded in ter=
ms of its initial value. Second, we analyze
               entropy regularized policy
            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient and show that it enjoys a=
 significantly faster linear convergence rate
            </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"442" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">

                                <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">

                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">toward softmax optimal policy. This resul=
                        t resolves an open question in the recent
                        literature. Finally,

                        d additional new </span><!-- l. 30 -->

                                    </mjx-mn>

                                        </mjx-mi=>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29="></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-mrow></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D=
" on" display=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"=display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>=CE=A9</mi>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>=
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mn>1</mn>

                                                    <mi>t</mi>
