                                        </mjx= -container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">lower bound results, we explain how entro=
                                            py regularization improves policy optimization,
                                            even with
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the true gradient, from the perspe=
                                            ctive of convergence rate. The separation of
                                            rates is further explained
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using the notion of non-uniform =
                                            =C5=81ojasiewicz degree. These results provide a
                                            theoretical understanding
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the impact of entropy and corro=
                                            borate existing empirical studies.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Melo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-310" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Melo, Jefferson G., Renato D. C. Monteiro, a=
            nd Hairong Wang. =E2=80=9CIteration-complexity
            of an inexact proximal acceleratedaugmented Lagrangian method f=
            or solving linearly
            constrainedsmooth nonconvex composite optimization problems=E2=
            =80=9D. In: (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper proposes and
                establishes the iteration-complexity of an inexact proximal =
                accelerated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">augmented Lagrangian (IPAAL) metho=
                    d for solving linearly constrained smooth
                    nonconvex composite
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization problems. Each IPAAL =
                    iteration consists of inexactly solving a
                    proximal augmented
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Lagrangian subproblem by an accele=
                    rated composite gradient (ACG) method followed
                    by a suitable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Lagrange multiplier update. It is =
                    shown that IPAAL generates an approximate
                    stationary solution in at
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">most </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-con= tainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    c=txtmenu_counter=3D"444" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <m= jx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-m= o class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>=
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mstyle class=3D"text">
                                        <mjx-mtext class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c6C"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c6F"></mjx-c>
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c67"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mtext>
                                    </mjx-mstyle>
                                    <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mr= ow>
                                            <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            <mjx-m= o class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F">
                                                    </mjx-= c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D70C T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                                            </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n=MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D70C TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                    </m= jx-mi>
                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mn>
                    </m= jx-mrow>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                            </= mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" disp=lay=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <ms= tyle class=3D"text">
                                        <mtext>log</mtext>
                                        </mstyle>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass=-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mn>1</mn>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                                <mi>=
                                                    =CF=81</mi>
                                            </mrow>

                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">ACG iterations, where </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -=
-><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabind=

ative;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mi class=3D"m=

ve-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the given tolerance. It is also shown =
that the previous complexity bound can be
               sharpened to</span>
            <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"

                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c67"></mjx-c>

                                        </mjx-mstyle>

                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D="3">

                                            </mjx-mn>

                                </mjx-m= ath>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>O</mi>
                                        <mrow>=
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                            <mrow>
                                                <mstyle class=3D"text">
                                                    <mtext>log<= /mtext>
                                                </mstyle>

                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                                    <mrow>
                                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                                        <m= o class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                                            <mi>=CF=81</mi>
                                                    </mrow>
                                                    <mo class=3D"=MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                                </mrow>

                                            <= mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>

                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                </mjx-= container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">under additional mildly stronger assumpti=
                                    ons. The above bounds are derived assuming that
                                    the initial
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">point is neither feasible nor the =
                                    domain of the composite term of the objective
                                    function is bounded.
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Some preliminary numerical results=
                                    are presented to illustrate the performance of
                                    the IPAAL method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-MendlerDuenner2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-311" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mendler-D=C3=BCnner, Celestine and Aurelien =
            Lucchi. =E2=80=9CRandomized Block-Diagonal
            Preconditioning for Parallel Learning=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2020)=
            . arXiv: 2006.13591v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We study preconditioned
                gradient-based optimization methods where the preconditionin=
                g
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix has block-diagonal form. Su=
                    ch a structural constraint comes with the
                    advantage that the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">update computation can be parallel=
                    ized across multiple independent tasks. Our
                    main contribution is to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate that the convergence o=
                    f these methods can significantly be improved
                    by a randomization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">technique which corresponds to rep=
                    artitioning coordinates across tasks during the
                    optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">procedure. We provide a theoretica=
                    l analysis that accurately characterizes the
                    expected convergence
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gains of repartitioning and valida=
                    te our findings empirically on various
                    traditional machine learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tasks. From an implementation pers=
                    pective, block separable models are well suited
                    for parallelization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and, when shared memory is availab=
                    le, randomization can be implemented on top of
                    existing methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">very efficiently to improve conver=
                    gence.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Menon2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-312" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Menon, Harshitha, Abhinav Bhatele, and Todd =
            Gamblin. =E2=80=9CAuto-tuning Parameter Choices
            in HPC Applications using Bayesian Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: <= span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the
                2020 IEEE
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">International Parallel and Distr=
                    ibuted Processing Symposium</span>. IPDPS =E2=80=9920.
                2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">High performance computing
                applications, runtimes, and platforms are becoming more
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">configurable to enable application=
                    s to obtain better performance. As a result,
                    users are increasingly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">presented with a multitude of opti=
                    ons to configure application-specific as well
                    as platform-level
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parameters. The combined effect of=
                    different parameter choices on application
                    performance is difficult
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to predict, and an exhaustive eval=
                    uation of this combinatorial parameter space is
                    practically infeasible.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">One approach to parameter selectio=
                    n is a user-guided exploration of a part of the
                    space. However,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such an ad hoc exploration of the =
                    parameter space can result in suboptimal
                    choices. Therefore, an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automatic approach that can effici=
                    ently explore the parameter space is needed. In
                    this paper, we propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">HiPerBOt, a Bayesian optimization =
                    based configuration selection framework to
                    identify application
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and platform-level parameters that=
                    result in high performing configurations. We
                    demonstrate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effectiveness of HiPerBOt in tunin=
                    g parameters that include compiler flags,
                    runtime settings, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application-level options for seve=
                    ral parallel codes, including, Kripke, Hypre,
                    LULESH, and OpenAtom.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mentus2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-313" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mentus, Cassidy and Marcus Roper. =E2=80=9CO=
            ptimal Mixing in Transport Networks: Numerical
            Optimization and Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 200=
            7.13637v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[nlin.AO]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many foraging microorganisms rely
                upon cellular transport networks to deliver nutrients,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fluid and organelles between diffe=
                    rent parts of the organism. Networked organisms
                    ranging from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">filamentous fungi to slime molds d=
                    emonstrate a remarkable ability to mix or
                    disperse molecules and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">organelles in their transport medi=
                    a. Here we introduce mathematical tools to
                    analyze the structure

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of energy efficient transport netw=
                    orks that maximize mixing and sending signals
                    originating from and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">arriving at each node. We define t=
                    wo types of entropies on flows to quantify
                    mixing and develop
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical algorithms to optimize t=
                    he combination of entropy and energy on
                    networks, given constraints
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the amount of available materia=
                    l. We present an in-depth exploration of
                    optimal single source-sink
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">networks on finite triangular grid=
                    s, a fundamental setting for optimal transport
                    networks in the plane.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Using numerical simulations and ri=
                    gorous proofs, we show that, if the constraint
                    on conductances
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is strict, the optimal networks ar=
                    e paths of every possible length. If the
                    constraint is relaxed, our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm produces loopy networks =
                    that fan out at the source and pour back into a
                    single path that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">flows to the sink. Taken together,=
                    our results expand the class of optimal
                    transportation networks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that can be compared with real bio=
                    logical data, and highlight how real network
                    morphologies may be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">shaped by tradeoffs between transp=
                    ort efficiency and the need to mix the
                    transported matter.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Miasnikof2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-314" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Miasnikof, Pierre, Seo Hong, and Yuri Lawrys=
            hyn. =E2=80=9CGraph Clustering Via QUBO and Digital
            Annealing=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.03872 <span cl=ass=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.SI]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This article empirically examines
                the computational cost of solving a known hard problem,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph clustering, using novel purp=
                    ose-built computer hardware. We express the
                    graph clustering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem as an intra-cluster distan=
                    ce or dissimilarity minimization problem. We
                    formulate our poblem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as a quadratic unconstrained binar=
                    y optimization problem and employ a novel
                    computer architecture
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to obtain a numerical solution. Ou=
                    r starting point is a clustering formulation
                    from the literature. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formulation is then converted to a=
                    quadratic unconstrained binary optimization
                    formulation. Finally,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we use a novel purpose-built compu=
                    ter architecture to obtain numerical solutions.
                    For benchmarking
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">purposes, we also compare computat=
                    ional performances to those obtained using a
                    commercial solver,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Gurobi, running on conventional ha=
                    rdware. Our initial results indicate the
                    purpose-built hardware
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provides equivalent solutions to t=
                    he commercial solver, but in a very small
                    fraction of the time required.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mills2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-315" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mills, Richard Tran et al. =E2=80=9CToward P=
            erformance-Portable PETSc for GPU-based Exascale
            Systems=E2=80=9D. In: (Nov. 2020). arXiv: 2011.00715 <span clas=s=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Portable Extensible Toolkit
                for Scientific computation (PETSc) library delivers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalable solvers for nonlinear tim=
                    e-dependent differential and algebraic
                    equations and for numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization.The PETSc design for =
                    performance portability addresses fundamental
                    GPU accelerator
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">challenges and stresses flexibilit=
                    y and extensibility by separating the
                    programming model used by the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application from that used by the =
                    library, and it enables application developers
                    to use their preferred
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programming model, such as Kokkos,=
                    RAJA, SYCL, HIP, CUDA, or OpenCL, on upcoming
                    exascale
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. A blueprint for using GPU=
                    s from PETSc-based codes is provided, and case
                    studies emphasize
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the flexibility and high performan=
                    ce achieved on current GPU-based
                    systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mishra2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-316" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mishra, Ashirbad, Shad Kirmani, and Kamesh M=
            adduri. =E2=80=9CFast Spectral Graph Layout
            on Multicore Platforms=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Pr=
                oceedings of the 49th International Conference on
                Parallel
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Processing</span>. ICPP 2020. AC=
            M, Aug. 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span=></span>:
                10.1145/3404397.3404471.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present ParHDE, a
                shared-memory parallelization of the High-Dimensional Embedd=
                ing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(HDE) graph algorithm. Originally =
                    proposed as a graph drawing algorithm, HDE
                    characterizes the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">global structure of a graph and is=
                    closely related to spectral graph computations
                    such as computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the eigenvectors of the graph Lapl=
                    acian. We identify compute- and
                    memory-intensive steps in HDE
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and parallelize these steps for ef=
                    ficient execution on shared-memory multicore
                    platforms. ParHDE can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">process graphs with billions of ed=
                    ges in minutes, is up to 18</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"447" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">faster than a prior parallel implementati=
                        on of HDE, and achieves up to a 24</span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"448" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                    </mjx-= c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-con= tainer>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">relative speedup on a 28-core system. We =
                            also implement several extensions of ParHDE and
                            demonstrate
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its utility in diverse graph compu=
                            tation-related applications.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-317" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mo, Tieqiang and Renfa Li. =E2=80=9CIterativ=
            ely solving sparse linear system based on PaRSEC task
            scheduling=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">The Internatio=
                nal Journal of High Performance Computing Applications
            </span>(Jan.
            2020), p. 109434201989999. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1177/1094342019899997.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">With the new architecture and new
                programming paradigms such as task-based scheduling
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">emerging in the parallel high perf=
                    ormance computing area, it is of great
                    importance to utilize these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">features to tune the monolithic co=
                    mputing codes. In this article, the classical
                    conjugate gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms targeting at sparse lin=
                    ear system </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_At=tached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"449" style=3D"fo=nt-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidd=en=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I">
                                </mjx= -c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-=n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx= -mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <= /mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline="><math xmlns=3D"
                        http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mi= mathvariant=3D"italic">Ax</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                            <mi>b</m= i></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">in Krylov subspace are pipelining to exec=
                    ute interdependent tasks on Parallel Runtime
                    Scheduling and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Execution Controller (PaRSEC) runt=
                    ime. Firstly, the sparse matrix </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"450" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                        <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>A=
                            </mi>
                        </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is split in rows to unfold more coarse-gr=
                    ained parallelism. Secondly, the partitioned
                    sub-vectors are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">not assembled into one full vector=
                    in RAM to run sparse matrix=E2=80=93vector product
                    (SpMV) operations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for eliminating the communication =
                    overhead. Moreover, in the SpMV computation, if
                    all elements of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">one column in the split sub-matrix=
                    are zeros, the corresponding product
                    operations of these elements
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">may be removed by reorganizing sub=
                    -vectors. Finally, the latency of migrating
                    sub-vector is partially
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overlapped by the duration of perf=
                    orming SpMV operations through the further
                    splitting in columns
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of sparse matrix on GPUs. In exper=
                    iments, a series of tests demonstrate that
                    optimal speedup and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">higher pipelining efficiency has b=
                    een achieved for the pipelined task scheduling
                    on PaRSEC runtime.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Fusing SpMV concurrency and dot pr=
                    oduct pipelining can achieve higher speedup and
                    efficiency.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mohammed2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-318" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mohammed, Thaha et al. =E2=80=9CDIESEL: A no=
            vel deep learning-based tool for SpMV computations
            and solving sparse linear equation systems=E2=80=9D. In: <span=class=3D"cmti-10">The Journal of
                Supercomputing
            </span>(Nov. 2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1007/s11227-020-03489-3.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse linear algebra is central
                to many areas of engineering, science, and business.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">The community has done considerabl=
                    e work on proposing new methods for sparse
                    matrix-vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication (SpMV) computations=
                    and iterative sparse solvers on graphical
                    processing units (GPUs).
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Due to vast variations in matrix f=
                    eatures, no single method performs well across
                    all sparse matrices. A
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">few tools on automatic prediction =
                    of best-performing SpMV kernels have emerged
                    recently and require
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">many more efforts to fully utilize=
                    their potential. The utilization of a GPU by
                    the existing SpMV kernels
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is far from its full capacity. Mor=
                    eover, the development and performance analysis
                    of SpMV techniques
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on GPUs have not been studied in s=
                    ufficient depth. This paper proposes DIESEL, a
                    deep learning-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tool that predicts and executes th=
                    e best performing SpMV kernel for a given
                    matrix using a feature
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">set carefully devised by us throug=
                    h rigorous empirical and mathematical
                    instruments. The dataset
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">comprises 1056 matrices from 26 di=
                    fferent real-life application domains including
                    computational fluid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dynamics, materials, electromagnet=
                    ics, economics, and more. We propose a range of
                    new metrics and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods for performance analysis, =
                    visualization, and comparison of SpMV tools.
                    DIESEL provides
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">better performance with its accura=
                    cy 88.2%, workload accuracy 91.96%, and average
                    relative loss 4.4%,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compared to 85.9%, 85.31%, and 7.6=
                    5% by the next best performing artificial
                    intelligence (AI)-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SpMV tool. The extensive results a=
                    nd analyses presented in this paper provide
                    several key insights into
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the performance of the SpMV tools =
                    and how these relate to the matrix datasets and
                    the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">metrics, allowing the community to=
                    further improve and compare basic and AI-based
                    SpMV tools in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the future.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mohanamuraly2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-319" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mohanamuraly, Pavanakumar and Gabriel Staffe=
            lbach. =E2=80=9CHardware Locality-Aware Partitioning
            and Dynamic Load-Balancing of Unstructured Meshes for Large-Sca=
            le Scientific Applications=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the Platform for Adv=
                anced Scientific Computing Conference</span>.
            ACM, June
            2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</= span></span>: 10.1145/3394277.3401851.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present an open-source
                topology-aware hierarchical unstructured mesh partitioning a=
                nd
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">load-balancing tool TreePart. The =
                    framework provides powerful abstractions to
                    automatically detect
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and build hierarchical MPI topolog=
                    y resembling the hardware at runtime. Using
                    this information
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it intelligently chooses between s=
                    hared and distributed parallel algorithms for
                    partitioning and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">loadbalancing. It provides a range=
                    of partitioning methods by interfacing with
                    existing shared and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed memory parallel partit=
                    ioning libraries. It provides powerful and
                    scalable abstractions like

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">onesided distributed dictionaries =
                    and MPI3 shared memory based halo communicators
                    for optimising
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">HPC codes. The tool was successful=
                    ly integrated into our in-house code and we
                    present results from a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large-eddy simulation of a combust=
                    ion problem.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Moeller2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-320" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">M=C3=B6ller, Matthias and Merel Schalkers. =
            =E2=80=9C
            <!-- l. 30 -->
            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"451"
                style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c7C">
                            </mjx-= c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43F TEX-I">
                            </mjx-= c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D456 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-=I"></mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mi>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                        <mjx-= c class=3D"mjx-c3E">
                            </mjx-c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                </mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselect=able=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/M=athML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">|</mo>
                        <mi mathvariant==3D"italic">Lib</mi>
                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">&gt;</mo>
                    </math></mjx-assis= tive-mml>
            </mjx-container>
            : A Cross-Platform Programming Framework for Quantum-Accelerate=
            d Scientific Computing=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Lecture Notes in Computer Science</= span>. Springer International Publishing,
                2020,
                pp. 451=E2=80=93464.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                    <= /span>: 10.1007/978-3-030-50433-5=CB=9935.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper introduces a new
                cross-platform programming framework for developing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quantum-accelerated scientific com=
                    puting applications and executing them on most
                    of today=E2=80=99s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cloud-based quantum computers and =
                    simulators. It makes use of C++ template
                    meta-programming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques to implement quantum al=
                    gorithms as generic, platform-independent
                    expressions, which get
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automatically synthesized into dev=
                    ice-specific compute kernels upon execution.
                    Our software framework
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">supports concurrent and asynchrono=
                    us execution of multiple quantum kernels via a
                    CUDA-inspired
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stream concept.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Montoison2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-321" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Montoison, Alexis and Dominique Orban. =E2=
            =80=9CTriCG and TriMR: Two Iterative Methods for
            Symmetric Quasi-Definite Systems=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). <sp= an class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1314=
                0/RG.2.2.12344.16645.
                arXiv: 2008.12863 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce iterative methods
                named TriCG and TriMR for solving symmetric
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quasi-definite systems based on th=
                    e orthogonal tridiagonalization process
                    proposed by Saunders, Simon
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and Yip in 1988. TriCG and TriMR a=
                    re tantamount to preconditioned block-CG and
                    block-MINRES
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with two right-hand sides in which=
                    the two approximate solutions are summed at
                    each iteration, but
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">require less storage and work per =
                    iteration. We evaluate the performance of TriCG
                    and TriMR on linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems generated from the SuiteSp=
                    arse Matrix Collection and from discretized and
                    stablized Stokes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations. We compare TriCG and Tr=
                    iMR with SYMMLQ and MINRES, the recommended
                    Krylov
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods for symmetric and indefini=
                    te systems. In all our experiments, TriCG and
                    TriMR terminate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">earlier than SYMMLQ and MINRES on =
                    a residual-based stopping condition with an
                    improvement of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">up to 50% in terms of number of it=
                    erations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mor2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-322" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mor, Uria and Haim Avron. =E2=80=9CSolving T=
            rust Region Subproblems Using Riemannian
            Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). arXiv: 2010.07547 <span= class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Trust Region Subproblem is a
                fundamental optimization problem that takes a pivotal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">role in Trust Region Methods. Howe=
                    ver, the problem, and variants of it, also
                    arise in quite a few
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">other applications. In this articl=
                    e, we present a family of globally convergent
                    iterative Riemannian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization algorithms for a vari=
                    ant of the Trust Region Subproblem that
                    replaces the inequality
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">constraint with an equality constr=
                    aint. Our approach uses either a trivial or a
                    non-trivial Riemannian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">geometry of the search-space, and =
                    requires only minimal spectral information
                    about the quadratic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">component of the objective functio=
                    n. We further show how the theory of Riemannian
                    optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">promotes a deeper understanding of=
                    the Trust Region Subproblem and its
                    difficulties, e.g. a deep
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">connection between the Trust Regio=
                    n Subproblem and the problem of finding affine
                    eigenvectors, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a new examination of the so-called=
                    hard case in light of the condition number of
                    the Riemannian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Hessian operator at a global optim=
                    um. Finally, we propose to incorporate
                    preconditioning via a careful
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">selection of a variable Riemannian=
                    metric, and establish bounds on the asymptotic
                    convergence rate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in terms of how well the precondit=
                    ioner approximates the input matrix.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Morinigo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-323" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mor=C3=AD=C3=B1igo, Jos=C3=A9 A. et al. =E2=
            =80=9CPerformance drop at executing communication-intensive parallel
            algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">The Journal of=
                Supercomputing </span>(Jan. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D="small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/s11227-019-03142-8.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This work summarizes the results
                of a set of executions completed on three fat-tree network
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">supercomputers: Stampede at TACC (=
                    USA), Helios at IFERC (Japan) and Eagle at PSNC
                    (Poland).

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Three MPI-based, communication-int=
                    ensive scientific applications compiled for
                    CPUs have been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">executed under weak-scaling tests:=
                    the molecular dynamics solver LAMMPS; the
                    finite element-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mini-kernel miniFE of NERSC (USA);=
                    and the three-dimensional fast Fourier
                    transform mini-kernel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bigFFT of LLNL (USA). The design o=
                    f the experiments focuses on the sensitivity of
                    the applications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to rather different patterns of ta=
                    sk location, to assess the impact on the
                    cluster performance. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accomplished weak-scaling tests st=
                    ress the effect of the MPI-based application
                    mappings (concentrated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">vs. distributed patterns of MPI ta=
                    sks over the nodes) on the cluster. Results
                    reveal that highly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed task patterns may impl=
                    y a much larger execution time in scale, when
                    several hundreds or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">thousands of MPI tasks are involve=
                    d in the experiments. Such a characterization
                    serves users to carry
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">out further, more efficient execut=
                    ions. Also researchers may use these
                    experiments to improve their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalability simulators. In additio=
                    n, these results are useful from the clusters
                    administration standpoint
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">since tasks mapping has an impact =
                    on the cluster throughput.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Moutafis2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-324" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Moutafis, B. E., G. A. Gravvanis, and C. K. =
            Filelis-Papadopoulos. =E2=80=9COn the design of
            two-stage
            multiprojection methods for distributed memory systems=E2=80=9D=
            . In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">The Journal of
                Supercomputing</span>
            (Feb. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps=">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/s11227-020-03201-5.
         </p><!-- l. 30 -->
         <p class=3D" noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Solving large sparse linear
                        systems, efficiently, on supercomputing infrastructures is a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">time-consuming component for a wid=
                    e variety of simulation processes. An effective
                    parallel solver should
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">meet the required specifications, =
                    concerning both convergence behavior and
                    scalability. Herewith,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a class of two-stage algebraic dom=
                    ain decomposition preconditioning schemes based
                    on the upper
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Schur complement method is propose=
                    d, in order to exploit appropriately
                    distributed memory systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with multicore processors. The des=
                    ign of the method has been focused on
                    homogeneous hybrid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel systems, i.e., distribute=
                    d and shared memory systems. However, the
                    proposed method can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">also be applied to heterogeneous s=
                    ystems, such as cloud infrastructures, or
                    hybrid parallel systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with accelerators, by modifying th=
                    e workload distribution algorithm and taking
                    into account the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different network latencies and ba=
                    ndwidths. The first stage of the proposed
                    schemes is related to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the assignment of the subdomains a=
                    mong the workstations of the distributed
                    system, whereas the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">second stage concerns the further =
                    redistribution of the subdomains to each core
                    of a processor. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proposed method utilizes multiproj=
                    ection techniques, based on semi-aggregated
                    subdomains, leading
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to improved convergence behavior a=
                    s the number of subdomains increases. Moreover,
                    a subspace
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compression technique is used, in =
                    order to improve the performance of the
                    preprocessing phase and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reduce the memory requirements of =
                    the proposed scheme. The preconditioning
                    schemes were combined
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with a parallel Krylov subspace me=
                    thod, i.e., the parallel preconditioned
                    GMRES(m) method. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence behavior, the performa=
                    nce and the scalability of the proposed
                    preconditioning schemes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are examined and compared to exist=
                    ing state-of-the-art methods, by conducting
                    several numerical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments on supercomputing infr=
                    astructures.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Moutafis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-325" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Moutafis, Byron E, George A Gravvanis, and C=
            hristos K Filelis-Papadopoulos. =E2=80=9CHybrid
            multi-projection method using sparse approximate inverses on GP=
            U clusters=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">The International Journal of High Perfo=
                rmance Computing Applications </span>(Feb.
            2020),
            p. 109434202090563. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sma=ll-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1177/1094342020905637.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The state-of-the-art
                supercomputing infrastructures are equipped with accelerator=
                s, such as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graphics processing units (GPUs), =
                    that operate as coprocessors for each
                    workstation of the distributed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory system. The multi-projectio=
                    n type methods are a class of algebraic domain
                    decomposition
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods based on semi-aggregation =
                    techniques. The multi-projection type methods
                    have improved
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence behavior, as the numbe=
                    r of subdomains increases, due to the
                    corresponding augmentation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the semi-aggregated local linea=
                    r systems with more coarse components, while
                    the number of fine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">components is reduced. Moreover, l=
                    imited amount of communications among the
                    workstations is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">required by the proposed method. T=
                    he utilization of the available GPUs allows an
                    increase in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number of subdomains along with fi=
                    ner-grained parallelism, leading to improved
                    performance. A
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">load-balancing algorithm that ensu=
                    res the concurrency of the computations on
                    multicore processors
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and GPUs is proposed. Flexible par=
                    allel preconditioned Krylov subspace iterative
                    methods enhanced

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with multi-projection type methods=
                    have been designed appropriately in order to
                    have improved
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance, compared to CPU-only =
                    or GPU-only executions, by exploiting the
                    available CPUs and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPUs of the distributed memory sys=
                    tem concurrently. The unsymmetric local linear
                    systems are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solved by the preconditioned Bi-Co=
                    njugate Gradient STABilized (BiCGSTAB) method
                    enhanced with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the modified generic factored appr=
                    oximate sparse inverse preconditioner, whereas
                    the preconditioned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conjugate gradient (CG) method alo=
                    ng with the symmetric factored approximate
                    sparse inverse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preconditioner is used for the sym=
                    metric positive definite local coefficient
                    matrices. Numerical results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regarding the convergence behavior=
                    , the performance, and the scalability of the
                    proposed method for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">several problems are given.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Moutafis2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-326" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Moutafis, Panagiotis et al. =E2=80=9CAlgorit=
            hms for processing the group K nearest-neighbor query
            on distributed frameworks=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10"=>Distributed and Parallel Databases
            </span>(Nov. 2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1007/s10619-020-07317-8.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Given two datasets of points
                (called Query and Training), the Group (K) Nearest-Neighbor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(GKNN) query retrieves (K) points =
                    of the Training with the smallest sum of
                    distances to every point
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the Query. This spatial query h=
                    as been studied during the recent years and
                    several performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improving techniques and pruning h=
                    euristics have been proposed. In previous work,
                    we presented the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">first MapReduce algorithm, consist=
                    ing of alternating local and parallel phases,
                    which can be used to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effectively process the GKNN query=
                    when the Query fits in memory, while the
                    Training one belongs to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Big Data category. In this pap=
                    er, we present a significantly improved
                    algorithm that incorporates
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a new high-performance refining me=
                    thod, a fast way to calculate distance sums for
                    pruning purposes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and several other minor coding and=
                    algorithmic improvements. Moreover, we
                    transform this algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(which has been implemented in the=
                    Hadoop framework) to SpatialHadoop (a popular
                    distributed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">framework that is dedicated to spa=
                    tial processing), using a novel two-level
                    partitioning method. Using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">real world and synthetic datasets,=
                    we also present a thorough experimental study
                    of the Hadoop and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SpatialHadoop versions of the algo=
                    rithm, including a backstage analysis of the
                    algorithm=E2=80=99s performance,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using metrics that highlight its i=
                    nternal functioning. Finally, we present an
                    experimental comparison
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the Hadoop, the SpatialHadoop v=
                    ersions and the version of our previous work,
                    showing that the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improved versions are the big winn=
                    ers, with the SpatialHadoop one being faster
                    than its Hadoop
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">counterpart.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Muehlebach2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-327" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Muehlebach, Michael and Michael I. Jordan. =
            =E2=80=9CContinuous-time Lower Bounds for
            Gradient-based Algorithms=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 200=
            2.03546 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This article derives lower bounds
                on the convergence rate of continuous-time gradient-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization algorithms. The algor=
                    ithms are subjected to a time-normalization
                    constraint that avoids
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a reparametrization of time in ord=
                    er to make the discussion of continuous-time
                    convergence rates
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">meaningful. We reduce the multi-di=
                    mensional problem to a single dimension,
                    recover well-known lower
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bounds from the discrete-time sett=
                    ing, and provide insights into why these lower
                    bounds occur. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">further explicitly provide algorit=
                    hms that achieve the proposed lower bounds,
                    even when the function
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">class under consideration includes=
                    certain non-convex functions.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mukhopadhyay2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-328" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mukhopadhyay, Samrat. =E2=80=9CStochastic Gr=
            adient Descent For Linear Systems With Sequential
            Matrix Entry Accumulation=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10"=>Signal Processing </span>(Jan. 2020), p.
            107494. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">=
                <span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.sigpro.2020.107494.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Conventional stochastic iterative
                methods are often employed for solving linear systems of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations involving large matrix s=
                    izes using low memory footprint. However, their
                    performances are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">often limited by the unavailabilit=
                    y of all the matrix entries, which is often
                    termed as the problem of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">missing data. Although Ma and Need=
                    ell [1] has recently proposed a method, termed
                    as mSGD, assuming
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a model for data missing that resu=
                    lts in improved convergence, their result is
                    also affected by constant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large variance of the stochastic g=
                    radient. In this paper we propose a SGD type
                    method termed as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cumulative information SGD (CISGD)=
                    for solving a linear system with missing data
                    with an additional

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provision to accumulate a very sma=
                    ll number of matrix entries sequentially per
                    iteration, termed as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the sequential matrix entry accumu=
                    lation (SEMEA) mechanism. CISGD uses the data
                    collected by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SEMEA mechanism along with the pri=
                    or model for data missing mechanism of [1] to
                    gradually reduce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variance of the stochastic gradien=
                    t. The convergence of the proposed CISGD is
                    theoretically analyzed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and some interesting implications =
                    of the result are investigated under a specific
                    SEMEA mechanism.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Finally, numerical experiments are=
                    performed along with simulations that
                    corroborate the theoretical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">findings regarding the efficacy of=
                    the proposed CISGD method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Mukunoki2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-329" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Mukunoki, Daichi and Takeshi Ogita. =E2=80=
            =9CPerformance and energy consumption of accurate and
            mixed-precision linear algebra kernels on GPUs=E2=80=9D. In: <s= pan class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of
                Computational and
                Applied
                </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Mathematics </span>(Jan. 2020), =
                p. 112701. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></= span>:
                    10.1016/j.cam.2019.112701.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper presents the
                implementation, performance, and energy consumption of accur=
                ate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and mixed-precision linear algebra=
                    kernels, including inner-product (DOT), dense
                    matrix=E2=80=93vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication (GEMV), dense matri=
                    x multiplication (GEMM), and sparse
                    matrix=E2=80=93vector
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication (SpMV) for the comp=
                    ressed sparse row (CSR) format (CSRMV), on
                    graphics processing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">units (GPUs). We employ a mixed-pr=
                    ecision design in our implementation, which
                    makes it possible to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">perform internal floating-point op=
                    erations with at least 2-fold the precision of
                    the input and output
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data precision: for binary32 data,=
                    the computation is performed on binary64, and
                    for binary64 data,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the computation is performed on 2-=
                    fold the precision with an accurate inner
                    product algorithm referred
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to as Dot2. We developed highly op=
                    timized implementations which can achieve
                    performance close to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the upper bound performance. From =
                    our evaluation on Titan V, a Volta architecture
                    GPU, we made
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the following observations: as the=
                    Dot2 operation consumes 11 times binary64
                    instructions, GEMM
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">requires the corresponding overhea=
                    ds (in terms of both execution time and energy
                    consumption),
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compared to the standard binary64 =
                    implementation. On the other hand, the accuracy
                    of DOT, GEMV,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and CSRMV is improved with a very =
                    small overhead to the execution time and up to
                    roughly 30%
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">overhead to the energy requirement=
                    .</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Murray2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>

            he Permuted Striped Block Model and its Factorization
            =E2=80=93 Algorithms with Recovery Guarantees=E2=80=9D. In: (Ap=
            r.&nbsp;10, 2020). arXiv: 2004.05094v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</spa= n>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce a novel class of
                matrices which are defined by the factorization </span>

                                class=3D"cm=ti-9">dissociated</span><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                                Matrices defined by this factorization can be expressed as a=
                                sum of</span>
                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"459" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
