                    and analyze their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quality using different DLB techni=
                    ques from the literature.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Pham2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-351" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Pham, Minh et al. =E2=80=9CAn efficient algo=
            rithm for minimizing multi non-smooth component
            functions=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of Comp=
                utational and Graphical Statistics </span>(Aug. 2020),
            pp. 1=E2=80=9323. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1080/10618600.2020.1804390.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many problems in statistics and
                machine learning can be formulated as an optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem of a finite sum of non-smo=
                    oth convex functions. We propose an algorithm
                    to minimize this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">type of objective functions based =
                    on the idea of alternating linearization. Our
                    algorithm retains the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simplicity of contemporary methods=
                    without any restrictive assumptions on the
                    smoothness of the loss
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">function. We apply our proposed me=
                    thod to solve two challenging problems:
                    overlapping group Lasso
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and convex regression with sharp p=
                    artitions (CRISP). Numerical experiments show
                    that our method
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is superior to the state-of-the-ar=
                    t algorithms, many of which are based on the
                    accelerated proximal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Pi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-352" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Pi, J., Honggang Wang, and Panos M. Pardalos=
            . =E2=80=9CA Dual Reformulation and Solution
            Framework for Regularized Convex Clustering Problems=E2=80=9D. =
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">European Journal of
                Operational
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Research </span>(Sept. 2020). <s= pan class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.101=
                6/j.ejor.2020.09.010.

        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Clustering techniques are
                powerful tools commonly used in statistical learning and dat=
                a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analytics. Most of the past resear=
                    ch formulates clustering tasks as a non-convex
                    problem, where a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">global optimum often cannot be fou=
                    nd. Recent studies show that hierarchical
                    clustering and k-means
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">clustering can be relaxed and anal=
                    yzed as a convex problem. Moreover, sparse
                    convex clustering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms are proposed to extend =
                    the convex clustering framework to
                    high-dimensional space by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">introducing an adaptive group-Lass=
                    o penalty term. Due to the non-smoothness
                    nature of the associated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">objective functions, there are sti=
                    ll no efficient fast-convergent algorithms for
                    clustering problems even
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with convexity. In this paper, we =
                    first review the structure of convex clustering
                    problems and prove the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">differentiability of their dual pr=
                    oblems. We then show that such reformulated
                    dual problems can be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiently solved by the accelera=
                    ted first-order methods with the feasibility
                    projection. Furthermore,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we present a general framework for=
                    convex clustering with regularization terms
                    and discuss a specific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation of this framework u=
                    sing </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"503" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-msub>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D43=F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-script style=3D"vertical-align: -=0.15em;">
                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c=31"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"=></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-script>
                        </mjx-msub>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assist= ive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3=.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <msub>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>L</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mro= w>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                    </mrow>
                            </msub>
                            </ma= th>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-norm.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We also derive the dual form for t=
                    he regularized convex clustering problems and
                    show that it can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be efficiently solved by embedding=
                    a projection operator and a proximal operator
                    in the accelerated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient method. Finally, we compa=
                    re our approach with several other
                    co-clustering algorithms using a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number of example clustering probl=
                    ems. Numerical results show that our models and
                    solution methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">outperform all the compared algori=
                    thms for both convex clustering and convex
                    co-clustering.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Pirkelbauer2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-353" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Pirkelbauer, Peter et al. =E2=80=9CXPlacer: =
            Automatic
            Analysis of Data AccessPatterns on Heterogeneous CPU/GPU System=
            s=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">the 2020 IEEE International Para=
                llel and Distributed Processing
                Symposium</span>. IPDPS =E2=80=9920. 2020.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1630806" class=3D"url="><span class=3D"
                        cmtt-10">https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1630806
            </span>=
            </a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper presents XPlacer, a
                framework to automatically analyze problematic data access
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">patterns in C++ and CUDA code. XPl=
                    acer records heap memory operations in both
                    host and device
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">code for later analysis. To this e=
                    nd, XPlacer instruments read and write
                    operations, function calls,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and kernel launches. Programmers m=
                    ark points in the program execution where the
                    recorded data is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analyzed and anomalies diagnosed. =
                    XPlacer reports data access anti-patterns,
                    including alternating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CPU/GPU accesses to the same memor=
                    y, memory with low access density, and
                    unnecessary data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">transfers. The diagnostic also pro=
                    duces summative information about the recorded
                    accesses, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">aids users in identifying code tha=
                    t could degrade performance.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr=-9">The paper evaluates
                    XPlacer using LULESH, a Lawrence Livermore
                    proxy application, Rodina benchmarks,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and an implementation of the Smith=
                    -Waterman algorithm. XPlacer diagnosed several
                    performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">issues in these codes. The elimina=
                    tion of a performance problem in LULESH
                    resulted in a 3</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"=MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter==3D"504" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class==3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math>
                        <= /mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">speedup on a heterogeneous platform combi=
                    ning Intel CPUs and Nvidia GPUs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ploskas2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-354" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ploskas, Nikolaos, Nikolaos V. Sahinidis, an=
            d Nikolaos Samaras. =E2=80=9CA triangulation and
            fill-reducing initialization procedure for the simplex algorith=
            m=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Mathematical
                Programming
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computation </span>(June 2020). =
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1=
            007/s12532-020-00188-1.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The computation of an initial
                basis is of great importance for simplex algorithms since it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">determines to a large extent the n=
                    umber of iterations and the computational
                    effort needed to solve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear programs. We propose three =
                    algorithms that aim to construct an initial
                    basis that is sparse and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">will reduce the fill-in and comput=
                    ational effort during LU factorization and
                    updates that are utilized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in modern simplex implementations.=
                    The algorithms rely on triangulation and
                    fill-reducing ordering
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques that are invoked prior =
                    to LU factorization. We compare the performance
                    of the CPLEX
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">12.6.1 primal and dual simplex alg=
                    orithms using the proposed starting bases
                    against CPLEX using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its default crash procedure over a=
                    set of 95 large benchmarks (NETLIB,
                    Kennington, M=C3=A9sz=C3=A1ros,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Mittelmann). The best proposed alg=
                    orithm utilizes METIS (Karypis and Kumar in
                    SIAM J Sci Comput
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">20:359=E2=80=93392, 1998), produce=
                    s remarkably sparse starting bases, and results in 5%
                    reduction of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">geometric mean of the execution ti=
                    me of CPLEX=E2=80=99s primal simplex algorithm.
                    Although the proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm improves CPLEX=E2=80=99s=
                    primal simplex algorithm across all problem types
                    studied in this paper,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">it performs better on hard problem=
                    s, i.e., the instances for which the CPLEX
                    default requires over
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">1000 s. For these problems, the pr=
                    oposed algorithm results in 37% reduction of
                    the geometric mean

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the execution time of CPLEX=E2=
                    =80=99s primal simplex algorithm. The proposed algorithm
                    also reduces
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the execution time of CPLEX=E2=80=
                    =99s dual simplex on hard instances by 10%. For the
                    instances that are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">most difficult for CPLEX, and for =
                    which CPLEX experiences numerical difficulties
                    as it approaches
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the optimal solution, the best pro=
                    posed algorithm speeds up CPLEX by more than 10
                    times. Finally,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the proposed algorithms lead to a =
                    natural way to parallelize CPLEX with speedups
                    over CPLEX=E2=80=99s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dual simplex of 1.2 and 1.3 on two=
                    and four cores, respectively.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Porcelli2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-355" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Porcelli, Margherita and Philippe L. Toint. =
            =E2=80=9CGlobal and local information in structured
            derivative free optimization with BFO=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020)=
            . arXiv: 2001.04801 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">A structured version of
                derivative-free random pattern search optimization algorithm=
                s is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">introduced which is able to exploi=
                    t coordinate partially separable structure
                    (typically associated with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparsity) often present in unconst=
                    rained and bound-constrained optimization
                    problems. This technique
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improves performance by orders of =
                    magnitude and makes it possible to solve large
                    problems that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">otherwise are totally intractable =
                    by other derivative-free methods. A library of
                    interpolation-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">modelling tools is also described,=
                    which can be associated to the structured or
                    unstructured versions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the initial BFO pattern search =
                    algorithm. The use of the library further
                    enhances performance,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">especially when associated with st=
                    ructure. The significant gains in performance
                    associated with these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">two techniques are illustrated usi=
                    ng a new freely-available release of BFO which
                    incorporates them.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">A interesting conclusion of the re=
                    sults presented is that providing global
                    structural information on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a problem can result in significan=
                    tly less evaluations of the objective function
                    than attempting to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">building local Taylor-like models.=
                </span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Priest2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-356" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Priest, Benjamin W., Alec Dunton, and Geoffr=
            ey Sanders. =E2=80=9CScaling Graph Clustering with
            Distributed Sketches=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.126=
            69v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The unsupervised learning of
                community structure, in particular the partitioning vertices
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">into clusters or communities, is a=
                    canonical and well-studied problem in
                    exploratory graph analysis.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">However, like most graph analyses =
                    the introduction of immense scale presents
                    challenges to traditional
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods. Spectral clustering in di=
                    stributed memory, for example, requires
                    hundreds of expensive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bulk-synchronous communication rou=
                    nds to compute an embedding of vertices to a
                    few eigenvectors
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of a graph associated matrix. Furt=
                    hermore, the whole computation may need to be
                    repeated if the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">underlying graph changes some low =
                    percentage of edge updates. We present a method
                    inspired by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">spectral clustering where we inste=
                    ad use matrix sketches derived from random
                    dimension-reducing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">projections. We show that our meth=
                    od produces embeddings that yield performant
                    clustering results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">given a fully-dynamic stochastic b=
                    lock model stream using both the fast
                    Johnson-Lindenstrauss and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CountSketch transforms. We also di=
                    scuss the effects of stochastic block model
                    parameters upon the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">required dimensionality of the sub=
                    sequent embeddings, and show how random
                    projections could
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significantly improve the performa=
                    nce of graph clustering in distributed
                    memory.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-357" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Qianqian Zhu, Andy Zaidman. =E2=80=9CMassive=
            ly Parallel, Highly Efficient, but What About the
            Test Suite Quality? Applying Mutation Testing to GPU Programs=
            =E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">International Conference on Soft=
                ware Testing, Verification, and
                Validation</span>. ICST =E2=80=9920. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Thanks to rapid advances in
                programmability and performance, GPUs have been widely
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applied in HighPerformance Computi=
                    ng (HPC) and safety-critical domains. As such,
                    quality assurance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of GPU applications has gained inc=
                    reasing attention. This brings us to mutation
                    testing, a fault-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">testing technique that assesses th=
                    e test suite quality by systematically
                    introducing small artificial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">faults. It has been shown to perfo=
                    rm well in exposing faults. In this paper, we
                    investigate whether
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU programming can benefit from m=
                    utation testing. In addition to conventional
                    mutation operators,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we propose nine GPU-specific mutat=
                    ion operators based on the core syntax
                    differences between CPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and GPU programming. We conduct a =
                    preliminary study on six CUDA systems. The
                    results show that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mutation testing can effectively e=
                    valuate the test quality of GPU programs:
                    conventional mutation

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operators can guide the engineers =
                    to write simple direct tests, while
                    GPU-specific mutation operators
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can lead to more intricate test ca=
                    ses which are better at revealing GPU-specific
                    weaknesses.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Quirynen2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-358" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Quirynen, Rien and Stefano Di Cairano.
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Block-Structured Preconditioning of Ite=
                rative Solverswithin a Primal Active-Set Method
                for fast
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">MPC</span>. Tech. rep. Mitsubish=
            i Electric Research Laboratories (MERL), 2020.
            TR: TR2020-134.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://www.merl.com/publications/docs/TR2020-134.pdf" c=lass=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www.merl.com/publications/docs=
                            /TR2020-134.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Model predictive control (MPC)
                for linear dynamical systems requires solving an optimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">control structured quadratic progr=
                    am (QP) at each sampling instant. This paper
                    proposes a primal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">active-set strategy, called PRESAS=
                    , for the efficient solution of such
                    block-sparse QPs, based on a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preconditioned iterative solver to=
                    compute the search direction in each
                    iteration. Rank-one factorization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">updates of the preconditioner resu=
                    lt in a per-iteration computational complexity
                    of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctxt=Menu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"505" styl=e=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ar=ia-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx=-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx= -mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D441 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-c=lose">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-scr=ipt">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi ma=thvariant=3D"italic">Nm</mi>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">=
                                    )</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-=9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where m denotes the number of stat=
                    e and control variables and N the number of
                    control intervals. Three
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different block-structured precond=
                    itioning techniques are presented and their
                    numerical properties are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">studied further. In addition, an a=
                    ugmented Lagrangian based implementation is
                    proposed to avoid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a costly initialization procedure =
                    to find a primal feasible starting point. Based
                    on a standalone C
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">code implementation, we illustrate=
                    the computational performance of PRESAS
                    against current state
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the art QP solvers for multiple=
                    linear and nonlinear MPC case studies. We also
                    show that the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solver is real-time feasible on a =
                    dSPACE MicroAutoBox-II rapid prototyping unit
                    for vehicle control
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications, and numerical reliab=
                    ility is illustrated based on experimental
                    results from a testbench of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">small-scale autonomous vehicles.</= span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Rahimian2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-359" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Rahimian, Hamed and Sanjay Mehrotra. =E2=80=
            =9CSequential Convexification of a Bilinear Set=E2=80=9D. In:
            (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url=
                </span></span>: <a href=3D"http://www.optimization-online.org/DB_FILE/2020/=01/7595.pdf"
                class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">http://www.optimization-=
                    online.org/DB_FILE/2020/01/7595.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a sequential
                convexification procedure to derive, in the limit, a set arb=
                itrary close
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to the convex hull of </span>
                <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"506" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi cla=ss=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>=
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"ht=tp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                            <= /math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-feasible
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solutions to a general nonconvex c=
                    ontinuous bilinear set. Recognizing that
                    bilinear terms can be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">represented with a finite number n=
                    onlinear nonconvex constraints in the lifted
                    matrix space, our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">procedure performs a sequential co=
                    nvexification with respect to all nonlinear
                    nonconvex constraints.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Moreover, our approach relies on g=
                    enerating liftand-project cuts using simple 0-1
                    disjunctions, where
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cuts are generated at all fraction=
                    al extreme point solutions of the current
                    relaxation. An implication of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our convexification procedure is t=
                    hat the constraints describing the convex hull
                    can be used in a cutting
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">plane algorithm to solve a linear =
                    optimization problem over the bilinear set to
                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"507" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hid=den=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I">
                                </mj= x-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline="><mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container><span cla=
ss=3D" cmr-9">-optimality</span>.
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Raponi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-360" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Raponi, Elena et al. =E2=80=9CHigh Dimension=
            al Bayesian Optimization Assisted by Principal
            Component Analysis=E2=80=9D. In: (July 2020). arXiv: 2007.00925=
            v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.NE]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Bayesian Optimization (BO) is a
                surrogate-assisted global optimization technique that has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">been successfully applied in vario=
                    us fields, e.g., automated machine learning and
                    design optimization.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Built upon a so-called infill-crit=
                    erion and Gaussian Process regression (GPR),
                    the BO technique suffers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from a substantial computational c=
                    omplexity and hampered convergence rate as the
                    dimension of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the search spaces increases. Scali=
                    ng up BO for high-dimensional optimization
                    problems remains a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">challenging task. In this paper, w=
                    e propose to tackle the scalability of BO by
                    hybridizing it with a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Principal Component Analysis (PCA)=
                    , resulting in a novel PCA-assisted BO (PCA-BO)
                    algorithm.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Specifically, the PCA procedure le=
                    arns a linear transformation from all the
                    evaluated points during
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the run and selects dimensions in =
                    the transformed space according to the
                    variability of evaluated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">points. We then construct the GPR =
                    model, and the infill-criterion in the space
                    spanned by the selected
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dimensions. We assess the performa=
                    nce of our PCA-BO in terms of the empirical
                    convergence rate and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CPU time on multi-modal problems f=
                    rom the COCO benchmark framework. The
                    experimental results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show that PCA-BO can effectively r=
                    educe the CPU time incurred on high-dimensional
                    problems, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">maintains the convergence rate on =
                    problems with an adequate global structure.
                    PCA-BO therefore

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provides a satisfactory trade-off =
                    between the convergence rate and computational
                    efficiency opening
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new ways to benefit from the stren=
                    gth of BO approaches in high dimensional
                    numerical optimization.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Regev2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-361" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Regev, Shaked and
            Michael A. Saunders. <span class=3D"cmti-10">SSAI: A Symmetric =
                Sparse Approximate Inverse Preconditioner for
                the
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Conjugate Gradient Methods PCG a=
                nd PCGLS</span>. Tech. rep. Stanford
            University, 2020. eprint:
            M130639. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">ur=
                    l</span></span>: <a href=3D"https://web.stanford.edu/group/SOL/reports/M130=639.pdf"
                class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://web.stanford.edu/gro=
                    up/SOL/reports/M130639.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We propose a method for solving a
                Hermitian positive definite linear system </span>
                <!-- l. 30 =
-->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabin=dex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"508" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: re=lative;">

                        <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D465=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">=
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"=4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44F TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assis= tive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w=3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">Ax</m= i>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                <mi>b</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>=
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">where </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"509" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math

                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            <= /mjx-container>
                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">is an explicit sparse matrix (real or com=
                                    plex). A sparse approximate right inverse
                                </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_A=ttached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"510" style=3D"f=ont-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline="><mi>M</mi></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D" cmr-9">is computed and replaced by its symmetriz=
                                            ation </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attache=d_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"511" style=3D"font-si=ze: 117.4%;
                                                position: relative;">

                                                            padding-left:=0.629em; margin-bottom: -0.611em;">
                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop" style=3D"widt=h: 0px; margin-left:
                                                                -0.5em;">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2DC TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mj= x-mo>
                                                        </mjx-over>
                                                        <mjx-base>
                                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c1D440 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                                        </mjx-base>
                                                    </mjx-mover>
                                                    </mjx= -math>
                                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                            xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                            display=3D"inline">
                                                            <mover accent=3D="true">
                                                                <mrow>
                                                                    <mi>M</mi>
                                                                </mrow>
                                                                <mo accent=3D"true">=CC=83</mo>
                                                            </mover>
                                                            </math=>
                                                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which is used as a left-right prec=
                                                onditioner in a modified version of the
                                                preconditioned conjugategradient
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method (PCG), where </span>
                                            <!-- l.=
 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                t=abindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"512" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                                position=: relative;">

                                                    <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                        xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mi>M</mi>
                                                    </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is modified occasionally, if necessary, t=
                                                o make it more positive definite. </span>
                                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"513" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%;
                                                position: relative;">
                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                        </m= jx-mi>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                                    <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mi>M=
                                                        </mi>
                                                    </math>
                                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">is formed column by column and can theref=
                                                ore be computed in parallel. PCG requires only
                                                matrix-vector
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplications with </span>
                                            <!-- l=
. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0"
                                                jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"514"
                                                style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positio=n: relative;">
                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                    <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D434 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <= mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                        xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mi>A</mi>
                                                    </math></mjx-= assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"515" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                                position: relative;">

                                                            0.629em; margin-bottom: -0.611em;">
                                                            <mjx-mo= class=3D"mjx-lop" style=3D"width: 0px; margin-left:
                                                                -0.5em;">
                                                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2DC TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                        </mjx-over>
                                                        <mjx-base>
                                                            <mjx-mrow>
                                                                <mjx-m= i class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                                    </mjx-= mrow>
                                                        </mjx-base>
                                                    </mjx-mover>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"o=n" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        di=splay=3D"inline">
                                                        <mover accent=3D"true">
                                                            <mrow>
                                                                <mi>M</mi>
                                                            </mrow>
                                                            <mo accent==3D"true">=CC=83</mo>
                                                        </mover>
                                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">(not solving a linear system with </span>=
                                            <!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"C=HTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"516" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                                p=osition: relative;">
                                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                    <mjx-m= over>
                                                        <mjx-over style=3D"padding-bottom: 0.105em; padding-left:
                                                            0.629em; mar=gin-bottom: -0.611em;">
                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop" style=3D"width: 0px; margi=n-left:
                                                                -0.5em;">
                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2DC TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                                            </mjx-mo>
                                                            </mjx-ov= er>
                                                            <mjx-base>
                                                                <mjx-mrow>
                                                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D440 T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                    </mjx-mi>
                                                                </mjx-mrow>
                                                            </mjx-base>
                                                            </mjx-mover>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-as= sistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                        xmlns=3D"http://ww=w.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mover accent=3D"true">
                                                            <mrow>=
                                                                <mi>M</mi>
                                                            </mrow>
                                                            <mo accent=3D"true">=CC=83</mo>
                                                        </mover>
                                                    </math></mjx-assist= ive-mml>
                                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">),
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and so too can be carried out in p=
                                                arallel. We compare it with incomplete Cholesky
                                                factorization (the
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gold standard for PCG) and with MA=
                                                TLAB=E2=80=99s backslash operator (sparse Cholesky) on
                                                matrices from
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">various applications. For least-sq=
                                                uares problems, we implement an analogous form
                                                of preconditioned
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Conjugate Gradient Least-Squares (=
                                                PCGLS) which is also shown to be robust.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Ren2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-362" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ren, Yanfei and David F. Gleich. =E2=80=9CA =
            Simple Study of Pleasing Parallelism on Multicore
            Computers=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Parallel Algori=
                thms in Computational Science and Engineering</span>.
            Springer
            International Publishing, 2020, pp. 325=E2=80=93346. <span clas=s=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/978-3-=
            030-43736-7=CB=9911.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Pleasingly parallel computations
                are those that involve completely independent work. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">investigate these in the context o=
                    f a problem we call AllPageRank. The
                    AllPageRank problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">involves computing a subset of acc=
                    urate PageRank entries for each possible seeded
                    PageRank vector.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">AllPageRank is representative of a=
                    wider class of possible computational
                    procedures that will run
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a large number of experiments on a=
                    single graph structure. Our study involves
                    computing the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">AllPageRank vectors for a multi-mi=
                    llion node graph within a reasonable timeframe
                    on a modern
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">shared memory, high-core count com=
                    puter. For this setting, we parallelize over
                    all of the seeded
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PageRank vector computations, whic=
                    h are all independent. The experiments
                    demonstrate that there are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-trivial complexities in obtain=
                    ing performance even in this ideal situation.
                    For instance, threading
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational environments gave sc=
                    aling problems with a shared graph structure in
                    memory. Also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse matrix ordering techniques =
                    and multivector, or SIMD, optimizations were
                    required to get a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">total runtime of a few days. We al=
                    so show how different algorithms for PageRank
                    that have different
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithmic advances and memory ac=
                    cess patterns behave to guide future
                    investigation of similar
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Rezaei2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-363" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Rezaei, Javad et al. =E2=80=9CEIA-CNDP: An E=
            xact Iterative Algorithm for Critical Node
            Detection Problem=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Compute=
                rs &amp; Operations Research </span>(Nov. 2020), p.
            105138. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi=
                </span></span>:
            10.1016/j.cor.2020.105138.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In designing reliable and
                impermeable networks, the robustness of the network is evalu=
                ated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">against the removal and failure of=
                    the node or edge where the network robustness
                    (network connectivity)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is measured using various metrics =
                    (objective functions) such as the number of
                    connected components,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">size of the largest connected comp=
                    onent, and pairwise connectivity. Critical node
                    detection problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(CNDP) is one of the main issues i=
                    n this literature, which aims to find a set of
                    vertices whose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">removal maximizes or minimizes som=
                    e objective function. In this paper, the focus
                    is on solving CNDP,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">considering the size of the larges=
                    t connected component as its objective
                    function. In this regard,

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we introduce a new problem called =
                    K-Group-Division-Problem and present a mixed
                    integer linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programming model to solve it. We =
                    prove that under certain circumstances, any
                    optimal solution of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new problem is also an optimal sol=
                    ution of CNDP. Analyzing the performance of the
                    proposed model on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving CNDP, indicates that this =
                    model is highly competitive against the base
                    model in the literature.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Furthermore, a novel exact algorit=
                    hm is introduced which improves the proposed
                    mixed integer linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programming model to address CNDP =
                    more efficiently. The results show that the
                    proposed algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is much more efficient, and, compa=
                    red with the base model, it can solve the
                    problem on networks with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a higher number of nodes.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Rodomanov2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-364" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Rodomanov, Anton and Yurii Nesterov. =E2=80=
            =9CGreedy Quasi-Newton Methods with Explicit
            Superlinear Convergence=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.=
            00657 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we study greedy
                variants of quasi-Newton methods. They are based on the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">updating formulas from a certain s=
                    ubclass of the Broyden family. In particular,
                    this subclass includes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the well-known DFP, BFGS and SR1 u=
                    pdates. However, in contrast to the classical
                    quasi-Newton
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods, which use the difference =
                    of successive iterates for updating the Hessian
                    approximations, our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods apply basis vectors, greed=
                    ily selected so as to maximize a certain
                    measure of progress. For
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">greedy quasi-Newton methods, we es=
                    tablish an explicit non-asymptotic bound on
                    their rate of local
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">superlinear convergence, which con=
                    tains a contraction factor, depending on the
                    square of the iteration
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">counter. We also show that these m=
                    ethods produce Hessian approximations whose
                    deviation from the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exact Hessians linearly convergenc=
                    es to zero.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Rodomanov2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-365" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CNew Results on Superlinea=
            r Convergence of Classical Quasi-Newton Methods=E2=80=9D. In: <span class==3D"cmti-10">CORE
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Discussion Papers ; 2020/13 (202=
                0) 24 pages http://hdl.handle.net/2078.1/229640
            </span>(Apr.
            2020). arXiv: 2004.14866v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</s= pan>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We present a new theoretical
                analysis of local superlinear convergence of the classical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">quasi-Newton methods from the conv=
                    ex Broyden class. Our analysis is based on the
                    potential function
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">involving the logarithm of determi=
                    nant of Hessian approximation and the trace of
                    inverse Hessian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approximation. For the well-known =
                    DFP and BFGS methods, we obtain the rates of
                    the form </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJa=x CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"517=" style=3D" font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">

                        </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mfenced open=3D"[" separators=3D"" c=lose=3D"]">
                                <mrow>

                                        </mrow>=
                                    </mfrac>
                                    <mfenced open=3D"(" separators=3D"" close=3D")">
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi class=3D"=qopname">exp</mi>
                                            <mo>=E2=81=A1</mo>

                                                <mrow>
