                    ation (AO) and the Alternating Direction Method
                    of Multipliers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(ADMM). The framework facilitates =
                    the use of a variety of constraints, loss
                    functions and couplings
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with linear transformations in a s=
                    eamless way. Numerical experiments on simulated
                    and real datasets
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate that the proposed appr=
                    oach is accurate, and computationally efficient
                    with comparable or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">better performance than available =
                    CMTF methods for Frobenius norm loss, while
                    being more flexible.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Using Kullback-Leibler divergence =
                    on count data, we demonstrate that the
                    algorithm yields accurate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results also for other loss functi=
                    ons.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Schmidt2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-375" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Schmidt, Drew. =E2=80=9CA Survey of Singular=
            Value Decomposition Methods for Distributed
            Tall/Skinny Data=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv: 2009.00761 =
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The Singular Value Decomposition
                (SVD) is one of the most important matrix factorizations,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">enjoying a wide variety of applica=
                    tions across numerous application domains. In
                    statistics and data

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis, the common applications =
                    of SVD such as Principal Components Analysis
                    (PCA) and linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regression. Usually these applicat=
                    ions arise on data that has far more rows than
                    columns, so-called
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">=E2=80=9Dtall/skinny=E2=80=9D matr=
                    ices. In the big data analytics context, this may take the form
                    of hundreds of millions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to billions of rows with only a fe=
                    w hundred columns. There is a need, therefore,
                    for fast, accurate,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and scalable tall/skinny SVD imple=
                    mentations which can fully utilize modern
                    computing resources.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">To that end, we present a survey o=
                    f three different algorithms for computing the
                    SVD for these kinds
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of tall/skinny data layouts using =
                    MPI for communication. We contextualize these
                    with common big
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data analytics techniques, princip=
                    ally PCA. Finally, we present both CPU and GPU
                    timing results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from the Summit supercomputer, and=
                    discuss possible alternative
                    approaches.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Scott2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-376" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Scott, J. and M T=C5=AFma. =E2=80=9CA comput=
            ational study of using black-box QR solvers for large-scale
            sparse-dense linear least squares problems=E2=80=9D. In: <span=class=3D"cmti-10">ACM Transactions on
                Mathematical
                Software</span>
            (2020). eprint: RAL-P-2020-004. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span=class=3D"small-caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"http://purl.org/net/epubs/manifestation/47616417/RAL=-P-2020-004.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://purl.org/net/=
                    epubs/manifestation/47616417/RAL-P-2020-004.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Large-scale overdetermined linear
                least squares problems arise in many practical applications,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both as subproblems of nonlinear l=
                    east squares problems and in their own right.
                    One popular solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method is based on the backward st=
                    able QR factorization of the system matrix A.
                    This paper focuses
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on sparse-dense linear least squar=
                    es problems, that is, problems where A is
                    sparse except from a small
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number of rows that are considered=
                    to be dense. For large-scale problems, the
                    direct application of a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">QR solver will fail because of a l=
                    ack of memory or will be unacceptably slow. We
                    study a number of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches for solving such proble=
                    ms using a sparse QR solver without modication.
                    We consider the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">case where the sparse part of A is=
                    rank-decient and show that either
                    preprocessing A using partial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix stretching or using regular=
                    ization and employing a direct-iterative
                    approach can be seamlessly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">combined with a black-box QR solve=
                    r. Furthermore, we propose extending the
                    augmented system
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">formulation with iterative renemen=
                    t for sparse problems to sparse-dense problems
                    and demonstrate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experimentally that multi-precisio=
                    n variants can be successfully used.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Scott2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-377" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Scott, Jennifer and
            Miroslav T=C5=AFma. =E2=80=9CA Null-Space Approach for Symmetri=
            c Saddle Point Systems With a Non
            Zero (2,2) Block=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">SIAM Jou=
                rnal on Scientific Computing </span>(2020). eprint:
            RAL-P-2020-003.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://www2.karlin.mff.cuni.cz/~mirektuma/ps/RAL-P-2020=-003.pdf"
                        class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://www2.karlin.mff.cun=
                            i.cz/~mirektuma/ps/RAL-P-2020-003.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Null-space methods have long been
                used to solve large-scale symmetric saddle point systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of equations in which the </span>
                <= !-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CH=TML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"546" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; po=sition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi= class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo= class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                        </mjx-c=>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" dis=play=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                                <mi>k</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                <mi>k</mi>
                                </ma= th>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"547" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathC=lass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                        <mn>2
                </= mn>
                </mrow>
                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                </mrow></math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">block is zero. This paper focuses on the =
                    case where the </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMen=u_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"548" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                    <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c32">
                                    </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx=-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        </= mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xm=lns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mrow>
                                    <mo cla=ss=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mn>2</mn>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,<= /mo>
                                                <mn>2</mn>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </math></mj= x-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">block is non zero. A novel null-space app=
                    roach is proposed to transform the saddle point
                    system into
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">another symmetric saddle point sys=
                    tem of the same order but with a zero </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"549" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c32"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c2C"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-c cl=ass=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <m= jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http=://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathC=lass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                    <mn>2</= mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">block of order at most </span>
                <!-- l. 30 =
-->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabin=dex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"550" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: re=lative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"=mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>2</mn>
                            <mi>k</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Success of any null-space approach=
                    is dependent on the construction of a suitable
                    null-space basis. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">not uncommon case of the off-diago=
                    nal block being a wide matrix that has far
                    fewer rows than columns
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and that may be dense is considere=
                    d. A number of approaches are explored with the
                    aim of balancing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stability of the transformed syste=
                    m with sparsity. Linear least squares problems
                    that contain a small
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">number of dense rows arising from =
                    practical applications are used to illustrate
                    our ideas and to explore
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">their potential for solving large-=
                    scale systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Selvitopi2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-378" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Selvitopi, Oguz, Saliya Ekanayake, et al. =
            =E2=80=9CDistributed Many-to-Many Protein Sequence
            Alignment using Sparse Matrices=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arX=
            iv: 2009.14467 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Identifying similar protein
                sequences is a core step in many computational biology pipel=
                ines
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such as detection of homologous pr=
                    otein sequences, generation of similarity
                    protein graphs for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">downstream analysis, functional an=
                    notation and gene location. Performance and
                    scalability of protein
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">similarity searches have proven to=
                    be a bottleneck in many bioinformatics
                    pipelines due to increases in

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cheap and abundant sequencing data=
                    . This work presents a new distributed-memory
                    software, PASTIS.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PASTIS relies on sparse matrix com=
                    putations for efficient identification of
                    possibly similar proteins.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We use distributed sparse matrices=
                    for scalability and show that the sparse
                    matrix infrastructure is a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">great fit for protein similarity s=
                    earches when coupled with a fully-distributed
                    dictionary of sequences
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that allows remote sequence reques=
                    ts to be fulfilled. Our algorithm incorporates
                    the unique bias in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">amino acid sequence substitution i=
                    n searches without altering the basic sparse
                    matrix model, and in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">turn, achieves ideal scaling up to=
                    millions of protein sequences.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Selvitopi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-379" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Selvitopi, Oguz, Md Taufique Hussain, et al.=
            =E2=80=9COptimizing High Performance Markov Clustering
            for Pre-Exascale Architectures=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmt=i-10">34th IEEE International Parallel and
                Distributed
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Processing Symposium (IPDPS), 20=
                20 </span>(Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.10083 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</s= pan>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">HipMCL is a high-performance
                distributed memory implementation of the popular Markov
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Cluster Algorithm (MCL) and can cl=
                    uster large-scale networks within hours using a
                    few thousand
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">CPU-equipped nodes. It relies on s=
                    parse matrix computations and heavily makes use
                    of the sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix-sparse matrix multiplicatio=
                    n kernel (SpGEMM). The existing parallel
                    algorithms in HipMCL
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are not scalable to Exascale archi=
                    tectures, both due to their communication costs
                    dominating the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">runtime at large concurrencies and=
                    also due to their inability to take advantage
                    of accelerators that are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">increasingly popular. In this work=
                    , we systematically remove scalability and
                    performance bottlenecks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of HipMCL. We enable GPUs by perfo=
                    rming the expensive expansion phase of the MCL
                    algorithm on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU. We propose a CPU-GPU joint di=
                    stributed SpGEMM algorithm called pipelined
                    Sparse SUMMA
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and integrate a probabilistic memo=
                    ry requirement estimator that is fast and
                    accurate. We develop a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">new merging algorithm for the incr=
                    emental processing of partial results produced
                    by the GPUs, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improves the overlap efficiency an=
                    d the peak memory usage. We also integrate a
                    recent and faster
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm for performing SpGEMM on=
                    CPUs. We validate our new algorithms and
                    optimizations with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extensive evaluations. With the en=
                    abling of the GPUs and integration of new
                    algorithms, HipMCL is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">up to 12.4x faster, being able to =
                    cluster a network with 70 million proteins and
                    68 billion connections
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">just under 15 minutes using 1024 n=
                    odes of ORNL=E2=80=99s Summit supercomputer.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Serafino2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-380" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Serafino, Daniela di, Gerardo Toraldo, and M=
            arco Viola. =E2=80=9CUsing gradient directions to get
            global convergence of Newton-type methods=E2=80=9D. In: (Apr. 2=
            020). arXiv: 2004.00968v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The renewed interest in Steepest
                Descent (SD) methods following the work of Barzilai and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Borwein [IMA Journal of Numerical =
                    Analysis, 8 (1988)] has driven us to consider a
                    globalization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategy based on SD, which is app=
                    licable to any line-search method. In
                    particular, we combine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Newton-type directions with scaled=
                    SD steps to have suitable descent directions.
                    Scaling the SD
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">directions with a suitable step le=
                    ngth makes a significant difference with
                    respect to similar globalization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approaches, in terms of both theor=
                    etical features and computational behavior. We
                    apply our strategy to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Newton=E2=80=99s method and the BF=
                    GS method, with computational results that appear
                    interesting compared
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with the results of well-establish=
                    ed globalization strategies devised ad hoc for
                    those methods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Sewall2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-381" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Sewall, Jason et al. =E2=80=9CInterpreting a=
            nd Visualizing Performance Portability Metrics=E2=80=9D. In:
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 IEEE/ACM Intern=
                ational Workshop on Performance, Portability
                and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Productivity in HPC</span>. P3HP=
            C =E2=80=9920 (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Recent work has introduced a
                number of tools and techniques for reasoning about the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">interplay between application perf=
                    ormance and portability, or =E2=80=9Cperformance
                    portability=E2=80=9D. These tools
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have proven useful for setting goa=
                    ls and guiding highlevel discussions, but our
                    understanding of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the performance portability proble=
                    m remains incomplete. Different views of the
                    same performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiency data offer different in=
                    sights into an application=E2=80=99s performance
                    portability (or lack thereof):
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">standard statistical measures such=
                    as the mean and standard deviation require
                    careful interpretation,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and even metrics designed specific=
                    ally to measure performance portability may
                    obscure differences
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">between applications.</span><br cl=ass=3D"newline"><span
                    class=3D"cmr-9">This paper
                    offers a critical assessment of existing approaches for summ=
                    arizing performance efficiency

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data across different platforms, a=
                    nd proposes visualization as a means to extract
                    useful information
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">about the underlying distribution.=
                    We explore a number of alternative
                    visualizations, outlining a new
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methodology that enables developer=
                    s to reason about the performance portability
                    of their applications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and how it might be improved. This=
                    study unpicks what it might mean to be
                    =E2=80=9Cperformance portable=E2=80=9D
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and provides useful tools to explo=
                    re that question.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Shi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-382" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Shi, Ziqiang. =E2=80=9CSingCubic: Cyclic Inc=
            remental Newton-type Gradient Descent with Cubic
            Regularization for Non-Convex Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. =
            2020). arXiv: 2002.06848 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this work, we generalized and
                unified two recent completely different works of [] and []
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">respectively into one by proposing=
                    the cyclic incremental Newton-type gradient
                    descent with cubic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regularization (SingCubic) method =
                    for optimizing non-convex functions. Through
                    the iterations of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SingCubic, a cubic regularized glo=
                    bal quadratic approximation using Hessian
                    information is kept
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and solved. Preliminary numerical =
                    experiments show the encouraging performance of
                    the SingCubic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm when compared to basic i=
                    ncremental or stochastic Newton-type
                    implementations. The results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and technique can be served as an =
                    initiate for the research on the incremental
                    Newton-type gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">descent methods that employ cubic =
                    regularization. The methods and principles
                    proposed in this paper
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be used to do logistic regress=
                    ion, autoencoder training, independent
                    components analysis, Ising
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model/Hopfield network training, m=
                    ultilayer perceptron, deep convolutional
                    network training and so
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on. We will open-source parts of o=
                    ur implementations soon.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Simonetto2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-383" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Simonetto, Andrea et al. =E2=80=9CTime-Varyi=
            ng Convex Optimization: Time-Structured Algorithms
            and Applications=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.08500v1=
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Optimization underpins many of
                the challenges that science and technology face on a daily
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">basis. Recent years have witnessed=
                    a major shift from traditional optimization
                    paradigms grounded on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">batch algorithms for medium-scale =
                    problems to challenging dynamic, time-varying,
                    and even huge-size
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">settings. This is driven by techno=
                    logical transformations that converted
                    infrastructural and social
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">platforms into complex and dynamic=
                    networked systems with even pervasive sensing
                    and computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">capabilities. The present paper re=
                    views a broad class of state-of-the-art
                    algorithms for time-varying
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization, with an eye to both =
                    algorithmic development and performance
                    analysis. It offers a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">comprehensive overview of availabl=
                    e tools and methods, and unveils open
                    challenges in application
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">domains of broad interest. The rea=
                    l-world examples presented include smart power
                    systems, robotics,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">machine learning, and data analyti=
                    cs, highlighting domain-specific issues and
                    solutions. The ultimate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">goal is to exempify wide engineeri=
                    ng relevance of analytical tools and pertinent
                    theoretical foundations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Slota2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-384" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Slota, George M. et al. =E2=80=9CScalable, M=
            ulti-Constraint, Complex-Objective Graph Partitioning=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE Transactions on Parallel and D=
                istributed Systems </span>31.12 (Dec. 2020),
            pp. 2789=E2=80=932801.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1109/tpds.2020.3002150.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce XtraPuLP , a
                distributed-memory graph partitioner designed to process
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">irregular trillion-edge graphs. Xt=
                    raPuLP is based on the scalable label
                    propagation community
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detection technique, which has bee=
                    n demonstrated in various prior works as a
                    viable means to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">produce high quality partitions of=
                    skewed and small-world graphs with minimal
                    computation time. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">XtraPuLP implementation can also b=
                    e generalized to compute partitions with an
                    arbitrary number
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of constraints, and it can compute=
                    partitions with balanced communication load
                    across all parts. On
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a collection of large sparse graph=
                    s, we show that XtraPuLP partitioning is
                    considerably faster than
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art partitioning meth=
                    ods, while also demonstrating that XtraPuLP can
                    produce partitions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of real-world graphs with billion+=
                    vertices and over a hundred billion edges in
                    minutes. Additionally,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we demonstrate XtraPuLP on a varie=
                    ty of applications, including large-scale graph
                    analytics and sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix-vector multiplication.</spa= n>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Sofranac2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-385" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Sofranac, Boro, Ambros Gleixner, and Sebasti=
            an Pokutta. =E2=80=9CAccelerating Domain Propagation:
            an Efficient GPU-Parallel Algorithm over Sparse Matrices=E2=80=
            =9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv: 2009.07785
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Fast domain propagation of linear
                constraints has become a crucial component of today=E2=80=99=
                s
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">best algorithms and solvers for mi=
                    xed integer programming and pseudo-boolean
                    optimization to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">achieve peak solving performance. =
                    Irregularities in the form of dynamic
                    algorithmic behaviour,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dependency structures, and sparsit=
                    y patterns in the input data make efficient
                    implementations of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">domain propagation on GPUs and, mo=
                    re generally, on parallel architectures
                    challenging. This is one
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the main reasons why domain pro=
                    pagation in state-of-the-art solvers is single
                    thread only. In this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paper, we present a new algorithm =
                    for domain propagation which (a) avoids these
                    problems and allows
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for an efficient implementation on=
                    GPUs, and is (b) capable of running
                    propagation rounds entirely
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the GPU, without any need for s=
                    ynchronization or communication with the CPU.
                    We present
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extensive computational results wh=
                    ich demonstrate the effectiveness of our
                    approach and show that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ample speedups are possible on pra=
                    ctically relevant problems: on state-of-the-art
                    GPUs, our geometric
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mean speed-up for reasonably-large=
                    instances is around 10x to 20x and can be as
                    high as 195x on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">favorably-large instances.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Solomonik2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-386" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Solomonik, Edgar and James Demmel. =E2=80=9C=
            Fast Bilinear Algorithms for Symmetric Tensor
            Contractions=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Computationa=
                l Methods in Applied Mathematics </span>(Feb. 2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1515/cmam-2019-0075.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In matrix-vector multiplication,
                matrix symmetry does not permit a straightforward reduction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in computational cost. More genera=
                    lly, in contractions of symmetric tensors, the
                    symmetries are not
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preserved in the usual algebraic f=
                    orm of contraction algorithms. We introduce an
                    algorithm that reduces
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the bilinear complexity (number of=
                    computed elementwise products) for most types
                    of symmetric tensor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">contractions. In particular, it lo=
                    wers the bilinear complexity of symmetrized
                    contractions of symmetric
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensors of order </span>
                <!-- l. 30=
 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabi=ndex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"551" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: r=elative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D="mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D="mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo=>
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D463 TEX-I">
                                    </mj= x-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline="><mi>s</mi><mo class=3D"
                            MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                            <mi>v</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assist= ive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">and </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container c=lass=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_=counter=3D"552" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math=class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D463 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin=" space=3D" 3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mj=x-i" space=3D"3">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-m= ath>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns==3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>v</mi>
                                <mo cl=ass=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                <mi>t</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-cont= ainer>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">by a factor of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-= container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                            tabindex=3D"0=" ctxtmenu_counter=3D" 553" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;"=>
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mfrac>
                                    <mjx-frac>
                                        <mjx= -num>
                                            <mjx-nstrut></mjx-nstrut>
                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                <mjx-mrow>
                                                    <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                    <mjx-mr= ow>
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D460 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-= mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                            <= /mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <= /mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B">
                                                </mj= x-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D463 TEX-I">
                                                </mj= x-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=punc">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c21"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        </mjx-mrow>
                                        </mjx-num>
                                        <mjx-d= box>
                                            <mjx-dtable>
                                                <mjx-line></mjx-line>
                                                <mjx-row>
                                                    <mjx-den>
                                                        <mjx-dstrut>
                                                            </mjx-ds= trut>
                                                            <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
                                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D46=0 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                                                    <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c21"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D461 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                                                    <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c21">
                                                                        </mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c1D463 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mi>
                                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-pun=c">
                                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c21"></mjx-c>
                                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                            </mjx-mrow>
                                                    </mjx-den>
                                                    </mjx-row=>
                                            </mjx-dtable>
                                            </mjx-dbox>
                                            </mjx-frac>
                                            </mjx-mfrac>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mfrac>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathCl=ass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mi>s</mi>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                            <mi>t</mi=>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                                <mi>v</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-=close">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">!</mo>
                                </mrow>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>s</= mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">!</mo>
                                        <mi>t</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc=">!</mo><mi>v</mi><mo class=3D" MathClass-punc">!
                                        </mo>
                                </mrow>
                            </mfrac>
                        </math>=
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">to leading order. The algorithm computes =
                    a symmetric tensor of bilinear products, then
                    subtracts
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unwanted parts of its partial sums=
                    . Special cases of this algorithm provide
                    improvements to the bilinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complexity of the multiplication o=
                    f a symmetric matrix and a vector, the
                    symmetrized vector outer
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">product, and the symmetrized produ=
                    ct of symmetric matrices. While the algorithm
                    requires more
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">additions for each elementwise pro=
                    duct, the total number of operations is in some
                    cases less than
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">classical algorithms, for tensors =
                    of any size. We provide a round-off error
                    analysis of the algorithm and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate that the error is not =
                    too large in practice. Finally, we provide an
                    optimized implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for one variant of the symmetry-pr=
                    eserving algorithm, which achieves speedups of
                    up to 4.58</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJ=ax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"55=4" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-=TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">for a particular tensor contraction, rela=
                    tive to a classical approach that casts the
                    problem as a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix-matrix multiplication.
            </spa= n>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-SoltanMohammadi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-387" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Soltan Mohammadi, Mahdi. =E2=80=9CAutomatic =
            Sparse Computation Parallelization By Utilizing
            Domain-Specific Knowledge In Data Dependence Analysis=E2=80=9D.=
            PhD thesis. Department of
            Computer Science, The University of Arizona, 2020. eprint: 2814=
            9621 (ProQuest).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Sparse vectors, matrices, and
                tensors are commonly used to compress nonzero values of big
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data manipulated in data analytics=
                    , scientific simulations, and machine learning
                    computations. As
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with general computations, paralle=
                    lization of loops in sparse computations, codes
                    manipulating sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">structures, is essential to effici=
                    ently utilize available parallel architectures.
                    The sparse computations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">often exhibit partial parallelism =
                    in loops that are sequential in the
                    corresponding dense computation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">due to sparsity of data dependenci=
                    es coming from indirect memory access through
                    index arrays, e.g.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">col in val[col[j]]. Such dependenc=
                    ies can only be discovered at runtime when
                    content of index arrays
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are available. Consequently, perfo=
                    rmance programmers typically use the
                    inspector/executor strategy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to take advantage of partial paral=
                    lelism in sparse computation. There,
                    programmers implement an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inspector code that creates iterat=
                    ion dependency graph at runtime from which
                    wavefronts of iterations

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are extracted and fed into a paral=
                    lel version of the computation called an
                    executor. The executor
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">executes iteration waves sequentia=
                    lly to respect sparse dependencies while
                    executing iterations inside
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">each wavefront in parallel.</span>=
                <br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-9">To
                    automate the generation of the inspector and executor code, =
                    compiler-based loop-carried data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">dependency analysis is needed. How=
                    ever, straightforward automatically generated
                    inspectors typically
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">have significantly higher overhead=
                    than hand written optimized ones.
                    Consequently, the specific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problem that I am addressing in th=
                    is dissertation is how can we automate the
                    strategies used by expert
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programmers to generate efficient =
                    runtime inspectors for parallelizing sparse
                    computation.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr-=9">The overarching contribution of this
                    dissertation is an approach for encoding index array propert=
                    ies for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">individual index arrays and relati=
                    onships between index arrays as universally
                    quantified constraints and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using them in compiler-based data =
                    dependence analysis. The dependence analysis is
                    then evaluated in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the context of finding wavefront p=
                    arallelism in sparse computations. More
                    specifically, one contribution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is an approach to automatically us=
                    e index array properties to prove more data
                    dependencies
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unsatisfiable, removing the need f=
                    or inspecting them at runtime. Other
                    contributions are methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to use the same properties to simp=
                    lify compiletime-satisfiable dependences by
                    finding equalities and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subset relationships enabling gene=
                    ration of faster runtime inspectors. The last
                    contribution includes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compile-time methods for expanding=
                    opportunities for array privatization in
                    sparse computations by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">defining an array as private if it=
                    s contents start and end each iteration with
                    the same value. Evaluation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results show my approach is able t=
                    o find seven fully parallel loops in seven
                    sparse computations where
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">previous compiler-based approach c=
                    ould not, and efficiently extract partial
                    parallelism from outer most
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">loops of five out of six sparse co=
                    mputations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Soltaniyeh2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-388" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Soltaniyeh, Mohammadreza, Richard P. Martin,=
            and Santosh Nagarakatte. =E2=80=9CSynergistic
            CPU-FPGA Acceleration of Sparse Linear Algebra=E2=80=9D. In: (A=
            pr. 2020). arXiv: 2004.13907v1
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper describes REAP, a
                software-hardware approach that enables high performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse linear algebra computations=
                    on a cooperative CPU-FPGA platform. REAP
                    carefully separates
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the task of organizing the matrix =
                    elements from the computation phase. It uses
                    the CPU to provide
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a first-pass re-organization of th=
                    e matrix elements, allowing the FPGA to focus
                    on the computation.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce a new intermediate re=
                    presentation that allows the CPU to communicate
                    the sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data and the scheduling decisions =
                    to the FPGA. The computation is optimized on
                    the FPGA for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effective resource utilization wit=
                    h pipelining. REAP improves the performance of
                    Sparse General
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Matrix Multiplication (SpGEMM) and=
                    Sparse Cholesky Factorization by 3.2X and
                    1.85X compared
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to widely used sparse libraries fo=
                    r them on the CPU, respectively.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Song2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-389" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Song, Yang et al. =E2=80=9CNonlinear Equatio=
            n Solving: A Faster Alternative to Feedforward
            Computation=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.03629 <span=class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Feedforward computations, such as
                evaluating a neural network or sampling from an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">autoregressive model, are ubiquito=
                    us in machine learning. The sequential nature
                    of feedforward
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computation, however, requires a s=
                    trict order of execution and cannot be easily
                    accelerated with parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing. To enable parrallelizat=
                    ion, we frame the task of feedforward
                    computation as solving a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">system of nonlinear equations. We =
                    then propose to find the solution using a
                    Jacobi or Gauss-Seidel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fixed-point iteration method, as w=
                    ell as hybrid methods of both. Crucially,
                    Jacobi updates operate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">independently on each equation and=
                    can be executed in parallel. Our method is
                    guaranteed to give
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exactly the same values as the ori=
                    ginal feedforward computation with a reduced
                    (or equal) number of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel iterations. Experimentall=
                    y, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our
                    approach in accelerating
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">1) the evaluation of DenseNets on =
                    ImageNet and 2) autoregressive sampling of MADE
                    and PixelCNN.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We are able to achieve between 1.2=
                    and 33 speedup factors under various
                    conditions and computation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">models.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Spiteri2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-390" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Spiteri, Pierre. =E2=80=9CParallel asynchron=
            ous algorithms: A survey=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Advances in =
                Engineering
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Software </span>149 (Nov. 2020),=
            p. 102896. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1016/j.advengsoft.2020.102896.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">This paper deals with a synthetic
                presentation of parallel iterative asynchronous algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and their extensions for the solut=
                    ion of large sparse linear or pseudo-linear
                    algebraic systems eventually
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">constrained. The behavior of these=
                    iterative parallel asynchronous algorithms is
                    studied by three
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distinct methods : contraction pro=
                    perty, partial ordering property linked to the
                    discrete maximum
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">principle and nested sets; the lin=
                    k between these three kinds of analysis is
                    presented. Stopping tests
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the iterations are presented bo=
                    th from computer science and from numerical
                    analysis approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">including in this last case approx=
                    imate contraction property, partial ordering
                    property linked to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the discrete maximum principle and=
                    nested sets. The principle of implementation
                    of these parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">asynchronous iterative methods is =
                    described for subdomain method without
                    overlapping and for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subdomain method with overlapping;=
                    the use of load balancing approach for
                    asynchronous parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms is also discussed. Vari=
                    ous applications modelled by linear equations
                    or pseudo linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations and solved by such paral=
                    lel algorithms are presented as well as the
                    uses of these methods in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computer security and Boolean calc=
                    ulation. The efficiency of parallel iterative
                    asynchronous algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is also discussed.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Squires2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-391" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Squires, Chandler, Joshua Amaniampong, and C=
            aroline Uhler. =E2=80=9CEfficient Permutation
            Discovery in Causal DAGs=E2=80=9D. In: (Nov. 2020). arXiv: 2011=
            .03610 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ME]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The problem of learning a
                directed acyclic graph (DAG) up to Markov equivalence is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equivalent to the problem of findi=
                    ng a permutation of the variables that induces
                    the sparsest graph.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Without additional assumptions, th=
                    is task is known to be NP-hard. Building on the
                    minimum degree
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm for sparse Cholesky deco=
                    mposition, but utilizing DAG-specific problem
                    structure, we introduce
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an efficient algorithm for finding=
                    such sparse permutations. We show that on
                    jointly Gaussian distributions,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our method with depth </span>
                <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"555" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi cla=ss=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D464 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>=
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"ht=tp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>w</mi>
                        </math></mjx= -assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">runs in </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-contain= er class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtm=enu_counter=3D"556" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-m= ath class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx= -mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D464 TEX-I">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mn cl=ass=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-m= row>
                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29">
                            </mjx-= c>
                    </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" dis=play=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi ma=thvariant=3D"italic">pw</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                                    <mn>3</mn>
                                    </m= row>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    <= /mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">time. We compare our method with </span>
                        <= !-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CH=TML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"557" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; po=sition: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi= class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D464 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                        <mjx-mo= class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D">
                                                </mjx-c=>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31">
                                                        </mj= x-c>
                                                </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"i=nline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                        display=3D"inline="><mi>w</mi><mo class=3D" MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                                        <mn>1</mn>
                                    </math></mjx-assi= stive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">to algorithms for finding sparse eliminat=
                                ion orderings of undirected graphs, and show
                                that taking
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">advantage of DAG-specific problem =
                                structure leads to a significant improvement in
                                the discovered
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">permutation. We also compare our a=
                                lgorithm to provably consistent causal
                                structure learning algorithms,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such as the PC algorithm, GES, and=
                                GSP, and show that our method achieves
                                comparable performance
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with a shorter runtime. Thus, our =
                                method can be used on its own for causal
                                structure discovery. Finally,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we show that there exist dense gra=
                                phs on which our method achieves almost perfect
                                performance, so
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that unlike most existing causal s=
                                tructure learning algorithms, the situations in
                                which our algorithm
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">achieves both good performance and=
                                good runtime are not limited to sparse
                                graphs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Srinivasa2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-392" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Srinivasa, Rakshith S, Mark A Davenport, and=
            Justin Romberg. =E2=80=9CLocalized sketching for
            matrix
            multiplication and ridge regression=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). =
            arXiv: 2003.09097v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ML]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We consider sketched approximate
                matrix multiplication and ridge regression in the novel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">setting of localized sketching, wh=
                    ere at any given point, only part of the data
                    matrix is available.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">This corresponds to a block diagon=
                    al structure on the sketching matrix. We show
                    that, under mild
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">conditions, block diagonal sketchi=
                    ng matrices require only </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"558" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D442 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </m= jx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-sop MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c= class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S1">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mfrac>
                                        <mjx-frac>
                                            <= mjx-num>
                                                <mjx-nstrut></mjx-nstrut>
                                                <mjx-mrow size=3D"s">
