                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"591" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45A TEX-I">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c32">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unsele=ctable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math=/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">mm</mi>
                            <mn>2</mn>
                            </m= ath>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">chip area under 45nm process technology a=
                    nd can run at 800MHz with on average 759.8mW
                    power
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consumption. ARENA also supports t=
                    he concurrent execution of multi-applications,
                    offering ideal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">architectural support for future h=
                    igh-performance parallel computing and data
                    analytics systems.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tan2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-401" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tan, Conghui et al. =E2=80=9CAccelerated Dua=
            l-Averaging Primal-Dual Method for Composite
            Convex Minimization=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Optim=
                ization Methods and Software 2020 </span>(Jan. 2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1080/10556788.2020.1713779. arXiv: arXiv:2001.05537v1 <span=class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Dual averaging-type methods are
                widely used in industrial machine learning applications
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">due to their ability to promoting =
                    solution structure (e.g., sparsity)
                    efficiently. In this paper, we propose
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a novel accelerated dual-averaging=
                    primal-dual algorithm for minimizing a
                    composite convex function.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We also derive a stochastic versio=
                    n of the proposed method which solves empirical
                    risk minimization,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and its advantages on handling spa=
                    rse data are demonstrated both theoretically
                    and empirically.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tanaka2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-402" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tanaka, Yuichi et al. =E2=80=9CSampling on G=
            raphs: From Theory to Applications=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020).
            arXiv: 2003.03957 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[eess.SP]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The study of sampling signals on
                graphs, with the goal of building an analog of sampling
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for standard signals in the time a=
                    nd spatial domains, has attracted considerable
                    attention recently.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Beyond adding to the growing theor=
                    y on graph signal processing (GSP), sampling on
                    graphs has various
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">promising applications. In this ar=
                    ticle, we review current progress on sampling
                    over graphs focusing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on theory and potential applicatio=
                    ns. Most methodologies used in graph signal
                    sampling are designed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to parallel those used in sampling=
                    for standard signals, however, sampling theory
                    for graph signals
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significantly differs from that fo=
                    r Shannon=E2=80=93Nyquist and shift invariant signals.
                    This is due in part to the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fact that the definitions of sever=
                    al important properties, such as shift
                    invariance and bandlimitedness,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are different in GSP systems. Thro=
                    ughout, we discuss similarities and differences
                    between standard
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and graph sampling and highlight o=
                    pen problems and challenges.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tandon2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-403" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tandon, Suyash et al. =E2=80=9CEnabling powe=
            r-performance balance with transprecision calculations
            for extreme-scale computations of turbulent flows=E2=80=9D. In:=
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020 AIAA
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Aviation Forum</span>. American =
            Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics, June
            2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</= span></span>:
                10.2514/6.2020-2922.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In modern scientific computing,
                the execution of floating-point operations emerges as a majo=
                r
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">contributor to the energy consumpt=
                    ion of a compute-intensive application with a
                    large dynamic range.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Experimental evidence shows that o=
                    ver 50% of the energy consumed by a core and
                    its data memory is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">related to floating-point computat=
                    ions. The adoption of floating-point formats
                    requiring lesser number
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of bits is an interesting opportun=
                    ity to reduce the energy consumption as it
                    allows simplification of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the arithmetic circuitry and reduc=
                    es the memory bandwidth required to transfer
                    the data between
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory and registers. In theory, t=
                    he adoption of multiple floating-point types
                    following the principle
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of transprecision computing allows=
                    fine-grained control of floating-point
                    arithmetic while meeting the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">desired standards on the accuracy =
                    of the final result. In this paper, the
                    power-performance trade-offs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for computing at different precisi=
                    on levels are analyzed for a parallel and
                    distributed framework based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on recovery-assisted discontinuous=
                    Galerkin (RADG) methods. The recovery operator
                    of the RADG,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operates on compact support from n=
                    eighboring elements and allows high-order
                    approximation of the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution, with potential for massi=
                    ve parallelism. Using PoLiMEr =E2=80=93 a power
                    monitoring and management
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tool for HPC applications =E2=80=
                    =93 fine-grained insights into the power characteristics
                    of the RADG code
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the supercomputer Theta at Argo=
                    nne National Laboratory are presented. 3D
                    benchmark tests
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">indicate a savings of approximatel=
                    y 5 W per node with single precision computing.
                    A mixed precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approach where all computations ex=
                    cept recovery operation is performed in single
                    precision shows
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">promising results, however, an aut=
                    omated approach for tuning floating-point types
                    and analyzing the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating-point sensitivity of vari=
                    ables and operations is desirable.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-404" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tang, Wentao and Prodromos Daoutidis. =E2=80=
            =9CFast and Stable Nonconvex Constrained Distributed
            Optimization: The ELLADA Algorithm=E2=80=9D. In: (Apr. 2020). a=
            rXiv: 2004.01977v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[eess.SY]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Distributed optimization, where
                the computations are performed in a localized and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">coordinated manner using multiple =
                    agents, is a promising approach for solving
                    large-scale optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems, e.g., those arising in m=
                    odel predictive control (MPC) of large-scale
                    plants. However, a

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distributed optimization algorithm=
                    that is computationally efficient, globally
                    convergent, amenable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to nonconvex constraints and gener=
                    al inter-subsystem interactions remains an open
                    problem. In this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paper, we combine three important =
                    modifications to the classical alternating
                    direction method of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multipliers (ADMM) for distributed=
                    optimization. Specifically, (i) an extra-layer
                    architecture is adopted
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to accommodate nonconvexity and ha=
                    ndle inequality constraints, (ii)
                    equality-constrained nonlinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">programming (NLP) problems are all=
                    owed to be solved approximately, and (iii) a
                    modified Anderson
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">acceleration is employed for reduc=
                    ing the number of iterations. Theoretical
                    convergence towards
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stationary solutions and computati=
                    onal complexity of the proposed algorithm,
                    named ELLADA, is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">established. Its application to di=
                    stributed nonlinear MPC is also described and
                    illustrated through a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benchmark process system.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tao2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-405" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tao, Wei et al. =E2=80=9CThe Strength of Nes=
            terov=E2=80=99s Extrapolation in the Individual Convergence of
            Nonsmooth Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">IE=
                EE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning
                Systems</span>
            (2020).
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The extrapolation strategy raised
                by Nesterov, which can accelerate the convergence rate of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient descent methods by orders=
                    of magnitude when dealing with smooth convex
                    objective, has led
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to tremendous success in training =
                    machine learning tasks. In this article, the
                    convergence of individual
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iterates of projected subgradient =
                    (PSG) methods for nonsmooth convex optimization
                    problems is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">theoretically studied based on Nes=
                    terov=E2=80=99s extrapolation, which we name individual
                    convergence. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prove that Nesterov=E2=80=99s extr=
                    apolation has the strength to make the individual
                    convergence of PSG
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimal for nonsmooth problems. In=
                    light of this consideration, a direct
                    modification of the subgradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluation suffices to achieve opt=
                    imal individual convergence for strongly convex
                    problems, which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be regarded as making an inter=
                    esting step toward the open question about
                    stochastic gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">descent (SGD) posed by Shamir. Fur=
                    thermore, we give an extension of the derived
                    algorithms to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solve regularized learning tasks w=
                    ith nonsmooth losses in stochastic settings.
                    Compared with other
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-theart nonsmooth methods,=
                    the derived algorithms can serve as an
                    alternative to the basic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SGD especially in coping with mach=
                    ine learning problems, where an individual
                    output is needed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to guarantee the regularization st=
                    ructure while keeping an optimal rate of
                    convergence. Typically,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our method is applicable as an eff=
                    icient tool for solving large-scale
                    l1-regularized hinge-loss learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems. Several comparison exper=
                    iments demonstrate that our individual output
                    not only achieves
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an optimal convergence rate but al=
                    so guarantees better sparsity than the averaged
                    solution.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Thayer2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-406" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Thayer, Samuel et al. =E2=80=9CArcherGear=E2=
            =80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 25th ACM
                SIGPLAN Symposium
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">on Principles and Practice of Pa=
                rallel Programming</span>. PPoPP =E2=80=9920. ACM, Feb.
            2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</= span></span>:
                10.1145/3332466.3374504.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">There is growing uptake of shared
                memory parallelism in high performance computing,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and this has increased the need fo=
                    r data race checking during the creation of new
                    parallel codes or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelizing existing sequential =
                    codes. While race checking concepts and
                    implementations have been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">around for many concurrency models=
                    , including tasking models such as Cilk and
                    PThreads (e.g., the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Thread Sanitizer tool), practicall=
                    y usable race checkers for other APIs such as
                    OpenMP have been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lagging. For example, the OpenMP p=
                    arallelization of an important library (namely
                    Hypre) was initially
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">unsuccessful due to inexplicable n=
                    ondeterminism introduced when the code was
                    optimized, and later
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">root-caused to a race by the then =
                    recently developed OpenMP race checker Archer
                    [2]. The open-source
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Archer now enjoys significant trac=
                    tion within several organizations.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Thebelt2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-407" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Thebelt, Alexander et al. =E2=80=9CENTMOOT: =
            A Framework for Optimization over Ensemble Tree
            Models=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.04774 <span class==3D"cmtt-10">[stat.ML]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Gradient boosted trees and other
                regression tree models perform well in a wide range
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of real-world, industrial applicat=
                    ions. These tree models (i) offer insight into
                    important prediction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">features, (ii) effectively manage =
                    sparse data, and (iii) have excellent
                    prediction capabilities. Despite
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">their advantages, they are general=
                    ly unpopular for decision-making tasks and
                    black-box optimization,

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which is due to their difficult-to=
                    -optimize structure and the lack of a reliable
                    uncertainty measure.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ENTMOOT is our new framework for i=
                    ntegrating (already trained) tree models into
                    larger optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems. The contributions of ENT=
                    MOOT include: (i) explicitly introducing a
                    reliable uncertainty
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">measure that is compatible with tr=
                    ee models, (ii) solving the larger optimization
                    problems that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">incorporate these uncertainty awar=
                    e tree models, (iii) proving that the solutions
                    are globally optimal,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">i.e. no better solution exists. In=
                    particular, we show how the ENTMOOT approach
                    allows a simple
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integration of tree models into de=
                    cision-making and black-box optimization, where
                    it proves as a strong
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">competitor to commonly-used framew=
                    orks.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tian2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-408" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tian, Zhaolu et al. =E2=80=9CSeveral relaxed=
            iteration methods for computing PageRank=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti=-10">Journal
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">of Computational and Applied Mat=
                hematics </span>(Nov. 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-=caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.cam.2020.113295.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, based on the
                iteration framework (Tian et al., 2019) and relaxed two-step
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">splitting (RTSS) iteration method =
                    (Xie and Ma, 2018), we present two relaxed
                    iteration methods for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solving the PageRank problem, whic=
                    h are the relaxed generalized inner-outer
                    (RGIO) and relaxed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generalized two-step splitting (RG=
                    TSS) iteration methods, respectively. Next,
                    their overall convergence
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">properties are analyzed in detail,=
                    and choices of the parameters in these
                    algorithms are also discussed.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Finally, several numerical example=
                    s are given to illustrate the effectiveness of
                    the proposed algorithms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Titolo2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-409" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Titolo, Laura, Mariano Moscato, Marco A. Fel=
            iu, et al. =E2=80=9CAutomatic Generation of
            Guard-Stable
            Floating-Point Code=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Lectu=
                re Notes in Computer Science</span>. Springer
            International
            Publishing, 2020, pp. 141=E2=80=93159. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"=><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1007/978-3-030-63461-2=CB=998.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In floating-point programs, guard
                instability occurs when the control flow of a conditional
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">statement diverges from its ideal =
                    execution under real arithmetic. This
                    phenomenon is caused by the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">presence of round-off errors in fl=
                    oating-point computations. Writing programs
                    that correctly handle
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">guard instability often requires e=
                    xpertise on finite precision arithmetic. This
                    paper presents a fully
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automatic toolchain that generates=
                    and formally verifies a guard-stable
                    floating-point C program from
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its functional specification in re=
                    al arithmetic. The generated program is
                    instrumented to soundly detect
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">when unstable guards may occur and=
                    , in these cases, to issue a warning. The
                    proposed approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">combines the PRECiSA floating-poin=
                    t static analyzer, the Frama-C software
                    verification suite, and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">PVS theorem prover.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Titolo2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-410" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Titolo, Laura, Mariano Moscato, and Cesar A.=
            Mu=C3=B1oz. =E2=80=9CAutomatic generation and verification
            of test-stable floating-point code=E2=80=9D. In: (Jan. 2020). a=
            rXiv: 2001.02981 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.PL]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Test instability in a
                floating-point program occurs when the control flow of the p=
                rogram
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">diverges from its ideal execution =
                    assuming real arithmetic. This phenomenon is
                    caused by the presence
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of round-off errors that affect th=
                    e evaluation of arithmetic expressions
                    occurring in conditional
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">statements. Unstable tests may lea=
                    d to significant errors in safety-critical
                    applications that depend on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical computations. Writing pr=
                    ograms that take into consideration test
                    instability is a difficult
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">task that requires expertise on fi=
                    nite precision computations and rounding
                    errors. This paper presents
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a toolchain to automatically gener=
                    ate and verify a provably correct test-stable
                    floating-point program
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from a functional specification in=
                    real arithmetic. The input is a real-valued
                    program written in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Prototype Verification System =
                    (PVS) specification language and the output is
                    a transformed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating-point C program annotated=
                    with ANSI/ISO C Specification Language (ACSL)
                    contracts.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">These contracts relate the floatin=
                    g-point program to its functional specification
                    in real arithmetic. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">transformed program detects if uns=
                    table tests may occur and, in these cases,
                    issues a warning and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">terminate. An approach that combin=
                    es the Frama-C analyzer, the PRECiSA round-off
                    error estimator,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and PVS is proposed to automatical=
                    ly verify that the generated program code is
                    correct in the sense
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that, if the program terminates wi=
                    thout a warning, it follows the same
                    computational path as its
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">real-valued functional specificati=
                    on.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tripathy2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-411" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tripathy, Alok, Katherine Yelick, and Aydin =
            Buluc. =E2=80=9CReducing Communication in Graph
            Neural Network Training=E2=80=9D. In: (May 2020). arXiv: 2005.0=
            3300v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are
                powerful and flexible neural networks that use the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">naturally sparse connectivity info=
                    rmation of the data. GNNs represent this
                    connectivity as sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, which have lower arithme=
                    tic intensity and thus higher communication
                    costs compared
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to dense matrices, making GNNs har=
                    der to scale to high concurrencies than
                    convolutional or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fully-connected neural networks. W=
                    e present a family of parallel algorithms for
                    training GNNs. These
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms are based on their coun=
                    terparts in dense and sparse linear algebra,
                    but they had not been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">previously applied to GNN training=
                    . We show that they can asymptotically reduce
                    communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compared to existing parallel GNN =
                    training methods. We implement a promising and
                    practical version
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that is based on 2D sparse-dense m=
                    atrix multiplication using torch.distributed.
                    Our implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelizes over GPU-equipped clu=
                    sters. We train GNNs on up to a hundred GPUs on
                    datasets that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">include a protein network with ove=
                    r a billion edges.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Trotter2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-412" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Trotter, James D., Johannes Langguth, and Xi=
            ng Cai. =E2=80=9CCache simulation for irregular memory
            traffic on multi-core CPUs: Case study on performance models fo=
            r sparse matrix=E2=80=93vector
            multiplication=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of=
                Parallel and Distributed Computing </span>144 (Oct.
            2020),
            pp. 189=E2=80=93205. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"sm=all-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.jpdc.2020.05.020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Parallel computations with
                irregular memory access patterns are often limited by the me=
                mory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subsystems of multi-core CPUs, tho=
                    ugh it can be difficult to pinpoint and
                    quantify performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bottlenecks precisely. We present =
                    a method for estimating volumes of data traffic
                    caused by irregular,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel computations on multi-cor=
                    e CPUs with memory hierarchies containing both
                    private and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">shared caches. Further, we describ=
                    e a performance model based on these estimates
                    that applies to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bandwidth-limited computations. As=
                    a case study, we consider two standard
                    algorithms for sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix=E2=80=93vector multiplicati=
                    on, a widely used, irregular kernel. Using three
                    different multi-core CPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems and a set of matrices that=
                    induce a range of irregular memory access
                    patterns, we demonstrate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that our cache simulation combined=
                    with the proposed performance model accurately
                    quantifies
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance bottlenecks that would=
                    not be detected using standard best- or
                    worst-case estimates of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the data traffic volume.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tsai2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-413" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tsai, Yuhsiang M., Terry Cojean, and Hartwig=
            Anzt. =E2=80=9CSparse Linear Algebra on AMD
            and&nbsp;NVIDIA GPUs =E2=80=93 The Race Is On=E2=80=9D. In: <sp= an class=3D"cmti-10">Lecture Notes in
                Computer Science</span>.
                Springer
                International Publishing, 2020, pp. 309=E2=80=93327. <span clas=s=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                        class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/978-3-=
                030-50743-5=CB=9916.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Efficiently processing sparse
                matrices is a central and performance-critical part of many
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scientific simulation codes. Recog=
                    nizing the adoption of manycore accelerators in
                    HPC, we evaluate in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this paper the performance of the =
                    currently best sparse matrix-vector product
                    (SpMV) implementations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on high-end GPUs from AMD and NVID=
                    IA. Specifically, we optimize SpMV kernels for
                    the CSR, COO,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ELL, and HYB format taking the har=
                    dware characteristics of the latest GPU
                    technologies into account.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We compare for 2,800 test matrices=
                    the performance of our kernels against AMD=E2=80=99s
                    hipSPARSE library
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and NVIDIA=E2=80=99s cuSPARSE libr=
                    ary, and ultimately assess how the GPU technologies
                    from AMD and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">NVIDIA compare in terms of SpMV pe=
                    rformance.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tsai2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-414" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tsai, Yuhsiang M., Terry Cojean, Tobias Ribi=
            zel, et al. =E2=80=9CPreparing Ginkgo for AMD GPUs =E2=80=93
            A Testimonial on Porting CUDA Code to HIP=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2=
            020). arXiv: 2006.14290v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">With AMD reinforcing their
                ambition in the scientific high performance computing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">ecosystem, we extend the hardware =
                    scope of the Ginkgo linear algebra package to
                    feature a HIP backend
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for AMD GPUs. In this paper, we re=
                    port and discuss the porting effort from CUDA,
                    the extension
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the HIP framework to add missin=
                    g features such as cooperative groups, the
                    performance price of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compiling HIP code for AMD archite=
                    ctures, and the design of a library providing
                    native backends for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">NVIDIA and AMD GPUs while minimizi=
                    ng code duplication by using a shared code
                    base.</span>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tsai2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-415" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tsai, Yuhsiang Mike, Terry Cojean, and Hartw=
            ig Anzt. =E2=80=9CEvaluating the Performance of
            NVIDIA=E2=80=99s A100 Ampere GPU for Sparse Linear Algebra Comp=
            utations=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020).
            arXiv: 2008.08478v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">GPU accelerators have become an
                important backbone for scientific high performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing, and the performance adv=
                    ances obtained from adopting new GPU hardware
                    are significant.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper we take a first look=
                    at NVIDIA=E2=80=99s newest server line GPU, the A100
                    architecture part
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the Ampere generation. Specific=
                    ally, we assess its performance for sparse
                    linear algebra operations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that form the backbone of many sci=
                    entific applications and assess the performance
                    improvements over
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">its predecessor.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tupitsa2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-416" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tupitsa, Nazarii et al. =E2=80=9CStrongly Co=
            nvex Optimization for the Dual Formulation of
            Optimal Transport=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Mathema=
                tical Optimization Theory and Operations
                Research</span>. Springer
            International Publishing, 2020, pp. 192=E2=80=93204. <span clas=s=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/978-3-=
            030-58657-7=CB=9917.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper we experimentally
                check a hypothesis, that dual problem to discrete entropy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regularized optimal transport prob=
                    lem possesses strong convexity on a certain
                    compact set. We present
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a numerical estimation technique o=
                    f parameter of strong convexity and show that
                    such an estimate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">increases the performance of an ac=
                    celerated alternating minimization algorithm
                    for strongly convex
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">functions applied to the considere=
                    d problem.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tutunov2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-417" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tutunov, Rasul et al. =E2=80=9CCompositional=
            ADAM: An Adaptive Compositional Solver=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb.
            2020). arXiv: 2002.03755 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>=
            .
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we present C-ADAM,
                the first adaptive solver for compositional problems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">involving a non-linear functional =
                    nesting of expected values. We proof that
                    C-ADAM converges to a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stationary point in </span>
                <!-- l.=
 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" t=abindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"592" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position=: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class==3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi=>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c1D6FF TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bi=n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D="mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2E"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn= class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"2">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                        <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-c=lose">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-= assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://=www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-scr=ipt">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>=
                                        =CE=B4</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                    <mn>2</mn>
                                    <mo class=3D="MathClass-punc">.</mo>
                                    <mn>25</mn>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</m= o>
                            </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">with </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container=class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu=_counter=3D"593" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math= class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c1D6FF TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unsel=ectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Mat=h/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>=CE=B4</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mj= x-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">being a precision parameter. Moreover, we=
                            demonstrate the importance of our results by
                            bridging,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for the first time, model-agnostic=
                            meta-learning (MAML) and compositional
                            optimisation showing
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fastest known rates for deep netwo=
                            rk adaptation to-date. Finally, we validate our
                            findings in a set of
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments from portfolio optimis=
                            ation and meta-learning. Our results manifest
                            significant sample
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complexity reductions compared to =
                            both standard and compositional solvers.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Tzovas2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-418" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Tzovas, Charilaos, Maria Predari, and Hennin=
            g Meyerhenke. =E2=80=9CDistributing Sparse
            Matrix/Graph Applications in Heterogeneous Clusters =E2=80=93 a=
            n Experimental Study=E2=80=9D. In: (Nov.
            2020). arXiv: 2011.01814 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>=
            .
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many problems in scientific and
                engineering applications contain sparse matrices or graphs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as main input objects, e.g. numeri=
                    cal simulations on meshes. Large inputs are
                    abundant these days and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">require parallel processing for me=
                    mory size and speed. To optimize the execution
                    of such simulations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on cluster systems, the input prob=
                    lem needs to be distributed suitably onto the
                    processing units
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(PUs). More and more frequently, s=
                    uch clusters contain different CPUs or a
                    combination of CPUs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and GPUs. This heterogeneity makes=
                    the load distribution problem quite
                    challenging. Our study is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">motivated by the observation that =
                    established partitioning tools do not handle
                    such heterogeneous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distribution problems as well as h=
                    omogeneous ones. In this paper, we first
                    formulate the problem
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of balanced load distribution for =
                    heterogeneous architectures as a
                    multi-objective, single-constraint
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization problem. We then spli=
                    t the problem into two phases and propose a
                    greedy approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to determine optimal block sizes f=
                    or each PU. These block sizes are then fed into
                    numerous existing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph partitioners, for us to exam=
                    ine how well they handle the above problem. One
                    of the tools we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consider is an extension of our ow=
                    n previous work (von Looz et al, ICPP=E2=80=9918)
                    called Geographer. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">experiments on well-known benchmar=
                    k meshes indicate that only two tools under
                    consideration are

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">able to yield good quality. These =
                    two are Parmetis (both the geometric and the
                    combinatorial variant)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and Geographer. While Parmetis is =
                    faster, Geographer yields better quality on
                    average.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Uribe2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-419" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Uribe, C=C3=A9sar A. and Ali Jadbabaie. =E2=
            =80=9CA Distributed Cubic-Regularized Newton Method for
            Smooth Convex Optimization over Networks=E2=80=9D. In: (July 20=
            20). arXiv: 2007.03562v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We propose a distributed,
                cubic-regularized Newton method for large-scale convex optim=
                ization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">over networks. The proposed method=
                    requires only local computations and
                    communications and is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">suitable for federated learning ap=
                    plications over arbitrary network topologies.
                    We show a </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJ=ax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"59=4" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-=TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D44=2 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi cla=ss=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo cla=ss=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                    <= /mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33">
                                                </mjx-= c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unse=lectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Ma=th/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>O</mi>
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">=
                                    (</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>k</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                    <mn>3</mn>
                                    <= /mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                </mrow>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml=>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rate when the cost function i=
                    s convex with Lipschitz gradient and Hessian,
                    with </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ct=xtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"595" st=yle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX=-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" disp=lay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi>k</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">being the number of iterations. We furthe=
                    r provide network-dependent bounds for the
                    communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">required in each step of the algor=
                    ithm. We provide numerical experiments that
                    validate our theoretical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Uroic2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-420" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Uroi=C4=87, Tessa and Hrvoje Jasak. =E2=80=
            =9CParallelisation of selective algebraic multigrid for
            block=E2=80=93pressure=E2=80=93velocity system in OpenFOAM=E2=
            =80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Computer Physics Communications
            </span>(Aug.
            2020), p. 107529. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small=-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.cpc.2020.107529.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In the world of computational
                fluid dynamics (CFD), solving the governing equations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of incompressible, turbulent, sing=
                    le=E2=80=93phase fluid flow still represents the basis
                    of many industrial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and academic applications. The imp=
                    licitly coupled (monolithic) solution approach
                    is still being
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developed and investigated for ind=
                    ustrial=E2=80=93size applications. A parallel selection
                    algebraic multigrid
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm (AMG) based on the domai=
                    n decomposition method is presented, applied
                    for the solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the linearised implicitly coupl=
                    ed pressure=E2=80=93velocity system discretised by the
                    finite volume method,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implemented in OpenFOAM. Since the=
                    setup phase of the selection AMG, i.e. sorting
                    the equations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">into coarse and fine subsets is in=
                    herently sequential, it was decided to perform
                    the setup phase locally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on each processing unit. The prolo=
                    ngation matrix for transferring the correction
                    from coarse to fine
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">level and restriction matrix for t=
                    ransferring the residual from fine to coarse
                    level are assembled locally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as well. Parallel communication is=
                    necessary only for the calculation of the
                    coarse level matrix, i.e. the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix elements which describe the=
                    cross=E2=80=93coupling of equations located on
                    different processing units.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">A localised version of the ILU fac=
                    torisation based on Crout=E2=80=99s algorithm is used
                    as a smoother in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multigrid cycle. A detailed analys=
                    is of the coarse level matrix complexity is
                    conducted in the context of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the finite volume method in domain=
                    decomposition mode. The performance and
                    scaling of our parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation is investigated for=
                    two test cases and the possible drawbacks of
                    the method are given.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-ValeroLara2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-421" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Valero-Lara, Pedro et al. =E2=80=9CsLASs: A =
            fully automatic auto-tuned linear algebra library based
            on OpenMP extensions implemented in OmpSs (LASs Library)=E2=80=
            =9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of Parallel and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Distributed
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Computing </span>(2020). <span c=lass=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">issn</span></span>: 0743-7315.=
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: htt=
            ps://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpdc.2019.12.002. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    cla=ss=3D"small-caps">url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S07=43731519303417" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://www.sciencedir=
                    ect.com/science/article/pii/S0743731519303417</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In this work we have implemented
                a novel Linear Algebra Library on top of the task-based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">runtime OmpSs-2. We have used some=
                    of the most advanced OmpSs-2 features; weak
                    dependencies and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">regions, together with the final c=
                    lause for the implementation of auto-tunable
                    code for the BLAS-3 trsm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">routine and the LAPACK routines np=
                    getrf and npgesv. All these implementations are
                    part of the first
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prototype of sLASs library, a nove=
                    l library for auto-tunable codes for linear
                    algebra operations based
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on LASs library. In all these case=
                    s, the use of the OmpSs-2 features presents an
                    improvement in terms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of execution time against other re=
                    ference libraries such as, the original LASs
                    library, PLASMA, ATLAS
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and Intel MKL. These codes are abl=
                    e to reduce the execution time in about 18% on
                    big matrices, by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">increasing the IPC on gemm and red=
                    ucing the time of task instantiation. For a few
                    medium matrices,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benefits are also seen. For small =
                    matrices and a subset of medium matrices,
                    specific optimizations that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">allow to increase the degree of pa=
                    rallelism in both, gemm and trsm tasks, are
                    applied. This strategy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">achieves an increment in performan=
                    ce of up to 40%.</span>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Vanover2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-422" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Vanover, Jackson,
            Xuan Deng, and Cindy Rubio-Gonz=C3=A1lez. =E2=80=9CDiscovering =
            Discrepancies in Numerical Libraries=E2=80=9D.
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the International Sy=
                mposium on Software Testing and
                Analysis</span>. ISSTA=E2=80=9920
            (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url=
                </span></span>: <a href=3D"https://web.cs.ucdavis.edu/~rubio/includes/issta=20.pdf" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">https://web.cs.ucdavis.edu/~r=
                    ubio/includes/issta20.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Numerical libraries constitute
                the building blocks for software applications that perform
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical calculations. Thus, it i=
                    s paramount that such libraries provide
                    accurate and consistent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results. To that end, this paper a=
                    ddresses the problem of finding discrepancies
                    between synonymous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">functions in different numerical l=
                    ibraries as a means of identifying incorrect
                    behavior. Our approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automatically finds such synonymou=
                    s functions, synthesizes testing drivers, and
                    executes differential
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tests to discover meaningful discr=
                    epancies across numerical libraries. We
                    implement our approach in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a tool named FPDiff, and provide a=
                    n evaluation on four popular numerical
                    libraries: GNU Scientific
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Library (GSL), SciPy, mpmath, and =
                    jmat. FPDiff finds a total of 126 equivalence
                    classes with a 95.8%
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">precision and 79.0% recall, and di=
                    scovers 655 instances in which an input
                    produces a set of disagreeing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">outputs between function synonyms,=
                    150 of which we found to represent 125 unique
                    bugs. We have
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reported all bugs to library maint=
                    ainers; so far, 30 bugs have been fixed, 9 have
                    been found to be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">previously known, and 25 more have=
                    been acknowledged by developers.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Vatai2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-423" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Vatai, Emil, Utsav Singhal, and Reiji Suda. =
            =E2=80=9CDiamond Matrix Powers Kernels=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10=">Proceedings
            </span><span class=3D" cmti-10">of the International Conference =
                on High Performance Computing in Asia-Pacific
                Region</span>.
            HPCAsia2020. Fukuoka, Japan: ACM, 2020, pp. 102=E2=80=93113. <s= pan class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">isbn</span></span>: 978-1=
                -4503-7236-7. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span=></span>:
                    10.1145/3368474.3368494. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class==3D"small-caps">url</span></span>: <a
                        href=3D"http://doi.acm.org/10.1145/33=68474.3368494" class=3D"url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">http://doi.acm.org/10.=
                            1145/3368474.3368494</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Matrix powers kernel calculates
                the vectors Akv, for k =3D 1, 2,..., m and they are the hear=
                t of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">various scientific computations, i=
                    ncluding communication avoiding iterative
                    solvers. In this paper we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">propose diamond matrix powers kern=
                    el - DMPK, which has the purpose to apply the
                    =E2=80=9Ddiamond tiling=E2=80=9D
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stencil algorithm to general matri=
                    ces. It can also be considered as an extension
                    of the PA1 and PA2
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms, introduced by Demmel e=
                    t al. Our approach enables us to control the
                    balance between the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">amount of communication avoidance =
                    and redundant computation inherently present in
                    communication
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">avoiding algorithms. We present a =
                    proof of concept implementation of the
                    algorithm using MPI
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">routines. The experiments we perfo=
                    rmed show that the control of the amount of
                    computation and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">communication is achievable, and w=
                    ith more thorough optimisations, DMPK is a
                    promising alternative
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to existing MPK approaches.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Vlaski2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-424" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Vlaski, Stefan and Ali H. Sayed. =E2=80=9CSe=
            cond-Order Guarantees in Centralized, Federated and
            Decentralized Nonconvex Optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020).=
            arXiv: 2003.14366v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.MA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Rapid advances in data collection
                and processing capabilities have allowed for the use of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">increasingly complex models that g=
                    ive rise to nonconvex optimization problems.
                    These formulations,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">however, can be arbitrarily diffic=
                    ult to solve in general, in the sense that even
                    simply verifying that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a given point is a local minimum c=
                    an be NP-hard [1]. Still, some relatively
                    simple algorithms have
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">been shown to lead to surprisingly=
                    good empirical results in many contexts of
                    interest. Perhaps the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">most prominent example is the succ=
                    ess of the backpropagation algorithm for
                    training neural networks.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Several recent works have pursued =
                    rigorous analytical justification for this
                    phenomenon by studying
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the structure of the nonconvex opt=
                    imization problems and establishing that simple
                    algorithms, such
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as gradient descent and its variat=
                    ions, perform well in converging towards local
                    minima and avoiding
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">saddle-points. A key insight in th=
                    ese analyses is that gradient perturbations
                    play a critical role in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">allowing local descent algorithms =
                    to efficiently distinguish desirable from
                    undesirable stationary points
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and escape from the latter. In thi=
                    s article, we cover recent results on
                    second-order guarantees for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stochastic first-order optimizatio=
                    n algorithms in centralized, federated, and
                    decentralized architectures.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wang2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-425" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wang, Cheng-Long et al. =E2=80=9CRevisiting =
            Fast Spectral Clustering with Anchor Graph=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmt=i-10">IEEE
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">International Conference on Acou=
                stics, Speech and Signal Processing
                (ICASSP)</span>. ICASSP 2020.
            IEEE, May 2020. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-c=aps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1109/icassp40776.2020.9053271.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Many anchor-graph-based spectral
                clustering methods have been proposed to accelerate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">spectral clustering for large scal=
                    e problems. In this paper, we revisit the
                    popular large-scale spectral
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">clustering method based on the anc=
                    hor graph which is equivalent to the spectral
                    decomposition on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a similar matrix obtained using a =
                    second-order transition probability. However,
                    due to the special
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">structure of the bipartite graph, =
                    there is no stable distribution of the random
                    walk process. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">even-order transition probabilitie=
                    s may only a side view of the bipartite
                    structure, resulting in breaking
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the independence of data points an=
                    d leading to undesired artifacts for boundary
                    samples. Therefore,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we propose a Fast Spectral Cluster=
                    ing based on the Random Walk Laplacian (FRWL)
                    method. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">random walk Laplacian balances exp=
                    licitly the popularity of anchors and the
                    independence of data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">points, which keeps the structure =
                    of boundary samples. The experimental results
                    demonstrate the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficiency and effectiveness of ou=
                    r method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-426" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wang, Hanzhi et al. =E2=80=9CExact Single-So=
            urce SimRank Computation on Large Graphs=E2=80=9D. In: (Apr.
            2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi<= /span></span>: 10.1145/3318464.3389781.
                arXiv:
                2004.03493v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">SimRank is a popular measurement
                for evaluating the node-to-node similarities based on the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph topology. In recent years, s=
                    ingle-source and top-</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"596" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" dis=play=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mi>k</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">SimRank queries have received increasing =
                    attention due to their applications in web
                    mining, social
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">network analysis, and spam detecti=
                    on. However, a fundamental obstacle in studying
                    SimRank has been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the lack of ground truths. The onl=
                    y exact algorithm, Power Method, is
                    computationally infeasible on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graphs with more than </span>
                <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"597" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mn cla=ss=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30">
                                </mj= x-c>
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c36"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml= unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/19=98/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>106</mn>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        <= /mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">nodes. Consequently, no existing work has=
                                evaluated the actual trade-offs between query
                                time and
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accuracy on large real-world graph=
                                s. In this paper, we present ExactSim, the
                                first algorithm that
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computes the exact single-source a=
                                nd top-</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attach=ed_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"598" style=3D"font-s=ize: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden==3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D458 TEX-I">
                                            </mjx-c=>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>
