                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inli=ne"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <= mi>k</mi>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">SimRank results on large graphs. With hig=
                                h probability, this algorithm produces ground
                                truths with a
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rigorous theoretical guarantee. We=
                                conduct extensive experiments on real-world
                                datasets to demonstrate
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the efficiency of ExactSim. The re=
                                sults show that ExactSim provides the ground
                                truth for any single-source
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SimRank query with a precision up =
                                to 7 decimal places within a reasonable query
                                time.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wang2020c" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-427" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wang, Huan et al. =E2=80=9CAssessing Local G=
            eneralization Capability in Deep Models=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-1=0">Proceedings
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">of the 23rd International Confer=
                ence on Artificial Intelligence and
                Statistics</span>. Vol.&nbsp;108.
            ASITATS 2020. 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">While it has not yet been proven,
                empirical evidence suggests that model generalization is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">related to local properties of the=
                    optima, which can be described via the
                    Hessian. We connect model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">generalization with the local prop=
                    erty of a solution under the PAC-Bayes
                    paradigm. In particular, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prove that model generalization ab=
                    ility is related to the Hessian, the
                    higher-order =E2=80=9Csmoothness=E2=80=9D terms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">characterized by the Lipschitz con=
                    stant of the Hessian, and the scales of the
                    parameters. Guided by the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proof, we propose a metric to scor=
                    e the generalization capability of a model, as
                    well as an algorithm
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that optimizes the perturbed model=
                    accordingly.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wang2020e" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-428" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wang, Jiulin and Yong Xia. =E2=80=9CClosing =
            the Gap between Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for
            Local Nonglobal Minimizer of Trust Region Subproblem=E2=80=9D. =
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">SIAM Journal on
                Optimization</span>
            30.3 (Jan. 2020), pp. 1980=E2=80=931995. <span class=3D"cmcsc-1=0"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1137/19m1294459.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The trust region subproblem has
                at most one local nonglobal minimizer. In characterizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this local solution, there is a cl=
                    ear gap between necessary and sufficient
                    conditions. In this paper,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we surprisingly show that the suff=
                    icient second-order optimality condition
                    remains necessary. As an
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application, we improve the state-=
                    of-the-art algorithm for computing a candidate
                    of the local nonglobal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">minimizer and then show that findi=
                    ng the local nonglobal minimizer or proving the
                    nonexistence can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be done in polynomial time.</span>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wang2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-429" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wang, Leyuan. =E2=80=9CParallel Algorithms o=
            n Graph Matching=E2=80=9D. PhD thesis. University of California
            Davis, 2020.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Subgraph matching is a basic task
                in querying graph dataset. It can also be called subgraph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">isomorphism search which consists =
                    to find all embeddings of a small query graph
                    in a large data graph.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">It is one of the key techniques fo=
                    r understanding the underlying structure of
                    graph datasets.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"c=mr-9">Graphs have been used to provide
                    meaningful representations of objects and patterns, as well =
                    as more
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">abstract descriptions. The represe=
                    ntative power of graphs lies in their ability
                    to characterize multiple
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">pieces of information, as well as =
                    the relationships between them. Because of
                    those properties, graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data structures have been leverage=
                    d in a wide spectrum of applications including
                    social media, the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">World Wide Web, biological and gen=
                    etic interactions, cyber network, co-author
                    networks, citations,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">etc.. And at the heart of graph th=
                    eory is the problem of graph matching, which
                    attempts to find a way
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to map one graph onto another in s=
                    uch a way that both the topological structure
                    and the node and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">edge labels are matched. For domai=
                    ns where data is noisy, an identical match may
                    not be possible,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">so an inexact graph matching algor=
                    ithm is used to search for the closest match,
                    minimizing some
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">similarity function.</span><br cla=ss=3D"newline"><span
                    class=3D"cmr-9">There have
                    been two completely different directions for supporting subg=
                    raph pattern matching. One
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">direction is to develop specialize=
                    d query processing engines, while the other
                    direction is to develop
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">efficient subgraph isomorphism alg=
                    orithms for general, labeled graphs.
                    Previously, both directions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">target distributed CPU systems. Bu=
                    t the expensive network transfer overhead
                    becomes a bottleneck.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In order to explore the efficiency=
                    and parallel abilities of a single computer,
                    we address latter direction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of Subgraph Matching.</span><br cl=ass=3D"newline"><span
                    class=3D"cmr-9">Most of
                    previous works of subgraph matching fall into three classes =
                    of approaches: depth-first tree
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">search, constraint propagation and=
                    graph indexing, all of which are not efficient
                    on GPUs. Former
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intention to run subgraph matching=
                    on GPUs only targets a specific application
                    and turns out to be
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">memory-bounded. My research intend=
                    s to tackle the bottleneck and further make
                    subgraph matching
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">meet the needs of a great spectrum=
                    of real-world applications.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wang2020d" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-430" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wang, Meiqi et al. =E2=80=9CA derived least =
            square fast learning network model=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Ap=
                plied Intelligence</span>
            (July 2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps=">doi</span></span>: 10.1007/s10489-020-01773-6.
         </p><!-- l. 30 -->
         <p class=3D" noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
                    <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The extreme learning machine
                        (ELM) requires a large number of hidden layer nodes in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">training process. Thus, random par=
                    ameters will exponentially increase and affect
                    network stability.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Moreover, the single activation fu=
                    nction affects the generalization capability of
                    the network. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">paper proposes a derived least squ=
                    are fast learning network (DLSFLN) to solve the
                    aforementioned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">problems. DLSFLN uses the inherita=
                    nce of some functions to obtain various
                    activation functions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">through continuous differentiation=
                    of functions. The types of activation
                    functions were increased and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the mapping capability of hidden l=
                    ayer neurons was enhanced when the random
                    parameter dimension
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">was maintained. DLSFLN randomly ge=
                    nerates the input weights and hidden layer
                    thresholds and uses
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the least square method to determi=
                    ne the connection weights between the output
                    and the input layers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and that between the output and th=
                    e input nodes. The regression and
                    classification experiments show
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that DLSFLN has a faster training =
                    speed and better training accuracy,
                    generalization capability, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stability compared with other neur=
                    al network algorithms, such as fast learning
                    network(FLN).</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wei2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-431" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wei, Xiaohui et al. =E2=80=9CG-SEAP: Analyzi=
            ng and
            characterizing soft-error aware approximation in GPGPUs=E2=80=
            =9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Future Generation
                Computer
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Systems </span>(Mar. 2020). <spa= n class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1016/=
                j.future.2020.03.040.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">As General-Purpose Graphics
                Processing Units (GPGPUs) become pervasive for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">High-Performance Computing (HPC), =
                    ensuring that programs can be protected from
                    soft errors has
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">become increasingly important. Sof=
                    t errors may cause Silent Data Corruptions
                    (SDCs), which produces
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">erroneous execution results silent=
                    ly. Due to the massive parallelism of GPGPUs,
                    fully protecting
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">them against soft errors introduce=
                    s nontrivial overhead. Fortunately, imprecise
                    execution outcomes are

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inherently tolerable for some HPC =
                    programs due to the nature of these
                    applications. Leveraging the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">feature, selective soft error prot=
                    ection can be applied to reduce energy
                    consumptions.</span><br class=3D"newline"><span class=3D"cmr=-9">In this work, we first propose a
                    GPGPU-based Soft-Error aware APproximation analysis framewor=
                    k
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(G-SEAP) to characterize the appro=
                    ximation characteristics of soft errors. Based
                    on G-SEAP, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">perform an exhaustive analysis for=
                    17 representative HPC benchmarks and observe
                    72.7% of SDCs on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">average are approximable. We also =
                    observe that the dataflow of application,
                    kernel function reliability
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">requirement, instruction-type, and=
                    data bit-location are all important factors
                    for program=E2=80=99s correctness.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Lastly, according to observations,=
                    we further design an approximate Error
                    Correction Codes (ECCs)
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">mechanism and an approximate instr=
                    uction duplication technique to illustrate how
                    G-SEAP provides
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">useful guidance for energy-efficie=
                    nt soft-error elimination in GPGPUs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wiebe2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-432" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wiebe, Johannes et al. =E2=80=9CA robust app=
            roach to warped Gaussian process-constrained
            optimization=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.08222v1 <sp= an class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Optimization problems with
                uncertain black-box constraints, modeled by warped Gaussian
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">processes, have recently been cons=
                    idered in the Bayesian optimization setting.
                    This work introduces
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a new class of constraints in whic=
                    h the same black-box function occurs multiple
                    times evaluated at
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different domain points. Such cons=
                    traints are important in applications where,
                    e.g., safety-critical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">measures are aggregated over multi=
                    ple time periods. Our approach, which uses
                    robust optimization,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reformulates these uncertain const=
                    raints into deterministic constraints
                    guaranteed to be satisfied
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with a specified probability, i.e.=
                    , deterministic approximations to a chance
                    constraint. This approach
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extends robust optimization method=
                    s from parametric uncertainty to uncertain
                    functions modeled by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">warped Gaussian processes. We anal=
                    yze convexity conditions and propose a custom
                    global optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">strategy for non-convex cases. A c=
                    ase study derived from production planning and
                    an industrially
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">relevant example from oil well dri=
                    lling show that the approach effectively
                    mitigates uncertainty in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">learned curves. For the drill sche=
                    duling example, we develop a custom strategy
                    for globally optimizing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">integer decisions.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wilkinson2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-433" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wilkinson, Leland and Hengrui Luo. =E2=80=9C=
            A Distance-preserving Matrix Sketch=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020).
            arXiv: 2009.03979 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.HC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Visualizing very large matrices
                involves many formidable problems. Various popular solutions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to these problems involve sampling=
                    , clustering, projection, or feature selection
                    to reduce the size and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complexity of the original task. A=
                    n important aspect of these methods is how to
                    preserve relative
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distances between points in the hi=
                    gher-dimensional space after reducing rows and
                    columns to fit in a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lower dimensional space. This aspe=
                    ct is important because conclusions based on
                    faulty visual reasoning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can be harmful. Judging dissimilar=
                    points as similar or similar points as
                    dissimilar on the basis of a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">visualization can lead to false co=
                    nclusions. To ameliorate this bias and to make
                    visualizations of very
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large datasets feasible, we introd=
                    uce a new algorithm that selects a subset of
                    rows and columns of a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">rectangular matrix. This selection=
                    is designed to preserve relative distances as
                    closely as possible. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">compare our matrix sketch to more =
                    traditional alternatives on a variety of
                    artificial and real datasets.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wongpanich2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-434" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wongpanich, Arissa, Hieu Pham, et al. =E2=80=
            =9CTraining EfficientNets at Supercomputer Scale: 8%
            ImageNet Top-1 Accuracy in One Hour=E2=80=9D. In: (Oct. 2020). =
            arXiv: 2011.00071 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">EfficientNets are a family of
                state-of-the-art image classification models based on effici=
                ently
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scaled convolutional neural networ=
                    ks. Currently, EfficientNets can take on the
                    order of days to train;
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for example, training an Efficient=
                    Net-B0 model takes 23 hours on a Cloud TPU v2-8
                    node. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this paper, we explore techniques =
                    to scale up the training of EfficientNets on
                    TPU-v3 Pods with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">2048 cores, motivated by speedups =
                    that can be achieved when training at such
                    scales. We discuss
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimizations required to scale tr=
                    aining to a batch size of 65536 on 1024 TPU-v3
                    cores, such as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">selecting large batch optimizers a=
                    nd learning rate schedules as well as utilizing
                    distributed evaluation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and batch normalization techniques=
                    . Additionally, we present timing and
                    performance benchmarks for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">EfficientNet models trained on the=
                    ImageNet dataset in order to analyze the
                    behavior of EfficientNets

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at scale. With our optimizations, =
                    we are able to train EfficientNet on ImageNet
                    to an accuracy of 83%
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in 1 hour and 4 minutes.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Wongpanich2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-435" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Wongpanich, Arissa, Yang You, and James Demm=
            el. =E2=80=9CRethinking the Value of Asynchronous
            Solvers for Distributed Deep Learning=E2=80=9D. In: <span class==3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the
                International
                Conference
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">on High Performance Computing in=
                Asia-Pacific Region</span>. HPCAsia2020.
            Fukuoka, Japan:
            ACM, 2020, pp. 52=E2=80=9360. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span cl=ass=3D"small-caps">isbn</span></span>:
            978-1-4503-7236-7.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1145/3368474.3368498. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                            class=3D"sm=all-caps">url</span></span>:
                    <a href=3D"http://doi.acm.org/10.1145/3368474.3368498" class=3D="url"><span
                            class=3D"cmtt-10">http://doi.acm.org/10.1145/3368474.3368498</s= pan></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In recent years, the field of
                machine learning has seen significant advances as data
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">becomes more abundant and deep lea=
                    rning models become larger and more complex.
                    However, these
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improvements in accuracy [2] have =
                    come at the cost of longer training time. As a
                    result, state-of-the-art
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">models like OpenAI=E2=80=99s GPT-2=
                    [18] or AlphaZero [20] require the use of distributed
                    systems or clusters
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in order to speed up training. Cur=
                    rently, there exist both asynchronous and
                    synchronous solvers
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for distributed training. In this =
                    paper, we implement state-of-the-art
                    asynchronous and synchronous
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solvers, then conduct a comparison=
                    between them to help readers pick the most
                    appropriate solver for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">their own applications. We address=
                    three main challenges: (1) implementing
                    asynchronous solvers that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">can outperform six common algorith=
                    m variants, (2) achieving state-of-the-art
                    distributed performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for various applications with diff=
                    erent computational patterns, and (3)
                    maintaining accuracy for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large-batch asynchronous training.=
                    For asynchronous algorithms, we implement an
                    algorithm called
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">EA-wild, which combines the idea o=
                    f non-locking wild updates from Hogwild! [19]
                    with EASGD. Our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation is able to scale to=
                    217,600 cores and finish 90 epochs of training
                    the ResNet-50 model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on ImageNet in 15 minutes (the bas=
                    eline takes 29 hours on eight NVIDIA P100
                    GPUs). We conclude
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that more complex models (e.g., Re=
                    sNet-50) favor synchronous methods, while our
                    asynchronous solver
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">outperforms the synchronous solver=
                    for models with a low
                    computation-communication ratio. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">results are documented in this pap=
                    er; for more results, readers can refer to our
                    supplemental website
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">1.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Xia2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-436" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Xia, Yuanyuan et al. =E2=80=9CError detectio=
            n of arithmetic expressions=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">The Journ=
                al of
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Supercomputing </span>(Nov. 2020=
            ). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 1=
            0.1007/s11227-020-03469-7.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Inspecting floating-point errors
                is essential to floating-point operations. In this paper, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">present floating-point error detec=
                    tor (FPED), an inspector of floating-point
                    errors for arithmetic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">expressions. FPED can pick a suita=
                    ble benchmark generation approach by analyzing
                    the distribution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the expression of a floating-po=
                    int operation, thereby minimizing the
                    possibilities of underreporting
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating-point errors. FPED is als=
                    o able to determine the significant sources of
                    errors in a floating-point
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operation according to the frequen=
                    cies of computation building blocks that
                    contribute most to the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">floating-point errors, benefiting =
                    the follow-up optimizations of computation
                    accuracies. We validate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the correctness and functionalitie=
                    s of FPED by conducting experiments on the
                    FPBench benchmark
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">suite. The experimental results de=
                    monstrate that FPED can obtain more accurate
                    detection results
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">than the random detecting approach=
                    with respect to floating-point error
                    detection. We also compare
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">FPED with the existing dynamic err=
                    or detection tools. The experimental results
                    show that in most
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the 33 test benchmarks, the max=
                    imum error results of FPED are greater than
                    Herbgrind and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">detection performance is higher th=
                    an Herbgrind.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Xin2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-437" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Xin, Ran, Usman A. Khan, and Soummya Kar. =
            =E2=80=9CA fast randomized incremental gradient
            method for decentralized non-convex optimization=E2=80=9D. In: =
            (Nov. 2020). arXiv: 2011.03853
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We study decentralized non-convex
                finite-sum minimization problems described over a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">network of nodes, where each node =
                    possesses a local batch of data samples. We
                    propose a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">single-timescale first-order rando=
                    mized incremental gradient method, termed as
                    GT-SAGA. GT-SAGA
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is computationally efficient since=
                    it evaluates only one component gradient per
                    node per iteration
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and achieves provably fast and rob=
                    ust performance by leveraging node-level
                    variance reduction and

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">network-level gradient tracking. F=
                    or general smooth non-convex problems, we show
                    almost sure
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and mean-squared convergence to a =
                    first-order stationary point and describe
                    regimes of practical
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significance where GT-SAGA achieve=
                    s a network-independent convergence rate and
                    outperforms the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">existing approaches respectively. =
                    When the global cost function further satisfies
                    the Polyak-Lojaciewisz
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">condition, we show that GT-SAGA ex=
                    hibits global linear convergence to an optimal
                    solution in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">expectation and describe regimes o=
                    f practical interest where the performance is
                    network-independent
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and improves upon the existing wor=
                    k. Numerical experiments based on real-world
                    datasets are included
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to highlight the behavior and conv=
                    ergence aspects of the proposed method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Xin2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-438" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Xin, Ran, Shi Pu, et al. =E2=80=9CA general =
            framework for decentralized optimization with
            first-order
            methods=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv: 2009.05837 <span cla=ss=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Decentralized optimization to
                minimize a finite sum of functions over a network of nodes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">has been a significant focus withi=
                    n control and signal processing research due to
                    its natural relevance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to optimal control and signal esti=
                    mation problems. More recently, the emergence
                    of sophisticated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computing and large-scale data sci=
                    ence needs have led to a resurgence of activity
                    in this area. In this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">article, we discuss decentralized =
                    first-order gradient methods, which have found
                    tremendous success in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">control, signal processing, and ma=
                    chine learning problems, where such methods,
                    due to their simplicity,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">serve as the first method of choic=
                    e for many complex inference and training
                    tasks. In particular, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provide a general framework of dec=
                    entralized first-order methods that is
                    applicable to undirected and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">directed communication networks al=
                    ike, and show that much of the existing work on
                    optimization and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">consensus can be related explicitl=
                    y to this framework. We further extend the
                    discussion to decentralized
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stochastic first-order methods tha=
                    t rely on stochastic gradients at each node and
                    describe how local
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variance reduction schemes, previo=
                    usly shown to have promise in the centralized
                    settings, are able to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">improve the performance of decentr=
                    alized methods when combined with what is known
                    as gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tracking. We motivate and demonstr=
                    ate the effectiveness of the corresponding
                    methods in the context
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of machine learning and signal pro=
                    cessing problems that arise in decentralized
                    environments.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Yang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-439" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Yang, Dongxu, Junhong Liu, and Junjie Lai. =
            =E2=80=9CEDGES: An Efficient Distributed
            GraphEmbedding System on GPU clusters=E2=80=9D. In: <span class==3D"cmti-10">IEEE Transactions on Parallel
                and
                Distributed
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Systems </span>(2020). <span cla=ss=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1109/tpds.=
            2020.3041219.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Graph embedding training models
                access parameters sparsely in a =E2=80=9Done-hot=E2=80=9D ma=
                nner. Currently,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the distributed graph embedding ne=
                    ural network is learned by data parallel with
                    the parameter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">server, which suffers significant =
                    performance and scalability problems. In this
                    paper, we analyze
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the problems and characteristics o=
                    f training this kind of models on distributed
                    GPU clusters for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the first time, and find that fixe=
                    d model parameters scattered among different
                    machine nodes are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a major limiting factor for effici=
                    ency.Based on our observation, we develop an
                    efficient distributed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph embedding system called EDGE=
                    S, which can utilize GPU clusters to train
                    large graph models
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with billions of nodes and trillio=
                    ns of edges using data and model parallelism.
                    Within the system,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we propose a novel dynamic partiti=
                    on architecture for training these models,
                    achieving at least one
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">half of communication reduction co=
                    mpared to existing training systems. According
                    to our evaluations
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on real-world networks, our system=
                    delivers a competitive accuracy for the
                    trained embeddings, and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">significantly accelerates the trai=
                    ning process of the graph node embedding neural
                    network,achieving a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">speedup of 7.23</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -=
-><mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabind=
ex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"599" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: rel=
ative;"><mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true"><mjx-mo class=3D"m=
jx-n MathClass-bin"><mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-math><m=
jx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http=
://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline"><mo class=3D"MathClass-b=
in">=C3=97</mo></math></mjx-assistive-mml></mjx-container>
            <span class=3D"cmr-9">and 18.6</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-contain= er class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtm=enu_counter=3D"600" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-m= ath class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo=>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">over the existing fastest training system=
                                on single node and multi-node, respectively.
                                As for the
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scalability, our experiments show =
                                that EDGES obtains a nearly linear
                                speedup.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Yasar2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-440" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ya=C5=9Far, Abdurrahman, Muhammed Fatih Bali=
            n, et al. =E2=80=9COn Symmetric Rectilinear Matrix
            Partitioning=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020). arXiv: 2009.07735 <spa= n class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Even distribution of irregular
                workload to processing units is crucial for efficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelization in many applicatio=
                    ns. In this work, we are concerned with a
                    spatial partitioning

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">called rectilinear partitioning (a=
                    lso known as generalized block distribution) of
                    sparse matrices. More
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">specifically, in this work, we add=
                    ress the problem of symmetric rectilinear
                    partitioning of a square
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix. By symmetric, we mean the =
                    rows and columns of the matrix are identically
                    partitioned yielding
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a tiling where the diagonal tiles =
                    (blocks) will be squares. We first show that
                    the optimal solution to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this problem is NP-hard, and we pr=
                    opose four heuristics to solve two different
                    variants of this problem.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We present a thorough analysis of =
                    the computational complexities of those
                    proposed heuristics. To
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">make the proposed techniques more =
                    applicable in real life application scenarios,
                    we further reduce their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computational complexities by util=
                    izing effective sparsification strategies
                    together with an efficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse prefix-sum data structure. =
                    We experimentally show the proposed algorithms
                    are efficient and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effective on more than six hundred=
                    test matrices. With sparsification, our
                    methods take less than 3
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">seconds in the Twitter graph on a =
                    modern 24 core system and output a solution
                    whose load imbalance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is no worse than 1%.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Yasar2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-441" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Ya=C5=9Far, Abdurrahman, Sivasankaran Rajama=
            nickam, et al. =E2=80=9CA Block-Based Triangle Counting
            Algorithm on Heterogeneous Environments=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 20=
            20). arXiv: 2009.12457 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Triangle counting is a
                fundamental building block in graph algorithms. In this pape=
                r,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">we propose a block-based triangle =
                    counting algorithm to reduce data movement
                    during both
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sequential and parallel execution.=
                    Our block-based formulation makes the
                    algorithm naturally suitable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for heterogeneous architectures. T=
                    he problem of partitioning the adjacency matrix
                    of a graph is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">well-studied. Our task decompositi=
                    on goes one step further: it partitions the set
                    of triangles in the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph. By streaming these small ta=
                    sks to compute resources, we can solve problems
                    that do not
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fit on a device. We demonstrate th=
                    e effectiveness of our approach by providing an
                    implementation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on a compute node with multiple so=
                    ckets, cores and GPUs. The current
                    state-of-the-art in triangle
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">enumeration processes the Friendst=
                    er graph in 2.1 seconds, not including data
                    copy time between CPU
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and GPU. Using that metric, our ap=
                    proach is 20 percent faster. When copy times
                    are included, our
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm takes 3.2 seconds. This =
                    is 5.6 times faster than the fastest published
                    CPU-only time.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Yelick2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-442" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Yelick, Katherine et al. =E2=80=9CThe parall=
            elism motifs of genomic data analysis=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10"=>Philosophical
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Transactions of the Royal Societ=
                y A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering
                Sciences</span>
            378.2166 (Jan. 2020), p. 20190394. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10">
                <sp= an class=3D"small-caps">doi
            </span></span>:
            10.1098/rsta.2019.0394.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Genomic datasets are growing
                dramatically as the cost of sequencing continues to decline
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and small sequencing devices becom=
                    e available. Enormous community databases store
                    and share
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">these data with the research commu=
                    nity, but some of these genomic data analysis
                    problems require
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large-scale computational platform=
                    s to meet both the memory and computational
                    requirements.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">These applications differ from sci=
                    entific simulations that dominate the workload
                    on high-end parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems today and place different =
                    requirements on programming support, software
                    libraries and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel architectural design. For=
                    example, they involve irregular communication
                    patterns such as
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">asynchronous updates to shared dat=
                    a structures. We consider several problems in
                    high-performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">genomics analysis, including align=
                    ment, profiling, clustering and assembly for
                    both single genomes and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">metagenomes. We identify some of t=
                    he common computational patterns or =E2=80=98motifs=E2=80=99
                    that help inform
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallelization strategies and com=
                    pare our motifs to some of the established
                    lists, arguing that at least
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">two key patterns, sorting and hash=
                    ing, are missing.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Yi2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-443" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Yi, Xinlei et al. =E2=80=9CA Primal-Dual SGD=
            Algorithm for Distributed Nonconvex Optimization=E2=80=9D.
            In: (June 2020). arXiv: 2006.03474v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[m=
                ath.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The distributed nonconvex
                optimization problem of minimizing a global cost function fo=
                rmed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by a sum of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <= mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"601" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assi= stive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.=w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>n</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">local cost functions by using local infor=
                            mation exchange is considered. This problem is
                            an important
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">component of many machine learning=
                            techniques with data parallelism, such as deep
                            learning and
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">federated learning. We propose a d=
                            istributed primal-dual stochastic gradient
                            descent (SGD) algorithm,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">suitable for arbitrarily connected=
                            communication networks and any smooth
                            (possibly nonconvex)

                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">cost functions. We show that the p=
                            roposed algorithm achieves the linear speedup
                            convergence rate</span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"602" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-S=C"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop MathClas=s-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28 TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mn=class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mn>
                                        <mjx-mo class=3D"=mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>=
                                        <mjx-msqrt space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-sqrt>
                                                <mjx-surd>
                                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n">
                                                        <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c221A"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                </mjx-surd>
                                                <mjx-box style=3D"padding-to=p: 0.163em;">
                                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D447 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                        </mjx-mi>
                                                        </mjx-mrow=>
                                                </mjx-box>
                                            </mjx-sqrt>
                                        </mjx-msqrt>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-lop MathC=lass-close">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29 TEX-S2"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                </m= jx-math>
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xml=ns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi mathvarian=t=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo=>
                                                <mrow>
                                                    <mn>1</mn>
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                                    <msqrt>
                                                        <mrow>
                                                            <mi= mathvariant=3D"italic">nT</mi>
                                                        </mrow>
                                                    </msqrt>
                                                </mrow>
                                                <mo class=3D"MathClass=-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">for general nonconvex cost functions and =
                            the well known </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMen=u_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"603" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c1D4AA TEX-SC"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-=n MathClass-open">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-= mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                            </mjx-mn>
                                            <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2F"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx= -mo>
                                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D447 TEX-I">
                                                        <= /mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                        <mjx-c c=lass=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml=unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/199=8/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=
                                        =AA</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mn>1</mn>
                                            <mo class==3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                            <mi>T</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-cl=ose">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">convergence rate when the global cost fun=
                            ction satisfies the Polyak-=C5=81ojasiewicz
                            condition, where</span>
                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"604" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D447 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </m= jx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                                <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>T=
                                    </mi>
                                </math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx-container>
                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">is the total number of iterations. We als=
                            o show that the output of the proposed
                            algorithm with
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fixed parameters linearly converge=
                            s to a neighborhood of a global optimum. We
                            demonstrate through
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">numerical experiments the efficien=
                            cy of our algorithm in comparison with the
                            baseline centralized SGD
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and recently proposed distributed =
                            SGD algorithms.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-You2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-444" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">You, X. et al. =E2=80=9CZerospy: Exploring S=
            oftware
            Inefficiency with Redundant Zeros=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"=cmti-10">Proceedings of the 2020
                International
                Conference for
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">High Performance Computing, Netw=
                orking, Storage and Analysis</span>. SC =E2=80=9920.
            Los Alamitos, CA,
            USA: IEEE Computer Society, Nov. 2020, pp. 397=E2=80=93410. <sp= an class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1109=
                /SC41405.2020.00033.
                <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">url</span>
                    <= /span>: <a href=3D"https://doi.ieeecomputersociety.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020=.00033"
                            class=3D"url"><span class=3D"cmtt-10">https://doi.ieeecomputersocie=
                                ty.org/10.1109/SC41405.2020.00033</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Redundant zeros cause
                inefficiencies in which the zero values are loaded and compu=
                ted
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">repeatedly, resulting in unnecessa=
                    ry memory traffic and identity computation that
                    waste memory
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">bandwidth and CPU resources. Optim=
                    izing compilers is difficult in eliminating
                    these zero-related
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inefficiencies due to limitations =
                    in static analysis. Hardware approaches, in
                    contrast, optimize
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">inefficiencies without code modifi=
                    cation, but are not widely adopted in commodity
                    processors. In
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">this paper, we propose ZeroSpy - a=
                    fine-grained profiler to identify redundant
                    zeros caused by
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">both inappropriate use of data str=
                    uctures and useless computation. ZeroSpy also
                    provides intuitive
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization guidance by revealing=
                    the locations where the redundant zeros happen
                    in source lines and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">calling contexts. The experimental=
                    results demonstrate ZeroSpy is capable of
                    identifying redundant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">zeros in programs that have been h=
                    ighly optimized for years. Based on the
                    optimization guidance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">revealed by ZeroSpy, we can achiev=
                    e significant speedups after eliminating
                    redundant zeros.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-You2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-445" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">You, Yang, Yuxiong He, et al. =E2=80=9CFast =
            LSTM by dynamic decomposition on cloud
            and distributed systems=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">K=
                nowledge and Information Systems </span>(July 2020).
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>:
                    10.1007/s10115-020-01487-8.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Long short-term memory (LSTM) is
                a powerful deep learning technique that has been widely
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">used in many real-world data-minin=
                    g applications such as language modeling and
                    machine translation.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we aim to minimize =
                    the latency of LSTM inference on cloud systems
                    without losing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accuracy. If an LSTM model does no=
                    t fit in cache, the latency due to data
                    movement will likely
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">be greater than that due to comput=
                    ation. In this case, we reduce model
                    parameters. If, as in most
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applications we consider, the LSTM=
                    models are able to fit the cache of cloud
                    server processors, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">focus on reducing the number of fl=
                    oating point operations, which has a
                    corresponding linear impact on
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the latency of the inference calcu=
                    lation. Thus, in our system, we dynamically
                    reduce model parameters
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">or flops depending on which most i=
                    mpacts latency. Our inference system is based
                    on singular value
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">decomposition and canonical polyad=
                    ic decomposition. Our system is accurate and
                    low latency. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluate our system based on model=
                    s from a series of real-world applications like
                    language modeling,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computer vision, question answerin=
                    g, and sentiment analysis. Users of our system
                    can use either
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">pre-trained models or start from s=
                    cratch. Our system achieves </span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"605" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class==3D"mjx-c35"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mn>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c=class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mn>15</mn>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            <= /math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">average speedup for six real-world applic=
                    ations without losing accuracy in inference. We
                    also design
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and implement a distributed optimi=
                    zation system with dynamic decomposition, which
                    can significantly
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reduce the energy cost and acceler=
                    ate the training process.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-You2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-446" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">You, Yang, Yuhui Wang, et al. =E2=80=9CThe L=
            imit of the Batch Size=E2=80=9D. In: (June 2020). arXiv:
            2006.08517v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Large-batch training is an
                efficient approach for current distributed deep learning sys=
                tems. It
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">has enabled researchers to reduce =
                    the ImageNet/ResNet-50 training from 29 hours
                    to around 1 minute.

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this paper, we focus on studyin=
                    g the limit of the batch size. We think it may
                    provide a guidance to AI
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">supercomputer and algorithm design=
                    ers. We provide detailed numerical optimization
                    instructions for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">step-by-step comparison. Moreover,=
                    it is important to understand the
                    generalization and optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performance of huge batch training=
                    . Hoffer et al. introduced =E2=80=9Dultra-slow
                    diffusion=E2=80=9D theory to large-batch
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">training. However, our experiments=
                    show contradictory results with the conclusion
                    of Hoffer et al. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provide comprehensive experimental=
                    results and detailed analysis to study the
                    limitations of batch size
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scaling and =E2=80=9Dultra-slow di=
                    ffusion=E2=80=9D theory. For the first time we scale the batch
                    size on ImageNet to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at least a magnitude larger than a=
                    ll previous work, and provide detailed studies
                    on the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of many state-of-the-art optimizat=
                    ion schemes under this setting. We propose an
                    optimization recipe
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that is able to improve the top-1 =
                    test accuracy by 18% compared to the
                    baseline.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Yu2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-447" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Yu, Tong and Hong Zhu. =E2=80=9CHyper-Parame=
            ter Optimization: A Review of Algorithms and
            Applications=E2=80=9D. In: (Mar. 2020). arXiv: 2003.05689v1 <sp= an class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.LG]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Since deep neural networks were
                developed, they have made huge contributions to everyday
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lives. Machine learning provides m=
                    ore rational advice than humans are capable of
                    in almost every
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">aspect of daily life. However, des=
                    pite this achievement, the design and training
                    of neural networks are
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">still challenging and unpredictabl=
                    e procedures. To lower the technical thresholds
                    for common users,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">automated hyper-parameter optimiza=
                    tion (HPO) has become a popular topic in both
                    academic and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">industrial areas. This paper provi=
                    des a review of the most essential topics on
                    HPO. The first section
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">introduces the key hyper-parameter=
                    s related to model training and structure, and
                    discusses their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">importance and methods to define t=
                    he value range. Then, the research focuses on
                    major optimization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms and their applicability=
                    , covering their efficiency and accuracy
                    especially for deep learning
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">networks. This study next reviews =
                    major services and toolkits for HPO, comparing
                    their support for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">state-of-the-art searching algorit=
                    hms, feasibility with major deep learning
                    frameworks, and extensibility
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for new modules designed by users.=
                    The paper concludes with problems that exist
                    when HPO is applied
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to deep learning, a comparison bet=
                    ween optimization algorithms, and prominent
                    approaches for model
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">evaluation with limited computatio=
                    nal resources.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Yuan2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-448" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Yuan, Rui, Alessandro Lazaric, and Robert M.=
            Gower. =E2=80=9CSketched Newton-Raphson=E2=80=9D. In: (June
            2020). arXiv: 2006.12120v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</s= pan>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We propose a new globally
                convergent stochastic second order method. Our starting poin=
                t is
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the development of a new Sketched =
                    Newton-Raphson (SNR) method for solving large
                    scale nonlinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">equations of the form </span>
                <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"606" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi cla=ss=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow s=pace=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28">=
                                </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4=65 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-clos=e">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mj=x-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3D"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <m= jx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c30"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mn=>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                        <math= xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>F</mi>=
                            <mrow>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                <mrow>
                                    <mi>x</mi>
                                </mrow>
                                <mo class==3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                            </mrow>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=3D</mo>
                            <mn>0=
                            </mn></math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">with </span><!-- l. 30 -->
