                <mjx-container=class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu=_counter=3D"607" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math= class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-c1D439 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-pu=nc" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c3A"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c211D TEX-A"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D="mjx-c1D451 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" s=pace=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2192"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx=-i" space=3D"4">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c211D TEX-A"></mjx-c>
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c1D451 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>F</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-punc">:</mo>
                                <mi math=variant=3D"italic">=E2=84=9Dd</mi>
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-rel">=E2=86=92</mo=>
                                    <mi mathvariant=3D"italic">=E2=84=9Dd</mi>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                        </mjx= -container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We then show how to design several=
                            stochastic second order optimization methods
                            by re-writing the
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimization problem of interest a=
                            s a system of nonlinear equations and applying
                            SNR. For instance, by
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">applying SNR to find a stationary =
                            point of a generalized linear model (GLM), we
                            derive completely new
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and scalable stochastic second ord=
                            er methods. We show that the resulting method
                            is very competitive
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as compared to state-of-the-art va=
                            riance reduced methods. Using a variable
                            splitting trick, we also
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">show that the Stochastic Newton me=
                            thod (SNM) is a special case of SNR, and use
                            this connection to
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">establish the first global converg=
                            ence theory of SNM. Indeed, by showing that SNR
                            can be interpreted
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">as a variant of the stochastic gra=
                            dient descent (SGD) method we are able to
                            leverage proof techniques
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of SGD and establish a global conv=
                            ergence theory and rates of convergence for
                            SNR. As a special case,
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">our theory also provides a new glo=
                            bal convergence theory for the original
                            Newton-Raphson method
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">under strictly weaker assumptions =
                            as compared to what is commonly used for global
                            convergence.
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">There are many ways to re-write an=
                            optimization problem as nonlinear equations.
                            Each re-write would
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lead to a distinct method when usi=
                            ng SNR. As such, we believe that SNR and its
                            global convergence
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">theory will open the way to design=
                            ing and analysing a host of new stochastic
                            second order methods.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zachariadis2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-449" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zachariadis, Orestis et al. =E2=80=9CAcceler=
            ating sparse matrix=E2=80=93matrix multiplication with GPU
            Tensor Cores=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Computers &a=
                mp; Electrical Engineering </span>88 (Dec. 2020), p.
            106848. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi=
                </span></span>:
            10.1016/j.compeleceng.2020.106848.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">parse general matrix=E2=80=93matrix
                multiplication (spGEMM) is an essential component in many
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scientific and data analytics appl=
                    ications. However, the sparsity pattern of the
                    input matrices and the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">interaction of their patterns make=
                    spGEMM challenging. Modern GPUs include Tensor
                    Core Units
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">(TCUs), which specialize in dense =
                    matrix multiplication. Our aim is to re-purpose
                    TCUs for sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices. The key idea of our spGE=
                    MM algorithm, tSparse, is to multiply sparse
                    rectangular blocks
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using the mixed precision mode of =
                    TCUs. tSparse partitions the input matrices
                    into tiles and operates
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">only on tiles which contain one or=
                    more elements. It creates a task list of the
                    tiles, and performs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrix multiplication of these til=
                    es using TCUs. To the best of our knowledge,
                    this is the first time
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that TCUs are used in the context =
                    of spGEMM. We show that spGEMM, with our tiling
                    approach,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">benefits from TCUs. Our approach s=
                    ignificantly improves the performance of spGEMM
                    in comparison
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to cuSPARSE, CUSP, RMerge2, Nspars=
                    e, AC-SpGEMM and spECK.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zanon2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-450" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zanon, Mattia et al. =E2=80=9CSparse Logisti=
            c Regression: Comparison of Regularization and Bayesian
            Implementations=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Algorithm=
                s </span>13.6 (June 2020), p. 137. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    class=3D"=small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.3390/a13060137.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In knowledge-based systems,
                besides obtaining good output prediction accuracy, it is cru=
                cial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to understand the subset of input =
                    variables that have most influence on the
                    output, with the goal of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gaining deeper insight into the un=
                    derlying process. These requirements call for
                    logistic model estimation
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques that provide a sparse s=
                    olution, i.e., where coefficients associated
                    with non-important variables
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are set to zero. In this work we c=
                    ompare the performance of two methods: the
                    first one is based on the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">well known Least Absolute Shrinkag=
                    e and Selection Operator (LASSO) which involves
                    regularization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">with an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-= container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0=" ctxtmenu_counter=3D" 608" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;"=>
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2195">
                                </mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml un=selectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/=Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                            <mi>=E2=86=95</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mm= l>
                        </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">1
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">norm; the second one is the Releva=
                            nce Vector Machine (RVM) which is based on a
                            Bayesian implementation
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the linear logistic model. The =
                            two methods are extensively compared in this
                            paper, on real and
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">simulated datasets. Results show t=
                            hat, in general, the two approaches are
                            comparable in terms of
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">prediction performance. RVM outper=
                            forms the LASSO both in term of structure
                            recovery (estimation
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of the correct non-zero model coef=
                            ficients) and prediction accuracy when the
                            dimensionality of the data
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tends to increase. However, LASSO =
                            shows comparable performance to RVM when the
                            dimensionality of
                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the data is much higher than numbe=
                            r of samples that is </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax C=txtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                            ctxtmenu_counter=3D"609" s=tyle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45D TE=X-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-rel" space=3D"4">
                                    <mj= x-c class=3D"mjx-c226B">
                                        </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"4="><mjx-c class=3D" mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                                    </mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assist= ive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3=.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>p</mi>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-=rel">=E2=89=AB</mo>
                                    <mi>n</mi>
                                </math>
                    </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>.
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zeni2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-451" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zeni, Alberto et al. =E2=80=9CLOGAN: High-Pe=
            rformance GPU-Based X-Drop Long-Read Alignment=E2=80=9D.
            In: IPDPS 2020. Feb. 2020. arXiv: 2002.05200 <span class=3D"cmt=t-10">[q-bio.GN]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Pairwise sequence alignment is
                one of the most computationally intensive kernels in genomic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data analysis, accounting for more=
                    than 90% of the runtime for key bioinformatics
                    applications. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">method is particularly expensive f=
                    or third-generation sequences due to the high
                    computational cost of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analyzing sequences of length betw=
                    een 1Kb and 1Mb. Given the quadratic overhead
                    of exact pairwise
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms for long alignments, th=
                    e community primarily relies on approximate
                    algorithms that search
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">only for high-quality alignments a=
                    nd stop early when one is not found. In this
                    work, we present the first
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">GPU optimization of the popular X-=
                    drop alignment algorithm, that we named LOGAN.
                    Results show
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that our high-performance multi-GP=
                    U implementation achieves up to 181.6 GCUPS and
                    speed-ups up
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to 6.6</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-co= ntainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                    tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"610" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <= mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n Math=Class-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assist= ive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3=.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=
                                    =97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container>
                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">and 30.7</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-contain= er class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtm=enu_counter=3D"611" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mjx-m= ath class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-bin">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-m= ml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/=1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo=>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">using 1 and 6 NVIDIA Tesla V100, respecti=
                                            vely, over the state-of-the-art software
                                            running on two
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">IBM Power9 processors using 168 CP=
                                            U threads, with equivalent accuracy. We also
                                            demonstrate a
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">2.3</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-conta= iner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctx=tmenu_counter=3D"612" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx= -math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-bin">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive= -mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.or=g/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        display=3D"inline">
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97
                    </= mo></math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">LOGAN speed-up versus ksw2, a state-of-ar=
                        t vectorized algorithm for sequence alignment
                        implemented
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in minimap2, a long-read mapping s=
                        oftware. To highlight the impact of our work on
                        a real-world
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">application, we couple LOGAN with =
                        a many-to-many long-read alignment software
                        called BELLA, and
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate that our implementatio=
                        n improves the overall BELLA runtime by up to
                        10.6</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"613" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"m=jx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" displ=ay=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-assistive= -mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.

                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Finally, we adapt the Roofline mod=
                        el for LOGAN and demonstrate that our
                        implementation is near-optimal
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the NVIDIA Tesla V100s.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020g" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-452" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Chenglong. =E2=80=9CA New Perspective=
            of Graph Data and A Generic and Efficient Method
            for Large Scale Graph Data Traversal=E2=80=9D. In: (Sept. 2020)=
            . arXiv: 2009.07463 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">The BFS algorithm is a basic
                graph data processing algorithm and many other graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data processing algorithms have si=
                    milar architectural features with BFS algorithm
                    and can be built
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on the basis of BFS algorithm mode=
                    l. We analyze the differences between graph
                    algorithms and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">traditional high-performance algor=
                    ithms in detail, propose a new way of
                    classifying algorithms into
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data independent algorithm and dat=
                    a correlation algorithm based on their run-time
                    correlation with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data, and use this new classificat=
                    ion to explain the validity of the methods
                    proposed in this paper.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Through a deeper analysis of graph=
                    data, we propose a new fundamental perspective
                    on understanding
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">graph data, establishing a link be=
                    tween two basic data structures, graph and
                    tree, and viewing graph
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data as consisting of smaller subg=
                    raphs and edge trees. Small degree vertices are
                    found to be one of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">important cause of random memory a=
                    ccess. Based on this, we propose a general,
                    easy to implement,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and efficient method for graph dat=
                    a processing, with the basic idea of treating
                    low-degree vertices and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">core subgraphs separately, thus si=
                    gnificantly reducing the size of random memory
                    access and improving
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the efficiency of memory access. F=
                    inally, we evaluated the performance of the
                    method on three major
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data center computing platforms (I=
                    ntel, AMD, and ARM), and the experiments showed
                    that it brought
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">19.7%, 31.8% and 17.9% performance=
                    improvement, respectively, with a
                    performance-power ratio of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">282.70 MTEPS/s on the ARM platform=
                    , ranking it among the Green graph500 in
                    November 2019.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">World No. 1 on the big dataset lis=
                    t.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020d" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-453" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Guanglu, Douglas Allaire, and Jonatha=
            n Cagan. =E2=80=9CAn Initial Guess Free Method
            for Least Squares Parameter Estimation in Nonlinear Models=E2=
            =80=9D. en. In: (2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps"=>doi</span></span>:
            10.13140/RG.2.2.32573.82402.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Fitting models to data is
                critical in many science and engineering fields. A major tas=
                k in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fitting models to data is to estim=
                    ate the value of each parameter in a given
                    model. Iterative methods,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">such as the Gauss-Newton method an=
                    d the Levenberg-Marquardt method, are often
                    employed for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parameter estimation in nonlinear =
                    models. However, practitioners must guess the
                    initial value for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">each parameter in order to initial=
                    ize these iterative methods. A poor initial
                    guess can contribute to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">non-convergence of these methods o=
                    r lead these methods to converge to a wrong
                    solution. In this paper,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">an initial guess free method is in=
                    troduced to find the optimal parameter
                    estimators in a nonlinear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">model that minimizes the squared e=
                    rror of the fit. The method includes three
                    algorithms that require
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different level of computational p=
                    ower to find the optimal parameter estimators.
                    The method constructs
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a solution interval for each param=
                    eter in the model. These solution intervals
                    significantly reduce the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">search space for optimal parameter=
                    estimators. The method also provides an
                    empirical probability
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">distribution for each parameter, w=
                    hich is valuable for parameter uncertainty
                    assessment. The initial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">guess free method is validated thr=
                    ough a case study in which Fick=E2=80=99s second law is
                    fit to an experimental
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">data set. This case study shows th=
                    at the initial guess free method can find the
                    optimal parameter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">estimators efficiently. A four-ste=
                    p procedure for implementing the initial guess
                    free method in practice
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is also outlined.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020h" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-454" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">=E2=80=94 =E2=80=9CTaking the Guess Work Out=
            of the Initial Guess: A Solution Interval Method for
            Least Squares Parameter Estimation in Nonlinear Models=E2=80=9D=
            . In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of Computing and
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Information Science in Engineeri=
                ng </span>(Oct. 2020), pp. 1=E2=80=9361. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span
                    cla=ss=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>: 10.1115/1.4048811.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Fitting a specified model to data
                is critical in many science and engineering fields. A major
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">task in fitting a specified model =
                    to data is to estimate the value of each
                    parameter in the model.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Iterative local methods, such as t=
                    he Gauss-Newton method and the
                    Levenberg-Marquardt method,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are often employed for parameter e=
                    stimation in nonlinear models. However,
                    practitioners must guess
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the initial value for each paramet=
                    er to initialize these iterative local methods.
                    A poor initial guess can

                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">contribute to non-convergence of t=
                    hese methods or lead these methods to converge
                    to a wrong or inferior
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solution. In this paper, a solutio=
                    n interval method is introduced to find the
                    optimal estimator for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">each parameter in a nonlinear mode=
                    l that minimizes the squared error of the fit.
                    The method includes
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">three algorithms that require diff=
                    erent level of computational power to find the
                    optimal parameter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">estimators. The method constructs =
                    a solution interval for each parameter in the
                    model. These solution
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">intervals significantly reduce the=
                    search space for optimal parameter estimators.
                    The method also
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">provides an empirical probability =
                    distribution for each parameter, which is
                    valuable for parameter
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">uncertainty assessment. The soluti=
                    on interval method is validated through two
                    case studies in which
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the Michaelis-Menten model and Fic=
                    k=E2=80=99s second law are fit to experimental data
                    sets, respectively. These
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">case studies show that the solutio=
                    n interval method can find optimal parameter
                    estimators efficiently.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">A four-step procedure for implemen=
                    ting the solution interval method in practice
                    is also outlined.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-455" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Jingzhao et al. =E2=80=9COn Complexit=
            y of Finding Stationary Points of Nonsmooth Nonconvex
            Functions=E2=80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.04130 <span cl=ass=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">We provide the first </span><span class=3D"cmti-9">non-as=
                    ymptotic </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">analysis for finding stationary point=
                    s of
                    nonsmooth,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nonconvex functions. In particular=
                    , we study the class of Hadamard
                    semi-differentiable functions,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">perhaps the largest class of nonsm=
                    ooth functions for which the chain rule of
                    calculus holds. This
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">class contains important examples =
                    such as ReLU neural networks and others with
                    non-differentiable
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">activation functions. First, we sh=
                    ow that finding an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ctx=tMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"614" sty=le=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" a=ria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-=I"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" displ=ay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D="inline">
                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <s= pan class=3D"cmr-9">-stationary
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">point with first-order methods is =
                        impossible in finite time. Therefore, we
                        introduce the notion of</span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"615" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-=c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c1D6FF TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                        <mjx= -c class=3D"mjx-c2C">
                                            </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"2">
                                        <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I">
                                            </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class==3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                </mjx-= mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">
                            <= math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mrow>=
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>=CE=B4</mi>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathCl=ass-punc">,</mo>
                                        <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)=
                                    </mo>
                                </mrow></math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmti-=9">-stationarity</span><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">a generalization that allows for a=
                        point to be within distance </span>
                    <!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"616" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FF TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </m= jx-mi>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline">=
                            <math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi>=
                                    =CE=B4</mi>
                            </math>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">of an </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container= class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmen=u_counter=3D"617" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-mat= h class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c cla=ss=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unse=lectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Ma=th/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-m= ml>
                                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-stationary
                                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">point and reduces to </span>
                                <!-- l=
. 30 -->
                                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"618" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positio=n: relative;">
                                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                        <mjx-mi clas=s=3D"mjx-i">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-math>
                                    <= mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            xmlns=3D"htt=p://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                    </= math>
                            </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-stationarit=
                                y
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for smooth functions. We propose a=
                                series of randomized first-order methods and
                                analyze their complexity
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of finding a </span><!-- l. 30 -->=
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex==3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"619" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relat=ive;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mrow>
                                        <mjx-mo cl=ass=3D"mjx-n MathClass-open">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx= -mrow>
                                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FF TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                                </m= jx-mi>
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-punc">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2C">
                                        </mjx= -c>
                                        </mjx-mo>
                                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"2">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716=TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-mi>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">=
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    </mjx-mrow>
                                </mjx-math>
                                <mjx-assist= ive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3=.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mrow>
                                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-open=">(</mo><mrow><mi>=CE=B4</mi><mo class=3D"
                                                MathClass-punc">,</mo>
                                            <mi>=F0=9D=
                                                =9C=96</mi>
                                        </mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-close">)</mo>
                                        </mrow>
                                    </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">-stationary
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">point. Furthermore, we provide a l=
                                ower bound and show that our stochastic
                                algorithm has min-max
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">optimal dependence on </span>
                            <!-- =
l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"=tabindex=3D"0"
                                ctxtmenu_counter=3D"620" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; positi=on: relative;">
                                <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mi cla=ss=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D6FF TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                </mjx-math>=
                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                        xmlns=3D"ht=tp://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                        <mi>=CE=B4</mi>
                                    </math>=
                                </mjx-assistive-mml>
                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Empirically, our methods perform w=
                                ell for training ReLU neural networks.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020c" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-456" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Qingsong et al. =E2=80=9CFaster Stoch=
            astic Quasi-Newton Methods=E2=80=9D. In: (Apr. 2020). arXiv:
            2004.06479v1 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.OC]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Recently, stochastic optimization
                methods are a class of powerful optimization tools in
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">machine learning. Stochastic gradi=
                    ent descent (SGD) is one of the representative
                    stochastic methods
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and is widely used for many machin=
                    e learning problems. However, SGD only uses the
                    first-order
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">information of problems to optimiz=
                    e them, which results in its some limitations
                    such as its solutions
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">without high accuracy. Thus, stoch=
                    astic quasi-Newton methods recently have been
                    widely concerned
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">due to utilizing approximate Hessi=
                    an information, which is more robust and can
                    achieve better accuracy
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">than stochastic first-order method=
                    s. Considering that existing stochastic
                    quasi-Newton methods still
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">do not reach the best known stocha=
                    stic first-order oracle (SFO) complexity, thus,
                    we propose a novel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">faster stochastic quasi-Newton met=
                    hod (SpiderSQN) based on the variance reduced
                    technique of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">SIPDER. Moreover, we prove that ou=
                    r SpiderSQN method reach the best known SFO
                    complexity of</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"621" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-S=C"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mi>
                        <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-=open">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            <mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"=mjx-i">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"=mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2B"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>=
                                <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i" space=3D"3">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D45B TEX-I">
                                        </mjx= -c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c31"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-= mn>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2=F"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 T=EX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <m= jx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212">
                                            </mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mo>
                                    <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"=3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c32"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mn>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mj=x-n MathClass-close">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c29"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-mrow>
                        <= /mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mi mathvari=ant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(
                        </= mo>
                        <mrow>
                            <mi>n</mi>
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">+</mo>
                            <mi>n</mi>
                            <mn>1</mn>
                            <m= o class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=95</mo>
                                <mn>2</mn>
                                <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                <mo=class=3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                <mn>2</mn>
                        </mrow>
                        <mo class=3D"MathCla=ss-close">)</mo>
                        </mrow></math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">in the finite-sum setting to obtain an </= span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax==3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"622" style=3D"font-size: 117.=4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <= mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mi>
                                <= /mjx-math>
                                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                            x=mlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                            <mi>=F0=9D=
                                                =9C=96</mi>
                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9"=>-first-order
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">stationary point. To further impro=
                        ve its practical performance, we incorporate
                        SpiderSQN with different
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">effective momentum schemes. Moreov=
                        er, the proposed algorithms are generalized to
                        the online setting,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the corresponding SFO complexi=
                        ty of </span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attache=d_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"623" style=3D"font-si=ze: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D="true">
                            <mjx-mi class=3D"mjx-sc mjx-b mjx-i">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D4AA TEX-=SC"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mi>
                            <mjx-mrow space=3D"2">
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass=-open">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c28"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                                <mjx-mrow>
                                    <mjx-mi class=3D="mjx-i">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c1D716 TEX-I"></mjx-c>
                                    </mjx-mi>
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D="mjx-n MathClass-bin" space=3D"3">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c2212"></mjx-c>
                                        </mjx-= mo>
                                        <mjx-mn class=3D"mjx-n" space=3D"3">
                                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-c33"></mjx-c>
                                            </m= jx-mn>
                                </mjx-mrow>
                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-close">
                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mj=x-c29"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-mrow>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectab=le=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/Mat=hML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mi mathvariant=3D"bold-script">=F0=9D=92=AA</mi>
                                <m= row>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-open">(</mo>
                                    <mrow>
                                        <mi>=F0=9D=9C=96</mi>
                                        <mo class==3D"MathClass-bin">=E2=88=92</mo>
                                        <mn>3</mn>
                                    </mrow>
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-cl=ose">)</mo>
                                    </mrow>
                            </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">is developed, which matches the existing =
                        best result. Extensive experiments on benchmark
                        datasets
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">demonstrate that the proposed Spid=
                        erSQN-type of algorithms outperform
                        state-of-the-art algorithms
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for nonconvex optimization.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-457" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Yi et al. =E2=80=9COptimality-based d=
            omain reduction for
            inequality-constrained NLP and MINLP problems=E2=80=9D. In: <sp= an class=3D"cmti-10">Journal of Global
                Optimization
                </span>(Feb.
                2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi<= /span></span>:
                    10.1007/s10898-020-00886-z.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">In spatial branch-and-bound
                algorithms, optimality-based domain reduction is normally
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">performed after solving a node and=
                    relies on duality information to reduce ranges
                    of variables.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">In this work, we propose novel opt=
                    imality conditions for NLP and MINLP problems
                    and apply
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">them for domain reduction prior to=
                    solving a node in branch-and-bound. The
                    conditions apply to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">nonconvex inequality-constrained p=
                    roblems for which we exploit monotonicity
                    properties of objectives
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and constraints. We develop three =
                    separate reduction algorithms for
                    unconstrained, one-constraint,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and multi-constraint problems. We =
                    use the optimality conditions to reduce ranges
                    of variables through
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">forward and backward bound propaga=
                    tion of gradients respective to each decision
                    variable. We
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">describe an efficient implementati=
                    on of these techniques in the branch-and-bound
                    solver BARON. The
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementation dynamically recogni=
                    zes and ignores inactive constraints at each
                    node of the search
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tree. Our computations demonstrate=
                    that the proposed techniques often reduce the
                    solution time and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">total number of nodes for continuo=
                    us problems; they are less effective for
                    mixed-integer programs.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020f" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-458" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Ying, Zhiqiang Zhao, and Zhuo Feng. =
            =E2=80=9CSF-GRASS: Solver-Free Graph Spectral
            Sparsification=E2=80=9D. In: (Aug. 2020). arXiv: 2008.07633v1 <= span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.DS]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Recent spectral graph
                sparsification techniques have shown promising performance i=
                n
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">accelerating many numerical and gr=
                    aph algorithms, such as iterative methods for
                    solving large sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, spectral partitioning of=
                    undirected graphs, vectorless verification of
                    power/thermal grids,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">representation learning of large g=
                    raphs, etc. However, prior spectral graph
                    sparsification methods rely
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">on fast Laplacian matrix solvers t=
                    hat are usually challenging to implement in
                    practice. This work,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">for the first time, introduces a s=
                    olver-free approach (SF-GRASS) for spectral
                    graph sparsification
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by leveraging emerging spectral gr=
                    aph coarsening and graph signal processing
                    (GSP) techniques.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We introduce a local spectral embe=
                    dding scheme for efficiently identifying
                    spectrally-critical edges
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">that are key to preserving graph s=
                    pectral properties, such as the first few
                    Laplacian eigenvalues
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and eigenvectors. Since the key ke=
                    rnel functions in SF-GRASS can be efficiently
                    implemented using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">sparse-matrix-vector-multiplicatio=
                    ns (SpMVs), the proposed spectral approach is
                    simple to implement
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and inherently parallel friendly. =
                    Our extensive experimental results show that
                    the proposed method can
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">produce a hierarchy of high-qualit=
                    y spectral sparsifiers in nearly-linear time
                    for a variety of real-world,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">large-scale graphs and circuit net=
                    works when compared with the prior
                    state-of-the-art spectral method.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020e" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-459" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Yongzhe, Ariful Azad, and Ayd=C4=B1n =
            Bulu=C3=A7. =E2=80=9CParallel algorithms for finding connected
            components using linear algebra=E2=80=9D. In: <span class=3D"cm=ti-10">Journal of Parallel and Distributed
                Computing
            </span>(May
            2020). <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi<= /span></span>: 10.1016/j.jpdc.2020.04.009.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Finding connected components is
                one of the most widely used operations on a graph. Optimal
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">serial algorithms for the problem =
                    have been known for half a century, and many
                    competing parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms have been proposed over=
                    the last several decades under various
                    different models of parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computation. This paper presents a=
                    class of parallel connected-component
                    algorithms designed using
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">linear-algebraic primitives. These=
                    algorithms are based on a PRAM algorithm by
                    Shiloach and Vishkin
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and can be designed using standard=
                    GraphBLAS operations. We demonstrate two
                    algorithms of this
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">class, one named LACC for Linear A=
                    lgebraic Connected Components, and the other
                    named FastSV
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">which can be regarded as LACC=E2=
                    =80=99s simplification. With the support of the
                    highly-scalable Combinatorial
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">BLAS library, LACC and FastSV outp=
                    erform the previous state-of-the-art algorithm
                    by a factor of up
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">to 12x for small to medium scale g=
                    raphs. For large graphs with more than 50B
                    edges, LACC and FastSV
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">scale to 4K nodes (262K cores) of =
                    a Cray XC40 supercomputer and outperform
                    previous algorithms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">by a significant margin. This rema=
                    rkable performance is accomplished by (1)
                    exploiting sparsity that
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">was not present in the original PR=
                    AM algorithm formulation, (2) using
                    high-performance primitives of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Combinatorial BLAS, and (3) identi=
                    fying hot spots and optimizing them away by
                    exploiting algorithmic
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">insights.</span>

        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhang2020b" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-460" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhang, Zhekai et al. =E2=80=9CSpArch: Effici=
            ent Architecture for Sparse Matrix Multiplication=E2=80=9D. In:
            (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.08947 <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[cs.AR]<= /span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Generalized Sparse Matri</span>
                <!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"624" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </mjx-= c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-con= tainer><span class=3D"cmr-9">-Matri</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container clas=s=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_cou=nter=3D"625" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math cla=ss=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <= mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                    </mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unse=lectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Ma=th/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                </math=>
                        </mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">Multiplication (SpGEMM) is a ubiquitous t=
                        ask in various engineering and scientific
                        applications.
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">However, inner product based SpGEN=
                        N introduces redundant input fetches for
                        mismatched nonzero
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">operands, while outer product base=
                        d approach suffers from poor output locality
                        due to numerous partial
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">product matrices. Inefficiency in =
                        the reuse of either inputs or outputs data
                        leads to e</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJ=ax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"62=6" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-=TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c clas=s=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                        </mjx-math>
                        <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable==3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathM=L" display=3D"inline">
                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                            </math></mjx-a= ssistive-mml>
                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">tensive
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and e</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-con= tainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        c=txtmenu_counter=3D"627" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <m= jx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathC=lass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                            </mjx-mo>
                            </mjx-math>
                            <mjx-assisti= ve-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.=org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97=
                                    </mo>
                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">pensi=
                    ve
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">DRAM access. To address this probl=
                    em, this paper proposes an efficient sparse
                    matri</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ct=xtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"628" st=yle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" disp=lay=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">multiplication accelerator architecture, =
                    SpArch, which jointly optimizes the data
                    locality for both input
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and output matrices. We first desi=
                    gn a highly parallelized streaming-based merger
                    to pipeline the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">multiply and merge stage of partia=
                    l matrices so that partial matrices are merged
                    on chip immediately
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">after produced. We then propose a =
                    condensed matri</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMen=u_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"629" style==3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" ari=a-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx=-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" di=splay=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display==3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-m= ml>
                </mjx-container>
                <span class=3D"cmr-9">representation that reduces the number of=
                    partial matrices by three orders of magnitude
                    and thus
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">reduces DRAM access by 5.4</span>
                <= !-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CH=TML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"630" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; po=sition: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo= class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                </mjx-mo>
                </= mjx-math>
                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math
                        xm=lns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">
                        <mo class=3D"=MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                </mjx-container><span=class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We further develop a Huffman tree =
                    scheduler to improve the scalability of the
                    merger for larger sparse
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">matrices, which reduces the DRAM a=
                    ccess by another 1.8</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ct=xtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"631" st=yle=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX"=aria-hidden=3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" disp=lay=3D"inline">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We also resolve the increased inpu=
                    t matri</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attach=ed_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                    ctxtmenu_counter=3D"632" style=3D"font-s=ize: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                    <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden==3D"true">
                        <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                            <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                </= mjx-c>
                        </mjx-mo>
                    </mjx-math>
                    <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D="inline"><math
                            xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inli=ne">
                            <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                    </mjx= -container>
                    <span class=3D"cmr-9">read induced by the new representation us=
                        ing a row prefetcher with near-optimal buffer
                        replacement
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">policy, further reducing the DRAM =
                        access by 1.5</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_=Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                        ctxtmenu_counter=3D"633" style=3D"=font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                        <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hi=dden=3D"true">
                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                <mjx-c
                                    class=3D"mjx-cD7="></mjx-c></mjx-mo></mjx-math><mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"
                                    on" displa=y=3D"inline"><math xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                        display=3D"=inline">
                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                    <= /mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">.
                                        </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Evaluated on 20 benchmarks, SpArch=
                                            reduces the total DRAM access by 2.8</span>
                                        <!-- l. 30 -->
                                        <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached=_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                            tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"634" style=3D"font-siz=e: 117.4%;
                                            position: relative;">
                                            <mjx-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"=true">
                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClass-bin">
                                                    <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7">
                                                        </mjx-= c>
                                                </mjx-mo>
                                            </mjx-math>
                                            <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inl=ine"><math
                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display=3D"inline">=
                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                            </mjx-con= tainer>
                                            <span class=3D"cmr-9">over previous state-of-the-art. On averag=
                                                e, SpArch achieves 4</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-container class=3D"MathJax Ct=xtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                tabindex=3D"0" ctxtmenu_counter=3D"635" st=yle=3D"font-size: 117.4%;
