                                                        <mjx-c class=3D"=mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>
                                                    </mjx-mo>
                                                </mjx-math>
                                                <mjx-assistive-mml unselectable=3D"on"=display=3D"inline"><math
                                                        xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                        disp=lay=3D"inline">
                                                        <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</mo>
                                                    </math></mjx-assistiv= e-mml>
                                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">19</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                            <mjx-contai= ner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"

                        <mjx-assistive-= mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math

                                <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97</m= o>

                    </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">18</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                    <mjx-contai= ner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"

                </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">17</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                <mjx-contai= ner class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"

                            </mjx-container><span class=3D"cmr-9">,
                            </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">1285</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                            <mjx-cont= ainer class=3D"MathJax CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML" tabindex=3D"0"
                                ct=xtmenu_counter=3D"639" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position: relative;">
                                <mj= x-math class=3D"MJX-TEX" aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                    <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCl=ass-bin">
                                        <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>

                                    <mjx-assistiv= e-mml unselectable=3D"on" display=3D"inline"><math

                                        </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                        </mjx-container>
                                        <span class=3D"cmr-9">speedup and 6</span><!-- l. 30 -->

                                            tabindex=3D"0"=ctxtmenu_counter=3D"640" style=3D"font-size: 117.4%;

                                                position: relative;">

                                                                <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathCla=ss-bin">

                                                                    </math></mjx-assistive-mml>

                                                                    </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">62</span><!-- l. 30 -->
                                                                    <mjx-contai= ner class=3D"MathJax
                                                                        CtxtMenu_Attached_0" jax=3D"CHTML"
                                                                        tabindex=3D"0" ctxt=menu_counter=3D"644"
                                                                        style=3D"font-size: 117.4%; position:
                                                                        relative;">
                                                                        <mjx-= math class=3D"MJX-TEX"
                                                                            aria-hidden=3D"true">
                                                                            <mjx-mo class=3D"mjx-n MathClas=s-bin">
                                                                                <mjx-c class=3D"mjx-cD7"></mjx-c>

                                                                                display=3D"inline"><math
                                                                                    xmlns=3D"http://www.w3.org=/1998/Math/MathML"
                                                                                    display=3D"inline">
                                                                                    <mo class=3D"MathClass-bin">=C3=97
                                                                                        </m= o>
                                                                                </math></mjx-assistive-mml>
                                                                                </mjx-container>
                                                                                <span class=3D"cmr-9">energy savings
                                                                                    over OuterSPACE, MKL, cuSP=
                                                                                    ARSE, CUSP, and ARM Armadillo,
                                                                                    respectively.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zheng2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-461" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zheng, Qingqing, Yuanzhe Xi, and Yousef Saad=
            . =E2=80=9CA power Schur complement Low-Rank
            correction preconditioner for general sparse linear systems=E2=
            =80=9D. In: (Feb. 2020). arXiv: 2002.00917
            <span class=3D"cmtt-10">[math.NA]</span>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">An effective power based parallel
                preconditioner is proposed for general large sparse linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">systems. The preconditioner combin=
                    es a power series expansion method with some
                    low-rank correction
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">techniques, where the Sherman-Morr=
                    ison-Woodbury formula is utilized. A matrix
                    splitting of the Schur
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complement is proposed to expand t=
                    he power series. The number of terms used in
                    the power series
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">expansion can control the approxim=
                    ation accuracy of the preconditioner to the
                    inverse of the Schur
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">complement. To construct the preco=
                    nditioner, graph partitioning is invoked to
                    reorder the original
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">coefficient matrix, leading to a s=
                    pecial block two-by-two matrix whose two
                    off-diagonal submatrices
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">are block diagonal. Variables corr=
                    esponding to interface variables are obtained
                    by solving a linear
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">system with the coeffcient matrix =
                    being the Schur complement. For the variables
                    related to the interior
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">variables, one only needs to solve=
                    a block diagonal linear system. This can be
                    performed efficiently
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in parallel. Various numerical exa=
                    mples are provided to illustrate that the
                    efficiency of the proposed
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">preconditioner.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhou2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-462" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhou, Keren, Mark Krentel, and John Mellor-C=
            rummey. =E2=80=9CA tool for top-down performance
            analysis of GPU-accelerated applications=E2=80=9D. In: <span cl=ass=3D"cmti-10">Proceedings of the 25th ACM
                SIGPLAN
            </span><span class=3D"cmti-10">Symposium on Principles and Prac=
                tice of Parallel Programming</span>. PPoPP =E2=80=9920.
            ACM, Feb. 2020.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi</span>
                <= /span>: 10.1145/3332466.3374534.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">To support performance
                measurement and analysis of GPU-accelerated applications, we
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">extended the HPCToolkit performanc=
                    e tools with several novel features. To support
                    efficient monitoring
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">of accelerated applications, HPCTo=
                    olkit employs a new wait-free data structure to
                    coordinate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">measurement and attribution betwee=
                    n each application thread and a GPU monitor
                    thread. To help
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">developers understand the performa=
                    nce of accelerated applications, HPCToolkit
                    attributes metrics to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">heterogeneous calling contexts tha=
                    t span both CPUs and GPUs. To support
                    fine-grain analysis and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">tuning of GPU-accelerated code, HP=
                    CToolkit collects PC samples of both CPU and
                    GPU activity to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">derive and attribute metrics at al=
                    l levels in a heterogeneous calling
                    context.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zhu2020a" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-463" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zhu, Y., Y. Liu, and G. Zhang. =E2=80=9CFT-P=
            BLAS: PBLAS-based Fault-tolerant Linear Algebra
            Computation on High-performance Computing Systems=E2=80=9D. In:=
            <span class=3D"cmti-10">IEEE Access </span>(2020), pp.
            1=E2=80=931.
            <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">issn</span>=
            </span>: 2169-3536. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">doi=
                </span></span>: 10.1109/ACCESS.2020.2975832.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">As high-performance computing
                (HPC) systems have scaled up, resilience has become a
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">great challenge. To guarantee resi=
                    lience, various kinds of hardware and software
                    techniques have been
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">proposed. However, among popular s=
                    oftware fault-tolerant techniques, both the
                    checkpoint-restart
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">approach and the replication techn=
                    ique face challenges of scalability in the era
                    of peta- and
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">exa-scale systems due to their num=
                    erous processes. In this situation,
                    algorithm-based approaches, or
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithm-based fault tolerance (A=
                    BFT) mechanisms, have become attractive because
                    they are efficient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and lightweight. Although the ABFT=
                    technique is algorithm-dependent, it is
                    possible to implement it
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">at a low level (e.g., in libraries=
                    for basic numerical algorithms) and make it
                    application-independent.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">However, previous ABFT approaches =
                    have mainly aimed at achieving fault tolerance
                    in integrated
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">circuits (ICs) or at the architect=
                    ure level and are therefore not suitable for
                    HPC systems; e.g., they
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">use checksums of rows and columns =
                    of matrices rather than checksums of blocks to
                    detect errors.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">Furthermore, they cannot deal with=
                    errors caused by node failure, which are
                    common in current
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">HPC systems. To solve these proble=
                    ms, this paper proposes FT-PBLAS, a PBLAS-based
                    library for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">fault-tolerant parallel linear alg=
                    ebra computations that can be regarded as a
                    fault-tolerant version of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the parallel basic linear algebra =
                    subprograms (PBLAS), because it provides a
                    series of fault-tolerant
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">versions of interfaces in PBLAS. T=
                    o support the underlying error detection and
                    recovery mechanisms
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">in the library, we propose a block=
                    -checksum approach for non-fatal errors and a
                    scheme for addressing
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">node failure, respectively. We eva=
                    luate two fault-tolerant mechanisms and
                    FT-PBLAS on HPC systems,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">and the experimental results demon=
                    strate the performance of our library.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zou2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-464" class=3D"thebibliography">
        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zou, Qinmeng and Fr=C3=A9d=C3=A9ric Magoul=
            =C3=A8s. =E2=80=9CReducing the effect of global synchronization in
            delayed gradient methods for symmetric linear systems=E2=80=9D.=
            In: <span class=3D"cmti-10">Advances in Engineering
                Software</span>
            147 (Sept. 2020), p. 102837. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span cla=ss=3D"small-caps">doi</span></span>:
            10.1016/j.advengsoft.2020.102837.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">Compared with arithmetic
                operation, communication cost is often the bottleneck on mod=
                ern
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">computers, and thus should be paid=
                    increasing attention when choosing algorithms.
                    Lagged gradient
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">methods are known for their error =
                    tolerance and fast convergence. However, it
                    appears that their
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">parallel behavior is not well unde=
                    rstood. In this paper, we explore the cyclic
                    formulations of lagged
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">gradient methods and s-dimensional=
                    methods for reducing global synchronizations.
                    We provide parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">implementations for these methods =
                    and propose some new variants. A comparison is
                    then reported for
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">different gradient iterative schem=
                    es. To illustrate the performance, we run a
                    number of experiments,
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">from which we conclude that our fo=
                    rmulations perform better than traditional
                    methods in view of both
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">iteration count and computing time=
                    .</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
    <dt id=3D"X0-Zounon2020" class=3D"thebibliography">
    </dt>
    <dd id=3D"bib-465" class=3D"thebibliography">

        <!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent">Zounon, Mawussi et al. =E2=80=9CPerformance =
            Evaluation of Mixed PrecisionAlgorithms for
            Solving Sparse Linear Systems=E2=80=9D. In: (2020). MIMS EPrint=
            : MIMS EPrint:2020.21. <span class=3D"cmcsc-10"><span class=3D"small-caps">=
                    url</span></span>:
            <a href=3D"http://eprints.maths.manchester.ac.uk/2783/1/paper.p=df" class=3D"url"><span
                    class=3D"cmtt-10">http://eprints.maths.manchester.a=
                    c.uk/2783/1/paper.pdf</span></a>.
        </p><!-- l. 30 -->
        <p class=3D"noindent"><span class=3D"cmti-9">Abstract: </span>
            <spa= n class=3D"cmr-9">It is well established that mixed
                precision algorithms that factorize a matrix at a precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">lower than the working precision c=
                    an reduce the execution time and the energy
                    consumption of parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">solvers for dense linear systems. =
                    Much less is known about the efficiency of
                    mixed precision parallel
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms for sparse linear syste=
                    ms, and existing work focuses on single core
                    experiments. We evaluate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the benefits of using single preci=
                    sion arithmetic in solving a double precision
                    sparse linear systems
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">using multiple cores, focusing on =
                    the key components of LU factorization and
                    matrix=E2=80=93vector products.
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">We find that single precision spar=
                    se LU factorization is prone to a severe loss
                    of performance due to
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the intrusion of subnormal numbers=
                    . We identify a mechanism that allows cascading
                    fill-ins to generate
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">subnormal numbers and show that au=
                    tomatically flushing subnormals to zero avoids
                    the performance
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">penalties. Our results show that t=
                    he anticipated speedup of 2 over a double
                    precision LU factorization
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">is obtained only for the very larg=
                    est of our test problems. For iterative
                    solvers, we find that for the
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">majority of the matrices computing=
                    or applying incomplete factorization
                    preconditioners in single
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">precision does not present suffici=
                    ent performance benefits to justify the loss of
                    accuracy compared with
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">the use of double precision. We al=
                    so find that using single precision for the
                    matrix=E2=80=93vector product
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">kernels provides an average speedu=
                    p of 1.5 over double precision kernels, but new
                    mixed precision
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">algorithms are needed to exploit t=
                    his benefit without losing the performance gain
                    in the process of
                </span><span class=3D"cmr-9">refining the solution to double pr=
                    ecision accuracy.</span>
        </p>
    </dd>
</dl>
